```mermaid
    style OUT fill:#e1ffe1
    style APP1 fill:#f0e1ff
    style APP2 fill:#f0e1ff
    style APP3 fill:#f0e1ff
```

### Diagram 3: Amplitude vs. Speed Relationship (The Resonance Curve)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sub-critical<br/>omega less than omega_n<br/>Small stable deflection] -->|omega increases| B[Approaching Critical<br/>omega approx omega_n<br/>Deflection grows]
    B -->|omega = omega_n| C[CRITICAL SPEED Nc<br/>Theoretically infinite<br/>deflection no damping]
    C -->|omega increases further| D[Super-critical<br/>omega greater than omega_n<br/>Self-centering small deflection]
    style A fill:#e1ffe1
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#ff9999
    style D fill:#e1f5ff
```

### Diagram 4: Deflection Modes — Static vs. Whirling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Shaft at Rest] --> S2[Disc weight W<br/>causes static<br/>deflection delta<br/>downward]
    S2 --> S3[At Critical Speed<br/>omega = omega_n]
    S3 --> S4[Deflection rotates<br/>with the shaft<br/>forming a whirl orbit]
    S4 --> S5[Mass center orbits<br/>geometric center<br/>at radius equal<br/>to deflection]
    style S1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style S2 fill:#fff4e1
    style S3 fill:#ff9999
    style S4 fill:#ffe1f5
    style S5 fill:#e1ffe1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023] Define critical speed of a shaft. What is whirling?**

**Model Answer:**
The **critical speed** of a shaft is the rotational speed at which the shaft's frequency of rotation coincides with its natural frequency of transverse (lateral) vibration, causing resonance. At this speed, the shaft undergoes large lateral oscillations.

**Whirling** is the transverse (lateral) vibration of a rotating shaft, where the shaft's axis traces a whirling orbit around the bearing axis. It occurs when unbalanced mass forces the shaft to deflect sideways, and at critical speed, this deflection becomes violently large due to resonance.

(3 marks: 1.5 for definition, 1.5 for whirling)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] State the formula for critical speed of a simply supported shaft carrying a single concentrated load. Explain the significance of the static deflection $\delta$ in this formula.**

**Model Answer:**
The critical speed of a simply supported shaft with a single concentrated disc at its center is:

$$N_c = \dfrac{946}{\sqrt{\delta}} \quad \text{rpm, with } \delta \text{ in mm}$$

or equivalently in SI units:

$$N_c = 9.55 \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta}} \quad \text{rpm, with } \delta \text{ in m}$$

**Significance of $\delta$:** The static deflection $\delta$ is the vertical deflection of the shaft at the disc's location due to the weight of the disc when the shaft is stationary. It characterizes the **flexibility** of the shaft — a larger $\delta$ means a softer (more flexible) shaft, which leads to a **lower** critical speed. The shaft's stiffness $k$ is inversely related to $\delta$ as $k = W/\delta$.

(3 marks: 1 for formula, 2 for significance)

---

### Part B Question — Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the critical speed of a simply supported shaft carrying a single concentrated disc at its center. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A steel shaft of length $1.5 \, \text{m}$ and diameter $50 \, \text{mm}$ is simply supported at its ends. It carries two discs: disc 1 of mass $30 \, \text{kg}$ at a distance of $0.5 \, \text{m}$ from the left support, and disc 2 of mass $20 \, \text{kg}$ at the center (i.e., $0.75 \, \text{m}$ from the left support). Given $E = 2 \times 10^5 \, \text{N/mm}^2$, find the critical speed of the shaft using Dunkerley's method. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation of Critical Speed for Single Disc at Center:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The shaft is massless; all the mass is concentrated at the disc.
2. The shaft is simply supported (pinned-pinned).
3. The disc is rigid.
4. Damping is neglected.
5. The system is linear (small deflections, Hookean material).

**Step 1:** Static deflection of a simply supported beam under a central point load $W$:

$$\delta = \dfrac{W L^3}{48 E I}$$

**Step 2:** Equivalent spring stiffness:

$$k = \dfrac{W}{\delta}$$

**Step 3:** Natural frequency of transverse vibration (mass-spring system):

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{W / \delta}{m}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{m g / \delta}{m}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta}}$$

**Step 4:** Critical speed (by definition, $\omega_c = \omega_n$):

$$\omega_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta}}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to rpm:

$$N_c = \dfrac{60 \omega_c}{2 \pi} = \dfrac{60}{2 \pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta}} = 9.55 \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta}} \quad \text{rpm (with } \delta \text{ in m)}$$

For $\delta$ in mm, using $g = 9810 \, \text{mm/s}^2$:

$$N_c = \dfrac{946}{\sqrt{\delta}} \quad \text{rpm (with } \delta \text{ in mm)}$$

**(b) Dunkerley's Method Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the area moment of inertia:

$$I = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{64} = \dfrac{\pi (50)^4}{64} = \dfrac{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^6}{64} = 3.068 \times 10^5 \, \text{mm}^4$$

**Step 2:** Calculate static deflection at each disc's location due to its own weight alone.

For a simply supported beam of length $L = 1500 \, \text{mm}$ with a point load $W$ at distance $a$ from the left support and $b = L - a$ from the right support, the deflection at the load point is:

$$y = \dfrac{W a^2 b^2}{3 E I L}$$

**For Disc 1:** $W_1 = 30 \times 9.81 = 294.3 \, \text{N}$, $a_1 = 500 \, \text{mm}$, $b_1 = 1000 \, \text{mm}$.

$$y_1 = \dfrac{294.3 \times (500)^2 \times (1000)^2}{3 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 3.068 \times 10^5 \times 1500}$$

$$y_1 = \dfrac{294.3 \times 2.5 \times 10^{11}}{2.761 \times 10^{14}} = \dfrac{7.358 \times 10^{13}}{2.761 \times 10^{14}} \approx 0.2665 \, \text{mm}$$

**For Disc 2:** $W_2 = 20 \times 9.81 = 196.2 \, \text{N}$, $a_2 = 750 \, \text{mm}$, $b_2 = 750 \, \text{mm}$.

$$y_2 = \dfrac{196.2 \times (750)^2 \times (750)^2}{3 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 3.068 \times 10^5 \times 1500}$$

$$y_2 = \dfrac{196.2 \times 3.164 \times 10^{11}}{2.761 \times 10^{14}} = \dfrac{6.208 \times 10^{13}}{2.761 \times 10^{14}} \approx 0.2248 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate critical speed if only one disc were present.

For Disc 1 alone:

$$N_1 = \dfrac{946}{\sqrt{0.2665}} = \dfrac{946}{0.5162} \approx 1832.6 \, \text{rpm}$$

For Disc 2 alone:

$$N_2 = \dfrac{946}{\sqrt{0.2248}} = \dfrac{946}{0.4741} \approx 1995.4 \, \text{rpm}$$

**Step 4:** Apply Dunkerley's equation:

$$\dfrac{1}{N_c^2} = \dfrac{1}{N_1^2} + \dfrac{1}{N_2^2}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{N_c^2} = \dfrac{1}{(1832.6)^2} + \dfrac{1}{(1995.4)^2} = \dfrac{1}{3.358 \times 10^6} + \dfrac{1}{3.982 \times 10^6}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{N_c^2} = 2.978 \times 10^{-7} + 2.512 \times 10^{-7} = 5.490 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$N_c^2 = \dfrac{1}{5.490 \times 10^{-7}} = 1.821 \times 10^6$$

$$N_c = \sqrt{1.821 \times 10^6} \approx 1349.4 \, \text{rpm}$$

**Answer:** The critical speed of the shaft (by Dunkerley's method) is approximately **1349 rpm**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain Rayleigh's method of finding the critical speed of a shaft carrying multiple discs. State the energy equation and discuss why it gives an upper bound on the natural frequency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shaft carries three masses: $m_1 = 10 \, \text{kg}$ at a location with static deflection $y_1 = 5 \, \text{mm}$; $m_2 = 15 \, \text{kg}$ at $y_2 = 8 \, \text{mm}$; $m_3 = 8 \, \text{kg}$ at $y_3 = 4 \, \text{mm}$. Using Rayleigh's method, find the natural frequency of transverse vibration and the critical speed in rpm. Take $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Rayleigh's Method Explanation:**

Rayleigh's method uses the **principle of energy conservation** to estimate the natural frequency of a multi-degree-of-freedom system.

**Energy Equation:**

At the natural frequency, the maximum kinetic energy (KE) of the system equals the maximum potential energy (PE) stored in the elastic shaft:

$$\dfrac{1}{2} \omega_n^2 \sum m_i y_i^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} g \sum m_i y_i$$

Solving for $\omega_n$:

$$\omega_n^2 = \dfrac{g \sum m_i y_i}{\sum m_i y_i^2}$$

**Why it gives an upper bound:**
Rayleigh's method assumes that the shaft whirls in a mode shape identical to the **static deflection curve** under gravity. In reality, the actual dynamic mode shape is different (the inertia forces change the deflected shape). Because Rayleigh's assumption is **kinematically constrained** (the assumed mode shape is not the true one), the calculated natural frequency is **higher** than the actual value. By the Rayleigh-Ritz theorem, any assumed mode shape yields an upper bound on the lowest natural frequency.

**(b) Rayleigh's Method Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the weighted sum $\sum m_i y_i$ (with masses in kg and deflections in meters):

- $m_1 y_1 = 10 \times 0.005 = 0.05 \, \text{kg·m}$
- $m_2 y_2 = 15 \times 0.008 = 0.12 \, \text{kg·m}$
- $m_3 y_3 = 8 \times 0.004 = 0.032 \, \text{kg·m}$

$$\sum m_i y_i = 0.05 + 0.12 + 0.032 = 0.202 \, \text{kg·m}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the weighted sum $\sum m_i y_i^2$:

- $m_1 y_1^2 = 10 \times (0.005)^2 = 10 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{kg·m}^2$
- $m_2 y_2^2 = 15 \times (0.008)^2 = 15 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-5} = 9.6 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{kg·m}^2$
- $m_3 y_3^2 = 8 \times (0.004)^2 = 8 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-5} = 1.28 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{kg·m}^2$

$$\sum m_i y_i^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} + 9.6 \times 10^{-4} + 1.28 \times 10^{-4} = 1.338 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{kg·m}^2$$

**Step 3:** Compute the natural frequency:

$$\omega_n^2 = \dfrac{g \sum m_i y_i}{\sum m_i y_i^2} = \dfrac{9.81 \times 0.202}{1.338 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{1.9816}{1.338 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 1481.0 \, \text{rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{1481.0} \approx 38.48 \, \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to critical speed in rpm:

$$N_c = \dfrac{60 \times 38.48}{2 \pi} = \dfrac{2308.8}{6.2832} \approx 367.4 \, \text{rpm}$$

**Answer:** The natural frequency of transverse vibration is approximately **38.48 rad/s**, and the critical speed is approximately **367 rpm**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit consistency is critical.** A common mistake is mixing units — using $\delta$ in mm with $g$ in m/s² (or vice versa). Always convert all quantities to the same unit system **before** substitution.
> 2. **The constant 946 (not 187.7) is correct** for $\delta$ in mm. Some students memorize the wrong constant from poor notes. The derivation $N_c = (60/2\pi)\sqrt{9810/\delta}$ gives exactly 945.97, which rounds to **946**.
> 3. **Dunkerley's method gives a lower bound, Rayleigh's gives an upper bound.** Writing the wrong direction of inequality in a "compare and justify" question can cost 1-2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the conversion $\omega \to N$:** Writing $N_c = 9.55\sqrt{g/\delta}$ assumes $\delta$ in m. If you substitute $\delta$ in mm, your answer will be off by a factor of $\sqrt{1000} \approx 31.6$.
> 5. **Static deflection formula must match the loading configuration.** Using $\delta = WL^3/(48EI)$ is valid only for a simply supported beam with a **central** point load. For off-center loads, use $y = Wa^2b^2/(3EIL)$.
> 6. **Don't confuse critical speed with whirling speed.** They are numerically equal in the undamped case, but the conceptual distinction is important for descriptive questions.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Critical speed** = the rotational speed at which resonance occurs between shaft rotation and transverse vibration natural frequency.
- **Whirling** is the lateral vibration of a rotating shaft; the shaft axis traces an orbit around the bearing axis.
- **Core formula (single disc, massless shaft, central load):** $N_c = \dfrac{946}{\sqrt{\delta}}$ rpm, where $\delta$ is in mm.
- **Alternative core formula:** $N_c = 9.55 \sqrt{g/\delta}$ rpm, where $\delta$ is in m and $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$.
- **Underlying relation:** $\omega_c = \omega_n = \sqrt{g/\delta}$ in rad/s.
- **Static deflection for central load on simply supported beam:** $\delta = \dfrac{WL^3}{48EI}$.
- **Dunkerley's method (multiple discs):** $\dfrac{1}{N_c^2} = \sum \dfrac{1}{N_i^2}$ — gives a **lower bound** on critical speed.
- **Rayleigh's method:** $\omega_n^2 = \dfrac{g \sum m_i y_i}{\sum m_i y_i^2}$ — gives an **upper bound** on natural frequency.
- **Three operating regimes:** Sub-critical (stable, offset center), Critical (resonance, infinite deflection), Super-critical (self-centering).
- **Practical design rule:** Keep operating speed at least **20%** away from any critical speed (a safety margin to avoid resonance amplification).
- **Multiple critical speeds:** A shaft has theoretically infinite critical speeds; in practice, the **first critical speed** is the most important design consideration.
- **Effect of damping:** Real shafts have damping, which limits the peak deflection at critical speed to a finite value, but does not change the critical speed location.
- **Effect of gyroscopic moments:** At very high speeds, gyroscopic effects can split the single critical speed into two — one for forward whirling and one for backward whirling.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load Characteristics of Generators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST521 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Load Characteristics of Generators # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is "Load Characteristic" of a Generator? A **Load Characteristic** of a generator is the graphical (or mathematical) relationship between the **terminal voltage (V)** delivered to the external load and the **load current (IL)** drawn by that load, while the machine is operating at its **rated speed (N)** and with a given **field excitation (If)**. In KTU terminology, the **External Characteristic** (V vs IL) and the **Internal Characteristic** (Generated EMF Eg vs Armature current Ia) are the two mandatory curves a student must plot and interpret for any DC generator problem. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PENST521 – Module 1):** > Students must be able to draw and explain the **external characteristics of (i) Shunt, (ii) Series, and (iii) Compound DC generators**, compute **voltage regulation**, and identify the **critical field resistance** and **critical speed** from the **Open-Circuit Characteristics (OCC)**. ## 1.2 Why Do We Care About It? A generator is not an ideal voltage source. As the load increases: - The armature current increases. - The **armature reaction** weakens the main flux. - The **armature resistance drop (IaRa)** increases. - The **brush contact drop** increases. All these effects cause the terminal voltage V to **drop** as IL increases. Knowing exactly *how much* it drops is critical for designing power systems, selecting voltage regulators, and predicting the behaviour of motors fed by these generators. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – "The Leaky Garden Hose" Imagine a **municipal water pump** (the generator) connected to a long **rubber garden hose** (the armature winding with resistance Ra and brush contacts). | Hose Analogy | Generator Equivalent | |---|---| | Water pressure at the tap | Terminal Voltage (V) | | Rate of water flow | Load Current (IL) | | Friction losses inside the hose | Armature copper loss (Ia2Ra) | | Hose bulging when flow is high | Armature reaction (flux weakening) | | Pressure drop when many taps open | Voltage drop (V decreases) | When only **one tap is open** (no load), the pressure at the tap equals the pump pressure. When **ten taps are open** (full load), the pressure at the tap drops — because of friction and because the pump "sags" a bit. The plot of "pressure vs flow" is exactly the **external characteristic** of a generator. > [!NOTE] > **Definition – Voltage Regulation (KTU 2024 Scheme):** > Voltage Regulation is the *percentage change* in terminal voltage when full load is removed, keeping field excitation and speed constant. > $$\text{VR} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\,\%$$ > A *good* generator has **low** voltage regulation (voltage is "stiff"). ## 1.4 Types of DC Generators Covered in KTU Module 1 1. **Separately Excited DC Generator** – Field winding fed by an independent DC source. 2. **Self-Excited DC Generator**, which has three sub-types: - **Shunt Generator** – Field winding in *parallel* with the armature. - **Series Generator** – Field winding in *series* with the armature. - **Compound Generator** – Both shunt and series field windings present. - **Cumulative Compound** – Series flux *aids* shunt flux. - **Differential Compound** – Series flux *opposes* shunt flux. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Fundamental EMF Equation of a DC Generator Every DC generator obeys the same induced-EMF equation (KTU board favourite): $$E_g = \frac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A}$$ Where: - **Eg** = Generated EMF (in Volts) - **Φ** = Flux per pole (in Weber, Wb) - **Z** = Total number of armature conductors - **N** = Speed of the armature (in RPM) - **P** = Number of poles - **A** = Number of parallel paths in the armature - A = 2 for **wave winding** - A = P for **lap winding** > [!NOTE] > For a **shunt generator**, Φ is approximately constant (since If is nearly constant) so Eg ∝ N. > For a **series generator**, Φ ∝ Ise (before saturation), so Eg ∝ Ise. ## 2.2 Voltage Equation (KVL around the armature loop) Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law to the armature circuit of a DC generator: $$V = E_g - I_a R_a - V_{brush}$$ For KTU board problems, brush drop is often neglected or lumped into Ra, giving the simplified form: $$V = E_g - I_a R_a$$ ## 2.3 Current Relations (Three Different Generators) | Generator Type | Armature Current Ia | Field Current If | Load Current IL | |---|---|---|---| | Separately Excited | Ia = IL | Independent | IL | | Shunt | Ia = IL + Ish | Ish = V / Rsh | IL | | Series | Ia = Ise = IL | Ise = IL | IL | | Compound | Ia = IL + Ish | Ish = V / Rsh | IL | ## 2.4 External Characteristics – Behaviour of V as IL Increases ### (a) Shunt Generator - As IL rises, Ia rises slightly more (since Ia = IL + Ish). - IaRa drop **increases** (linear) → V falls. - Increased Ia also causes **armature reaction** which weakens Φ → Eg falls → V falls further. - Ish = V/Rsh decreases as V falls → field weakens → Eg falls further (a vicious cycle). - **Net result:** V drops by **5 % – 10 %** from no-load to full load. Curve is **drooping** (gentle downward slope). ### (b) Series Generator - Ia = IL = Ise. - At low loads, low Ise means low Φ → low Eg → low V. - As IL increases, Φ increases (until saturation) → Eg rises sharply. - After saturation, Φ is roughly constant, so V follows the equation **V = Eg − Ia(Ra + Rse)** and **droops linearly**. - **Net result:** The V–I curve is initially rising, reaches a maximum, and then **drops linearly**. This makes the series generator unsuitable for constant-voltage applications but ideal as a **constant-current source** (welding, arc lamps). ### (c) Compound Generator - **Cumulative Compound:** Series MMF *aids* shunt MMF. As load increases, Φ increases, which counteracts the IaRa drop. - If shunt MMF >> series MMF → **Under-Compound** (slight droop, similar to shunt). - If shunt MMF = series MMF effect → **Flat-Compound** (VNL ≈ VFL, ideal for lighting feeders). - If series MMF > shunt MMF → **Over-Compound** (VFL > VNL, used to compensate for feeder line drop). - **Differential Compound:** Series MMF *opposes* shunt MMF. As IL increases, Φ decreases sharply, causing V to drop very rapidly. This is **inherently unstable** and rarely used. ## 2.5 Internal Characteristics (Eg vs Ia) The internal characteristic plots the **generated EMF Eg** (before the IaRa drop) against armature current Ia. Since Eg = V + IaRa, the internal curve is **higher** than the external curve by an amount equal to IaRa at every point. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight (Board Marker Point):** > The internal characteristic represents the EMF **induced inside the machine**, while the external characteristic represents the voltage **delivered to the load**. Both curves start at the same point on the y-axis (no-load) but diverge as IL increases due to the IaRa drop. ## 2.6 Open-Circuit Characteristic (OCC) – The Magnetisation Curve The OCC is plotted with the generator running at **rated speed on no-load**, by varying the field current If from zero to a value giving about **125 % of rated voltage**. The terminal voltage V is recorded (since IL = 0, V = Eg). - Initially, the curve is **linear** (unsaturated region). - Then it **bends** (knee point) as iron saturates. - Finally, it **flattens out** (deep saturation). The slope of the linear part gives the **critical field resistance** concept used to determine whether a **self-excited shunt generator will build up voltage**. ## 2.7 Critical Resistance and Voltage Build-Up The **critical resistance Rc** of a shunt generator is the **maximum value of shunt field circuit resistance** (including the field regulator) at which the machine will just fail to build up voltage at a given speed. - The OCC is plotted in volts vs If. - A straight line is drawn from the origin with slope = **Rsh** (field circuit resistance). - If this line is **steeper** than the initial linear portion of OCC → no build-up (Rsh > Rc). - If this line is **flatter** than the initial linear portion → voltage builds up until the line meets the OCC. > [!TIP] > **Condition for self-excitation (shunt generator):** > Rsh < Rc **AND** residual magnetism must be present in the field poles. ## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | Unit / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ | Generated EMF equation | Volts | | 2 | $V = E_g - I_a R_a$ | Terminal voltage (no brush drop) | Volts | | 3 | $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$ | Shunt/Compound armature current | Ampere | | 4 | $I_{sh} = V / R_{sh}$ | Shunt field current | Ampere | | 5 | $\text{VR}\% = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100$ | Voltage regulation | Percentage | | 6 | $R_c = \dfrac{V_{oc}}{I_{f,oc}}$ (slope of tangent at linear part) | Critical field resistance | Ohms | | 7 | $P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 R_a$ | Armature copper loss | Watts | | 8 | $P_{cu,sh} = I_{sh}^2 R_{sh} = V I_{sh}$ | Shunt field copper loss | Watts | | 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{V I_L}{V I_L + I_a^2 R_a + V I_{sh} + P_{iron} + P_{mech}}$ | Efficiency (DC generator) | Per-unit | | 10 | Condition for max efficiency: $I_a^2 R_a = \text{Constant Losses}$ | Variable loss = Constant loss | — | > [!NOTE] > All constants like **Φ, Z, N, P, A** in row 1 are **per machine** and must be taken from the nameplate or the problem statement. ## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Shunt generators** are used for **lighting and battery charging** (nearly constant V). - **Series generators** were historically used for **arc lamps** (constant current) and as **boosters** in feeders. - **Cumulative compound generators** dominate as **DC welding generators**, **railway traction generators**, and **standby DC power supplies** in industries. - The **OCC** is the foundation of the **synchronous generator's Open-Circuit Characteristic** used later in Module 2 of the KTU syllabus. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 – EMF Equation of a DC Generator (Eg = ΦZNP/60A) **Statement:** Derive the expression for the EMF induced in a DC generator. **Given:** A multi-pole DC generator with **P** poles, **Z** total armature conductors, running at **N** RPM, with **A** parallel paths, each conductor cutting a flux of **Φ** Weber per pole per revolution. **Proof:** **Step 1 — Flux cut by one conductor per revolution.** Each conductor moves past **P** poles in one complete revolution, so the flux cut per conductor per revolution is: $$\text{Flux per conductor per rev} = \Phi \times P \text{ Weber}$$ **Step 2 — Revolutions per second.** The armature rotates at N revolutions per minute, so revolutions per second is: $$\text{Revs/sec} = \frac{N}{60}$$ **Step 3 — Average EMF induced in one conductor.** By Faraday's law, the average EMF in one conductor is: $$e_{\text{avg}} = \Phi \times P \times \frac{N}{60} \text{ Volts}$$ **Step 4 — Total EMF in the armature.** The Z conductors are distributed among **A** parallel paths, so each path has **Z/A** conductors connected in **series**. The total EMF across the armature (across the brushes) is the sum of EMFs in series in one path: $$E_g = \frac{Z}{A} \times e_{\text{avg}} = \frac{Z}{A} \times \Phi \times P \times \frac{N}{60}$$ **Step 5 — Final expression:** $$E_g = \frac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A} \;\; \text{Volts}$$ > **Q.E.D.** --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 – Voltage Regulation of a Shunt Generator (Worked Numerical) **Problem Statement (KTU Style):** A 10 kW, 250 V, 1000 RPM DC shunt generator has an armature resistance of **0.1 Ω** and a shunt field resistance of **250 Ω**. The armature reaction weakens the field by **4 %** at full load. The rotational losses (iron + friction + windage) total **500 W**. Calculate: (a) The voltage regulation. (b) The efficiency at full load. (c) The condition for maximum efficiency and the load at which it occurs. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Find rated load current.** The rated output is 10 kW at 250 V, so: $$I_L = \frac{P}{V} = \frac{10000}{250} = 40 \text{ A}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the shunt field current.** $$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{250} = 1 \text{ A}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the armature current at full load.** $$I_a = I_L + I_{sh} = 40 + 1 = 41 \text{ A}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the no-load generated EMF.** The full-load generated EMF (Eg,FL) is: $$E_{g,FL} = V_{FL} + I_a R_a = 250 + (41)(0.1) = 250 + 4.1 = 254.1 \text{ V}$$ Since armature reaction reduces the effective flux by 4 % at full load, the no-load generated EMF (at the same If and N) is **higher** by 4 %: $$E_{g,NL} = \frac{E_{g,FL}}{1 - 0.04} = \frac{254.1}{0.96} = 264.69 \text{ V}$$ **Step 5 — Voltage regulation.** At no-load, terminal voltage VNL = Eg,NL = 264.69 V (since Ia ≈ 0 at no-load). At full load, VFL = 250 V. $$\text{VR} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100 = \frac{264.69 - 250}{250} \times 100$$ $$\text{VR} = \frac{14.69}{250} \times 100 = 5.876\,\%$$ **Step 6 — Efficiency at full load.** First, compute the losses: Armature copper loss: $$P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 R_a = (41)^2 \times 0.1 = 168.1 \text{ W}$$ Shunt field copper loss: $$P_{cu,sh} = V I_{sh} = 250 \times 1 = 250 \text{ W}$$ Constant (rotational) losses: $$P_{const} = 500 \text{ W}$$ Total losses: $$P_{loss} = 168.1 + 250 + 500 = 918.1 \text{ W}$$ Output power: $$P_{out} = 10\,000 \text{ W}$$ Input power: $$P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{loss} = 10\,000 + 918.1 = 10\,918.1 \text{ W}$$ Efficiency: $$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{10000}{10918.1} \times 100 = 91.59\,\%$$ **Step 7 — Condition for maximum efficiency.** Maximum efficiency occurs when **variable loss = constant loss**: $$I_a^2 R_a = P_{const}$$ $$I_a^2 (0.1) = 500 \implies I_a^2 = 5000 \implies I_a = 70.71 \text{ A}$$ The corresponding load current: $$I_L = I_a - I_{sh} = 70.71 - 1 = 69.71 \text{ A}$$ The load at maximum efficiency is therefore approximately **1.74 times the rated load** (since rated IL = 40 A). In practice, generators are *not* operated at this point because it would cause overheating, but the theoretical value is the design target. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 – Condition for Maximum Efficiency of a DC Generator (General) **Statement:** Show that the efficiency of a DC generator is maximum when the **variable (copper) loss equals the constant (iron + mechanical) loss**. **Proof:** **Step 1 — Write the efficiency expression.** For a DC generator (neglecting brush loss): $$\eta = \frac{V I_L}{V I_L + P_{cu} + P_c}$$ where: - **Pcu** = total copper loss (variable, ∝ I2) - **Pc** = total constant loss (iron + mechanical + stray) **Step 2 — Approximation for analysis.** We assume V ≈ constant (shunt generator). Also, for small load, Ish is small, so IL ≈ Ia. The copper loss is: $$P_{cu} \approx I_a^2 R_a \quad (\text{variable})$$ **Step 3 — Define the efficiency function.** $$\eta = \frac{V I_a}{V I_a + I_a^2 R_a + P_c} = \frac{V I_a}{I_a (V + I_a R_a) + P_c}$$ **Step 4 — Differentiate η with respect to Ia and set to zero.** $$\frac{d\eta}{dI_a} = \frac{(V + 2 I_a R_a)(V I_a + I_a^2 R_a + P_c) - V I_a (V + 2 I_a R_a)}{(V I_a + I_a^2 R_a + P_c)^2} = 0$$ **Step 5 — Simplify the numerator.** $$(V I_a + I_a^2 R_a + P_c) - V I_a = 0 \implies I_a^2 R_a + P_c - V I_a = 0$$ Wait — let us re-derive more cleanly. Rewrite η as: $$\eta = \frac{V I_a}{V I_a + I_a^2 R_a + P_c}$$ Dividing numerator and denominator by V Ia: $$\eta = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{I_a R_a}{V} + \dfrac{P_c}{V I_a}}$$ **Step 6 — Minimise the denominator.** Let $f(I_a) = \dfrac{I_a R_a}{V} + \dfrac{P_c}{V I_a}$ $$\frac{df}{dI_a} = \frac{R_a}{V} - \frac{P_c}{V I_a^2} = 0$$ $$R_a = \frac{P_c}{I_a^2} \implies I_a^2 R_a = P_c$$ **Step 7 — Conclusion:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Maximum efficiency occurs when:** > $$\boxed{P_{cu} = I_a^2 R_a = P_c = \text{constant loss}}$$ > Equivalently: the load current at maximum efficiency is $I_a = \sqrt{P_c / R_a}$. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Comparison of External Characteristics | Property | Shunt | Series | Cumulative Compound | Differential Compound | |---|---|---|---|---| | V at no load | Eg0 | Low (residual only) | Eg0 | Eg0 | | V at full load | Slightly less than Eg0 | Lower than peak | ≈ Eg0 (flat) or higher (over) | Much lower | | Voltage regulation | 5 % – 10 % | Very high | Near zero (flat) or negative (over) | Very high, unstable | | Self-excitation? | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | | Load type | Constant V | Constant I (approx) | Constant V (flat) | Special (arc furnaces) | | Typical use | Lighting, charging | Welding (older), boosters | Traction, welding | Arc welding (stabilised) | --- ## 3.5 Sample KTU 3-Mark Style Quick Computation **Problem:** A 220 V shunt generator supplies a load of 50 A. The armature and shunt field resistances are 0.05 Ω and 110 Ω respectively. Find the EMF generated. **Step 1:** Find Ish = V/Rsh = 220/110 = **2 A**. **Step 2:** Find Ia = IL + Ish = 50 + 2 = **52 A**. **Step 3:** Apply the voltage equation: Eg = V + IaRa = 220 + (52)(0.05) = 220 + 2.6 = **222.6 V**. > **Answer:** Eg = **222.6 V**. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture – How a DC Generator's Terminal Voltage Reacts to Load ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Mechanical Input
Shaft Torque x Speed N] --> B[Armature Rotation
in Magnetic Field Phi] B --> C[Faraday Induction
EMF Eg = Phi Z N P / 60A] C --> D[Armature Circuit
with Resistance Ra] D --> E[Brush Contact Drop
V_brush neglected here] E --> F[Terminal Voltage V
Delivered to External Load] G[External Load
Current IL] --> H[Load Current Splits
IL and Ish] H --> I[Shunt Field Winding
with Resistance Rsh] I --> J[Field Flux Phi
depends on Ish] J --> C K[Armature Reaction
Demagnetising Effect] --> J F --> L[External Characteristic
V vs IL Curve] C --> M[Internal Characteristic
Eg vs Ia Curve] L --> N[Voltage Regulation
VR percent] M --> N N --> O[Design Decision
Select Compound Level] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture – Comparison of Generator Types by Field Connection ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SEP[Separately Excited DC Generator] A1[Battery / External DC] --> A2[Field Winding F] A3[Armature A] --> A4[Load] end subgraph SHU[Shunt DC Generator] B1[Armature A] --> B2[Node V] B2 --> B3[Field Winding in Parallel Rsh] B2 --> B4[External Load] end subgraph SER[Series DC Generator] C1[Armature A] --> C2[Series Field Fse] C2 --> C3[External Load] end subgraph COM[Compound DC Generator] D1[Armature A] --> D2[Series Field Fse] D2 --> D3[Node V] D3 --> D4[Shunt Field in Parallel Rsh] D3 --> D5[External Load] end ``` ## 4.3 Decision Flow – Selecting the Right Generator Type ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Need a DC Generator] --> Q1{Load requires constant voltage?} Q1 -->|Yes, light loads| S1[Use Shunt Generator] Q1 -->|No, requires V rise with load| S2[Use Over-Compound Generator] Q1 -->|No, requires nearly flat V| S3[Use Flat-Compound Generator] Start --> Q2{Load requires constant current?} Q2 -->|Yes, arc or welding| S4[Use Series or Differential Compound] Start --> Q3{Need precise V control independent of load?} Q3 -->|Yes| S5[Use Separately Excited with AVR] ``` ## 4.4 Self-Excitation Build-Up Process (Shunt Generator) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Res as Residual Flux participant Arm as Armature participant Fld as Field Winding Rsh participant V as Terminal Voltage Res->>Arm: Small EMF (say 2 to 5 V) induced Arm->>Fld: Small current Ish flows Fld->>Res: Flux increases (mutual reinforcement) Res->>Arm: Higher EMF induced Arm->>Fld: Higher Ish Fld->>Res: Flux rises more Note over Arm,V: Repeats until OCC and Field R-line intersect V->>V: Steady-state voltage = Intersection Point ``` > [!NOTE] > This **positive-feedback loop** is what makes the shunt generator a "self-excited" machine. If residual magnetism is lost (e.g., the machine has been idle for months or subjected to a strong opposite field), the machine will fail to build up — a common **practical troubleshooting scenario** in KTU labs. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define voltage regulation of a DC generator. Why is a low value of voltage regulation desirable?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Voltage regulation is defined as the change in terminal voltage when the load is removed (full load to no load), expressed as a percentage of the full-load voltage, while keeping the field excitation and speed constant: $$\text{VR}\% = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100$$ A **low** voltage regulation is desirable because it indicates that the terminal voltage remains nearly constant irrespective of load fluctuations. This is essential for: - Lighting circuits (avoiding flickering). - DC motors fed from the generator (constant speed). - Sensitive electronic loads. A high regulation means the voltage sags under load, leading to poor equipment performance and the need for additional voltage regulators. *(1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark justification)* --- ### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Distinguish between the external and internal characteristics of a DC generator.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | External Characteristic | Internal Characteristic | |---|---|---| | Y-axis | Terminal voltage **V** | Generated EMF **Eg** | | X-axis | Load current **IL** | Armature current **Ia** | | Physical meaning | Voltage at the load terminals | EMF induced inside the armature | | Shape (shunt) | Drooping curve | Slightly higher drooping curve | | Relation | Eg = V + IaRa | Eg > V by IaRa at every load point | *(1 mark each row)* --- ## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B questions in the End Semester Examination carry **14 marks** and may have an **internal choice**. Two alternative questions are provided below; the student answers **ONE**. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] **(a)** Draw and explain the **external characteristics of (i) shunt, (ii) series, and (iii) cumulative compound DC generators**. Discuss how armature reaction affects each curve. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 250 V, 25 kW DC shunt generator has an armature resistance of 0.15 Ω and a shunt field resistance of 100 Ω. The rotational losses are 800 W. Armature reaction causes a 5 % reduction in flux at full load. Calculate: 1. The generated EMF at full load. 2. The voltage regulation. 3. The efficiency at full load. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Shunt Generator External Characteristic:** The V–IL curve for a shunt generator **droops gently** as IL increases. Three causes work together: - **IaRa drop** (linear) increases with load. - **Armature reaction** (demagnetising) reduces Φ → reduces Eg. - Reduced V also reduces Ish → reduces Φ further → Eg falls more. ``` V ^ | * | * | * (Shunt - drooping curve) | * | * | *________________> I_L ``` **Series Generator External Characteristic:** The curve has **three regions**: - **Initial rising** (unsaturated) — V rises as Ise = IL rises, building Φ. - **Knee** — saturation begins. - **Final linear drop** — V = Eg − Ia(Ra + Rse) falls as load copper drop dominates. **Cumulative Compound Generator External Characteristic:** The series field MMF *aids* the shunt field MMF. As IL rises, the **demagnetising armature reaction is partially or fully compensated** by the series MMF: - **Under-compound:** V drops slightly (like shunt). - **Flat-compound:** V remains nearly constant. - **Over-compound:** V rises with load (compensates feeder drop). *(3 marks for diagram + 2 marks for armature reaction explanation + 2 marks for comparison.)* #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** VFL = 250 V, Pout = 25 kW, Ra = 0.15 Ω, Rsh = 100 Ω, Prot = 800 W, AR weakening = 5 %. **Step 1: Load current at full load.** $$I_L = \frac{25000}{250} = 100 \text{ A}$$ **Step 2: Shunt field current.** $$I_{sh} = \frac{250}{100} = 2.5 \text{ A}$$ **Step 3: Armature current.** $$I_a = I_L + I_{sh} = 100 + 2.5 = 102.5 \text{ A}$$ **Step 4: Generated EMF at full load.** $$E_{g,FL} = V_{FL} + I_a R_a = 250 + (102.5)(0.15) = 250 + 15.375 = 265.375 \text{ V}$$ **Step 5: Generated EMF at no-load** (no armature reaction effect, and Ia is negligible so VNL ≈ Eg,NL). Since AR reduces flux by 5 % at full load, the no-load flux is higher, so the no-load generated EMF (at the *same* field current and speed) is: $$E_{g,NL} = \frac{E_{g,FL}}{1 - 0.05} = \frac{265.375}{0.95} = 279.34 \text{ V}$$ Therefore, VNL = 279.34 V. **Step 6: Voltage regulation.** $$\text{VR}\% = \frac{279.34 - 250}{250} \times 100 = \frac{29.34}{250} \times 100 = 11.74\,\%$$ **Step 7: Efficiency at full load.** Armature copper loss: $$P_{cu,a} = (102.5)^2 \times 0.15 = 1575.94 \text{ W}$$ Shunt field copper loss: $$P_{cu,sh} = (250)(2.5) = 625 \text{ W}$$ Total losses: $$P_{loss} = 1575.94 + 625 + 800 = 3000.94 \text{ W}$$ $$\eta = \frac{25000}{25000 + 3000.94} \times 100 = \frac{25000}{28000.94} \times 100 = 89.28\,\%$$ **Final Answers:** - Eg,FL ≈ **265.38 V** - VR ≈ **11.74 %** - η ≈ **89.28 %** *(Distribution: 2 marks for setup, 2 marks for Eg,FL, 2 marks for VR, 1 mark for efficiency.)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **Open-Circuit Characteristic (OCC)** of a DC generator. Define **critical field resistance** and explain the **conditions for voltage build-up in a self-excited shunt generator**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 4-pole, 500 V, wave-wound DC shunt generator has 600 armature conductors. The armature resistance is 0.2 Ω and the shunt field resistance is 200 Ω. The flux per pole is 0.02 Wb. The machine supplies a load of 100 A. Calculate: 1. The speed at which the machine must be driven. 2. The armature current. 3. The copper losses in the armature and field. 4. The electromagnetic (developed) torque. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **OCC Definition and Sketch:** The OCC (also called the magnetisation curve or no-load saturation curve) is the plot of the **no-load terminal voltage V** (which equals generated EMF Eg since Ia = 0) versus the **field current If**, with the machine running at **constant rated speed N**. ``` V (or Eg) ^ | ___________________ | / | / (Knee point) | / | / (Linear / unsaturated region) | / | / (Residual magnetism) | /______________________________> I_f ``` **Three Regions:** - **Foot of the curve** — voltage due to residual magnetism. - **Linear region** — Φ ∝ If (iron unsaturated). - **Knee and saturation region** — Φ increases slowly, Eg levels off. **Critical Field Resistance (Rc):** The critical field resistance is the slope of the tangent drawn to the **initial linear portion** of the OCC. Numerically, Rc is the value of field circuit resistance above which the machine **fails to build up voltage** at the given speed. **Conditions for Self-Excitation Build-Up (Shunt Generator):** 1. There must be **residual magnetism** in the field poles. 2. The **shunt field circuit resistance Rsh must be less than the critical resistance Rc**. 3. The **field winding must be connected in such a polarity** that its MMF *aids* the residual flux (positive feedback). 4. The **speed must be above the critical speed** for the given Rsh. The build-up stops when the straight line **V = If Rsh** (field rheostat line) intersects the OCC. *(3 marks for OCC sketch + 2 marks for Rc definition + 2 marks for build-up conditions.)* #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** P = 4, V = 500 V, wave wound → **A = 2**, Z = 600, Ra = 0.2 Ω, Rsh = 200 Ω, Φ = 0.02 Wb, IL = 100 A. **Step 1: Armature current.** $$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{500}{200} = 2.5 \text{ A}$$ $$I_a = I_L + I_{sh} = 100 + 2.5 = 102.5 \text{ A}$$ **Step 2: Generated EMF.** $$E_g = V + I_a R_a = 500 + (102.5)(0.2) = 500 + 20.5 = 520.5 \text{ V}$$ **Step 3: Speed from EMF equation.** $$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A} \implies N = \frac{E_g \cdot 60 \cdot A}{\Phi Z P}$$ $$N = \frac{520.5 \times 60 \times 2}{0.02 \times 600 \times 4} = \frac{62460}{48} = 1301.25 \text{ RPM}$$ **Step 4: Copper losses.** Armature copper loss: $$P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 R_a = (102.5)^2 \times 0.2 = 2101.25 \text{ W}$$ Shunt field copper loss: $$P_{cu,sh} = V I_{sh} = 500 \times 2.5 = 1250 \text{ W}$$ **Step 5: Electromagnetic (developed) torque.** Mechanical power developed (Pm) equals the electrical power converted from mechanical to electrical form: $$P_m = E_g \times I_a = 520.5 \times 102.5 = 53351.25 \text{ W}$$ Angular speed in rad/s: $$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1301.25}{60} = 136.27 \text{ rad/s}$$ Torque: $$T_e = \frac{P_m}{\omega} = \frac{53351.25}{136.27} = 391.43 \text{ N·m}$$ **Final Answers:** - Ia = **102.5 A** - N = **1301.25 RPM** - Pcu,a = **2101.25 W**, Pcu,sh = **1250 W** - Te = **391.43 N·m** *(Distribution: 2 marks for Ia and Eg, 2 marks for N, 1 mark for copper losses, 2 marks for torque calculation.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Using V instead of Eg in the EMF equation.** Students often write Eg = V (which is true only at no-load). Induced EMF must include the IaRa drop. > > 2. **Confusing IL with Ia.** For a shunt generator, Ia = IL + Ish. Many students forget to add the 1–3 A field current, losing 1–2 marks. > > 3. **Armature reaction sign error.** AR *reduces* the flux, so the no-load EMF is *higher* than the full-load EMF for the same If. Students sometimes add the percentage *to* the full-load value instead of dividing by (1 − AR). > > 4. **Brush contact drop ignored in board problems** unless specified. The simplified equation V = Eg − IaRa is used when brush drop is "neglected" — make sure the problem states this. > > 5. **Unit mismatch in torque.** Power in Watts and speed in rad/s give T in N·m. If the student uses N in RPM without converting, the torque answer will be 9.55 times too large (since 1/ω = 60/(2πN) = 9.55/N). > > 6. **Series generator OC voltage** — at zero load current, the output voltage is just the residual EMF (≈ 2–5 % of rated). Students wrongly assume it is zero or rated value. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **EMF Equation:** $E_g = \Phi Z N P / (60 A)$. **Memorise this verbatim.** - **Voltage Equation:** $V = E_g - I_a R_a$ (no-load: V = Eg). - **Shunt generator** has **drooping** V–IL curve (VR ≈ 5–10 %). - **Series generator** V–I curve is **non-monotonic** (rises, peaks, falls linearly). - **Cumulative compound** can be *under*, *flat*, or *over-compounded* depending on series MMF strength. - **Differential compound** is unstable; used only in arc welding for current stabilisation. - **Voltage Regulation** = (VNL − VFL) / VFL × 100 %. - **OCC** is the magnetisation curve; it is the same for all DC generators of similar frame size. - **Critical resistance** = slope of tangent to initial linear part of OCC. - **Build-up conditions:** Residual magnetism + Rsh < Rc + correct polarity. - **Maximum efficiency:** when variable loss = constant loss, i.e., Ia2Ra = Pconstant. - **Electromagnetic torque:** $T_e = P_m / \omega = (E_g \times I_a) / \omega$, with ω in **rad/s**. - **Wave winding:** A = 2; **Lap winding:** A = P. Always confirm from the problem statement. - **Always** convert RPM to rad/s when computing torque: $\omega = 2\pi N/60$. - **Parallel paths A** is **even** for DC machines; A is **always 2 or a multiple of 2**. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic for KTU Board Exam — "VIRA":** > **V**oltage equation → **I**aRa drop → **R**eduction in flux (AR) → **A**RMATURE reaction effects. > Use VIRA to remember the four reasons V drops as IL increases.

topics.title: Losses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST521 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Losses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Direct Current (DC) Machines**, the term **"Losses"** refers to the total amount of input electrical power (in case of a motor) or input mechanical power (in case of a generator) that is converted into undesirable forms of energy — primarily **heat, sound, and mechanical friction** — instead of being transformed into the intended useful output. Mathematically, it is the algebraic difference between **Input Power** and **Output Power**.

$$P_{\text{loss}} = P_{\text{input}} - P_{\text{output}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Standard Terminology):**
> Losses in a DC machine are categorized into **electrical losses** (copper + iron), **mechanical losses** (friction + windage), and **stray load losses**. The **efficiency** of the machine is the ratio of useful power output to total power input, expressed as a percentage.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are filling a large water tank using a long hose connected to a municipal pump.

- The **water that actually reaches the tank** is your *useful output*.
- However, some water **leaks out through cracks in the hose** (this is like **copper losses** — power lost as heat in windings due to resistance).
- Some water **evaporates** as it flows (like **iron/core losses** — energy lost in magnetizing the iron core).
- Some water is **lost to splashing and friction** at the nozzle (like **mechanical losses** — friction at bearings and air resistance on the rotating part).
- A tiny amount is **lost to spillage** at the tank mouth (like **stray load losses** — small unaccounted losses due to leakage flux and harmonics).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Goal of an Electrical Machine Designer:** Minimize these losses to maximize efficiency. KTU frequently tests both the *identification* of these losses and the *numerical calculation* of efficiency under varying load conditions.

## Classification of Losses — Master Overview

The losses in a DC machine are broadly classified as follows:

| **S.No.** | **Loss Category** | **Sub-Category** | **Where it Occurs** |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | **Electrical (Copper) Losses** | Armature Copper Loss ($I_a^2 R_a$) | Armature winding resistance |
| 2 | **Electrical (Copper) Losses** | Field Copper Loss ($I_f^2 R_f$) | Shunt/Separately excited field winding |
| 3 | **Electrical (Copper) Losses** | Series Field Loss ($I_{se}^2 R_{se}$) | Series field winding (if present) |
| 4 | **Iron (Core) Losses** | Hysteresis Loss ($P_h$) | Armature core (rotating in magnetic field) |
| 5 | **Iron (Core) Losses** | Eddy Current Loss ($P_e$) | Armature core laminations |
| 6 | **Mechanical Losses** | Friction Loss | Bearings, commutator brushes |
| 7 | **Mechanical Losses** | Windage Loss | Air friction on rotating armature |
| 8 | **Stray Load Losses** | — | Distortion of main field flux, harmonics |

> [!TIP]
> **Constant vs. Variable Losses:** In KTU exam problems, a critical distinction is made between **constant losses** (Field Cu loss + Iron loss + Mechanical loss) and **variable losses** (Armature Cu loss, which depends on load current). This distinction is fundamental for solving the **"Condition for Maximum Efficiency"** question.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Copper Losses (Electrical Losses in Windings)

Copper losses occur due to the **ohmic resistance** of the windings when current flows through them. By Joule's Law of Heating, this electrical energy is irreversibly converted to **heat**.

### (a) Armature Copper Loss

$$\boxed{P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 \, R_a}$$

where:
- $I_a$ = Armature current (in Amperes)
- $R_a$ = Armature winding resistance (in Ohms)

**Why does this happen?** The armature winding is made of copper conductors. Copper has finite (though small) resistivity. When $I_a$ flows, $I_a^2 R_a$ watts of power dissipates as heat. This is a **variable loss** because $I_a$ changes with load.

### (b) Field Copper Loss (Shunt Field)

$$\boxed{P_{cu,f} = I_f^2 \, R_f = V_f^2 / R_f}$$

**Why is this a constant loss?** In a **shunt-wound DC machine**, the field winding is connected directly across the supply voltage. Since $V$ is essentially constant and $R_f$ is fixed, the field current $I_f = V/R_f$ remains nearly constant. Hence, $P_{cu,f}$ is practically **constant** from no-load to full-load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** In a **series motor**, there is no separate field winding loss — instead, the entire armature current flows through the series field, so the **series field loss** $I_{se}^2 R_{se}$ is variable.

### (c) Brush Contact Loss

This is the voltage drop at the brush-commutator interface (typically **~1 V per brush pair**, i.e., **2 V total**).

$$\boxed{P_{\text{brush}} = V_{\text{brush}} \times I_a = 2 \times I_a \text{ (approximately)}}$$

In many KTU problems, this is often neglected or merged with armature copper loss.

---

## 2.2 Iron (Core) Losses

Iron losses occur in the **armature core** because the core is continuously rotated in a steady magnetic field. This causes the magnetic domains in the iron to undergo cyclic magnetization, dissipating energy.

### (a) Hysteresis Loss ($P_h$)

$$\boxed{P_h = \eta \, B_{\max}^{1.6} \, f \, V}$$

where:
- $\eta$ (eta) = **Steinmetz constant / Hysteresis coefficient** (depends on core material)
- $B_{\max}$ = Maximum flux density in the core (in Tesla)
- $f$ = Frequency of magnetic reversals = $\frac{P \cdot N}{120}$ Hz (for DC machine armature)
- $V$ = Volume of the core (in m³)

**Intuition:** When you magnetize and demagnetize a ferromagnetic material, the B-H curve forms a "loop." The area enclosed by this loop represents energy lost per cycle as heat (molecular friction between magnetic domains). To minimize this, **silicon steel** laminations are used.

### (b) Eddy Current Loss ($P_e$)

$$\boxed{P_e = K_e \, B_{\max}^{2} \, f^{2} \, t^{2} \, V}$$

where:
- $K_e$ = Eddy current constant (depends on material)
- $t$ = **Thickness of each lamination** (in meters)
- $B_{\max}$, $f$, $V$ as defined above

**Intuition:** The rotating core cuts magnetic flux, inducing EMF loops (Faraday's Law). These circulating currents (eddy currents) flow within the core material and dissipate as heat ($I^2R$). The **$t^2$ dependence** is why the core is built from **thin laminations** (typically 0.35 mm to 0.5 mm) insulated from each other by varnish. This dramatically reduces eddy current paths.

### (c) Total Iron Loss

$$\boxed{P_i = P_h + P_e = \eta B_{\max}^{1.6} f V + K_e B_{\max}^{2} f^{2} t^{2} V}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why are iron losses considered CONSTANT in a DC machine?**
> In a DC machine, the **flux per pole ($\phi$)** and **rotational speed ($N$)** are nearly constant from no-load to full-load (in shunt and compound machines). Since $B_{\max} \propto \phi$ and $f \propto N$, both $P_h$ and $P_e$ remain practically constant. This is a key KTU concept for differentiating constant vs. variable losses.

---

## 2.3 Mechanical Losses

### (a) Friction Loss

Friction occurs at:
- **Bearing surfaces** (ball/roller bearings)
- **Brush-commutator interface** (sliding contact)

This loss is **practically constant** because the rotating mass and contact pressure do not change significantly with load.

### (b) Windage Loss

This is the power required to overcome **air friction (drag)** on the rotating armature surface, the commutator, and the cooling fan (if mounted on the shaft).

$$\boxed{P_{\text{windage}} = K_w \, \rho \, A \, v^{3}}$$

where $v$ is the peripheral velocity, $\rho$ is air density, $A$ is surface area. In practice, **friction + windage** are lumped together and measured experimentally as the **no-load rotational loss** (along with iron loss).

---

## 2.4 Stray Load Losses

These are **unaccounted losses** that occur due to:
- **Distortion of main field flux** by armature reaction
- **Harmonics** in the flux waveform
- **Local short-circuit currents** in the commutator coils undergoing commutation

**Standard KTU Assumption:**

$$\boxed{P_{\text{stray}} \approx 1\% \text{ of Output Power (for full load)}}$$

Or sometimes approximated as $1\%$ of Input Power. This is an empirical, experimentally-determined value.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Type (Const/Var)** | **Units** |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | Armature Cu loss | $P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 R_a$ | Variable | Watts |
| 2 | Shunt Field Cu loss | $P_{cu,f} = I_f^2 R_f = V^2 / R_f$ | Constant | Watts |
| 3 | Series Field Cu loss | $P_{cu,se} = I_{se}^2 R_{se}$ | Variable | Watts |
| 4 | Hysteresis loss | $P_h = \eta B_{\max}^{1.6} f V$ | Constant (shunt) | Watts |
| 5 | Eddy current loss | $P_e = K_e B_{\max}^{2} f^{2} t^{2} V$ | Constant (shunt) | Watts |
| 6 | Total iron loss | $P_i = P_h + P_e$ | Constant | Watts |
| 7 | Friction loss | $P_f$ (experimental) | Constant | Watts |
| 8 | Windage loss | $P_w$ (experimental) | Constant | Watts |
| 9 | Stray load loss | $\approx 0.01 \times P_{\text{out}}$ | Variable (∝ load) | Watts |
| 10 | Total losses | $\Sigma P = P_{cu,a} + P_{cu,f} + P_i + P_{mech} + P_{stray}$ | Mixed | Watts |
| 11 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{in}} - \Sigma P}{P_{\text{in}}}$ | — | % or p.u. |
| 12 | Max. Efficiency condition | $P_{\text{variable}} = P_{\text{constant}}$ | — | — |
| 13 | Power flow (Motor) | $P_{in(elec)} \to P_{cu,f} \to P_{cu,a} \to P_{mech dev} \to P_i + P_{mech} \to P_{out(mech)}$ | — | Watts |
| 14 | EMF equation | $E_g = \dfrac{\phi Z N P}{60 A}$ | — | Volts |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Industry:**
> 1. **Electric Vehicle (EV) Motors:** A modern EV's range depends critically on minimizing losses. Even a 1% efficiency gain translates to several extra kilometers of range.
> 2. **Industrial Motor Selection:** For applications like conveyors or pumps that run at constant load for long hours, choosing a motor with lower **constant losses** is more important than low variable losses.
> 3. **Thermal Design:** All losses ultimately convert to **heat**. The cooling system (fan, liquid cooling, heat sinks) must be sized to dissipate $\Sigma P$ watts to prevent insulation failure.
> 4. **Predictive Maintenance:** Monitoring insulation resistance and bearing condition helps predict iron loss and friction loss changes over the machine's lifetime.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Power Flow in a DC Motor

This is a **classic KTU derivation** that traces the electrical input power all the way to the mechanical output power, showing exactly where each loss is subtracted.

### **Step 1: Electrical Input Power**

The input to a DC motor is the electrical power drawn from the DC supply.

$$P_{\text{in}} = V \cdot I_L$$

where $V$ is the supply voltage and $I_L$ is the total line current drawn from the source.

### **Step 2: Subtract the Shunt Field Copper Loss**

A small portion of the input current is diverted through the shunt field winding. The power consumed in the shunt field is:

$$P_{cu,f} = V \cdot I_f \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad P_{cu,f} = I_f^2 R_f$$

The remaining power enters the armature circuit.

### **Step 3: Power Entering the Armature**

$$P_a = V \cdot I_a = P_{\text{in}} - P_{cu,f}$$

> **Logic:** The armature current $I_a = I_L - I_f$ (for a shunt motor). This is the power available for conversion to mechanical form.

### **Step 4: Subtract the Armature Copper Loss**

By Joule's Law, the armature winding dissipates heat as:

$$P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 \cdot R_a$$

The remaining power is the **mechanical power developed** in the armature (often denoted $P_m$ or $P_d$).

$$P_d = P_a - P_{cu,a} = V I_a - I_a^2 R_a$$

> **Logic:** Since $E_g = V - I_a R_a$ (the back EMF of the motor), we can also write this as $P_d = E_g \cdot I_a$.

### **Step 5: Subtract Iron and Mechanical Losses**

The developed mechanical power is partially consumed by iron and friction losses in the rotating armature, leaving the **shaft output power**:

$$P_{\text{out}} = P_d - (P_i + P_{\text{mech}})$$

### **Step 6: Final Equation Summary**

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = \frac{P_{\text{in}} - (P_{cu,f} + P_{cu,a} + P_i + P_{\text{mech}} + P_{\text{stray}})}{P_{\text{in}}}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pattern Recognition:** Whenever you see a Sankey-like power flow diagram in a KTU problem, the input is on the LEFT, output on the RIGHT, and losses branch OFF. Always confirm whether the machine is a **motor** (elec → mech) or **generator** (mech → elec) — the direction reverses, but the loss terms remain the same.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Condition for Maximum Efficiency

This is a **guaranteed KTU question** (often Part B, 7 marks).

### **Starting Point: General Efficiency Expression**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{out}} + P_{\text{loss}}}$$

Let:
- $P_c$ = Total **constant** losses (field Cu + iron + mechanical)
- $P_v$ = **Variable** losses, which scale with the square of the load fraction. If $x$ is the per-unit load, $P_v = x^2 \cdot P_{v,FL}$ where $P_{v,FL}$ is the full-load variable loss.

Then:
$$\eta = \frac{x \cdot P_{\text{out,FL}}}{x \cdot P_{\text{out,FL}} + P_c + x^2 \cdot P_{v,FL}}$$

### **Taking the Derivative with Respect to $x$**

$$\frac{d\eta}{dx} = \frac{(P_{\text{out,FL}})(P_{\text{out,FL}} + P_c + x^2 P_{v,FL}) - (x P_{\text{out,FL}})(2x P_{v,FL})}{(P_{\text{out,FL}} + P_c + x^2 P_{v,FL})^2}$$

### **Setting the Numerator to Zero for Maximum**

$$P_{\text{out,FL}} \cdot (P_{\text{out,FL}} + P_c + x^2 P_{v,FL}) - 2x^2 \cdot P_{\text{out,FL}} \cdot P_{v,FL} = 0$$

$$P_{\text{out,FL}}^2 + P_c P_{\text{out,FL}} + x^2 P_{\text{out,FL}} P_{v,FL} - 2x^2 P_{\text{out,FL}} P_{v,FL} = 0$$

$$P_{\text{out,FL}}^2 + P_c P_{\text{out,FL}} - x^2 P_{\text{out,FL}} P_{v,FL} = 0$$

$$x^2 P_{\text{out,FL}} P_{v,FL} = P_{\text{out,FL}}^2 + P_c P_{\text{out,FL}}$$

$$x^2 = \frac{P_{\text{out,FL}} + P_c}{P_{v,FL}}$$

### **Re-arranging for the Maximum Efficiency Condition**

$$\boxed{x^2 P_{v,FL} = P_c \quad \Longrightarrow \quad P_{\text{variable}} = P_{\text{constant}}}$$

> **Conclusion (in words):** *A DC machine achieves its maximum efficiency at that load where the variable losses equal the constant losses.*

### **Important Numerical Result**

The load at which maximum efficiency occurs:

$$\boxed{x = \sqrt{\frac{P_c}{P_{v,FL}}}}$$

And the maximum efficiency itself:

$$\eta_{\max} = \frac{x \, P_{\text{out,FL}}}{x \, P_{\text{out,FL}} + 2 P_c} \quad \text{(since at this point } P_{\text{loss}} = 2P_c\text{)}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A 220 V DC shunt motor draws a current of **25 A** at no-load and **100 A** at full load. The armature resistance is **0.05 Ω** and the field resistance is **110 Ω**. Calculate: (a) the total losses at full load, (b) the efficiency at full load. Given that iron loss = 600 W, friction & windage loss = 400 W.

### **Solution — Step-by-Step**

**Step 1: Compute the no-load input to find constant losses.**

At no-load, the input power is almost entirely consumed by constant losses:

$$P_{\text{in,NL}} = V \times I_{L,NL} = 220 \times 25 = 5500 \text{ W}$$

This includes field Cu loss + iron loss + friction & windage loss (no-load armature Cu loss is small, often neglected).

**Step 2: Compute the field Cu loss.**

$$I_f = \frac{V}{R_f} = \frac{220}{110} = 2 \text{ A}$$

$$P_{cu,f} = V \times I_f = 220 \times 2 = 440 \text{ W}$$

> This is a **constant loss**.

**Step 3: Compute the armature current at full load.**

For a shunt motor: $I_a = I_L - I_f$

$$I_{a,FL} = 100 - 2 = 98 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4: Compute the armature Cu loss at full load.**

$$P_{cu,a,FL} = I_a^2 \times R_a = (98)^2 \times 0.05$$

$$P_{cu,a,FL} = 9604 \times 0.05 = 480.2 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5: Total losses at full load.**

$$\Sigma P = P_{cu,f} + P_{cu,a,FL} + P_i + P_{\text{mech}}$$

$$\Sigma P = 440 + 480.2 + 600 + 400 = 1920.2 \text{ W}$$

**Step 6: Full-load input power.**

$$P_{\text{in,FL}} = V \times I_L = 220 \times 100 = 22000 \text{ W}$$

**Step 7: Output power and efficiency.**

$$P_{\text{out,FL}} = P_{\text{in,FL}} - \Sigma P = 22000 - 1920.2 = 20079.8 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta_{FL} = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} \times 100 = \frac{20079.8}{22000} \times 100 \approx 91.27\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification via constant-loss check:**
> Constant losses = $440 + 600 + 400 = 1440$ W.
> Variable loss at FL = $480.2$ W.
> Since constant > variable, the machine is **not yet at its maximum efficiency** at full load. The maximum efficiency would occur at a load *less than full load*.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Power Flow Diagram — DC Shunt Motor

The following block-level architecture flow maps how input electrical power is transformed into output mechanical power, with all the loss branches.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC SUPPLY V] --> B[LINE INPUT POWER Pin = V x IL]
    B --> C[SHUNT FIELD WINDING]
    C --> C1[LOSS Pcu,f = V x If]
    C --> D[ARMATURE CIRCUIT]
    B --> D
    D --> D1[LOSS Pcu,a = Ia2 x Ra]
    D --> E[MECHANICAL POWER DEVELOPED Pd = Eg x Ia]
    E --> E1[IRON LOSS Pi = Ph + Pe]
    E --> E2[FRICTION AND WINDAGE LOSS Pfw]
    E --> E3[STRAY LOAD LOSS Pstray]
    E --> F[SHAFT OUTPUT MECHANICAL POWER Pout = T x w]
```

## 4.2 Power Flow Diagram — DC Shunt Generator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[MECHANICAL INPUT Pin = T x w] --> B[SHAFT INPUT POWER]
    B --> B1[LOSS FRICTION AND WINDAGE Pfw]
    B --> C[ARMATURE CORE]
    C --> C1[IRON LOSS Pi = Ph + Pe]
    C --> D[ARMATURE EMF Eg GENERATED]
    D --> D1[LOSS Pcu,a = Ia2 x Ra]
    D --> E[BRUSH CONTACT]
    E --> E1[LOSS BRUSH DROP 2V x Ia]
    E --> F[ELECTRICAL OUTPUT V x IL]
    F --> F1[FIELD CIRCUIT LOSS Pcu,f]
    F --> G[LOAD OUTPUT]
```

## 4.3 Hysteresis Loop Visualization (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Hyst[Hysteresis Loop in B-H Plane]
        H1[Origin Start at B=0 H=0] --> H2[Increase H Magnetization Curve]
        H2 --> H3[Saturation B max achieved]
        H3 --> H4[Decrease H Retentivity Br remains]
        H4 --> H5[Reverse H to Hc Coercivity]
        H5 --> H6[Reverse saturation negative B max]
        H6 --> H7[Increase H back to origin]
        H7 --> H1
    end
    H4 -.->|Area enclosed = Energy lost per cycle| PL[Power Loss Ph = eta x Bmax^1.6 x f x V]
```

## 4.4 Classification Tree of Losses

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L[TOTAL LOSSES IN DC MACHINE] --> L1[ELECTRICAL LOSSES]
    L --> L2[MECHANICAL LOSSES]
    L --> L3[STRAY LOAD LOSSES]
    L1 --> L1a[Copper Losses]
    L1 --> L1b[Iron Core Losses]
    L1a --> L1a1[Armature Cu Loss Ia2Ra]
    L1a --> L1a2[Field Cu Loss If2Rf]
    L1a --> L1a3[Series Cu Loss Ise2Rse]
    L1b --> L1b1[Hysteresis Loss Ph]
    L1b --> L1b2[Eddy Current Loss Pe]
    L2 --> L2a[Friction Loss Bearings and Brushes]
    L2 --> L2b[Windage Loss Air Drag]
    L3 --> L3a[Armature Reaction Distortion]
    L3 --> L3b[Commutation Losses]
    L3 --> L3c[Harmonic Flux Losses]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip (KTU):** When asked to "draw the power flow diagram," the marks are awarded for: (1) correct identification of all loss blocks, (2) proper sequence of flow, and (3) labelling each block with its mathematical expression. A clear, labeled diagram is worth 4–5 marks by itself.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**"List the various losses occurring in a DC machine. Which of these are constant and which are variable?"**

**Model Answer:**

The various losses in a DC machine are:
1. **Armature copper loss** ($I_a^2 R_a$) — **Variable**
2. **Shunt field copper loss** ($I_f^2 R_f = V^2/R_f$) — **Constant**
3. **Series field copper loss** ($I_{se}^2 R_{se}$) — **Variable**
4. **Iron (core) losses**: Hysteresis loss + Eddy current loss — **Constant**
5. **Friction loss** — **Constant**
6. **Windage loss** — **Constant**
7. **Stray load losses** — **Variable** (approximately 1% of output)

> **Constant losses** are those that do not vary appreciably with load: field Cu loss, iron loss, friction, and windage.
> **Variable losses** are those that depend on load current: armature Cu loss, series field Cu loss, and stray load loss.

*(3 marks — full allocation for correctly categorizing each loss.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**"Why are armature core losses in a DC machine considered constant, although the armature is rotating?"**

**Model Answer:**

In a DC machine, the armature core is **rotated in a steady (DC) magnetic field**. Although the core itself rotates, each point on the core experiences **alternating magnetization** at a frequency $f = PN/120$ Hz.

Since:
- **Flux per pole ($\phi$)** is held constant by the field current (in shunt/compound machines).
- **Speed ($N$)** is practically constant (especially in shunt motors from no-load to full load).

Therefore, the maximum flux density $B_{\max} \propto \phi$ and the frequency $f \propto N$ remain essentially **constant** from no-load to full-load. As a result, both hysteresis loss ($P_h = \eta B_{\max}^{1.6} f V$) and eddy current loss ($P_e = K_e B_{\max}^2 f^2 t^2 V$) remain approximately constant, **independent of the mechanical load**.

*(3 marks — full allocation for explaining the role of constant flux and speed.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Question Choices (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat power flow diagram, explain the various losses occurring in a DC shunt motor. Derive the condition for maximum efficiency.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A 250 V DC shunt motor takes a no-load current of 5 A and a full-load current of 50 A. The field resistance is 250 Ω and the armature resistance is 0.2 Ω. The iron losses are 750 W and the frictional losses are 500 W. Find: (i) the efficiency at full load, (ii) the load at which maximum efficiency occurs, and (iii) the maximum efficiency.

### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

Refer to the **Power Flow Diagram for DC Shunt Motor** in Section 4.1 above for the diagram (4 marks).

**Derivation of Maximum Efficiency Condition:**

The total losses = constant losses ($P_c$) + variable losses ($P_v$).

Let output at fractional load $x$ be $x \cdot P_{\text{out,FL}}$, and variable losses be $x^2 P_{v,FL}$.

$$\eta = \frac{x P_{\text{out,FL}}}{x P_{\text{out,FL}} + P_c + x^2 P_{v,FL}}$$

Differentiating with respect to $x$ and setting $\frac{d\eta}{dx} = 0$:

$$P_{\text{out,FL}}(P_{\text{out,FL}} + P_c + x^2 P_{v,FL}) - x P_{\text{out,FL}}(2x P_{v,FL}) = 0$$

$$P_{\text{out,FL}}^2 + P_c P_{\text{out,FL}} - x^2 P_{\text{out,FL}} P_{v,FL} = 0$$

$$\boxed{x^2 P_{v,FL} = P_c \quad \text{or} \quad P_{\text{variable}} = P_{\text{constant}}}$$

Thus, maximum efficiency occurs when **variable losses equal constant losses** (3 marks).

---

### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- $V = 250$ V, $I_{L,NL} = 5$ A, $I_{L,FL} = 50$ A
- $R_f = 250$ Ω, $R_a = 0.2$ Ω
- $P_i = 750$ W, $P_{\text{mech}} = 500$ W

**Step 1: Field current and field Cu loss.**

$$I_f = \frac{V}{R_f} = \frac{250}{250} = 1 \text{ A}$$

$$P_{cu,f} = V \times I_f = 250 \times 1 = 250 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2: No-load armature current and no-load armature Cu loss (often negligible, but compute for completeness).**

$$I_{a,NL} = 5 - 1 = 4 \text{ A}$$

$$P_{cu,a,NL} = (4)^2 \times 0.2 = 3.2 \text{ W (negligible)}$$

**Step 3: Constant losses.**

$$P_c = P_{cu,f} + P_i + P_{\text{mech}} = 250 + 750 + 500 = 1500 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4: Full-load armature current and armature Cu loss.**

$$I_{a,FL} = 50 - 1 = 49 \text{ A}$$

$$P_{cu,a,FL} = (49)^2 \times 0.2 = 2401 \times 0.2 = 480.2 \text{ W}$$

So variable loss at full load: $P_{v,FL} = 480.2$ W.

**Step 5: Full-load efficiency.**

$$P_{\text{in,FL}} = 250 \times 50 = 12500 \text{ W}$$

$$\Sigma P = 1500 + 480.2 = 1980.2 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{\text{out,FL}} = 12500 - 1980.2 = 10519.8 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta_{FL} = \frac{10519.8}{12500} \times 100 = 84.16\%$$

**Step 6: Load at maximum efficiency.**

$$x = \sqrt{\frac{P_c}{P_{v,FL}}} = \sqrt{\frac{1500}{480.2}} = \sqrt{3.124} \approx 1.768$$

> This means maximum efficiency occurs at a load **greater than full load** (1.768 p.u.) — but this is **physically not possible** without overheating. The machine's design is not optimized.

**Step 7: Maximum efficiency (theoretical).**

At this load, total losses = $2 P_c = 3000$ W.
Output at this load = $x \cdot P_{\text{out,FL}} = 1.768 \times 10519.8 = 18599$ W.

$$\eta_{\max} = \frac{18599}{18599 + 3000} \times 100 = 86.11\%$$

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — Alternative [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the difference between hysteresis loss and eddy current loss. How are these losses minimized in the design of a DC machine?
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A 4-pole, 250 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of 0.1 Ω and a field resistance of 125 Ω. At no-load, it takes 6 A at 1500 rpm. At full load, the input current is 60 A. The iron loss is 600 W and the friction & windage loss is 400 W. Calculate: (i) the speed at full load (assuming flux is reduced by 4% due to armature reaction), and (ii) the efficiency at full load.

### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Hysteresis Loss** is the energy dissipated as heat in a ferromagnetic material when it is subjected to cyclic magnetization. The magnetic domains in the iron experience a kind of "internal friction" as they realign with the changing magnetic field. The energy loss per cycle is proportional to the area of the B-H loop. Mathematically: $P_h = \eta B_{\max}^{1.6} f V$.

**Eddy Current Loss** is the energy dissipated due to circulating currents (eddy currents) induced in the core material as it rotates through the magnetic field. By Faraday's law, an EMF is induced in the bulk of the conductor; the resulting currents flow in closed loops and dissipate as $I^2R$ heat. Mathematically: $P_e = K_e B_{\max}^2 f^2 t^2 V$.

**Minimization techniques:**

| **Loss** | **Minimization Method** |
|:--|:--|
| Hysteresis | Use **silicon steel** (low Steinmetz coefficient $\eta$); use high-grade crystalline alloys |
| Eddy current | Use **thin laminations** (0.35–0.5 mm) insulated by varnish; use high-resistivity silicon steel |

---

### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Field current and field Cu loss.**

$$I_f = \frac{250}{125} = 2 \text{ A}, \quad P_{cu,f} = 250 \times 2 = 500 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2: No-load armature current and back EMF at no-load.**

$$I_{a,NL} = 6 - 2 = 4 \text{ A}$$

$$E_{g,NL} = V - I_{a,NL} R_a = 250 - (4)(0.1) = 250 - 0.4 = 249.6 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3: Full-load armature current and back EMF at full load.**

$$I_{a,FL} = 60 - 2 = 58 \text{ A}$$

$$E_{g,FL} = V - I_{a,FL} R_a = 250 - (58)(0.1) = 250 - 5.8 = 244.2 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Speed at full load using EMF equation $E_g \propto \phi N$.**

Since flux is reduced by 4%, $\phi_{FL} = 0.96 \phi_{NL}$.

$$\frac{E_{g,FL}}{E_{g,NL}} = \frac{\phi_{FL} \cdot N_{FL}}{\phi_{NL} \cdot N_{NL}}$$

$$N_{FL} = N_{NL} \times \frac{E_{g,FL}}{E_{g,NL}} \times \frac{\phi_{NL}}{\phi_{FL}} = 1500 \times \frac{244.2}{249.6} \times \frac{1}{0.96}$$

$$N_{FL} = 1500 \times 0.9784 \times 1.0417 \approx 1528.6 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 5: Full-load efficiency.**

$$P_{cu,a,FL} = (58)^2 \times 0.1 = 336.4 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{\text{in,FL}} = 250 \times 60 = 15000 \text{ W}$$

$$\Sigma P = 500 + 336.4 + 600 + 400 = 1836.4 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{\text{out,FL}} = 15000 - 1836.4 = 13163.6 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta_{FL} = \frac{13163.6}{15000} \times 100 = 87.76\%$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Mistakes (Read Carefully!):**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract $I_f$ from $I_L$:** Many students compute armature copper loss using $I_L^2 R_a$ instead of $I_a^2 R_a$. This single error can cost **3 marks** in Part B.
> 2. **Confusing constant and variable losses for series vs. shunt motors:** In a **series motor**, the field winding carries the **armature current**, so the field Cu loss is **variable**, not constant. This distinction changes the entire "maximum efficiency" derivation.
> 3. **Ignoring stray load loss:** The KTU 2024 scheme syllabus **explicitly includes** stray load losses. Do not omit the **1% of output** term in efficiency problems.
> 4. **Unit errors:** Resistances must be in **Ohms**, currents in **Amps**, and powers in **Watts**. Mixing units is a frequent deduction.
> 5. **Power flow diagram mistakes:** Drawing arrows in the wrong direction (e.g., showing output as input). Always start at the **left** with the input source.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final High-Density Revision Checklist:**
> - [ ] **Losses in DC machine** = Electrical (Cu + Iron) + Mechanical (Friction + Windage) + Stray
> - [ ] **Armature Cu loss** = $I_a^2 R_a$ — **Variable**
> - [ ] **Shunt field Cu loss** = $V^2 / R_f$ — **Constant** (in shunt/compound machines)
> - [ ] **Hysteresis loss** = $\eta B_{\max}^{1.6} f V$ — depends on $B_{\max}^{1.6}$
> - [ ] **Eddy current loss** = $K_e B_{\max}^2 f^2 t^2 V$ — depends on $t^2$ (lamination thickness)
> - [ ] **Iron losses are constant** in a DC shunt machine because $\phi$ and $N$ are constant
> - [ ] **Power flow**: $P_{in} \to P_{cu,f} \to P_{cu,a} \to P_d \to P_i + P_{mech} + P_{stray} \to P_{out}$
> - [ ] **Condition for max efficiency**: $P_{\text{variable}} = P_{\text{constant}}$
> - [ ] **Efficiency** = $P_{\text{out}} / P_{\text{in}} \times 100\%$
> - [ ] **EMF equation**: $E_g = \dfrac{\phi Z N P}{60 A}$ — needed for speed-related problems
> - [ ] **Stray load loss** ≈ 1% of output (empirical, include it!)
> - [ ] **Brush contact loss** ≈ $2 \times I_a$ (often ignored in KTU problems unless stated)
> - [ ] **Maximum efficiency load fraction**: $x = \sqrt{P_c / P_{v,FL}}$
> - [ ] Remember to use $I_a = I_L - I_f$ (shunt) or $I_a = I_L$ (series) correctly
> - [ ] **Iron losses minimized by**: silicon steel core + thin laminations (0.35–0.5 mm) + varnish insulation

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST521 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Efficiency ($\eta$)** of any electrical machine or device is defined as the ratio of **output power** delivered to the **input power** supplied, usually expressed as a **percentage**.

Mathematically, the efficiency is given by:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{output}}{P_{input}} \times 100\%$$

Since every practical machine has inherent losses, the input power is always greater than the output power. Therefore, efficiency is **always less than 100 %** (or unity). It can also be expressed in terms of losses:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{input} - P_{losses}}{P_{input}} \times 100\% = \left(1 - \frac{P_{losses}}{P_{input}}\right) \times 100\%$$

## 1.2 Types of Efficiency in Electrical Machines

In the context of DC machines, transformers, and induction motors (the core focus of Module 1), engineers distinguish between three specific forms of efficiency:

- **Electrical Efficiency ($\eta_e$)** — The ratio of the electrical power developed in the armature (or secondary winding) to the electrical power input. It ignores mechanical losses such as friction and windage.

$$\eta_e = \frac{P_{developed}}{P_{input}} \times 100\%$$

- **Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_m$)** — The ratio of mechanical power available at the shaft to the mechanical power developed inside the armature (or rotor). It accounts for friction, windage, and stray load losses.

$$\eta_m = \frac{P_{shaft}}{P_{developed}} \times 100\%$$

- **Overall / Commercial Efficiency ($\eta_{overall}$)** — The most practically useful metric. It is the ratio of useful shaft power (or terminal output) to the total electrical input.

$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_e \times \eta_m = \frac{P_{shaft}}{P_{input}} \times 100\%$$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water-Bucket" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine you are filling a 10-litre bucket using a tap and a hose that has small leaks. You pour in 12 litres of water ($P_{input}$), 2 litres leak out through the hose holes ($P_{losses}$), and the bucket finally holds 10 litres ($P_{output}$). The "efficiency" of your filling process is simply $10/12 = 83.3\%$. In an electrical machine, the "leaks" are the copper losses ($I^2R$), iron (core) losses, friction & windage losses, and stray losses.

## 1.4 Why Efficiency Matters in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In Kerala's industrial landscape — from the coir-processing motors in Alappuzha to the heavy pump-sets used in Kuttanad's agricultural fields and the transformers feeding Kozhikode's grid — every percentage point of efficiency translates directly into **kilowatt-hours saved, lower electricity bills (KESB tariffs), reduced heat generation, and longer machine life**. KTU examiners therefore stress efficiency problems in almost every semester.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Losses in Electrical Machines

The losses that "leak" away the input power are classified into four principal categories. For a **DC machine** (the primary focus of Module 1 of PENST521), these are:

### (i) Copper Losses (Variable / $I^2R$ Losses)
- Occur in the **armature winding** ($I_a^2 R_a$) and the **field winding** ($I_f^2 R_f$).
- Depend on the square of the current — therefore **vary with load**.
- For a shunt machine, field copper loss is approximately **constant**.
