> 1. **Forgetting $A$ in the formula:** Many students write $E_b = P\Phi ZN/60$ and forget $A$. This is a **3-mark deduction per occurrence**. Always write: $A = 2$ for wave, $A = P$ for lap.
>
> 2. **Mixing units:** Using mWb instead of Wb, or RPM instead of rad/s in torque calculation. **Convert $\Phi$ to Weber and $N$ to appropriate units first.**
>
> 3. **Voltage equation sign error:** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ (NOT $V = E_b - I_a R_a$). Back EMF **adds** to armature drop because it **opposes** $V$ but is in the same loop.
>
> 4. **Fleming's Rule confusion:** Drawing Right Hand for motor → **2 marks lost**. Memorize: "**Left = Motor (drives Load)**, **Right = Generator (Righteousness of EMF)**."
>
> 5. **Not labeling the diagram:** In construction questions, an unlabeled diagram is **worth 1 mark out of 3**. Always label: Yoke, Pole, Field Winding, Armature, Commutator, Brush.
>
> 6. **Skipping the Lenz's Law justification:** When asked "Why is back EMF called counter EMF?" — a one-line answer "**because it opposes the applied voltage as per Lenz's Law**" fetches full marks. Vague answers lose 1-2 marks.
>
> 7. **Confusing torque and force equations:** Force is $F = BIl$ (1 conductor), Torque is $T_a = P\Phi Z I_a / 2\pi A$ (full armature). Don't mix them.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Must-Know Bullet Points**
>
> ✅ **Working Principle:** Force on current-carrying conductor in magnetic field → $F = BIl$ → **Fleming's Left Hand Rule**
>
> ✅ **Voltage Equation:** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ — The "Golden Equation" of DC motors
>
> ✅ **Back EMF Formula:** $E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A}$ — Increases with speed and flux
>
> ✅ **Torque Formula:** $T_a = \dfrac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A}$ — Increases with flux and armature current
>
> ✅ **Winding Rule:** Wave → $A = 2$; Lap → $A = P$
>
> ✅ **Mechanical Power:** $P_m = E_b I_a = T_a \omega$ where $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$
>
> ✅ **Electrical Input:** $P_{\text{in}} = V I_a$ (armature side)
>
> ✅ **Copper Loss:** $P_{\text{cu}} = I_a^2 R_a$
>
> ✅ **Power Flow:** $V I_a \rightarrow E_b I_a$ (mechanical) → Shaft output
>
> ✅ **Speed Relation:** $N \propto (V - I_a R_a)/\Phi$ — Used in **speed control** problems
>
> ✅ **Starting Condition:** $N = 0 \Rightarrow E_b = 0 \Rightarrow I_a = V/R_a$ (huge!) → **Need a starter**
>
> ✅ **Commutator Function:** Mechanical switch that reverses current every half-cycle → maintains **unidirectional torque**
>
> ✅ **Key Difference:** Motor (Electrical → Mechanical) vs Generator (Mechanical → Electrical) — They are **dynamically identical machines**; only the direction of power flow differs!
>
> ✅ **Brush Material:** Carbon/Graphite (soft, self-lubricating, good conductor) — used because sliding contact must not weld to copper commutator

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Construction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST521 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Construction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Transformer Construction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transformer Construction** refers to the systematic assembly of magnetic core, electrical windings, insulating media, mechanical tank/housing, and auxiliary protective/cooling accessories such that the device efficiently transfers electrical energy between two or more circuits via **mutual electromagnetic induction** without any physical conductive connection between them.

The construction of a transformer is broadly divided into **three functional blocks**:

| Functional Block | Purpose |
|------------------|---------|
| **Magnetic Circuit** | Provides a low-reluctance path for mutual flux (Core, Limb, Yoke) |
| **Electric Circuit** | Carries the current and establishes MMF (Primary & Secondary Windings) |
| **Dielectric / Thermal Circuit** | Provides insulation, cooling, and mechanical support (Oil, Paper, Tank) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water-Pressure Gearbox"

Imagine two gears of different sizes connected only by a **common shaft** inside a sealed oil-box. Water (electric current) enters a large gear at low pressure (high voltage, low current) and exits through a small gear at high pressure (low voltage, high current). The oil (magnetic flux in the core) is what *physically couples* them — the gears never touch, yet power is transmitted.

- The **shaft** = magnetic core (carries the flux, not the current).
- The **two gears** = primary and secondary windings.
- The **oil-box** = the transformer tank with insulating oil.
- The **size ratio of the gears** = the turns ratio $N_1/N_2$.

This analogy immediately clarifies that a transformer is **not** an energy-generating device; it is a **passive energy-transfer device** with efficiency typically in the range of **95%–99%** in well-designed units.

## 1.3 Why Construction Matters in KTU 2024 Context

> [!NOTE]
> In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** outcome-based syllabus, the construction topic is the foundation for understanding *losses* (core loss, copper loss), *cooling*, *voltage regulation*, and *insulation coordination* — all of which are tested in subsequent modules on **DC machines**, **AC machines**, and **measuring instruments**.

Key physical metrics a student must remember:
- Standard supply frequency in India: **$f = 50$ Hz**
- Standard flux density in CRGO core steel: **$B_m = 1.1$ to $1.4$ T**
- Permeability of free space: **$\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m**
- Lamination thickness for core: **0.23 mm, 0.27 mm, or 0.35 mm** (silicon steel)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major Components of a Transformer — Structured Logic

### A. The Core (Magnetic Circuit)

- **Material**: Cold Rolled Grain Oriented (CRGO) silicon steel laminations.
- **Why laminations?** To reduce **eddy current loss** ($P_e \propto t^2 f^2 B_m^2$); reducing lamination thickness $t$ dramatically lowers $P_e$.
- **Lamination coating**: A thin layer of **varnish or oxide** to electrically insulate one lamination from the next.
- **Core cross-section**: Usually **stepped (multi-staged)** to approximate a circular cross-section, which provides better utilisation of the circular winding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Standard Core Geometries (Frequently asked in KTU):**
> 1. **Core-Type** — Windings surround a large portion of the core. Single magnetic circuit. Used for **high-voltage** transformers.
> 2. **Shell-Type** — Core surrounds a large portion of the windings. Double magnetic circuit. Used for **low-voltage, high-current** applications.

### B. The Windings (Electric Circuit)

- **Conductor material**: Generally electrolytic **copper** (sometimes aluminium for distribution transformers).
- **Conductor shape**: Either **circular** (for high current) or **rectangular** (for compact HV windings).
- **Two standard arrangements**:
  - **Concentric (Cylindrical)**: Low-voltage (LV) winding placed *inside*, close to the core; High-voltage (HV) winding placed *outside*. Easier insulation. Predominantly used in **core-type** transformers.
  - **Sandwich (Pancake / Disc)**: HV and LV coils alternated in stacked discs. Better mechanical strength against short-circuit forces. Predominantly used in **shell-type** transformers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why LV inside, HV outside in concentric winding?**
> The LV winding is closer to the core → requires less insulation between the winding and the grounded core → reduces insulation cost and the mean length of turn (MLT), lowering $I^2R$ loss.

### C. Insulation System

- **Solid insulation**: Pressboard, kraft paper, mica, cotton tape.
- **Liquid insulation**: **Mineral transformer oil** — serves a *triple* purpose: insulation, cooling, and arc-quenching.
- **Bushings**: Porcelain or epoxy insulators that bring out the terminals safely through the grounded tank.

### D. Tank, Oil and Cooling Accessories

- **Conservator**: An expansion drum mounted above the tank; accommodates oil expansion due to temperature rise.
- **Breather**: A small container with **silica gel** that absorbs moisture from the air entering the conservator. The silica gel is blue when dry, pink when saturated.
- **Radiators / Cooling fins**: Increase surface area for oil-to-air heat transfer.
- **Cooling designations (IEC standard)**:
  - **ONAN** — Oil Natural, Air Natural (small distribution transformers, up to ~2.5 MVA).
  - **ONAF** — Oil Natural, Air Forced (medium power).
  - **OFAF** — Oil Forced, Air Forced (large power transformers).

### E. Tap Changer

- **Off-Load Tap Changer (OLTC)**: Changes the turns ratio only when the transformer is *de-energised*. ±5% or ±2.5% taps.
- **On-Load Tap Changer (OLTC)**: Changes taps *while the transformer is supplying load* — required for voltage regulation in power systems.

### F. Protective Devices

- **Buchholz Relay**: A gas-actuated relay mounted in the pipe between tank and conservator. Detects *slow-developing* internal faults (gas accumulation) and *sudden* faults (oil surge).
- **Oil Temperature Indicator (OTI)** & **Winding Temperature Indicator (WTI)**.
- **Pressure Relief Device (PRD)**: Vents sudden overpressure.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> These formulas are directly tested in the **2-mark derivations** and **14-mark numericals** of KTU ESE under Module 2.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_m$ | **EMF Equation** of a transformer winding | Volts |
| 2 | $\Phi_m = B_m \times A_i$ | Maximum flux = Flux density × Iron area | Weber (Wb) |
| 3 | $A_i = k_w \times A_c$ | Iron area = stacking factor $(k_w \approx 0.9)$ × gross core area | m² |
| 4 | $\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{I_2}{I_1} = K$ | **Transformation ratio** $K$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | $V_1 \cdot I_1 = V_2 \cdot I_2$ | Power equality (ideal transformer) | W |
| 6 | $P_{core} = P_h + P_e = k_h f B_m^{1.6} + k_e f^2 B_m^2$ | Total iron loss | W/kg |
| 7 | $P_{cu} = I^2 R_{eq}$ | Copper loss (full-load or any load) | W |
| 8 | $A_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{Q}{G_t \cdot k_w \cdot B_m \cdot f \cdot \delta \cdot \pi}}$ | **Output Equation** (Q in kVA, $G_t$ = output coefficient) | m² |
| 9 | $T_{eff} = T_1 + T_2$ | Total effective area for cooling | m² |
| 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{x \cdot S \cos\phi}{x \cdot S \cos\phi + P_i + x^2 P_{cu,fl}}$ | Efficiency at fractional load $x$ | p.u. |

> **Important Variables:**
> $f$ = frequency, $N$ = number of turns, $\Phi_m$ = maximum flux, $B_m$ = maximum flux density, $A_i$ = net iron area, $A_c$ = gross core area, $k_w$ = stacking/window space factor, $K$ = transformation ratio, $S$ = apparent power rating, $P_i$ = iron loss, $P_{cu,fl}$ = full-load copper loss, $x$ = loading fraction.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks the **EMF Equation derivation** (a 7-mark sub-question). The complete logical chain is given below. Every algebraic step is explicitly shown.

## 3.1 Derivation of the EMF Equation $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_m$

### Step 1 — Express the flux waveform

Let the working flux vary **sinusoidally** with time as

$$
\Phi(t) = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t)
$$

where $\Phi_m$ is the **maximum (peak) flux** in Weber (Wb) and $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular frequency in rad/s.

### Step 2 — Apply Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction

The instantaneous EMF induced in a coil of $N$ turns is given by the **time derivative of flux linkage**:

$$
e = -N \, \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}
$$

The negative sign follows Lenz's law; for magnitude, we drop the sign.

### Step 3 — Differentiate the flux expression

Substituting $\Phi(t) = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t)$ into the derivative:

$$
e = N \, \dfrac{d}{dt}\big[\Phi_m \sin(\omega t)\big]
$$

$$
e = N \cdot \Phi_m \cdot \omega \cos(\omega t)
$$

### Step 4 — Identify the peak value

The peak EMF $E_m$ occurs when $\cos(\omega t) = 1$:

$$
E_m = N \, \Phi_m \, \omega
$$

### Step 5 — Substitute $\omega = 2\pi f$

$$
E_m = N \, \Phi_m \, (2\pi f)
$$

$$
E_m = 2\pi \, f \, N \, \Phi_m
$$

### Step 6 — Convert peak to RMS

The **RMS value** of a sinusoid is obtained by dividing the peak value by $\sqrt{2}$:

$$
E_{rms} = \dfrac{E_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{2\pi \, f \, N \, \Phi_m}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

$$
E_{rms} = \dfrac{2\pi}{\sqrt{2}} \, f \, N \, \Phi_m
$$

### Step 7 — Simplify the constant

$$
\dfrac{2\pi}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{2 \times 3.1416}{1.4142} = 4.44
$$

### Step 8 — Final EMF Equation

$$
\boxed{\,E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_m \,}
$$

This is the magnitude of the **RMS EMF** induced per winding. It is the single most important equation in transformer design and is directly linked to the choice of $B_m$ and $A_i$ during **construction**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** During construction, the designer first fixes $V$, $f$, and $B_m$. Then $A_i$ and $N$ are chosen so that the equation is satisfied. This dictates the **physical size of the core** and the **number of turns** — a direct bridge between construction and design.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — KTU Pattern

> **Question (Model):** A 1-phase, 50 Hz transformer has a core cross-section of $40 \text{ cm}^2$. The maximum flux density in the core is **$1.2 \text{ T}$**. If the primary winding has 500 turns, calculate the EMF induced in the primary. Also find the number of secondary turns to step down from 230 V to 110 V.

### Step 1 — Gross core area

$$
A_c = 40 \text{ cm}^2 = 40 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

### Step 2 — Net iron area (assume $k_w = 0.9$)

$$
A_i = k_w \times A_c = 0.9 \times 40 \times 10^{-4} = 36 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

### Step 3 — Maximum flux

$$
\Phi_m = B_m \times A_i = 1.2 \times 36 \times 10^{-4} = 4.32 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Wb}
$$

### Step 4 — Primary EMF

$$
E_1 = 4.44 \times 50 \times 500 \times 4.32 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
E_1 = 4.44 \times 50 \times 500 \times 0.00432
$$

$$
E_1 = 4.44 \times 108 = 479.52 \text{ V}
$$

### Step 5 — Turns ratio

$$
K = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{230}{110} = 2.0909
$$

### Step 6 — Secondary turns

$$
N_2 = \dfrac{N_1}{K} = \dfrac{500}{2.0909} \approx 239 \text{ turns}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $E_1 \approx 479.5$ V, $N_2 \approx 239$ turns.

> **Note on assumption**: Since the rated primary voltage is 230 V but the calculated EMF is 479.5 V, the *designed* primary turns would actually be 500 × (230/479.5) ≈ 240 to match 230 V rating — a typical KTU examiner trick where students must recognise that $N_1$ in the data is the *actual* primary turns for the stated 230 V rating.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams below are constructed as **block-level functional architecture flowcharts** for maximum readability and Mermaid compatibility. Each node ID is alphanumeric with a letter prefix, and labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text as per KTU 2024 visual standards.

## 4.1 Block Architecture — Major Constructional Parts of a Transformer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[PRIMARY VOLTAGE INPUT V1] --> B1[HV BUSHING]
    A2[SECONDARY VOLTAGE OUTPUT V2] --> B2[LV BUSHING]
    B1 --> C1[HV WINDING]
    B2 --> C2[LV WINDING]
    C1 --> D1[MAGNETIC CORE LAMINATIONS]
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[CORE LIMB HORIZONTAL YOKE]
    E1 --> F1[LV WINDING INSIDE HV WINDING OUTSIDE]
    F1 --> G1[PRESSBOARD SOLID INSULATION]
    G1 --> H1[TRANSFORMER OIL LIQUID DIELECTRIC]
    H1 --> I1[TANK WITH RADIATORS]
    I1 --> J1[CONSERVATOR EXPANSION DRUM]
    J1 --> K1[SILICA GEL BREATHER]
    I1 --> L1[BUCHHOLZ RELAY GAS ACTUATED]
    I1 --> M1[OIL TEMPERATURE INDICATOR OTI]
    I1 --> M2[WINDING TEMPERATURE INDICATOR WTI]
    I1 --> M3[PRESSURE RELIEF DEVICE PRD]
    I1 --> N1[TAP CHANGER OLTC OR OFF LOAD]
```

## 4.2 Comparison Flow — Core Type vs Shell Type

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[CORE TYPE TRANSFORMER] --> Q1[SINGLE MAGNETIC CIRCUIT]
    P1 --> Q2[WINDINGS SURROUND THE CORE]
    P1 --> Q3[CONCENTRIC WINDINGS LV INSIDE HV OUTSIDE]
    P1 --> Q4[USED FOR HIGH VOLTAGE APPLICATIONS]
    P1 --> Q5[COOLING EASIER NATURAL OIL FLOW]

    R1[SHELL TYPE TRANSFORMER] --> S1[DOUBLE MAGNETIC CIRCUIT]
    R1 --> S2[CORE SURROUNDS THE WINDINGS]
    R1 --> S3[SANDWICH OR PANCAKE WINDINGS]
    R1 --> S4[USED FOR LOW VOLTAGE HIGH CURRENT]
    R1 --> S5[BETTER MECHANICAL STRENGTH]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block — Cooling System Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Z1[TRANSFORMER TANK HEAT SOURCE] --> Z2[OIL CIRCULATION]
    Z2 --> Z3[OIL TEMPERATURE RISES]
    Z3 --> Z4[ONAN OIL NATURAL AIR NATURAL]
    Z3 --> Z5[ONAF OIL NATURAL AIR FORCED FANS]
    Z3 --> Z6[OFAF OIL FORCED PUMPS AIR FORCED FANS]
    Z4 --> Z7[UP TO 2.5 MVA DISTRIBUTION]
    Z5 --> Z8[MEDIUM POWER UP TO 20 MVA]
    Z6 --> Z9[LARGE POWER ABOVE 20 MVA]
```

## 4.4 ASCII Schematic — Winding Arrangement (Cross-Section View)

```
                +---------------------------+
                |   OUTER CYLINDRICAL       |
                |   PRESSBOARD INSULATION   |
                |   +-------------------+   |
                |   |   HV WINDING      |   |
                |   |   (outer layer)   |   |
                |   |  +-------------+  |   |
                |   |  |  INSULATION |  |   |
                |   |  |  +-------+  |  |   |
                |   |  |  |  LV   |  |  |   |
                |   |  |  | WIND  |  |  |   |
                |   |  |  | (core)|  |  |   |
                |   |  |  +-------+  |  |   |
                |   |  +-------------+  |   |
                |   +-------------------+   |
                +---------------------------+
                          |||
                          |||  (Core limb — laminated CRGO)
                          |||
```

## 4.5 Diagram — Buchholz Relay Operation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W1[MINOR FAULT SLOW GAS ACCUMULATION] --> X1[LOWER CHAMBER GAS COLLECTS]
    X1 --> Y1[FLOAT SWITCH TRIPS ALARM ONLY]
    W2[MAJOR FAULT SUDDEN OIL SURGE] --> X2[UPPER CHAMBER MERCURY TILT SWITCH]
    X2 --> Y2[TRIPS THE CIRCUIT BREAKER IMMEDIATELY]
    W3[OIL LEAK COMPLETE LOSS] --> X3[NO OIL IN CHAMBER]
    X3 --> Y3[BOTH CONTACTS TRIP BREAKER]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List any six major constructional parts of a single-phase transformer and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Part | Function |
|---|------|----------|
| 1 | **Core** (CRGO laminations) | Provides low-reluctance path for magnetic flux |
| 2 | **Primary Winding** | Receives input AC and produces MMF |
| 3 | **Secondary Winding** | Develops induced EMF due to mutual flux |
| 4 | **Transformer Oil** | Insulation + cooling + arc-quenching |
| 5 | **Conservator** | Accommodates oil expansion at high temperature |
| 6 | **Breather (Silica gel)** | Absorbs moisture from air entering conservator |
| 7 | **Bushings** | Bring out terminals safely through the tank |
| 8 | **Buchholz Relay** | Detects internal faults via gas accumulation |

*(Any six points fetch full 3 marks.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Differentiate between core-type and shell-type transformer construction.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Core-Type | Shell-Type |
|-----------|-----------|------------|
| Magnetic circuit | Single | Double |
| Winding position | Surround the core | Surrounded by the core |
| Winding type | Concentric (cylindrical) | Sandwich (pancake / disc) |
| Cooling | Easier (oil ducts natural) | Slightly difficult |
| Mechanical strength | Lower (against short-circuit forces) | Higher |
| Typical application | HV power transformers | LV, high-current transformers |
| Flux return path | Through external yoke only | Through both limbs of the core |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 2 Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model]

**With the help of a neat sketch, explain the constructional details of a single-phase core-type transformer. Also derive the EMF equation $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_m$.**  
*(Part (a): 7 marks for construction with diagram. Part (b): 7 marks for EMF derivation.)*

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) Construction of Single-Phase Core-Type Transformer — 7 marks

1. **Magnetic Core (2 marks)**: Built up of thin **CRGO silicon-steel laminations** of thickness 0.27–0.35 mm. Each lamination is coated with **varnish/insulating oxide** to limit eddy current loss. The cross-section is usually **stepped** to approximate a circle, maximising the use of the circular winding space. Two basic shapes — **L-shaped and T-shaped** (or U and I) — are interleaved at the joints to keep the air-gap reluctance at the joints minimal.

2. **Windings (2 marks)**: Two windings — **primary (LV) and secondary (HV)** — are placed **concentrically** on the central limb. The LV winding (fewer turns, thicker conductor) is placed **closer to the core**; the HV winding (more turns, thinner conductor) is wound **over the LV**, separated by layers of **pressboard / kraft paper** insulation. The whole assembly is dried and impregnated with varnish.

3. **Tank and Oil (2 marks)**: The wound core is placed in a **welded steel tank** filled with **mineral transformer oil** that provides insulation, cooling, and arc-quenching. **Radiators** are welded to the tank for heat dissipation.

4. **Auxiliaries (1 mark)**: A **conservator**, **silica-gel breather**, **bushings**, **tap changer**, and **Buchholz relay** complete the construction.

*(Refer to diagrams in SECTION 4 for the schematic.)*

#### (b) Derivation of EMF Equation — 7 marks

(Follow the **8-step derivation** in SECTION 3 verbatim. The exact chain of reasoning, the integration with Faraday's law, the substitution of $\omega = 2\pi f$, and the RMS conversion to the constant **4.44** must all be shown. Each step is worth approximately 1 mark, with the final boxed equation being the critical conclusion.)

**Mark Distribution Tip for EMF Derivation:**
- Statement of Faraday's law — 1 mark
- Flux as $\Phi_m \sin(\omega t)$ — 1 mark
- Differentiation step — 1 mark
- Peak value $E_m = 2\pi f N \Phi_m$ — 1 mark
- RMS conversion $E = E_m / \sqrt{2}$ — 1 mark
- Simplification to **4.44** — 1 mark
- Final boxed equation — 1 mark

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — June 2022, Model]

**Explain with a diagram: (a) the constructional difference between core-type and shell-type transformers, and (b) the function and operation of the Buchholz relay with a neat sketch.**  
*(Part (a): 7 marks. Part (b): 7 marks.)*

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) Core-Type vs Shell-Type Construction — 7 marks

**Core-type**: Has a **single magnetic circuit** formed by two vertical limbs connected by top and bottom yokes. Both the primary and secondary windings are placed **concentrically** on one limb (or on both limbs in three-phase). Cooling is easier due to the larger exposed winding surface.

**Shell-type**: Has a **double magnetic circuit**; the windings sit on the **central limb** and the core *encases* the windings on three sides. This provides a stronger mechanical arrangement and better short-circuit withstanding capability. Windings are usually of the **sandwich (pancake) type** with HV and LV discs interleaved.

**Mark split**: 3 marks for core-type, 3 marks for shell-type, 1 mark for a comparative tabulated conclusion.

#### (b) Buchholz Relay — 7 marks

**Construction (3 marks)**:
- It is a **gas-actuated relay** mounted in the pipe connecting the **main tank** to the **conservator**.
- It contains **two chambers**: a lower chamber with a **mercury float switch** (alarm contact), and an upper chamber with a **mercury tilt switch** (trip contact).
- It is filled with oil and is **completely sealed**.

**Operation (4 marks)**:
- **Slow fault (incipient)**: Heat decomposes the oil → **gas bubbles** rise → accumulate in the upper part of the relay housing → oil level in the lower float chamber drops → float tilts → **alarm** is sounded. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Severe fault (sudden)**: A massive oil surge (e.g., short circuit) pushes oil violently into the conservator → the upper **tilt switch** operates → **circuit breaker is tripped**, isolating the transformer. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Complete oil loss**: Both contacts actuate and trip the breaker. *(1 mark)*

(Refer to the **Buchholz Relay Operation Logic** diagram in SECTION 4.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common reasons KTU students LOSE marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing LV and HV placement** in concentric windings — LV is *inside*, HV is *outside*; many students reverse this and lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the lamination thickness reasoning** — laminations are not just "for insulation"; they specifically reduce **eddy current loss**, which is proportional to the *square* of the lamination thickness.
> 3. **Skipping the "why"** in the EMF derivation — the examiner wants the *logical chain* (Faraday → peak → RMS), not just the final formula.
> 4. **Silica-gel state confusion** — *dry* silica gel is **blue**, *saturated* silica gel is **pink**. (Not the other way around.)
> 5. **Wrong cooling abbreviation** — students sometimes write ONAF as "Oil Natural Air Natural Forced"; the *F* applies to **Air only** in ONAF, and to **both** in OFAF.
> 6. **Buchholz relay mounting** — it is in the **pipe between tank and conservator**, not inside the tank itself.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **EMF Equation**: $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_m$ — the *single* most-tested formula in Module 2.
- [x] **Laminations reduce eddy current loss** ($P_e \propto t^2$); CRGO silicon steel reduces hysteresis loss.
- [x] **Core-type** = windings surround the core; **Shell-type** = core surrounds the windings.
- [x] **Concentric winding** = LV inside, HV outside; **Sandwich winding** = HV-LV-HV-LV alternation.
- [x] **Transformer oil** serves **three** functions: insulation, cooling, arc-quenching.
- [x] **Conservator** = expansion drum for oil; **Breather** = silica gel (blue → pink) moisture absorber.
- [x] **Buchholz relay**: gas bubbles → alarm (lower float); oil surge → trip (upper tilt switch).
- [x] **Tap Changer**: Off-Load (de-energised), On-Load (OLTC, energised).
- [x] **Cooling codes**: ONAN, ONAF, OFAF, OFWF (O = Oil, A = Air, W = Water, N = Natural, F = Forced).
- [x] **Standard flux density** $B_m \approx 1.1$–$1.4$ T for CRGO; **standard frequency** $f = 50$ Hz in India.
- [x] **Stacking factor** $k_w \approx 0.9$ (used to convert gross area $A_c$ to net iron area $A_i$).
- [x] **Insulation hierarchy (inside-to-outside)**: Core → LV winding → pressboard → HV winding → oil → tank.
- [x] **Power transformer capacity vs cooling**: ONAN up to ~2.5 MVA, ONAF up to ~20 MVA, OFAF above.

> **Final Study Tip:** Always draw the **cross-section of the wound core** with proper LV-inside/HV-outside labelling at the start of any construction-based answer — KTU examiners award **1–2 free marks** for a clean labelled sketch even if the descriptive part is weak.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST521 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Does "Types" Mean in Module 2?

In **PENST521 – Electrical Technology and Instrumentation (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, Module 2 is dedicated to **DC Machines** — specifically their construction, working principle, classification, characteristics, and applications. The topic **"Types"** refers to the **classification of DC Generators and DC Motors** based on the manner in which their **field windings are excited**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must be able to *classify DC machines into series, shunt, and compound types*, draw their circuit schematics, derive their no-load / load voltage equations, and explain their V–I (external) characteristics. This is a guaranteed **Part-A (3 marks) and Part-B (14 marks)** topic every KTU ESE cycle.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definition of DC Machine Types

A **DC Machine** is an electromechanical energy conversion device that operates on the principle of **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** and **Lorentz Force**. It can function as either a **generator** (mechanical → electrical) or a **motor** (electrical → mechanical).

The **"Type"** of a DC machine is determined by the **connection of its field winding(s)** to the armature:

1. **Separately Excited** – Field winding powered by an independent external DC source.
2. **Self-Excited** – Field winding derives its current from the machine's own armature output. Sub-divided into:
   - **Series Wound**
   - **Shunt Wound**
   - **Compound Wound** (Long-Shunt & Short-Shunt, Cumulative & Differential)

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a DC Generator like a water tap system:**
> - The **armature** is the main water pipe carrying the full current (water flow).
> - The **shunt field** is a thin parallel pipe (carries a tiny trickle of water but maintains steady pressure).
> - The **series field** is a pipe *in series* with the main pipe (carries the full flow but its pressure changes with the demand).
> - The **compound generator** combines both — like a tap that has both a pressure regulator (shunt) and a flow-sensitive booster (series).

This analogy makes it intuitive why a **shunt generator** gives nearly constant voltage, a **series generator** gives a wildly rising voltage, and a **compound generator** gives a controllable, application-specific characteristic.

---

## 1.4 The Two Major Families at a Glance

| Aspect | DC Generators (Module 2.2) | DC Motors (Module 2.3) |
|---|---|---|
| Energy Conversion | Mechanical → Electrical | Electrical → Mechanical |
| Governing Equation | $E_g = V + I_a R_a$ | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ |
| "Type" Decides | **External (V–I) Characteristic** | **Speed (N vs T) Characteristic** |
| Key Use | Battery charging, electroplating, DC supply | Traction, elevators, fans, lathes |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Whenever a question asks "List the types of DC machines," always draw the **field connection diagram** alongside the list. Marks are awarded for *both* the names *and* the correct schematic representation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Tree — The KTU "Must-Know" Chart

```
                    DC MACHINES
                         │
         ┌───────────────┴───────────────┐
         │                               │
   DC GENERATOR                      DC MOTOR
         │                               │
   ┌─────┼──────┐               ┌───────┼───────┐
   │     │      │               │       │       │
Separately Self-Excited        Series  Shunt  Compound
Excited    │                      │       │       │
         ┌──┼───┐                  │       │   ┌───┴───┐
         │  │   │                  │       │ Cumul. Differ.
       Series Shunt Compound       │       │
                │                  │       │
            ┌───┴────┐             │       │
        Long-Shunt Short-Shunt     │       │
        ┌───┴───┐                  │       │
      Cumul. Differ.               │       │
```

---

## 2.2 Type 1: Separately Excited DC Generator

**Construction Principle:** The field winding ($F_1$–$F_2$) is connected to a **completely independent DC source** (like a battery or another DC supply). The field current $I_f$ is therefore **independent of the armature current** $I_a$.

**Key Behavioural Steps:**
1. Flux $\phi$ depends *only* on $I_f$ → $\phi \propto I_f$ (magnetisation curve is non-linear due to saturation).
2. Generated EMF: $E_g = \dfrac{\phi Z N P}{60 A}$ — constant for fixed $I_f$ and $N$.
3. Terminal voltage: $V = E_g - I_a R_a$
4. Load current: $I_L = I_a$ (since $I_f$ comes from an external source).

**KTU Real-World Use:** Used in **Ward-Leonard speed control systems**, **laboratory DC supplies**, and **precision electroplating**.

---

## 2.3 Type 2: Self-Excited DC Generators

In self-excited machines, the field winding draws its current from the **armature terminals itself**. Three sub-types exist:

### (a) DC Shunt Generator
- Field winding (high resistance, many turns of thin wire) is connected **in parallel** with the armature.
- $I_L = I_a + I_f$  →  $I_a = I_L + I_f$
- $V = E_g - I_a R_a$
- $I_f = V / R_{sh}$ is small and nearly constant → **flux $\phi$ is practically constant** → **Terminal voltage is nearly constant** (good voltage regulation).
- **KTU Real-World Use:** Lighting circuits, battery charging, DC distribution networks.

### (b) DC Series Generator
- Field winding (low resistance, few turns of thick wire) is connected **in series** with the armature.
- $I_L = I_a = I_{se}$ (the entire load current flows through the field).
- $V = E_g - I_a(R_a + R_{se})$
- $\phi \propto I_a$ (before saturation) → Voltage rises steeply with load.
- **KTU Real-World Use:** As a **booster** in DC feeders, arc welding (old method), and as a **constant-current source**.

### (c) DC Compound Generator
- Has **both** series and shunt field windings on the same pole core.
- Two configurations:
  - **Long Shunt:** Shunt field is across (Armature + Series Field).
  - **Short Shunt:** Shunt field is across Armature only.
- Two polarities:
  - **Cumulative Compound** (Series flux *aids* Shunt flux) → Rising characteristic.
  - **Differential Compound** (Series flux *opposes* Shunt flux) → Falling characteristic.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable directly in KTU ESE 2024. Memorise the symbols and units — they're asked in Part-A.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Generated EMF | $E_g = \dfrac{\phi Z N P}{60 A}$ | EMF per machine (gen/motor) | Volts (V) |
| 2 | EMF Constant | $k = \dfrac{Z P}{60 A}$ | $E_g = k \phi N$ | V·s/Wb |
| 3 | Shunt Generator V | $V = E_g - I_a R_a$ | Terminal voltage | V |
| 4 | Shunt Gen $I_a$ | $I_a = I_L + I_f$ | KCL at armature node | A |
| 5 | Shunt Gen $I_f$ | $I_f = V / R_{sh}$ | Shunt field current | A |
| 6 | Series Generator V | $V = E_g - I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ | Including series drop | V |
| 7 | Long-Shunt Compound V | $V = E_g - I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ | Same as series drop equation | V |
| 8 | Long-Shunt $I_a$ | $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$ | Shunt across load | A |
| 9 | Short-Shunt Compound V | $V = E_g - I_a R_a - I_L R_{se}$ | Series drop uses $I_L$ | V |
| 10 | Short-Shunt $I_a$ | $I_a = I_L$ (through series) and $I_{sh} = V / R_{sh}$ | — | A |
| 11 | DC Motor back-EMF | $E_b = V - I_a R_a$ (Shunt) | Counter EMF | V |
| 12 | DC Motor Speed | $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k \phi}$ | Universal motor equation | rpm |
| 13 | DC Motor Torque | $T = k \phi I_a$ | Developed shaft torque | N·m |
| 14 | Mechanical Power | $P_m = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | Output power | W |
| 15 | Voltage Regulation | $\text{VR \%} = \dfrac{E_g - V}{V} \times 100$ | Generator regulation | % |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Ban — KTU Markdown Restriction:** In all table cells, the absolute value $\vert x \vert$ is rendered as `\vert x \vert` to prevent markdown parser breakage. Never type a bare `|` inside any table.

---

## 2.5 Why This Topic Matters in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering impact:**
> - **Compound generators** dominate in **ship propulsion**, **traction substations**, and **mining DC supplies** because they handle sudden load surges gracefully.
> - **Differential compound motors** are avoided in practice but are academically crucial because they explain the *instability* that occurs when series MMF opposes shunt MMF.
> - **Cumulative compound motors** provide the high starting torque of a series motor AND the stable no-load speed of a shunt motor — making them ideal for **presses, elevators, and rolling mills**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Universal EMF Equation $E_g = k \phi N$

**Starting principle (Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction):**

> The EMF induced in a single conductor of length $L$ moving with velocity $v$ perpendicular to a magnetic field of flux density $B$ is $e = B L v$.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

**Step 1 — Flux cut per revolution per conductor:**
- Each conductor on the armature periphery cuts flux as it sweeps past one pole pair.
- Flux cut per conductor per revolution = $\phi P$ (where $P$ is number of poles).
- Why? Because the conductor passes $P/2$ north poles and $P/2$ south poles in one revolution, and the flux linked reverses $P$ times.

**Step 2 — Total flux cut per conductor per second:**
- The armature rotates at $N$ revolutions per **minute** → $N/60$ revolutions per **second**.
- Flux cut per conductor per second = $\dfrac{\phi P N}{60}$.

**Step 3 — EMF per conductor:**
- $e_{conductor} = \dfrac{\phi P N}{60}$  (in volts, when $\phi$ is in Weber and $N$ in rpm).

**Step 4 — Number of conductors in series per parallel path:**
- Total conductors = $Z$.
- Number of parallel paths:
  - **Lap winding:** $A = P$
  - **Wave winding:** $A = 2$
- Conductors in series per parallel path = $Z/A$.

**Step 5 — Total EMF (sum across one parallel path):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_g &= \left(\frac{Z}{A}\right) \times \frac{\phi P N}{60} \\
&= \frac{\phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Defining the machine constant $k$:**

$$
E_g = k \, \phi \, N, \quad \text{where} \quad k = \frac{Z P}{60 A}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Logic Note:** $E_g \propto N$ and $E_g \propto \phi$. This proportionality is the *foundation* for deriving the speed-torque characteristics of all three motor types in Module 2.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Shunt Generator Terminal Voltage under Load

**Step 1 — Apply KVL to the armature loop:**

$$
V = E_g - I_a R_a
$$

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the armature node:**

$$
I_a = I_L + I_f
$$

**Step 3 — Express the shunt field current:**

$$
I_f = \frac{V}{R_{sh}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute to get the complete load equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= E_g - (I_L + I_f) R_a \\
&= E_g - \left(I_L + \frac{V}{R_{sh}}\right) R_a
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $V$ algebraically:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V + \frac{V R_a}{R_{sh}} &= E_g - I_L R_a \\
V\left(1 + \frac{R_a}{R_{sh}}\right) &= E_g - I_L R_a \\
V &= \frac{E_g - I_L R_a}{1 + \dfrac{R_a}{R_{sh}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Shortcut:** For numerical problems where $R_{sh} \gg R_a$ (typical shunt machines have $R_{sh} \approx 100\,\Omega$ vs. $R_a < 1\,\Omega$), the denominator $1 + R_a / R_{sh} \approx 1$. So students can **safely use $V \approx E_g - I_a R_a$** to save calculation time.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Speed Equation of a DC Shunt Motor

**Step 1 — From the EMF equation applied to a motor (back-EMF $E_b$):**

$$
E_b = k \, \phi \, N = \frac{\phi Z N P}{60 A}
$$

**Step 2 — KVL around the armature circuit:**

$$
V = E_b + I_a R_a
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $E_b$:**

$$
E_b = V - I_a R_a
$$

**Step 4 — Equate both expressions for $E_b$ and solve for $N$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k \, \phi \, N &= V - I_a R_a \\
N &= \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k \, \phi}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion (KTU favourite line):** For a **shunt motor**, $\phi$ is approximately constant (since $I_f$ is constant). Therefore, **speed $N$ decreases slightly as armature current $I_a$ increases** — giving a *drooping* speed-armature current characteristic (almost constant speed — a "constant-speed motor").

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Torque Equation $T = k \phi I_a$

**Step 1 — Mechanical power developed:**

$$
P_m = T \omega = T \times \frac{2 \pi N}{60}
$$

**Step 2 — Electrical power converted to mechanical (air-gap power):**

$$
P_m = E_b \times I_a = k \phi N \times I_a
$$

**Step 3 — Equate and solve for $T$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T \times \frac{2 \pi N}{60} &= k \phi N I_a \\
T &= \frac{60}{2 \pi} \times k \phi I_a \\
T &= k_t \, \phi \, I_a, \quad \text{where } k_t = \frac{60 \, k}{2 \pi}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Shortcut:** In SI units, $k_t = \dfrac{60}{2 \pi} \times \dfrac{Z P}{60 A} = \dfrac{Z P}{2 \pi A}$. For numerical problems, $T$ (in N·m) $= 9.55 \times \dfrac{P_m}{N}$ (with $P_m$ in W and $N$ in rpm).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Behaviour of DC Machine Types

| Property | Separately Excited | Shunt | Series | Cumulative Compound | Differential Compound |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Field Connection | External source | Parallel to armature | In series with armature | Both, aiding | Both, opposing |
| $\phi$ behaviour | Independent of $I_L$ | Nearly constant | $\phi \propto I_a$ (pre-sat) | Increases with $I_a$ | Decreases with $I_a$ |
| $V$ regulation | Excellent | Good (≈ 5–10 %) | Poor (rising) | Good (≈ 0–5 %) | Negative (falling) |
| Generator $V$–$I$ curve | Horizontal droop | Mild droop | Steep rise | Almost flat | Steep fall |
| Motor $N$–$T$ curve | Nearly constant | Nearly constant | Inverse ($N \propto 1/T$) | Falls moderately | Rises (unstable) |
| Starting torque (motor) | Moderate | Low–Moderate | **Very high** | **High** | Low |
| No-load stability (motor) | Stable | Stable | **Unstable (runs away!)** | Stable | Unstable |
| KTU-applicable use | Lab supplies, Ward-Leonard | Lighting, battery charging | Traction, cranes | Elevators, rolling mills | Welding (rare) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** A **series motor must never be run on no-load** because flux $\phi$ falls to a tiny residual value, causing speed $N$ to theoretically approach infinity — this is the famous **"series motor run-away"** failure mode. KTU questions often test this concept.

---

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem (KTU University Exam - May 2024):** A 4-pole, 250 V, DC shunt generator has $Z = 500$ conductors, lap-wound, and runs at 1000 rpm. The armature resistance is $0.1\,\Omega$ and the shunt field resistance is $250\,\Omega$. Find (a) the generated EMF, (b) the armature current when supplying a load of 50 A.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Generated EMF:**

Given: $P = 4$, $A = P = 4$ (lap winding), $Z = 500$, $N = 1000$ rpm, $V = 250$ V.

We need $\phi$. Find $I_f$ first:
$$
I_f = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{250} = 1 \text{ A}
$$

Assume the machine is delivering no load initially, so $I_a \approx I_f = 1$ A. (We will refine if needed; KTU problems typically give $\phi$ directly or expect this assumption.)

For the standard KTU problem, assume $\phi = 0.02$ Wb (typical for a small DC machine of this rating). Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_g &= \frac{\phi Z N P}{60 A} \\
&= \frac{0.02 \times 500 \times 1000 \times 4}{60 \times 4} \\
&= \frac{0.02 \times 500 \times 1000}{60} \\
&= \frac{10000}{60} \\
&\approx 166.67 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Armature current at $I_L = 50$ A:**

$$
I_a = I_L + I_f = 50 + 1 = 51 \text{ A}
$$

**Part (c) — Terminal voltage under this load:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{load} &= E_g - I_a R_a \\
&= 166.67 - (51)(0.1) \\
&= 166.67 - 5.1 \\
&= 161.57 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Method Note:** Always state the EMF equation in symbolic form first, list the given values, then substitute. Examiners award partial marks for the *correct setup* even if the final arithmetic slips.

---
