<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Module 2: Brake Test — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *What is a brake test? Why is it called a direct method of testing?*

**Model Answer:**
A **brake test** is a method of determining the **efficiency of a DC machine** by directly loading it through a **mechanical frictional braking device** (rope, band, or prony brake) attached to the machine's shaft.

It is called a **direct method** because:
1. The machine is **actually loaded** with real mechanical work.
2. The **output power is directly measured** via force and speed.
3. There is **no assumption of constant losses** (unlike Swinburne's test).

It is, however, classified as a **wasteful method** because the entire output is dissipated as **heat** through friction.

**(3 marks)** — 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for justification of "direct method."

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Why is the brake test not suitable for large DC machines?*

**Model Answer:**
The brake test is **not suitable for large DC machines** (typically > 20 kW) for the following reasons:

1. **Heat Dissipation Problem:** The entire mechanical output is converted to heat at the brake. For large powers, this heat cannot be removed quickly enough, leading to **rope burning, pulley damage, or fire hazard**.
2. **Cooling Difficulty:** Continuous water/air cooling becomes impractical at high load levels.
3. **Mechanical Stress:** The braking force required is enormous, causing **rope slippage or breakage**.
4. **Measurement Errors:** The spring balance readings become unstable due to high vibration.

Hence, **indirect methods like Swinburne's test** (for no-load losses) or **regenerative tests (Hopkinson's)** are preferred for large machines.

**(3 marks)** — 1 mark per valid point × 3.

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**OR**

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

#### **Question A:**

> **[a]** With a neat sketch, explain the procedure of conducting a **rope brake test** on a **DC shunt motor**. (7 marks)
>
> **[b]** A **DC shunt motor** takes a current of **85 A at 250 V** and runs at **750 rpm**. During a rope brake test, the readings are: **$W_1$ = 14 kg, $W_2$ = 2 kg**, **R = 30 cm**. Calculate: **(i)** Torque developed, **(ii)** Output power, **(iii)** Efficiency. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for [a]:**

**Apparatus Required:**
1. DC shunt motor under test
2. Rope brake setup (hemp rope, two spring balances/weights)
3. Pulley mounted on motor shaft (R = 30 cm here)
4. Voltmeter, Ammeter, Tachometer
5. Variable DC supply, Rheostat

**Procedure:**
1. **Connect the motor** to the DC supply through a voltmeter (across terminals) and ammeter (in series with armature). Connect a rheostat in the field circuit for speed control.
2. **Wrap the rope** once around the pulley mounted on the motor shaft.
3. **Attach dead weight $W_1$** to one side of the rope and a **spring balance (reading $W_2$)** to the other side.
4. **Start the motor** and adjust the field rheostat to obtain rated voltage at rated speed (no-load).
5. **Apply load gradually** by tightening the rope or adding weights; record $W_1$, $W_2$, $V$, $I_L$, and $N$ at each load step.
6. **Tabulate** the readings and calculate torque, output, and efficiency.
7. **Stop the motor** after the test and allow it to cool.

**Diagram (Neat Sketch) — Verbal Description:**
- A horizontal pulley on the motor shaft.
- Rope wrapped over the top of the pulley.
- Left end: dead weight $W_1$ hanging vertically.
- Right end: spring balance (reading $W_2$) hanging vertically.
- The motor rotates such that the heavier side tends to go down.

**Model Solution for [b]:**

Given:
- $V = 250$ V, $I_L = 85$ A, $N = 750$ rpm
- $W_1 = 14$ kg, $W_2 = 2$ kg
- $R = 30$ cm $= 0.30$ m, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**(i) Torque developed:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= (W_1 - W_2) \times g = (14 - 2) \times 9.81 = 12 \times 9.81 = 117.72 \text{ N} \\
T_{sh} &= F \times R = 117.72 \times 0.30 = 35.316 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Output power:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{out} &= \frac{2\pi N T_{sh}}{60} = \frac{2 \times \pi \times 750 \times 35.316}{60} \\
        &= \frac{166{,}424.7}{60} = 2773.7 \text{ W} \approx 2.774 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Efficiency:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{in} &= V \times I_L = 250 \times 85 = 21{,}250 \text{ W} \\
\eta &= \frac{2773.7}{21{,}250} \times 100 = 13.05 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often calculate **shaft torque** as $T_{sh}$ but the question might ask for **armature torque $T_a$**, which includes friction & windage. Read the verb carefully — "developed" usually means $T_a$, "brake/shaft" means $T_{sh}$. **2 marks lost on average for this confusion.**

---

#### **Question B (Alternative Choice):**

> **[a]** Compare the **Brake Test** and **Swinburne's Test** for DC shunt motors. (7 marks)
>
> **[b]** In a brake test on a DC shunt motor, the following data was recorded: **Spring balance reading = 4.2 kg**, **Dead weight = 12 kg**, **Pulley diameter = 40 cm**, **Speed = 1440 rpm**, **Line current = 28 A**, **Supply voltage = 230 V**. Calculate the **shaft torque, output power, and efficiency**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for [a]:**

| Parameter | Brake Test | Swinburne's Test |
|-----------|------------|-------------------|
| Method | Direct loading | Indirect (no-load run) |
| Loading | Actual mechanical load | No load; field current varied |
| Losses measured | All losses together | Constant losses only |
| Power range | Small machines (< 20 kW) | Large machines |
| Heat generated | Yes (problematic) | Negligible |
| Accuracy | Higher (real conditions) | Lower (assumes constant losses) |
| Speed | Can vary with load | Approximately constant |
| Time required | Short (rapid test) | Long (warm-up required) |
| Field weakening test | Not possible | Possible |
| Back-to-back check | No | No (Hopkinson's required) |

**Conclusion:** Brake test is accurate but wasteful; Swinburne's is efficient but assumes constant losses.

**(7 marks)** — 1 mark each for 5–6 meaningful comparison points + 1 mark for conclusion.

**Model Solution for [b]:**

Given:
- $W_1 = 12$ kg (dead weight), $W_2 = 4.2$ kg (spring balance)
- $D = 40$ cm → $R = 0.20$ m
- $N = 1440$ rpm, $I_L = 28$ A, $V = 230$ V, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Shaft Torque:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= (W_1 - W_2) \times g = (12 - 4.2) \times 9.81 = 7.8 \times 9.81 = 76.518 \text{ N} \\
T_{sh} &= F \times R = 76.518 \times 0.20 = 15.30 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Output Power:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{out} &= \frac{2\pi N T_{sh}}{60} = \frac{2 \times \pi \times 1440 \times 15.30}{60} \\
        &= \frac{138{,}377.6}{60} = 2306.3 \text{ W} \approx 2.306 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Efficiency:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{in} &= 230 \times 28 = 6440 \text{ W} \\
\eta &= \frac{2306.3}{6440} \times 100 = 35.81 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Mark Loss Warning:**
> - **No unit for R:** 1 mark lost.
> - **Forgetting 2π in power formula:** 2 marks lost.
> - **Confusing line current with armature current** in compound motors: 2 marks lost. In shunt motors, $I_L \approx I_a$ (negligible field current for rating purposes), but for compound motors, subtract $I_{se}$ first.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Brake Test**

- [x] **Brake Test = Direct Method** of efficiency determination for DC machines.
- [x] **Two main types:** Rope brake (most common in KTU) and Prony brake.
- [x] **Friction force:** $F = (W_1 - W_2) \times g$ in Newtons; ensure weight in **kgf** is converted.
- [x] **Braking torque formula:** $T_{sh} = F \times R$, with **R in metres** (not cm).
- [x] **Output power formula:** $P_{out} = \dfrac{2\pi N T_{sh}}{60}$ — this is the **single most important equation** in this module.
- [x] **Input power (motor):** $P_{in} = V \times I_L$ — line current includes both armature and shunt field.
- [x] **Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ — express as a percentage.
- [x] **Direction of rotation:** The heavier weight $W_1$ must be on the **descending side**; otherwise, the formula sign changes.
- [x] **Effective radius R** = radius of the **pulley** (not the shaft or the rope).
- [x] **Limitation:** Not suitable for large machines (> 20 kW) due to **heat dissipation** issues.
- [x] **Cooling:** Water-cooled pulley setups are used in lab demonstrations to prolong the test duration.
- [x] **Difference from Swinburne's:** Swinburne's is **indirect/no-load**; brake test is **direct/loaded**.
- [x] **KTU 2024 Focus Area:** Rope brake on **DC shunt motor** with numerical calculation is the **highest-weightage** problem type (7–14 marks).

> **Final KTU Exam Mantra:**
> *"Convert R to metres, multiply weights by g, use 2πNT/60, and never confuse the line current with armature current in a compound machine."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torque Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST521 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Torque Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Torque Equation** of a rotating electrical machine (specifically the DC machine) establishes the fundamental mathematical relationship between the **electromagnetic torque developed in the armature** and the **magnetic flux per pole** along with the **armature current**. 

In its general form, the electromagnetic torque developed by a DC machine is given by:

$$T_a = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{P \cdot Z}{A} \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a \;\;\; \text{N·m}$$

where every term has strict unit definitions as per the **SI system**, and the constant $k = \frac{PZ}{2\pi A}$ is known as the **machine construction constant** or **armature constant**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Torque Equation is derived from the fundamental principle of **electromechanical energy conversion** — the conversion of electrical input power into mechanical rotational power at the shaft. This equation is the cornerstone for analyzing the **speed-torque characteristics**, **load characteristics**, and **starting performance** of all DC machines.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding a **steering wheel** of a heavy truck. The amount of "twisting effort" you need to apply to turn the wheel depends on:
- How **strongly the magnetic field** is pulling at the wheel's rim (this is analogous to the **flux per pole, $\Phi$**)
- How **much current is flowing** through the electromagnetic coils behind the wheel (analogous to the **armature current, $I_a$**)

The torque produced is the **rotational push** — not a linear push, but a *twisting* push — that makes the shaft rotate. In a DC motor, the magnetic flux from the field poles and the current-carrying armature conductors interact to produce this twisting force.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics:**
> - **SI Unit of Torque:** Newton-meter (**N·m**)
> - **SI Unit of Flux ($\Phi$):** Weber (**Wb**)
> - **SI Unit of Current ($I_a$):** Ampere (**A**)
> - **Standard Reference Speed:** $N$ in **r.p.m. (revolutions per minute)**
> - **Number of Poles ($P$):** Always an **even integer** (2, 4, 6, ...)
> - **Number of Conductors ($Z$):** Always an **even integer** (physical constraint of closed winding)
> - **Parallel Paths ($A$):** Equals $2$ for wave winding; equals $P$ for lap winding

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Three Pillars

The torque equation is not arbitrary; it emerges from three foundational engineering principles working in harmony:

### Pillar I: Lorentz Force on a Current-Carrying Conductor
When a current-carrying conductor of effective length $l$ is placed in a magnetic field of flux density $B$, it experiences a force:
$$F = B \cdot I \cdot l \;\;\; \text{Newtons}$$

This is the **microscopic origin** of torque. Every armature conductor is pushed by this force, and collectively they produce a twisting moment about the shaft.

### Pillar II: Torque as Force × Lever Arm
A force applied at a distance $r$ from the axis of rotation produces a torque. With **$Z$ conductors** arranged around the armature periphery of radius $r$ (mean radius), the total torque is the sum of forces from all conductors:
$$T_a = F_{total} \times r$$

### Pillar III: Power Equivalence (Electrical = Mechanical + Rotational)
The electrical power converted to mechanical form at the armature must equal the mechanical rotational power:
$$E_b \cdot I_a = T_a \cdot \omega$$
This is the **energy conservation bridge** that links the EMF equation to the torque equation.

## 2.2 Logic Flow — Why Each Variable Appears

| Variable | Engineering Justification |
|----------|---------------------------|
| $\Phi$ (flux/pole) | Stronger magnetic field $\Rightarrow$ stronger Lorentz force on each conductor $\Rightarrow$ more torque |
| $I_a$ (armature current) | More current in conductors $\Rightarrow$ stronger Lorentz force $\Rightarrow$ more torque (linear dependence) |
| $P$ (poles) | More poles mean flux is "shared" — the factor $P/A$ governs how flux is distributed per parallel path |
| $Z$ (conductors) | More conductors means more force-producing elements around the periphery |
| $A$ (parallel paths) | Distributes total armature current; for wave $A=2$, for lap $A=P$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Inference for KTU:** For a **shunt motor**, $\Phi$ is nearly **constant** (field current is fixed by supply), so $T_a \propto I_a$ — a *linear* relationship. For a **series motor**, $\Phi \propto I_a$ (before saturation), so $T_a \propto I_a^2$ — a *quadratic* relationship, which is why series motors produce very high starting torque.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $T_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot Z}{A} \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$ | **General electromagnetic torque equation** | N·m |
| 2 | $T_a = k \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$ where $k = \dfrac{PZ}{2\pi A}$ | **Compact form** with machine constant $k$ | N·m |
| 3 | $T_a = 0.159 \times \dfrac{PZ}{A} \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$ | **Numerical form** (since $1/2\pi \approx 0.159$) | N·m |
| 4 | $T_a \propto \Phi \cdot I_a$ | **Proportionality** form (most used in KTU problems) | — |
| 5 | $T_{sh} = T_a - T_f$ | **Shaft torque** (after subtracting friction losses) | N·m |
| 6 | $P_m = T_a \cdot \omega = T_a \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | **Mechanical power developed** at armature | W |
| 7 | $P_{out} = T_{sh} \cdot \omega$ | **Output (shaft) power** delivered to load | W |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{input}} = \dfrac{T_{sh} \cdot \omega}{V \cdot I_L}$ | **Efficiency** of the motor | dimensionless |
| 9 | $T_{sh} = 9.55 \times \dfrac{P_{out}}{N}$ | **Shaft torque from output power & speed** | N·m |
| 10 | $T_a = 9.55 \times \dfrac{E_b \cdot I_a}{N}$ | **Armature torque from internal EMF & power** | N·m |

> [!NOTE]
> The constant **9.55** arises from $\dfrac{60}{2\pi} = 9.549 \approx 9.55$. This conversion factor is **extremely high-yield** in KTU numericals — committing it to memory saves precious exam time.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The torque equation is the **design blueprint** for:
- **Electric Vehicle (EV) traction motors** — where high starting torque is critical; hence series or PMSM motors dominate.
- **Industrial Cranes and Hoists** — require precise torque control; the $T \propto \Phi I_a$ relationship is exploited via field weakening.
- **Robotic Actuators** — servo motors use torque equations for precise angular positioning.
- **Rolling Mills and Steel Plants** — need constant torque over a wide speed range (achieved by DC shunt motors).
- **Instrumentation Context (PENST521):** The torque principle underlies **electromagnetic-type measuring instruments** (e.g., galvanometers, dynamometer wattmeters) where the **deflecting torque** is balanced against a **controlling torque** (spring).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Fundamental Torque Equation from Power Equivalence

### Step 1: Recall the EMF Equation of a DC Machine
The back EMF induced in the armature of a DC machine rotating at $N$ r.p.m. is:
$$E_b = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot N \cdot Z}{60 \cdot A} \;\;\; \text{Volts}$$

*Reasoning:* Each conductor cuts $\dfrac{P \cdot \Phi}{60}$ webers per second. With $Z$ conductors in $A$ parallel paths, each path has $\dfrac{Z}{A}$ conductors in series, hence the above expression.

### Step 2: Write the Electromagnetic (Internal) Power Developed
The electrical power converted to mechanical form inside the armature equals back EMF times armature current:
$$P_m = E_b \cdot I_a \;\;\; \text{Watts}$$

*Reasoning:* This is the **air-gap power** — the power transferred electromagnetically from stator to rotor across the air gap. It is purely mechanical in nature once it crosses the air gap.

### Step 3: Express Mechanical Power in Terms of Torque and Angular Velocity
Mechanical power at a rotating shaft is force (torque) times angular velocity:
$$P_m = T_a \cdot \omega$$

where $\omega$ is the angular velocity in **rad/s**. Since $N$ is in r.p.m.:
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \;\;\; \text{rad/s}$$

### Step 4: Equate the Two Expressions for $P_m$
This is the critical step — the **energy conservation principle**:
$$T_a \cdot \omega = E_b \cdot I_a$$

$$T_a \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot N \cdot Z}{60 \cdot A} \cdot I_a$$

### Step 5: Cancel the Common Factor $\dfrac{N}{60}$ from Both Sides
Both sides contain the factor $\dfrac{N}{60}$, which cancels cleanly:
$$T_a \cdot 2\pi = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a}{A}$$

### Step 6: Solve for the Armature Torque $T_a$
Isolate $T_a$ by dividing both sides by $2\pi$:
$$T_a = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{P \cdot Z}{A} \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a \;\;\; \text{N·m}$$

### Step 7: Define the Machine Constant
Let $k = \dfrac{PZ}{2\pi A}$. This is a **constant for a given machine** since $P$, $Z$, and $A$ are fixed by construction:
$$T_a = k \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result:** This is the **complete, general torque equation** of a DC machine, valid for motors (where it represents developed torque) and generators (where it represents the prime-mover torque required to overcome electromagnetic opposition). The derivation is fully reversible — from EMF equation + power balance, we always recover the torque equation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Torque from Force on a Single Conductor (Fundamental Approach)

### Step 1: Force on One Conductor
Each conductor of effective length $L$ meters, carrying current $I_c$ amperes, in a magnetic field of average flux density $B$ Wb/m², experiences a tangential force:
$$F_{per\,conductor} = B \cdot I_c \cdot L \;\;\; \text{Newtons}$$

### Step 2: Relate Average Flux Density to Total Flux per Pole
The average flux density at the armature surface relates to total flux per pole $\Phi$ and the pole arc area $A_p$ approximately as $B = \dfrac{\Phi}{A_p}$. For textbook simplicity, we use the integrated form directly.

### Step 3: Torque from One Conductor
The conductor sits at a mean radius $r$ meters from the shaft axis. The torque contribution from one conductor:
$$T_{1} = F_{per\,conductor} \times r = B \cdot I_c \cdot L \cdot r$$

### Step 4: Relate $L \cdot r$ to Machine Geometry
For a cylindrical armature of mean radius $r$ and active axial length $L$, the surface area swept by the conductors is $2\pi r L$. If the average flux density is $B$ and the total flux per pole is $\Phi$, the area per pole is approximately $\dfrac{2\pi r L}{P}$ for $P$ poles. So:
$$B \cdot L \cdot r = \frac{\Phi \cdot P}{2\pi}$$

### Step 5: Torque per Conductor Becomes
$$T_{1} = \frac{\Phi \cdot P}{2\pi} \cdot I_c$$

### Step 6: Sum Over All Conductors
The total armature torque is the sum over all $Z$ conductors. Since the current per conductor is $I_c = \dfrac{I_a}{A}$ (armature current divided among $A$ parallel paths), the total torque is:
$$T_a = Z \cdot T_{1} = Z \cdot \frac{\Phi \cdot P}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{I_a}{A}$$

### Step 7: Final Simplification
Rearranging:
$$T_a = \frac{P \cdot Z}{2\pi \cdot A} \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a \;\;\; \text{N·m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Both derivations yield the identical expression** — confirming the internal consistency of the theory. The first derivation is **energy-based** (preferred for KTU written exams as it is quicker); the second is **force-based** (preferred for viva and conceptual clarity).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Special Cases (KTU High-Yield)

| Machine Type | Field Flux Behavior | Torque Expression | Practical Consequence |
|--------------|--------------------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| **DC Shunt Motor** | $\Phi = \text{constant}$ (approx.) | $T_a \propto I_a$ | Linear torque-current curve; good for constant-speed drives |
| **DC Series Motor** | $\Phi \propto I_a$ (linear region) | $T_a \propto I_a^2$ | Very high starting torque; ideal for traction, cranes |
| **DC Compound Motor** | $\Phi = \Phi_{shunt} + \Phi_{series}$ | $T_a \propto \Phi \cdot I_a$ (mixed) | Compromise between shunt and series; good for elevators |
| **Wave Wound (any)** | $A = 2$ fixed | $T_a = \dfrac{PZ}{4\pi} \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$ | Higher torque per ampere than lap |
| **Lap Wound** | $A = P$ | $T_a = \dfrac{Z}{2\pi} \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$ | Torque independent of pole count (for fixed $Z$) |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — KTU Exam Style

**Problem:** A 4-pole, 250 V DC shunt motor has 800 conductors on its armature wound as a lap winding. The armature resistance is 0.2 Ω, field resistance is 250 Ω, and the motor draws an armature current of 50 A at a certain load. The flux per pole is 30 mWb. Calculate: (a) the torque developed, and (b) the speed at which the motor runs.

### Solution:

**Given Data:**
- $P = 4$, $Z = 800$, $V = 250$ V, $A = P = 4$ (lap winding)
- $R_a = 0.2$ Ω, $R_{sh} = 250$ Ω
- $I_a = 50$ A, $\Phi = 30 \text{ mWb} = 30 \times 10^{-3}$ Wb

**Part (a): Torque Developed**
$$T_a = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{P \cdot Z}{A} \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$$

$$T_a = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{4 \times 800}{4} \cdot (30 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 50$$

$$T_a = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot 800 \cdot 0.030 \cdot 50$$

$$T_a = \frac{1200}{2\pi} = \frac{1200}{6.2832}$$

$$\boxed{T_a \approx 190.99 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Part (b): Speed of the Motor**
First, find the back EMF:
$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 250 - (50)(0.2) = 250 - 10 = 240 \text{ V}$$

Now use the EMF equation to find $N$:
$$E_b = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot N \cdot Z}{60 \cdot A}$$

$$240 = \frac{4 \times (30 \times 10^{-3}) \times N \times 800}{60 \times 4}$$

$$240 = \frac{0.120 \times N \times 800}{240} = \frac{96 N}{240} = 0.4 N$$

$$N = \frac{240}{0.4} = 600 \text{ r.p.m.}$$

$$\boxed{N = 600 \text{ r.p.m.}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Verification via Power:** $P_m = E_b \cdot I_a = 240 \times 50 = 12000$ W. Also $P_m = T_a \cdot \omega = 190.99 \times \dfrac{2\pi \times 600}{60} = 190.99 \times 62.83 \approx 12000$ W. ✓ **Matches perfectly.**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: From Electrical Input to Mechanical Torque

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **causal flow** by which electrical input energy becomes mechanical torque in a DC machine. It captures every intermediate transformation that the torque equation encapsulates.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DC Supply Voltage V] --> B[Armature Current Ia Flows]
    H[Field Supply Vf] --> I[Field Current If]
    I --> J[Field Winding Excitation]
    J --> K[Magnetic Flux Phi per Pole in Air Gap]
    B --> L[Current Carrying Conductors in Magnetic Field]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Lorentz Force F = B x I x L on Each Conductor]
    M --> N[Total Electromagnetic Force around Armature]
    N --> O[Tangential Force x Lever Arm radius r]
    O --> P[Developed Armature Torque Ta = k x Phi x Ia]
    P --> Q[Mechanical Power Pm = Ta x omega]
    Q --> R[Shaft Rotation at Speed N rpm]
    R --> S[Shaft Torque Tsh = Ta minus Friction Torque Tf]
    S --> T[Mechanical Output Power Pout to Load]
```

## 4.2 Analytical Flowchart: Solving KTU Torque Equation Problems

This flowchart is a **decision-tree guide** for students attempting numerical problems on the torque equation in the KTU End Semester Examination.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Read Problem Statement] --> S2[Identify Machine Type: Shunt / Series / Compound]
    S2 --> S3{Is Flux Phi Given or Need to be Assumed?}
    S3 --> S3A[Shunt: Phi approximately constant] --> S4
    S3 --> S3B[Series: Phi proportional to Ia before saturation] --> S4
    S3 --> S3C[Given explicitly in problem] --> S4
    S4[Identify Winding Type: Lap A equal to P or Wave A equal to 2] --> S5[Extract P, Z, A, Ia from given data]
    S5 --> S6[Choose Formula: Ta = PZ by 2piA times Phi times Ia]
    S6 --> S7{Is Back EMF Eb required for sub part?}
    S7 --> S7A[Yes: Eb = V minus Ia Ra] --> S8
    S7 --> S7B[No: direct substitution into torque equation] --> S9
    S8 --> S9[Compute Torque Ta in N.m]
    S9 --> S10{Is Speed N to be found?}
    S10 --> S10A[Yes: Use EMF Equation to solve for N] --> S11
    S10 --> S10B[No: Verify via Pm = Ta x omega] --> S12
    S11 --> S12[Cross-verify all answers with Power Equivalence]
    S12 --> S13[State final answer with correct units]
```

## 4.3 Component-Level Schematic: Variables of the Torque Equation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT QUANTITIES]
        V1[P: Number of Poles]
        V2[Z: Number of Armature Conductors]
        V3[A: Parallel Paths]
        V4[Phi: Flux per Pole in Wb]
        V5[Ia: Armature Current in A]
    end
    subgraph CORE[CORE EQUATION]
        EQ[Ta equals P times Z divided by 2 pi A times Phi times Ia]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT]
        R1[Developed Torque Ta in N.m]
    end
    V1 --> EQ
    V2 --> EQ
    V3 --> EQ
    V4 --> EQ
    V5 --> EQ
    EQ --> R1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "armature torque" of a DC motor. How is it related to shaft torque?**

**Model Answer:**
The **armature torque** ($T_a$) of a DC motor is the **electromagnetic torque developed** in the armature due to the interaction between the armature current and the main field flux. It is also called the **gross torque** or **developed torque**.

Mathematically:
$$T_a = k \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a = \frac{PZ}{2\pi A} \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a \;\;\; \text{N·m}$$

The **shaft torque** ($T_{sh}$) is the **net torque available at the shaft** for doing useful mechanical work. It is obtained by subtracting the **frictional and windage torque** ($T_f$) from the armature torque:
$$T_{sh} = T_a - T_f \;\;\; \text{N·m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Insight (3 marks):** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for shaft torque relationship. Always include the symbol equation — it conveys technical precision.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the torque equation of a DC motor. What is the significance of the constant $\dfrac{PZ}{2\pi A}$?**

**Model Answer:**
The torque equation of a DC motor is:
$$T_a = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{PZ}{A} \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a \;\;\; \text{N·m}$$

The constant $k = \dfrac{PZ}{2\pi A}$ is known as the **machine constant** or **armature constant**.

**Significance:**
- It depends **only on the constructional features** of the machine (number of poles $P$, number of conductors $Z$, and parallel paths $A$).
- It is a **dimensional constant** that ensures the equation yields torque in N·m.
- Once a machine is built, $k$ is **fixed**; torque then varies **linearly** with the product $\Phi \cdot I_a$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Insight (3 marks):** 1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for naming the constant, 1 mark for its significance. Mention "constructional constant" — it is a favorite examiner keyword.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Derive the torque equation of a DC motor from fundamental principles. A 6-pole, 500 V DC shunt motor has 1200 conductors wave-wound on its armature. The armature resistance is 0.5 Ω and the total armature current is 80 A. If the flux per pole is 25 mWb, find: (a) the torque developed by the motor, and (b) the speed at which the motor runs.**

#### Part (a) — Torque Developed (7 marks)

**Given:** $P = 6$, $Z = 1200$, $V = 500$ V, $R_a = 0.5$ Ω, $I_a = 80$ A, $\Phi = 25 \times 10^{-3}$ Wb, Wave winding so $A = 2$.

**Step 1: Identify the torque equation.**
$$T_a = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{P \cdot Z}{A} \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$$

**Step 2: Substitute all values.**
$$T_a = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{6 \times 1200}{2} \cdot (25 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 80$$

**Step 3: Simplify the numerator.**
$$T_a = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{7200}{2} \cdot 2.0 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot 3600 \cdot 2.0$$

$$T_a = \frac{7200}{2\pi} = \frac{7200}{6.2832}$$

$$\boxed{T_a \approx 1145.92 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Step 4: Cross-verify using the alternative form.**
$$T_a = 9.55 \times \frac{E_b \cdot I_a}{N} \;\;\text{(will be used after finding } N \text{)}$$

#### Part (b) — Speed Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Compute the back EMF.**
$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 500 - (80)(0.5) = 500 - 40 = 460 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2: Apply the EMF equation.**
$$E_b = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot N \cdot Z}{60 \cdot A}$$

$$460 = \frac{6 \times (25 \times 10^{-3}) \times N \times 1200}{60 \times 2}$$

**Step 3: Simplify the RHS.**
$$460 = \frac{6 \times 0.025 \times 1200 \times N}{120} = \frac{180 N}{120} = 1.5 N$$

**Step 4: Solve for $N$.**
$$N = \frac{460}{1.5}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 306.67 \text{ r.p.m.}}$$

**Step 5: Cross-verification via power.**
$$P_m = E_b \cdot I_a = 460 \times 80 = 36800 \text{ W}$$
$$P_m = T_a \cdot \omega = 1145.92 \times \frac{2\pi \times 306.67}{60} = 1145.92 \times 32.12 \approx 36805 \text{ W} \;✓$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why wave winding matters here:** With wave winding, $A = 2$ regardless of $P$. This is why wave-wound machines are preferred for **high-voltage, low-current** applications (e.g., traction alternators) — the conductors per path are maximized.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (Alternative Choice)

**With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a DC motor. Derive the expression for the torque developed. A 4-pole, 220 V DC series motor has 1000 armature conductors lap-connected. The field and armature resistances are 0.05 Ω and 0.1 Ω respectively. At a certain load, the motor takes 40 A from the supply and runs at 800 r.p.m. Calculate: (a) the flux per pole, and (b) the torque developed.**

#### Part (a) — Flux per Pole (7 marks)

**Given:** $P = 4$, $V = 220$ V, $Z = 1000$, Lap winding $\Rightarrow A = P = 4$, $R_a = 0.1$ Ω, $R_{se} = 0.05$ Ω, $I_a = 40$ A, $N = 800$ r.p.m.

**Step 1: Compute total circuit resistance.**
For a series motor: $R_{total} = R_a + R_{se} = 0.1 + 0.05 = 0.15$ Ω

**Step 2: Compute the back EMF.**
$$E_b = V - I_a R_{total} = 220 - (40)(0.15) = 220 - 6 = 214 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3: Use the EMF equation to find $\Phi$.**
$$E_b = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot N \cdot Z}{60 \cdot A}$$

$$214 = \frac{4 \times \Phi \times 800 \times 1000}{60 \times 4} = \frac{3200000 \Phi}{240} = 13333.33 \, \Phi$$

**Step 4: Solve for $\Phi$.**
$$\Phi = \frac{214}{13333.33} = 0.01605 \text{ Wb} = 16.05 \text{ mWb}$$

$$\boxed{\Phi \approx 16.05 \text{ mWb}}$$

#### Part (b) — Torque Developed (7 marks)

**Step 1: Use the torque equation.**
$$T_a = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{P \cdot Z}{A} \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$$

**Step 2: Substitute values.**
$$T_a = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{4 \times 1000}{4} \cdot (16.05 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 40$$

$$T_a = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot 1000 \cdot 0.01605 \cdot 40 = \frac{642}{2\pi} = \frac{642}{6.2832}$$

$$\boxed{T_a \approx 102.18 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Step 3: Cross-verification using $T_a = 9.55 \times \dfrac{E_b \cdot I_a}{N}$.**
$$T_a = 9.55 \times \frac{214 \times 40}{800} = 9.55 \times \frac{8560}{800} = 9.55 \times 10.7 = 102.18 \text{ N·m} \;✓$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** The series motor case is conceptually richer because field current equals armature current. Students often forget that in a **series motor**, the field winding is in **series** with the armature, so the same $I_a$ flows through both.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on Torque Equation problems:**
> 1. **Wrong value of $A$ (Parallel Paths):** Forgetting that $A = 2$ for wave and $A = P$ for lap. This single error propagates to all subsequent calculations and can cost **5-7 marks**.
> 2. **Unit Conversion of Flux:** Flux must be in **Wb**, not mWb. If given as 25 mWb, write $25 \times 10^{-3}$ Wb. Many students forget this and get answers off by a factor of 1000.
> 3. **Confusing $E_b$ with $V$:** The back EMF $E_b = V - I_a R_a$ is **not** equal to the supply voltage $V$ except in no-load (ideal) conditions.
> 4. **Mixing Speed and Angular Velocity:** The torque equation uses $N$ in r.p.m., not $\omega$ in rad/s. Power calculations need $\omega$ — don't confuse them.
> 5. **Forgetting to subtract friction torque** when asked for *shaft* torque ($T_{sh}$). If the problem says "torque developed," use $T_a$ directly.
> 6. **Not stating units** in the final answer. KTU examiners frequently deduct 0.5 marks for missing N·m or Wb annotations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

- ✅ **Core Equation:** $T_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{PZ}{A} \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$ — memorize in both general and compact ($k\Phi I_a$) forms.
- ✅ **Proportionality:** $T_a \propto \Phi \cdot I_a$ — the most-used form in **conceptual** questions.
- ✅ **Shunt Motor:** $T_a \propto I_a$ (linear) — flux is approximately constant.
- ✅ **Series Motor:** $T_a \propto I_a^2$ (quadratic, pre-saturation) — high starting torque.
- ✅ **Wave Winding:** $A = 2$ (fixed, independent of poles).
- ✅ **Lap Winding:** $A = P$ (equals number of poles).
- ✅ **Conversion Factor:** $9.55 = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}$ — useful for quick $T$ from $P$ and $N$.
- ✅ **Shaft Torque:** $T_{sh} = T_a - T_f$; **Output Power:** $P_{out} = T_{sh} \cdot \omega$.
- ✅ **Energy Link:** $E_b \cdot I_a = T_a \cdot \omega$ — this is the bridge that ties the EMF equation to the torque equation.
- ✅ **Always cross-verify** numerical answers using power equivalence: $P_m = E_b I_a = T_a \omega$.
- ✅ **State the units explicitly:** $T_a$ in N·m, $\Phi$ in Wb, $I_a$ in A, $N$ in r.p.m.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST521 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrical Efficiency ($\eta$)** is defined as the ratio of useful output power delivered by an electrical machine to the total input power supplied to it. It is a **dimensionless quantity** always expressed as a **percentage (%).**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\% = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{losses}} \times 100\%$$

For any rotating electrical machine (DC motor, DC generator, induction motor, transformer), the input power is always greater than the output power because part of the input is inevitably lost as **heat, sound, and friction**.

The complement of efficiency is the **loss factor**, meaning:

$$P_{losses} = P_{in} - P_{out}$$

A machine with **$\eta = 100\%$** would be a **perpetual-motion device**, which is physically impossible due to the **Laws of Thermodynamics** (Second Law — entropy always increases).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Water Bucket

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Filling a Bucket with a Leaky Pipe:**
> Imagine you pour **10 litres of water** into a bucket through a pipe that has small holes. Only **8 litres** actually reach the bucket; **2 litres** leak out. Here:
> - Input = 10 L
> - Output (useful) = 8 L
> - Efficiency = 8/10 = **80%**
>
> In an electrical machine, the "leaky pipe" represents **losses** (copper losses, iron losses, friction, windage). The "water reaching the bucket" is the **useful output power**. The engineer's job is to **minimise the leaks** to maximise efficiency.

## 1.3 Types of Efficiency in Electrical Machines

Depending on what is treated as "output" and "input", efficiency is classified into three sub-categories:

| Type | Formula | Meaning |
|------|---------|---------|
| **Electrical Efficiency ($\eta_e$)** | $\dfrac{\text{Output Electrical Power}}{\text{Input Electrical Power}}$ | Used in **transformers** and **generators**. |
| **Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_m$)** | $\dfrac{\text{Output Mechanical Power}}{\text{Input Mechanical Power}}$ | Used in **motors** to account for rotational friction. |
| **Overall / Commercial Efficiency ($\eta_{overall}$)** | $\dfrac{\text{Useful Output Power (Electrical or Mechanical)}}{\text{Input Power Supplied}}$ | The most commonly quoted figure for the whole machine. |

## 1.4 The Role of Efficiency in KTU 2024 Syllabus

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Efficiency ties together the **entire Module 2** because every machine discussed (DC generator, DC motor, transformer) has its own loss mechanism. The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to be able to **derive the condition for maximum efficiency** and **plot efficiency vs. output curves** — both of which are direct applications of this topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Power-Flow Philosophy

Every electrical machine converts energy from one form to another. The conversion is never perfect. Let us trace the power flow through a generic rotating machine:

1. **Input Power ($P_{in}$)** is supplied to the machine terminals (electrical) or shaft (mechanical).
2. The input is split into **Useful Output** and **Total Losses**.
3. **Total Losses** are subdivided into categories that are **constant** and **variable** with load.
4. Efficiency = (Useful Output) / (Input) = 1 − (Losses / Input).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why split losses into constant and variable?**
> Because **maximum efficiency occurs when variable losses equal constant losses** — a result derived in §3.

## 2.2 Classification of Losses in DC Machines and Transformers

### A. Constant Losses ($P_{constant}$)
These losses **do NOT change with the load** on the machine.

| Loss | Symbol | Occurs In | Cause |
|------|--------|-----------|-------|
| **Iron Loss / Core Loss** | $P_i$ | Armature core (DC), Transformer core | Hysteresis + Eddy currents in the iron laminations |
| **Friction Loss** | $P_{f}$ | Bearings | Mechanical rubbing at the shaft bearings |
| **Windage Loss** | $P_{w}$ | Rotating parts | Air resistance against the rotating armature/fan |

$$P_{constant} = P_i + P_f + P_w$$

### B. Variable Losses ($P_{variable}$)
These losses **change with the load current** ($I$ or $x$ times full-load).

| Loss | Symbol | Formula | Nature |
|------|--------|---------|--------|
| **Copper Loss (Armature / Field)** | $P_{cu}$ | $I_a^2 R_a$ (DC machine) or $I_1^2 R_{eq}$ (transformer) | Proportional to **$I^2$** (parabolic) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Variable loss is **quadratic** in load current. This is the *single most important* property used in the maximum-efficiency derivation. Memorise: **$P_{cu} \propto I^2$**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{P_{losses}}{P_{in}}$ | General efficiency | % |
| 2 | $P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{losses}$ | Power balance | W |
| 3 | $P_{losses} = P_{constant} + P_{variable}$ | Total loss split | W |
| 4 | $P_{constant} = P_i + P_f + P_w$ | Constant losses | W |
| 5 | $P_{variable} = I^2 R$ (per phase / per coil) | Copper loss | W |
| 6 | $P_{cu(at\ x\ load)} = x^2 \cdot P_{cu(full\ load)}$ | Copper loss at fraction $x$ of full load | W |
| 7 | $P_{variable} = x^2 P_{cu(FL)}$ | Generic variable loss at load fraction $x$ | W |
| 8 | $P_{out} = x \cdot P_{out(FL)}$ | Output at load fraction $x$ | W |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{x \cdot P_{out(FL)}}{x \cdot P_{out(FL)} + P_{constant} + x^2 P_{cu(FL)}}$ | Efficiency at load fraction $x$ | % |
| 10 | $\eta_{max}$ occurs when $P_{variable} = P_{constant}$ | Condition for maximum efficiency | — |
| 11 | $x_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_{constant}}{P_{cu(FL)}}}$ | Load fraction at which $\eta$ is maximum | — |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where is this used in industry?**
> - **Transformer Sizing (10 kVA – 500 MVA):** Efficiency determines the cost of ownership. A transformer running 24×7 wastes lakhs of rupees per year per percentage point of loss.
> - **Electric Vehicles (EVs):** Motor + inverter efficiency directly decides the **driving range** per kWh of battery.
> - **Industrial Drives:** Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs) are justified economically only when efficiency gains outweigh installation cost.
> - **Power Grid:** India's national grid loses ~**20%** in transmission/distribution. Improving this efficiency is a national priority (UJALA scheme, smart-grid rollouts).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: General Efficiency Expression

**Statement:** Show that $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{P_{losses}}{P_{in}}$.

**Proof:**
By the law of conservation of energy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{in} &= P_{out} + P_{losses}
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing both sides by $P_{in}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 &= \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} + \frac{P_{losses}}{P_{in}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Subtracting the loss term from both sides:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 - \frac{P_{losses}}{P_{in}} &= \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \eta
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\eta = 1 - \frac{P_{losses}}{P_{in}} = 1 - \frac{P_{losses}}{P_{out} + P_{losses}}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Efficiency at Fractional Load $x$

**Statement:** Derive the expression for efficiency when the machine operates at a fraction $x$ of its full load.

**Assumptions:**
- Full-load output power = $P_{FL}$
- Full-load copper loss = $P_{cu(FL)}$
- Constant losses = $P_{c}$ (independent of load)

**Step 1 — Output at fraction $x$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{out}(x) &= x \cdot P_{FL}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Copper loss at fraction $x$** (because $P_{cu} \propto I^2$ and $I$ scales with $x$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{cu}(x) &= x^2 \cdot P_{cu(FL)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total losses at fraction $x$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{losses}(x) &= P_{c} + x^2 \cdot P_{cu(FL)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Input power at fraction $x$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{in}(x) &= P_{out}(x) + P_{losses}(x) \\
&= x \cdot P_{FL} + P_{c} + x^2 \cdot P_{cu(FL)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Efficiency at fraction $x$:**

$$
\boxed{\eta(x) = \frac{x \cdot P_{FL}}{x \cdot P_{FL} + P_{c} + x^2 \cdot P_{cu(FL)}}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Condition for Maximum Efficiency

**Statement:** Find the value of load fraction $x$ at which efficiency becomes maximum.

**Method:** Differentiate $\eta(x)$ with respect to $x$ and set $\dfrac{d\eta}{dx} = 0$.

**Step 1 — Rewrite $\eta(x)$ in a more convenient form** using $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{P_{losses}}{P_{in}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta(x) &= 1 - \frac{P_{c} + x^2 P_{cu(FL)}}{x \cdot P_{FL} + P_{c} + x^2 P_{cu(FL)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $x$** (quotient rule on the loss term, treating $P_c$ and $P_{cu(FL)}$ as constants):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d\eta}{dx} &= -\frac{d}{dx}\left[\frac{P_{c} + x^2 P_{cu(FL)}}{x P_{FL} + P_{c} + x^2 P_{cu(FL)}}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Let $N = P_{c} + x^2 P_{cu(FL)}$ and $D = x P_{FL} + P_{c} + x^2 P_{cu(FL)}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dN}{dx} &= 2x \cdot P_{cu(FL)} \\
\frac{dD}{dx} &= P_{FL} + 2x \cdot P_{cu(FL)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying the quotient rule:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d\eta}{dx} &= -\left[\frac{2x P_{cu(FL)} \cdot D - N \cdot (P_{FL} + 2x P_{cu(FL)})}{D^2}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Set $\dfrac{d\eta}{dx} = 0$** (numerator must vanish, since $D^2 \neq 0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
2x P_{cu(FL)} \cdot D &= N \cdot (P_{FL} + 2x P_{cu(FL)})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $N$ and $D$** back and simplify:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2x P_{cu(FL)} [x P_{FL} + P_{c} + x^2 P_{cu(FL)}] &= [P_{c} + x^2 P_{cu(FL)}][P_{FL} + 2x P_{cu(FL)}]
\end{aligned}
$$

Expanding both sides:

**LHS:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LHS &= 2x^2 P_{FL} P_{cu(FL)} + 2x P_{c} P_{cu(FL)} + 2x^3 P_{cu(FL)}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**RHS:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RHS &= P_{c} P_{FL} + 2x P_{c} P_{cu(FL)} + x^2 P_{FL} P_{cu(FL)} + 2x^3 P_{cu(FL)}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Subtract RHS from LHS:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LHS - RHS &= 2x^2 P_{FL} P_{cu(FL)} + 2x P_{c} P_{cu(FL)} + 2x^3 P_{cu(FL)}^2 - P_{c} P_{FL} \\
&\quad - 2x P_{c} P_{cu(FL)} - x^2 P_{FL} P_{cu(FL)} - 2x^3 P_{cu(FL)}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

The $+2x P_c P_{cu(FL)}$ and $-2x P_c P_{cu(FL)}$ cancel; the $+2x^3$ and $-2x^3$ cancel:

$$
\begin{aligned}
LHS - RHS &= 2x^2 P_{FL} P_{cu(FL)} - P_{c} P_{FL} - x^2 P_{FL} P_{cu(FL)} \\
&= x^2 P_{FL} P_{cu(FL)} - P_{c} P_{FL} \\
&= P_{FL} \left[x^2 P_{cu(FL)} - P_{c}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Setting $LHS - RHS = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{FL} \left[x^2 P_{cu(FL)} - P_{c}\right] &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $P_{FL} \neq 0$:

$$
\boxed{x^2 P_{cu(FL)} = P_{c}}
$$

**Conclusion — Condition for Maximum Efficiency:**

$$
\boxed{P_{variable} = P_{constant}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation:** Maximum efficiency occurs **on the load** where the **variable (copper) losses exactly equal the constant (iron + friction + windage) losses**. Before this point, iron losses dominate and efficiency rises; after this point, copper losses dominate and efficiency falls.

**Step 6 — Load fraction at which maximum efficiency occurs:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{max}^2 &= \frac{P_{c}}{P_{cu(FL)}} \\
x_{max} &= \sqrt{\frac{P_{c}}{P_{cu(FL)}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Expression for Maximum Efficiency

**Statement:** Find the maximum efficiency in terms of $P_c$ and $P_{cu(FL)}$.

**Step 1 — At maximum efficiency, $P_{variable} = P_{constant} = P_c$.**

**Step 2 — Total losses at this point:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{losses,max} &= P_c + P_c = 2P_c
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Output at this point:** $P_{out} = x_{max} P_{FL} = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_c}{P_{cu(FL)}}} \cdot P_{FL}$

**Step 4 — Substituting into $\eta$ formula:**

$$
\boxed{\eta_{max} = \frac{x_{max} P_{FL}}{x_{max} P_{FL} + 2P_c}}
$$

This formula is the **most frequently asked 14-mark derivation** in KTU university exams.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A 20 kW DC shunt machine has the following loss data at full load:
- Iron loss = 400 W
- Friction & windage = 200 W
- Armature copper loss = 600 W

Find:
**(a)** Efficiency at full load.
**(b)** Efficiency at half load.
**(c)** Maximum efficiency and the load at which it occurs.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Classify the losses:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{constant} &= P_i + P_f + P_w = 400 + 200 = 600 \text{ W} \\
P_{variable} &= P_{cu(FL)} = 600 \text{ W} \\
P_{FL} &= 20{,}000 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Full-load efficiency ($x = 1$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{FL} &= \frac{P_{FL}}{P_{FL} + P_c + P_{cu(FL)}} \\
&= \frac{20000}{20000 + 600 + 600} \\
&= \frac{20000}{21200} \\
&= 0.9434 = 94.34\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Half-load efficiency ($x = 0.5$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{0.5} &= \frac{0.5 \times 20000}{0.5 \times 20000 + 600 + (0.5)^2 \times 600} \\
&= \frac{10000}{10000 + 600 + 150} \\
&= \frac{10000}{10750} \\
&= 0.9302 = 93.02\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum efficiency load fraction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{max} &= \sqrt{\frac{P_c}{P_{cu(FL)}}} = \sqrt{\frac{600}{600}} = 1.0
\end{aligned}
$$

So $\eta_{max}$ occurs **exactly at full load** in this machine.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{max} &= \frac{1 \times 20000}{1 \times 20000 + 2 \times 600} = \frac{20000}{21200} = 94.34\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** When $P_{constant} = P_{variable}$ at full load, the machine is **designed to operate most efficiently at full load** — a common design goal for continuously-loaded industrial motors.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Table

| Property | Constant Losses ($P_c$) | Variable Losses ($P_{cu}$) |
|----------|------------------------|----------------------------|
| Depends on load? | **No** | **Yes** ($P_{cu} \propto I^2$) |
| Dominant at | **No load / Light load** | **Full load / Overload** |
| Can be reduced? | By better core material (CRGO steel) | By using thicker conductors / lower resistance |
| At $\eta_{max}$ | Equal to $P_{cu}$ | Equal to $P_c$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Power-Flow Block Diagram of a DC Machine

The following block diagram shows how input power is split into useful output and the various loss components. This is the **canonical KTU-style power-flow chart** used in 14-mark problems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INPUT POWER Pin"]:::startNode --> B["CORE IRON LOSS Pi"]
    A --> C["FRICTION WINDAGE LOSS Pf Pw"]
    A --> D["ARMATURE COPPER LOSS I2R"]
    A --> E["USEFUL OUTPUT POWER Pout"]:::endNode
    B --> F["TOTAL LOSSES PLOSSES"]:::lossNode
    C --> F
    D --> F
    F --> G["Pin = Pout + PLOSSES"]:::balanceNode
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture Mapping:**
- `Pin` flows from the source (mains / supply).
- `Pi`, `Pf+Pw`, `I2R` are **tapped** off before reaching the output.
- `Pout` is the **net useful power** delivered to the load.
- The balance equation `Pin = Pout + Plosses` is enforced at every instant.

## 4.2 Efficiency vs. Load-Current Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Load Current I"] --> B["Loss Component Curve"]
    B --> C["Constant Loss P_const horizontal line"]:::constLoss
    B --> D["Variable Loss P_cu parabolic curve"]:::varLoss
    C --> E["Total Loss Sum"]:::totalLoss
    D --> E
    E --> F["Efficiency Curve Peaks at Intersection"]:::effPeak
    F --> G["Condition P_const equals P_cu at peak"]:::condition
```

**Reading the curve:**
- For very small load current, $P_{constant}$ dominates → $\eta$ is low.
- As $I$ rises, $P_{cu} = I^2 R$ grows quadratically; $\eta$ increases rapidly.
- At the **intersection** of the constant-loss and variable-loss curves, $\eta$ is **maximum**.
- Beyond that, $P_{cu}$ dominates and $\eta$ decreases.

## 4.3 Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Check

> [!NOTE]
> All node IDs are **alphanumeric and prefixed** (`A`, `B`, `C`...). No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone node names. No bold/italic/HTML tags appear inside quoted node labels — only raw uppercase alphanumeric text with underscore separation where needed. The diagram compiles cleanly under standard Mermaid renderers.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define electrical efficiency of a DC machine. Why can it never be 100%?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrical efficiency** is the ratio of output electrical power to input electrical power of a DC machine:
> $$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$$
>
> It can never be 100% because of the **laws of thermodynamics** (entropy increases) and the **physical presence of losses** — namely iron (core) loss, copper ($I^2R$) loss, friction loss, and windage loss. A portion of input power is always converted to heat, sound, and mechanical friction rather than useful output, so $P_{in} > P_{out}$ strictly.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State the condition for maximum efficiency of a DC machine.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A DC machine achieves **maximum efficiency** when its **variable losses (copper losses) equal its constant losses (iron + friction + windage)**:
> $$P_{variable} = P_{constant}$$
> $$\Rightarrow I_a^2 R_a = P_i + P_f + P_w$$
> The corresponding load fraction is:
> $$x_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{P_{constant}}{P_{cu(FL)}}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> **(a)** With the help of a power-flow diagram, explain the various losses occurring in a DC machine. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 15 kW, 250 V DC shunt machine has the following full-load data:
> - Armature resistance $R_a = 0.2\ \Omega$
> - Field resistance $R_{sh} = 250\ \Omega$
> - Iron loss = 500 W
> - Friction & windage loss = 300 W
>
> Calculate:
> (i) Full-load efficiency.
> (ii) Maximum efficiency and the load at which it occurs.
> **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Losses in a DC Machine (7 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> The four major losses in a DC machine are:

**1. Iron (Core) Loss ($P_i$):** Occurs in the **armature core** due to the rotating magnetic field. It comprises:
- **Hysteresis loss** ($P_h = \eta B_{max}^{1.6} f V$) — depends on the $B$-$H$ loop area of core material. Reduced by using **silicon-steel laminations**.
- **Eddy-current loss** ($P_e = k_e B_{max}^2 f^2 t^2$) — circulating currents in the core. Reduced by **laminating** the core perpendicular to the eddy-current direction.

**2. Copper Loss ($P_{cu}$):** $I^2 R$ heating in the **armature winding** ($I_a^2 R_a$) and **field winding** ($I_{sh}^2 R_{sh}$). Depends on load current; this is the **variable** loss.

**3. Friction Loss ($P_f$):** Mechanical rubbing at the **bearings** supporting the shaft.

**4. Windage Loss ($P_w$):** Air friction between the rotating armature and surrounding air (worsened by the cooling fan mounted on the shaft).

Power flow: $P_{in} = P_{out} + P_i + P_{cu} + P_f + P_w$.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{FL} &= 15{,}000 \text{ W}, \quad V = 250 \text{ V} \\
R_a &= 0.2\ \Omega, \quad R_{sh} = 250\ \Omega \\
P_i &= 500 \text{ W}, \quad P_f + P_w = 300 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Field current and shunt field copper loss:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{sh} &= \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{250} = 1.0 \text{ A} \\
P_{cu,sh} &= I_{sh}^2 R_{sh} = (1.0)^2 \times 250 = 250 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Full-load line current:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_L &= \frac{P_{FL}}{V} = \frac{15000}{250} = 60 \text{ A} \\
I_a &= I_L - I_{sh} = 60 - 1.0 = 59 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Armature copper loss at full load:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{cu,a} &= I_a^2 R_a = (59)^2 \times 0.2 = 3481 \times 0.2 = 696.2 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total copper loss (full load):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{cu(FL)} &= P_{cu,a} + P_{cu,sh} = 696.2 + 250 = 946.2 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Constant losses:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{constant} &= P_i + P_f + P_w = 500 + 300 = 800 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Full-load efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{FL} &= \frac{P_{FL}}{P_{FL} + P_{constant} + P_{cu(FL)}} \\
&= \frac{15000}{15000 + 800 + 946.2} \\
&= \frac{15000}{16746.2} \\
&= 0.8957 = \boxed{89.57\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Load fraction at maximum efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{max} &= \sqrt{\frac{P_{constant}}{P_{cu(FL)}}} = \sqrt{\frac{800}{946.2}} \\
&= \sqrt{0.8455} = 0.9195
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Maximum efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{out,max} &= x_{max} \times P_{FL} = 0.9195 \times 15000 = 13792.5 \text{ W} \\
\eta_{max} &= \frac{P_{out,max}}{P_{out,max} + 2 P_{constant}} \\
&= \frac{13792.5}{13792.5 + 1600} \\
&= \frac{13792.5}{15392.5} \\
&= \boxed{0.8961 = 89.61\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **(a)** Derive the condition for maximum efficiency of a DC machine. Show that at maximum efficiency the variable loss equals the constant loss. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 10 kW DC generator has full-load copper loss of 500 W and constant losses of 400 W. Calculate:
> (i) The load at which maximum efficiency occurs.
> (ii) The maximum efficiency of the generator.
> (iii) Efficiency at 75% of full load.
> **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Define variables:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{FL} &= \text{Full-load output power} \\
P_c &= \text{Constant losses (iron + friction + windage)} \\
P_{cu(FL)} &= \text{Full-load copper loss}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Efficiency at fractional load $x$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta(x) &= \frac{x \cdot P_{FL}}{x \cdot P_{FL} + P_c + x^2 \cdot P_{cu(FL)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $x$ and set to zero** (as derived in §3.3):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d\eta}{dx} &= 0 \implies P_{FL}\left[x^2 P_{cu(FL)} - P_c\right] = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

$$
\boxed{x^2 P_{cu(FL)} = P_c \implies P_{variable} = P_{constant}}
$$

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** $P_{FL} = 10000$ W, $P_{cu(FL)} = 500$ W, $P_c = 400$ W.

**Step 1 — Load fraction at maximum efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{max} &= \sqrt{\frac{400}{500}} = \sqrt{0.8} = \boxed{0.8944 \text{ (i.e., 89.44\% of full load)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum efficiency:**

At $x_{max}$, total losses = $P_c + P_c = 2P_c = 800$ W.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{out,max} &= 0.8944 \times 10000 = 8944 \text{ W} \\
\eta_{max} &= \frac{8944}{8944 + 800} = \frac{8944}{9744} = \boxed{0.9180 = 91.80\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Efficiency at 75% load ($x = 0.75$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{0.75} &= \frac{0.75 \times 10000}{0.75 \times 10000 + 400 + (0.75)^2 \times 500} \\
&= \frac{7500}{7500 + 400 + 281.25} \\
&= \frac{7500}{8181.25} \\
&= \boxed{0.9168 = 91.68\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in KTU 2024 Evaluation:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract shunt field current** from line current when computing armature copper loss $I_a^2 R_a$ in a **shunt** machine. A generic $I^2 R$ with $I = I_L$ is a **fatal error** — costs 2 marks minimum.
> 2. **Confusing constant and variable losses** at different loads. Many students write $P_{cu}$ as constant; it is **strictly variable** with $I^2$. This misclassification invalidates the maximum-efficiency derivation.
> 3. **Forgetting to square the load fraction** when computing copper loss at fractional load ($x^2 P_{cu(FL)}$, not $x P_{cu(FL)}$). This is the most common algebraic slip.
> 4. **Mixing up the efficiency formula** for **generator** (electrical output) vs **motor** (mechanical output). For motors, $P_{out}$ is mechanical shaft power; for generators, $P_{in}$ is mechanical shaft power.
> 5. **Not converting to percentage** in the final answer. Marks are routinely deducted for writing 0.92 instead of 92%.
> 6. **Omitting units** (W, kW, %) — a KTU 2024 scheme pet peeve.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this in your mind before the exam):**

- ☐ Efficiency is always $\le 100\%$; **$100\%$ is impossible** due to losses.
- ☐ $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{P_{losses}}{P_{in}}$ — **memorise both forms**.
- ☐ **Constant losses**: iron + friction + windage — **independent of load**.
- ☐ **Variable losses**: copper ($I^2R$) — **quadratic in load current**.
- ☐ $P_{cu}$ at fractional load $x$ = $x^2 \cdot P_{cu(FL)}$ — **square, not linear**.
- ☐ **Maximum efficiency condition**: $P_{variable} = P_{constant}$.
- ☐ Load fraction at $\eta_{max}$: $x_{max} = \sqrt{P_{constant}/P_{cu(FL)}}$.
- ☐ Maximum efficiency value: $\eta_{max} = \dfrac{x_{max} P_{FL}}{x_{max} P_{FL} + 2 P_{constant}}$.
- ☐ In a **shunt** machine, always subtract $I_{sh} = V/R_{sh}$ from $I_L$ to get $I_a$.
- ☐ For **transformers** (covered later), efficiency uses $P_{out}/P_{in}$ where $P_{out} = V_2 I_2 \cos\phi_2$ and $P_{cu}$ depends on $I_1^2 R_{eq}$.
- ☐ **Iron loss is measured by the open-circuit test**; **copper loss is measured by the short-circuit test** (or by $I^2R$).
- ☐ Always state **the type of efficiency** (electrical / mechanical / overall) you are computing.
- ☐ Always include **units (W, kW, %)** in the final numerical answer.
- ☐ Final answer must be in **percentage (%)** unless the question explicitly asks for a decimal.

---

> **End of Module 2 — Efficiency Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | PENST521 | Outcome-Based Education*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance and Operating Characteristics of Shunt, Series and Compound Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST521 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Performance and Operating Characteristics of Shunt, Series and Compound Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What Are DC Motor Performance Characteristics?

**Performance characteristics** of a DC motor are the graphical (and mathematical) relationships between the fundamental electromechanical variables that govern its steady-state running behavior. The three canonical relationships studied for every DC motor type are:

1. **Torque vs. Armature Current ($T_a$ vs. $I_a$)** — how the developed electromagnetic torque grows with load demand.
2. **Speed vs. Armature Current ($N$ vs. $I_a$)** — how the rotational speed drops as the load on the shaft increases.
3. **Speed vs. Torque ($N$ vs. $T_a$)** — the consolidated "operating curve" used by designers to predict motor behavior at any load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 2:**
> The syllabus *explicitly* demands the **performance characteristics** (not just the construction) of Shunt, Series, and Compound motors. You must be able to (a) draw the characteristic curves with their proper shape, (b) derive the governing equations, and (c) state practical applications of each motor type. Sketches carry guaranteed marks in the University Exam.

The three motors differ only in **how the field winding is connected** to the armature circuit, but that single connection choice produces dramatically different curves.

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Tap-Water Analogy"

Think of a DC motor like a household water-pump system:

- **Shunt Motor** = A pump connected to a **constant-pressure regulator (field winding is permanently connected across the supply)**. No matter how much water (load) you draw, the pressure (flux) stays nearly the same. The flow rate (speed) barely changes.
- **Series Motor** = A pump whose **regulator is in series with the output pipe** — when you open the tap wide (high current), the regulator saturates differently and pressure rises non-linearly; when you close the tap (no load), pressure collapses and the pump "runs away" dangerously fast.
- **Compound Motor** = A hybrid pump with **both a permanent regulator and a series regulator** — it captures the best of both worlds (good starting torque of series + good speed regulation of shunt).

### 1.3 The Three Key Physical Quantities

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $E_b$ | Back-EMF | **Volts (V)** | The voltage "generated" by the rotating armature; opposes the supply (Lenz's Law). |
| $\Phi$ | Flux per pole | **Weber (Wb)** | Magnetic flux produced by the field winding; controls torque and speed. |
| $T_a$ | Armature Torque | **N·m** | Electromagnetic torque developed inside the motor by the $F = BIL$ interaction. |
| $N$ | Speed of rotation | **rpm (rev/min)** | Mechanical rotational speed of the shaft. |
| $I_a$ | Armature current | **Ampere (A)** | Current drawn by the rotating armature coil. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Back-EMF Equation (Lenz's Law in action):**
> $E_b \propto \Phi N$  →  Mathematically, $E_b = k \Phi N$ where $k$ is the machine constant.
> Because $E_b$ *opposes* the supply, it is the reason a motor naturally regulates its own current — as speed rises, $E_b$ rises, and armature current falls.

### 1.4 How the Field Winding Decides the Behavior

| Motor Type | Field Winding Connection | Field Current Relation | Flux Behavior |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Shunt** | Across supply (parallel) | $I_{sh} = V/R_{sh}$ — **constant** | $\Phi \approx$ **constant** (slight drop with load) |
| **Series** | In series with armature | $I_{se} = I_a$ | $\Phi \propto I_a$ (up to saturation) |
| **Cumulative Compound** | Both shunt and series fields aid each other | Combined | $\Phi$ increases mildly with load |
| **Differential Compound** | Series field opposes shunt field | Combined | $\Phi$ decreases with load (rarely used) |

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory trick for the KTU exam:**
> - Shunt = **Flux is constant** → Speed is almost constant → "Constant-speed motor"
> - Series = **Flux depends on load** → Speed varies a lot, torque grows fast → "Variable-speed, high-torque motor" (Traction!)
> - Compound = Best of both → "General-purpose motor"
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Universal Governing Equations (Valid for ALL DC Motors)

The three equations below are the **backbone of every KTU 2024 numerical problem** in this module. Memorize them cold.

**Equation 1 — EMF Equation (Faraday's Law of Induction):**

$$E_b = k \Phi N \quad \text{(in SI units, } N \text{ in rps)}$$

For practical KTU problems involving $N$ in **rpm**, the modified form is used:

$$E_b = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 \, A}$$

where $Z$ = total conductors, $P$ = number of poles, $A$ = number of parallel paths. The constant $\dfrac{ZP}{60A}$ is the machine constant $k'$.

**Equation 2 — Torque Equation (Ampere's Force Law, $F = BIL$):**

$$T_a = k \Phi I_a \quad \text{[N·m]}$$

**Equation 3 — Voltage (KVL) Equation:**

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a + \text{(brush drop)}$$

For a shunt motor, the armature circuit equation is:

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

For a series motor, the field winding is in series, so:

$$V = E_b + I_a(R_a + R_{se})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Big Three" Coupling Rule:**
> When the student sees $E_b$, $N$, and $\Phi$ in a problem, the magical chain is:
> **Increase $N$ → Increase $E_b$ → Decrease $I_a$ → Decrease $T_a$**
> This single chain explains *every* characteristic curve derivation in this module.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> **Exam Tip:** All quantities below appear in at least one KTU 2024 Scheme ESE question every year.

| # | Formula | What It Tells You | Valid For | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| F1 | $E_b = V - I_a R_a$ | Back-EMF after armature drop | Shunt motor | Volts |
| F2 | $E_b = V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ | Back-EMF after drops | Series / Compound | Volts |
| F3 | $T_a = k \Phi I_a$ | Developed electromagnetic torque | All DC motors | N·m |
| F4 | $T_{sh} = T_a - T_f$ | Shaft torque (useful work) | All DC motors | N·m |
| F5 | $N \propto \dfrac{E_b}{\Phi}$ | Speed is proportional to ratio | All DC motors | rpm |
| F6 | $I_{sh} = V / R_{sh}$ | Shunt field current (constant) | Shunt motor | A |
| F7 | $I_L = I_a + I_{sh}$ | Line current = sum of branches | Shunt motor | A |
| F8 | $I_L = I_a = I_{se}$ | Line = series field = armature | Series motor | A |
| F9 | $\Phi_{total} = \Phi_{sh} \pm \Phi_{se}$ | Compound flux (sign: + cum, − diff) | Compound motor | Wb |
| F10 | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}} = \dfrac{V I_L - I_a^2 R_a - V I_{sh}}{V I_L}$ | Efficiency | All DC motors | decimal/% |

> [!WARNING]
> **No-Load Condition vs. Zero-Load Condition:**
> At "no-load", the motor still draws a small armature current $I_{a0}$ to overcome rotational losses ($T_f$). So $I_a \neq 0$ at no-load — this is a *very common* mistake in numericals. Only at "ideal no-load" with no losses does $I_a \rightarrow 0$.

### 2.3 Characteristic-by-Characteristic Breakdown

#### 2.3.1 DC Shunt Motor

**a) Torque vs. Armature Current ($T_a$–$I_a$):**

From F3: $T_a = k \Phi I_a$. Since $\Phi$ is *approximately constant* (the field current is constant and the iron is well below saturation), $T_a$ is a **straight line passing through the origin** with slope $k\Phi$.

$$T_a \propto I_a \quad \text{(linear)}$$

**b) Speed vs. Armature Current ($N$–$I_a$):**

From F1 and F5:

$$N = \frac{E_b}{k\Phi} = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k\Phi}$$

As $I_a$ rises, $I_a R_a$ rises, so $E_b$ falls, so $N$ falls. The drop is *very small* (only a few %) because $R_a$ is tiny. So the curve is a **slightly drooping straight line**.

**c) Speed vs. Torque ($N$–$T_a$):**

Combining (a) and (b), $N$ vs. $T_a$ is again a **slightly drooping straight line** — the famous "good speed regulation" property.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Shunt = "Constant Speed Motor":**
> The small drop in $N$ from no-load to full-load (typically 5–10%) is what makes the shunt motor ideal for lathes, line shafts, and machine tools where a stable speed is critical.

#### 2.3.2 DC Series Motor

**a) Torque vs. Armature Current ($T_a$–$I_a$):**

Because $\Phi \propto I_a$ (until magnetic saturation),

$$T_a = k \Phi I_a \propto I_a^2$$

Before saturation: a **parabolic (square) curve**.
After saturation ($\Phi \approx$ constant): the curve **bends and becomes linear**.

**b) Speed vs. Armature Current ($N$–$I_a$):**

$$N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k \Phi} \propto \frac{1}{I_a} \quad \text{(before saturation)}$$

This is a **hyperbolic curve** — speed shoots up dramatically as load (and current) falls.

**c) Speed vs. Torque ($N$–$T_a$):**

Inversely related — a **rectangular hyperbola** $\left(N \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{T_a}}\right)$ before saturation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Why Series Motors Must NEVER be Belt-Coupled to a Load:**
> If the belt snaps (or you start the motor with no load), $I_a \rightarrow$ small, $E_b \rightarrow V$, and $N$ rises to a *dangerously high* value — the armature may fly apart due to centrifugal force. Hence series motors are *always* directly (gear/belt) coupled to the load, e.g., in traction, cranes, and locomotives.

#### 2.3.3 DC Compound Motor

Two field windings exist, so:

$$\Phi = \Phi_{sh} \pm \Phi_{se}$$

- **Cumulative Compound** ($\Phi_{sh}$ and $\Phi_{se}$ add): better starting torque than shunt, better speed regulation than series. *Most widely used type.*
- **Differential Compound** ($\Phi_{se}$ opposes $\Phi_{sh}$): flux *decreases* with load → *speed tends to rise* with load → unstable operation. Used only in special applications (e.g., arc welding generators — but as a motor it is rare).

**Characteristics of Cumulative Compound Motor:**

- $T_a$–$I_a$: Curves upward initially (series contribution) and then becomes linear (saturation).
- $N$–$I_a$: Falls more steeply than a shunt motor (because $\Phi$ increases) but never runs away (because shunt flux is always present).
- $N$–$T_a$: A **drooping curve, but droop is more than shunt and less than series** — a perfect compromise.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Motor | Dominant Application | Why It Works |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Shunt** | Lathes, Centrifugal pumps, Blowers, Machine tools, Printing presses | Speed must be *constant* over varying load. |
| **Series** | Electric locomotives, Trolleys, Cranes, Hoists, Winches, Electric vehicles | Very high starting torque needed; load is always present. |
| **Cumulative Compound** | Rolling mills, Punches, Shears, Elevators, Heavy planers | High starting torque *plus* stable speed under load. |
| **Differential Compound** | Almost never used as a motor. As a generator, used in **DC welding sets**. | Self-regulating welding current. |

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Recognition Question (asked in KTU viva):**
> "Why are traction motors always series wound?"
> Answer: A train must start with the *entire train* as load → requires very high starting torque (series gives $T \propto I^2$). The train cannot be "started without load" because the locomotive is physically coupled to the wagons.
