Lower slip ⇒ higher efficiency. | # 🔷 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Slip and Rotor Frequency Relationship We start from Faraday's Law applied to a rotor conductor cutting the stator's rotating flux. **Given:** * Synchronous speed of RMF: $N_s$ (in rpm) = $\frac{120 f}{P}$ * Actual rotor speed: $N_r$ (in rpm) * Synchronous angular velocity: $\omega_s = \frac{2\pi N_s}{60}$ rad/s * Rotor angular velocity: $\omega_r = \frac{2\pi N_r}{60}$ rad/s **Step 1:** Express relative angular velocity (slip speed in rad/s) as: $$\omega_{slip} = \omega_s - \omega_r = \frac{2\pi}{60}(N_s - N_r)$$ **Step 2:** Substitute the definition of slip $s = (N_s - N_r) / N_s$, which gives $N_s - N_r = s N_s$. Therefore: $$\omega_{slip} = \frac{2\pi}{60} \cdot s N_s = s \cdot \omega_s$$ **Step 3:** The relative motion of the rotor conductors with respect to the stator RMF occurs at the slip speed. The frequency of the flux "seen" by the rotor is therefore the slip speed divided by the **electrical equivalent** of one revolution. **Step 4:** Recall that for a $P$-pole machine, the flux completes one full electrical cycle per pair of poles, i.e., per $N_s / f$ relationship. In one mechanical revolution of the RMF, the rotor conductors experience $P/2$ electrical cycles. So: $$f_r = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{\omega_{slip}}{2\pi} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad f_r = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{N_s - N_r}{60}$$ **Step 5:** Substitute $N_s = 120 f / P$: $$f_r = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{(s \cdot 120 f / P)}{60} = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{2 s f}{1} = s f \cdot \frac{120}{120} = s f$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{f_r = s f}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification with sanity check:** At standstill, $N_r = 0$, so $s = 1$, giving $f_r = f$ ✓. At synchronous speed, $N_r = N_s$, so $s = 0$, giving $f_r = 0$ ✓. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Slip and Rotor Copper Loss Ratio This is one of the **most repeated KTU derivations**. We will show that the ratio of rotor copper loss to air-gap power equals the slip. **Step 1 — Air-Gap Power:** The total power transferred across the air gap from stator to rotor (per phase) is the power input to the rotor's equivalent circuit. For a 3-phase motor: $$P_g = 3 I_2^2 \cdot \frac{R_2}{s}$$ This is the **standard per-phase air-gap power formula** derived from the rotor equivalent circuit, where the rotor resistance referred to slip is $R_2 / s$. **Step 2 — Rotor Copper Loss:** Power actually dissipated as heat in the rotor winding resistance is: $$P_{cu} = 3 I_2^2 R_2$$ **Step 3 — Form the Ratio:** $$\frac{P_{cu}}{P_g} = \frac{3 I_2^2 R_2}{3 I_2^2 \cdot (R_2 / s)} = \frac{R_2}{R_2 / s} = s$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{s = \frac{P_{cu}}{P_g}}$$ **Step 4 — Mechanical Power Developed:** Subtract rotor copper loss from air-gap power: $$P_m = P_g - P_{cu} = P_g - s P_g = (1 - s) P_g$$ This is the **Gross Mechanical Power Developed** inside the motor (before mechanical losses like friction and windage). --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Slip at Maximum Torque This is a classic KTU 14-mark derivation. We differentiate the torque expression with respect to slip and set the derivative to zero. **Starting Equation:** Mechanical torque developed is: $$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}$$ **Step 1:** Since $\frac{3 E_2^2}{\omega_s}$ is a constant for a given motor, we can define a simplified function: $$T \propto f(s) = \frac{s R_2}{R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2}$$ **Step 2:** Differentiate $f(s)$ with respect to $s$ using the **quotient rule**: $$\frac{df}{ds} = \frac{(R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2) \cdot R_2 - (s R_2) \cdot (2 s X_2^2)}{(R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2)^2}$$ **Step 3:** For maximum torque, set the numerator of $\frac{df}{ds}$ to zero: $$(R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2) R_2 - 2 s^2 R_2 X_2^2 = 0$$ **Step 4:** Expand and simplify: $$R_2^3 + s^2 R_2 X_2^2 - 2 s^2 R_2 X_2^2 = 0$$ $$R_2^3 - s^2 R_2 X_2^2 = 0$$ $$R_2^2 = s^2 X_2^2$$ **Step 5:** Take the positive square root (since slip is positive in motoring mode): $$s_{mT} = \frac{R_2}{X_2}$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{s_{mT} = \frac{R_2}{X_2}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Significance:** This is why **slip-ring induction motors** can achieve high starting torque. By adding external rotor resistance, $R_2$ increases, and therefore $s_{mT}$ shifts closer to $1$ (the starting point). The maximum torque point is shifted to coincide with the starting condition. **Bonus — Maximum Torque Value:** Substitute $s_{mT} = R_2 / X_2$ back into the torque equation: $$T_{max} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{(R_2 / X_2) \cdot E_2^2 \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + (R_2 / X_2)^2 \cdot X_2^2} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{R_2^2 E_2^2 / X_2}{R_2^2 + R_2^2} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{R_2^2 E_2^2 / X_2}{2 R_2^2}$$ $$\boxed{T_{max} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^2}{2 X_2}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Crucial Insight:** $T_{max}$ is **independent of rotor resistance $R_2$**! This means adding rotor resistance increases starting torque (shifts $s_{mT}$) but does *not* increase the maximum torque value itself. It only changes *where* the maximum occurs on the slip axis. --- ## 3.4 Work-Out Problem — KTU Numerical Style **Problem (Model Question Pattern):** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has a full-load slip of 4%. Compute: (a) Synchronous speed (b) Actual rotor speed at full load (c) Rotor frequency (d) Slip speed (e) If rotor copper loss is 800 W, find the air-gap power and developed mechanical power. **Solution:** **(a)** Synchronous speed: $$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$ **(b)** Actual rotor speed: $$N_r = N_s (1 - s) = 1500 \times (1 - 0.04) = 1500 \times 0.96 = 1440 \text{ rpm}$$ **(c)** Rotor frequency: $$f_r = s f = 0.04 \times 50 = 2 \text{ Hz}$$ **(d)** Slip speed: $$N_{slip} = s N_s = 0.04 \times 1500 = 60 \text{ rpm}$$ **(e)** Given $P_{cu} = 800$ W, $s = 0.04$. Using $s = P_{cu} / P_g$: $$P_g = \frac{P_{cu}}{s} = \frac{800}{0.04} = 20{,}000 \text{ W} = 20 \text{ kW}$$ $$P_m = (1 - s) P_g = (1 - 0.04) \times 20{,}000 = 0.96 \times 20{,}000 = 19{,}200 \text{ W} = 19.2 \text{ kW}$$ # 🔷 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Slip as a Function of Operating Region (Flowchart) The following Mermaid block diagrams the **physical interpretation of slip** across all five operating regions of an induction machine. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Induction Machine Rotor] --> B{Is N_r Less Than, Equal To, or Greater Than N_s ?} B -- N_r = 0
Standstill --> C[Region 1: Starting
s = 1.0 100 percent
Max rotor current, max rotor EMF] B -- 0 less than N_r less than N_s
Motoring --> D[Region 2: Normal Motoring
s = 0.02 to 0.05
Stable torque production] B -- N_r = N_s
Theoretical --> E[Region 3: Synchronous Speed
s = 0
No relative motion, no torque] B -- N_r greater than N_s
Over-driven --> F[Region 4: Induction Generator
s is negative
Power flows rotor to stator] B -- N_r reversed
Plugging --> G[Region 5: Braking Mode
s greater than 1
Fast deceleration] C --> H[Rotor EMF E2r equals E2
Rotor reactance X2r equals X2] D --> I[Rotor EMF E2r equals s times E2
Rotor reactance X2r equals s times X2] E --> J[Zero induced EMF
Zero rotor current] F --> K[Negative relative speed
Active power generation] G --> L[High rotor currents
Plugging resistor dissipates energy] ``` ## 4.2 Torque vs Slip Characteristic (Block Architecture Flow) The complete torque-slip curve is the **most important graph** in induction motor analysis. The Mermaid diagram below maps the **functional regions** of this curve (since plotting a 2D mathematical curve directly in Mermaid is not feasible, we map the **operational architecture** of the curve's regions). ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Slip s equals 1
Starting Point] --> A2[Slip Region 0 to smT
STABLE OPERATING REGION] A2 --> A3[Slip equals smT equals R2 divided by X2
PULL-OUT TORQUE Point] A3 --> A4[Slip Region smT to 1
UNSTABLE OPERATING REGION] A4 --> A5[Slip s equals 0
Synchronous Speed Ideal] subgraph StableRegion [STABLE REGION Properties] B1[Small changes in load produce
small changes in speed] B2[Normal running zone
2 to 5 percent slip] B3[Slope dT by ds is POSITIVE] end subgraph UnstableRegion [UNSTABLE REGION Properties] C1[Slope dT by ds is NEGATIVE] C2[High currents, low torque
Motor stalls here] C3[Transitional during start-up] end A2 -.describes.-> StableRegion A4 -.describes.-> UnstableRegion ``` ## 4.3 Power Flow Cascade Diagram (Showing Slip's Role) ```mermaid flowchart TD P_in[Stator Input Power Pin] --> P_sc[Stator Core Loss Pcore] --> P_g1[Air Gap Power Pg] P_in --> P_scl[Stator Copper Loss Pscl] --> P_g1 P_g1 --> P_cu[Rotor Copper Loss Pcu equals s times Pg] P_g1 --> P_m[Mechanical Power Developed Pm equals 1 minus s times Pg] P_m --> P_fw[Friction and Windage Loss Pfw] P_m --> P_str[Stray Load Loss Pstray] P_fw --> P_out[Shaft Output Power Pout] P_str --> P_out Q1[Slip s equals Pcu divided by Pg] -.annotates.-> P_cu Q2[Efficiency hint equals Pout divided by Pin] -.annotates.-> P_out ``` > [!TIP] > **How to Read Diagram 4.3:** Trace the power from the electrical input on the left, through the air gap, and out to the mechanical shaft on the right. The **slip determines what fraction of the air-gap power becomes heat in the rotor** versus useful mechanical power. # 🔷 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]** **Define slip of an induction motor. Why can the rotor of a 3-phase induction motor never run at synchronous speed?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** * **Definition (1.5 marks):** Slip is defined as the ratio of the difference between the synchronous speed ($N_s$) of the stator's rotating magnetic field and the actual rotor speed ($N_r$) to the synchronous speed. $$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$ * **Reason for never reaching synchronous speed (1.5 marks):** The rotor can never reach synchronous speed because if it did, there would be **no relative motion** between the rotating magnetic field and the rotor conductors. As a result, **no EMF would be induced**, **no rotor current would flow**, and **no electromagnetic torque** could be produced. The rotor would then decelerate due to friction and load torque, re-establishing slip and regaining torque. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]** **A 4-pole, 50 Hz, 3-phase induction motor runs at 1440 rpm. Calculate (i) synchronous speed, (ii) slip, and (iii) rotor frequency.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. **Synchronous speed (1 mark):** $$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$ 2. **Slip (1 mark):** $$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \frac{1500 - 1440}{1500} = \frac{60}{1500} = 0.04 = 4\%$$ 3. **Rotor frequency (1 mark):** $$f_r = s f = 0.04 \times 50 = 2 \text{ Hz}$$ --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Pattern]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the **slip at which maximum torque occurs** in a three-phase induction motor. Hence, show that the **maximum torque is independent of rotor resistance**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has rotor resistance of $0.2 \, \Omega$ and standstill rotor reactance of $1.0 \, \Omega$ per phase. At a slip of 4%, the rotor EMF per phase is observed to be 1.2 V. Calculate: **(i)** rotor current per phase, **(ii)** mechanical power developed, **(iii)** rotor copper loss, and **(iv)** developed torque. **(7 Marks)** ### **Model Solution to Question A:** #### **Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):** **Starting equation** of developed torque: $$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}$$ **Step 1 (1 mark):** Since $\frac{3 E_2^2}{\omega_s}$ is constant, maximize $f(s) = \frac{s R_2}{R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2}$. **Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply $\frac{df}{ds} = 0$ using the quotient rule: $$\frac{d}{ds}\left[\frac{s R_2}{R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2}\right] = \frac{R_2(R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2) - s R_2(2 s X_2^2)}{(R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2)^2} = 0$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Simplify numerator: $$R_2^3 + s^2 R_2 X_2^2 - 2 s^2 R_2 X_2^2 = 0 \implies R_2^2 = s^2 X_2^2$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Take positive root: $$s_{mT} = \frac{R_2}{X_2}$$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Substitute back to get: $$T_{max} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^2}{2 X_2}$$ This contains **no $R_2$**, proving that maximum torque is **independent of rotor resistance**. **Step 6 (1 mark):** Conclude: Adding rotor resistance shifts the point of maximum torque along the slip axis (changing $s_{mT}$) but does not change the magnitude of $T_{max}$ itself. #### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):** **Given:** $P = 6$, $f = 50$ Hz, $R_2 = 0.2 \, \Omega$, $X_2 = 1.0 \, \Omega$, $s = 0.04$, $E_2 = 1.2$ V. **Step 1 (1 mark) — Synchronous speed:** $$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ rpm}, \quad \omega_s = \frac{2\pi \times 1000}{60} = 104.72 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Rotor current per phase:** At $s = 0.04$: $X_{2r} = s X_2 = 0.04 \times 1.0 = 0.04 \, \Omega$. $$I_2 = \frac{s E_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}} = \frac{0.04 \times 1.2}{\sqrt{0.2^2 + 0.04^2}} = \frac{0.048}{\sqrt{0.04 + 0.0016}} = \frac{0.048}{\sqrt{0.0416}} = \frac{0.048}{0.2040} = 0.2353 \text{ A}$$ **Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Rotor copper loss (3-phase):** $$P_{cu} = 3 I_2^2 R_2 = 3 \times (0.2353)^2 \times 0.2 = 3 \times 0.0554 \times 0.2 = 0.0332 \text{ W}$$ **Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Mechanical power developed:** Using $s = P_{cu} / P_g$, find air-gap power first: $$P_g = \frac{P_{cu}}{s} = \frac{0.0332}{0.04} = 0.83 \text{ W}$$ $$P_m = (1 - s) P_g = (1 - 0.04) \times 0.83 = 0.96 \times 0.83 = 0.797 \text{ W}$$ **Step 5 (1.5 marks) — Developed torque:** $$T = \frac{P_m}{\omega_r}, \quad \omega_r = (1 - s) \omega_s = 0.96 \times 104.72 = 100.53 \text{ rad/s}$$ $$T = \frac{0.797}{100.53} = 7.93 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N·m} \approx 7.93 \text{ mN·m}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Pattern]** **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **torque-slip characteristic** of a 3-phase induction motor. Mark the **stable region**, **unstable region**, **starting torque**, and **maximum torque** points on the curve. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor develops a torque of $150 \text{ N·m}$ at a slip of 4%. The rotor resistance per phase is $0.1 \, \Omega$. Calculate: **(i)** the slip for maximum torque, **(ii)** the maximum torque developed, and **(iii)** the starting torque of the motor. **(7 Marks)** ### **Model Solution to Question B:** #### **Part (a) — Torque-Slip Characteristic (7 Marks):** **Sketch description (4 marks):** A torque-slip curve is plotted with slip $s$ on the x-axis (from 0 to 1) and torque $T$ on the y-axis. The curve: * Starts at $T = T_{st}$ when $s = 1$ (starting torque). * Rises to a peak $T_{max}$ at slip $s_{mT} = R_2 / X_2$ (typically 0.1 to 0.2 for normal motors). * Falls back to $T = 0$ as $s \to 0$ (synchronous speed point). **Regions (2 marks):** * **Stable operating region** ($0 < s < s_{mT}$): Slope $dT/ds$ is **positive**. Motor operates here under normal load. Small load changes cause small speed changes. * **Unstable operating region** ($s_{mT} < s < 1$): Slope $dT/ds$ is **negative**. Motor cannot run steadily here. Used only transiently during acceleration. **Key points marked (1 mark):** * Starting point at $(s = 1, T = T_{st})$ * Pull-out point at $(s = s_{mT}, T = T_{max})$ * Synchronous point at $(s = 0, T = 0)$ #### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):** **Given:** $P = 4$, $f = 50$ Hz, $T = 150$ N·m at $s = 0.04$, $R_2 = 0.1 \, \Omega$. **Step 1 (1 mark) — Compute synchronous speed and angular velocity:** $$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}, \quad \omega_s = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Find $E_2$ and $X_2$ ratio using the given operating point:** The torque equation gives: $$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}$$ $$150 = \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{0.04 \times E_2^2 \times 0.1}{0.1^2 + (0.04 X_2)^2}$$ **Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Find $E_2^2 / X_2$ ratio using the formula for $T_{max}$ and assuming the question wants direct computation:** Since the problem gives rotor resistance but not $X_2$ explicitly, we use a **standard assumption**: For typical small induction motors, $X_2 \approx 10 \times R_2$, so $X_2 = 1.0 \, \Omega$ is a reasonable estimate. Then $s_{mT} = R_2 / X_2 = 0.1 / 1.0 = 0.1$ (10%). **Step 4 (1 mark) — Slip for maximum torque:** $$s_{mT} = \frac{R_2}{X_2} = \frac{0.1}{1.0} = 0.1 \text{ (10%)}$$ **Step 5 (1 mark) — Maximum torque:** $$T_{max} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^2}{2 X_2}$$ First, solve for $E_2^2$ from the operating point: $$150 \times 157.08 / 3 = \frac{0.04 \times E_2^2 \times 0.1}{0.01 + 0.0016} = \frac{0.004 E_2^2}{0.0116}$$ $$E_2^2 = \frac{150 \times 157.08 \times 0.0116}{3 \times 0.004} = \frac{273.3}{0.012} = 22{,}776 \text{ V}^2$$ $$T_{max} = \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{22{,}776}{2 \times 1.0} = 0.0191 \times 11{,}388 = 217.5 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 6 (1 mark) — Starting torque:** $$T_{st} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2} = \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{22{,}776 \times 0.1}{0.01 + 1.0} = 0.0191 \times \frac{2277.6}{1.01} = 0.0191 \times 2254 = 43.1 \text{ N·m}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 3** for three-phase motors when calculating $P_{cu}$, $P_g$, or $P_m$ from per-phase values. This single error costs **2 marks** instantly. > 2. **Mixing up $N_s$ and $N_r$** in the slip formula. Always remember: **Slip = (Synchronous − Rotor) / Synchronous**. > 3. **Using $\omega_s$ instead of $\omega_r$** when computing developed torque. Use $\omega_r = (1 - s)\omega_s$ with mechanical power, or $\omega_s$ with air-gap power. > 4. **Forgetting that $X_{2r} = s X_2$** at running conditions — many students incorrectly use $X_2$ (standstill value) in the rotor current equation. > 5. **Not converting rpm to rad/s** before computing torque. $T = P / \omega$ requires rad/s, not rpm. > 6. **Confusing $E_2$ (standstill rotor EMF) with $E_{2r} = s E_2$ (rotor EMF at slip $s$)**. This is a **3-mark trap** in KTU papers. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Use this checklist for last-minute revision before your KTU exam.** **Core Concepts:** * ⭐ Slip $s$ is **dimensionless** and is the **fractional difference** between synchronous speed and rotor speed. * ⭐ Slip exists **only because of relative motion**; without slip, there is no induction, no current, no torque. * ⭐ In motoring: $0 < s < 1$ | Generator: $s < 0$ | Braking: $s > 1$. **Must-Memorize Equations (16 formulas in Section 2.2):** * ⭐ $N_s = 120f / P$ (synchronous speed in rpm) * ⭐ $s = (N_s - N_r) / N_s$ (slip definition) * ⭐ $f_r = s f$ (rotor frequency is slip times supply frequency) * ⭐ $E_{2r} = s E_2$ and $X_{2r} = s X_2$ (rotor EMF and reactance scale with slip) * ⭐ $s = P_{cu} / P_g = R_{2,\text{referred}} / (R_{2,\text{referred}} + R_{\text{load,referred}})$ (power ratio) * ⭐ $s_{mT} = R_2 / X_2$ (slip at maximum torque) * ⭐ $T_{max} \propto E_2^2 / X_2$ (independent of $R_2$) **High-Yield Derivations for 14-Mark Questions:** * ⭐ Derivation of $s_{mT} = R_2 / X_2$ using $dT/ds = 0$. * ⭐ Derivation of the relation $s = P_{cu} / P_g$. * ⭐ Derivation of $f_r = s f$ using Faraday's law. * ⭐ Torque equation starting from mechanical power and equivalent circuit. **Critical Numerical Patterns in KTU Papers:** * ⭐ "Compute $N_s$, $N_r$, $s$, $f_r$" → 4-part easy question (3 marks). * ⭐ "Given rotor copper loss and slip, find $P_g$ and $P_m$" → uses $P_g = P_{cu}/s$. * ⭐ "Find $s_{mT}$ and $T_{max}$ from a given operating point" → uses the standard torque equation. * ⭐ "Show that maximum torque is independent of $R_2$" → guaranteed 7-mark question in every KTU paper. **Key Conceptual Traps:** * ⭐ Slip is **not a fixed parameter** — it varies with load. Light load ⇒ low slip; heavy load ⇒ high slip. * ⭐ The synchronous speed depends only on $f$ and $P$, **not on the load**. * ⭐ The torque-slip curve has two regions: **stable** ($0 < s < s_{mT}$) and **unstable** ($s_{mT} < s < 1$). The motor **always operates in the stable region**. * ⭐ Adding rotor resistance **does not increase $T_{max}$** — it merely shifts the point of $T_{max}$ to higher slip values. * ⭐ $T_{max}$ depends on $E_2^2$ — so reducing supply voltage drastically reduces maximum torque. * ⭐ In an **induction generator**, the rotor is driven **faster** than the synchronous speed (e.g., by a wind turbine), giving **negative slip**. **One-Line Mantra for the Exam Hall:** > *"Slip is to an induction motor what breath is to a living being — it is the very essence of its operation."*

topics.title: Performance Characteristics and Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST521 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Performance Characteristics and Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of electrical machines and instrumentation systems, **Performance Characteristics** refer to the graphical and mathematical relationships that describe how an electrical device behaves under varying operating conditions, particularly with respect to **load current, terminal voltage, power factor, speed, torque, and losses**. **Efficiency** is the quantitative measure of how effectively a machine converts input electrical energy into useful output energy, expressed as the ratio of output power to input power.

The general definition is:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\% = \frac{P_{in} - P_{losses}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$$

where every machine obeys the **Law of Conservation of Energy**: input power = output power + total losses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In Module 3, performance characteristics and efficiency are evaluated for **transformers, DC machines, and induction motors**. Each machine has its own characteristic curves and loss categorization, but the underlying principle of input = output + losses is universal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Efficiency (η):** The ratio of useful output power delivered by a machine to the total input power supplied, usually expressed as a percentage. A perfectly efficient machine would have η = 100% (an ideal, lossless device), but practical machines always have losses.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a water tank with a small leak at the bottom. You pour 100 litres of water in (input), but only 90 litres come out of the tap at the bottom (output). The 10 litres lost through the leak represent the **losses**, and the efficiency of your tank is 90/100 = 90%.

In an **electrical machine**:
- The water tank = the machine itself
- Input water = electrical input power
- Output water = mechanical/electrical output power
- The leak = losses (heat, sound, friction, stray)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Consider an electric fan. You supply 100 W of electrical power, but the fan blade delivers only about 70 W of useful air-moving (mechanical) power. The remaining 30 W is "lost" as heat in the windings, sound, vibration, and bearing friction. So, the fan's efficiency is roughly 70%. The fan manufacturer tries to minimize these losses to give you more "output" for the same "input."

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024

The topic forms the **backbone of electrical machine analysis** and appears in:
- **Part A** (3 marks): Direct definition-based questions
- **Part B** (14 marks): Numerical problems involving efficiency calculations, maximum efficiency condition, and characteristic curve interpretation
- **Laboratory viva**: Practical efficiency testing using no-load and blocked-rotor tests

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used:**
> - **Output Power** ($P_{out}$) — measured in **Watts (W)** or **Kilowatts (kW)**
> - **Input Power** ($P_{in}$) — measured in **W** or **kW**
> - **Efficiency** ($\eta$) — dimensionless, expressed as **%**
> - **Power Factor** ($\cos\phi$) — dimensionless, between 0 and 1
> - **Losses** — in **W** or **kW**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Losses in Electrical Machines

Every electrical machine suffers from various losses. These losses are broadly classified into two categories:

**A. Constant Losses (Independent of Load)**
- **Core / Iron Losses ($P_i$)**: Hysteresis loss + Eddy current loss in the iron core
  - Hysteresis Loss: $P_h = \eta_h \cdot B_{max}^{1.6} \cdot f \cdot V$
  - Eddy Current Loss: $P_e = \eta_e \cdot B_{max}^2 \cdot f^2 \cdot t^2$
- **Mechanical Losses ($P_m$)**: Friction at bearings, windage (air resistance on rotating parts)
- **Brush Contact Loss (in DC machines)**: Loss at brush-commutator interface

**B. Variable Losses (Load-Dependent)**
- **Copper Losses ($P_{cu}$)**: $I^2R$ losses in the windings
- **Stray Load Losses ($P_{stray}$)**: Minor additional losses due to leakage flux, harmonics

> [!NOTE]
> **Total Losses:** $P_{losses} = P_i + P_m + P_{cu} + P_{stray}$

### 2.2 Condition for Maximum Efficiency

For any machine, **efficiency is maximum when the variable losses equal the constant losses**.

Let:
- Constant loss = $P_c$ (includes iron loss + mechanical loss)
- Variable loss = $P_{cu} = I^2 R$ ∝ (load)² (i.e., proportional to the square of the fraction of full load)

If the machine operates at a load fraction $x$ (where $x = \text{actual load} / \text{full load}$), then:

$$P_{cu} \text{ at load } x = x^2 \cdot P_{cu,FL}$$

Total loss: $P_{total} = P_c + x^2 P_{cu,FL}$

Input power: $P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{c} + x^2 P_{cu,FL}$

Efficiency:
$$\eta_x = \frac{x \cdot P_{out,FL}}{x \cdot P_{out,FL} + P_c + x^2 \cdot P_{cu,FL}}$$

**For maximum efficiency**, differentiate $\eta_x$ with respect to $x$ and set $\frac{d\eta_x}{dx} = 0$:

$$x^2 P_{cu,FL} = P_c$$

This gives the **load fraction at which maximum efficiency occurs**:

$$x_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{P_c}{P_{cu,FL}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** This result means that maximum efficiency occurs at that load where **variable copper loss = constant (iron + mechanical) loss**. The design engineer sizes the machine such that this condition is met at the most common operating load (often 75% to 100% of full load).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | General definition | % |
| Efficiency via losses | $\eta = \frac{P_{in} - P_{losses}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | Alternate form | % |
| Transformer efficiency | $\eta = \frac{V_2 I_2 \cos\phi}{V_2 I_2 \cos\phi + P_i + P_{cu}}$ | Single-phase transformer | — |
| DC Machine efficiency | $\eta = \frac{E_b I_a}{V I_a}$ | Motor | — |
| DC Generator efficiency | $\eta = \frac{V I_L}{E_g I_a}$ | Generator | — |
| Condition for $\eta_{max}$ | $x^2 P_{cu,FL} = P_c$ | Variable loss = Constant loss | — |
| Load at $\eta_{max}$ | $x = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_c}{P_{cu,FL}}}$ | Fraction of full load | — |
| Copper loss | $P_{cu} = I^2 R$ | Resistive heating | W |
| Iron loss | $P_i = P_h + P_e$ | Core losses | W |
| All-day efficiency | $\eta_{ad} = \dfrac{W_{out,24h}}{W_{in,24h}}$ | Energy efficiency over 24h | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on table formatting:** In the LaTeX column "Formula" above, all instances of the absolute-value pipe notation that would normally appear (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) are intentionally avoided to keep the markdown table structurally clean. In the actual mathematical derivations, vertical bars use the LaTeX command `\vert` or `\mid`.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of Performance Characteristics

- **Transformer Sizing:** Manufacturers use efficiency curves to advertise product performance.
- **Industrial Motor Selection:** Engineers pick motors with peak efficiency at typical operating load (avoiding oversized motors that run inefficiently at low loads).
- **Energy Auditing:** Industries calculate all-day efficiency of distribution transformers to plan replacement schedules.
- **Cost Analysis:** Higher efficiency → lower electricity bills → lower carbon footprint.

### 2.5 Types of Efficiency

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Important Variants of Efficiency Used in KTU Examinations:**

1. **Conventional / Commercial Efficiency** — Used for transformers and DC machines; based on output power.
2. **All-Day Efficiency** — Used for distribution transformers that operate 24 hours; based on energy (kWh) rather than power (kW), because such transformers remain energized even when load is zero.
3. **Maximum Efficiency** — The peak value of the efficiency curve; occurs at the load where variable loss = constant loss.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Condition for Maximum Efficiency

**Statement:** The efficiency of an electrical machine is maximum when the variable (copper) loss equals the constant (iron + mechanical) loss.

**Setup:**
Let a machine deliver an output power $P$ at full load. At a fractional load $x$:

$$P_{out} = x \cdot P$$

Variable copper loss at fractional load $x$:
$$P_{cu,x} = x^2 \cdot P_{cu,FL}$$

where $P_{cu,FL}$ is the copper loss at full load.

Constant loss:
$$P_c = P_i + P_m \quad \text{(constant, independent of load)}$$

Total input power:
$$P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{cu,x} + P_c = x P + x^2 P_{cu,FL} + P_c$$

Efficiency:
$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{x P}{x P + x^2 P_{cu,FL} + P_c}$$

**Differentiation:**

To find the maximum, we set $\frac{d\eta}{dx} = 0$:

$$\frac{d\eta}{dx} = \frac{d}{dx}\left[\frac{x P}{x P + x^2 P_{cu,FL} + P_c}\right] = 0$$

Using the quotient rule $\frac{d}{dx}\left[\frac{u}{v}\right] = \frac{u'v - uv'}{v^2}$:

Let $u = xP$ and $v = xP + x^2 P_{cu,FL} + P_c$

Then $u' = P$ and $v' = P + 2x P_{cu,FL}$

Setting the numerator of the derivative to zero:

$$P \cdot (xP + x^2 P_{cu,FL} + P_c) - xP \cdot (P + 2x P_{cu,FL}) = 0$$

Expand the first term:
$$P \cdot xP + P \cdot x^2 P_{cu,FL} + P \cdot P_c = x P^2 + x^2 P \cdot P_{cu,FL} + P \cdot P_c$$

Expand the second term:
$$xP \cdot P + xP \cdot 2x P_{cu,FL} = x P^2 + 2 x^2 P \cdot P_{cu,FL}$$

Subtract:
$$(x P^2 + x^2 P \cdot P_{cu,FL} + P \cdot P_c) - (x P^2 + 2 x^2 P \cdot P_{cu,FL}) = 0$$

Simplify:
$$P \cdot P_c - x^2 P \cdot P_{cu,FL} = 0$$

Divide by $P$:
$$P_c - x^2 P_{cu,FL} = 0$$

Therefore:
$$x^2 P_{cu,FL} = P_c \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x = \sqrt{\frac{P_c}{P_{cu,FL}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The efficiency is maximum at a load such that the variable copper loss equals the constant loss. Below this load, iron loss dominates; above it, copper loss dominates. At the crossover point, the sum of losses (relative to load) is minimized.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Transformer Efficiency from No-Load and Short-Circuit Tests

**Given Test Data:**
- From **Open-Circuit (OC) Test** on LV side: No-load power $P_0$ = iron loss $P_i$
- From **Short-Circuit (SC) Test** on HV side: Full-load copper loss $P_{cu,FL}$ (at the test current)

**Output power at fractional load $x$ of a transformer:**
$$P_{out} = x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi$$

where $S$ is the rated apparent power (in VA) and $\cos\phi$ is the load power factor.

**Efficiency at fractional load $x$:**
$$\eta_x = \frac{x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi}{x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi + P_i + x^2 P_{cu,FL}}$$

**Load at maximum efficiency:**
$$x_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{P_i}{P_{cu,FL}}}$$

**Maximum efficiency value:**
$$\eta_{max} = \frac{x_{max} \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi}{x_{max} \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi + 2 P_i}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $+2P_i$?** At maximum efficiency, $x^2 P_{cu,FL} = P_i$, so the total loss = $P_i + P_i = 2P_i$. The input becomes $P_{out} + 2P_i$.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

**Problem:** A 10 kVA, 2000/200 V single-phase transformer has iron loss of 80 W and full-load copper loss of 120 W. Calculate:
1. Efficiency at full load at 0.8 power factor lagging.
2. Efficiency at half load at unity power factor.
3. Load at which maximum efficiency occurs.
4. Maximum efficiency at 0.8 power factor lagging.

**Solution:**

**Given:**
- Rated apparent power $S = 10$ kVA = 10000 VA
- Iron loss $P_i = 80$ W
- Full-load copper loss $P_{cu,FL} = 120$ W

**Part (1): Full-load efficiency at $\cos\phi = 0.8$**

Output power:
$$P_{out,FL} = S \cdot \cos\phi = 10000 \times 0.8 = 8000 \text{ W}$$

Total losses at full load:
$$P_{losses} = P_i + P_{cu,FL} = 80 + 120 = 200 \text{ W}$$

Efficiency:
$$\eta_{FL} = \frac{8000}{8000 + 200} \times 100 = \frac{8000}{8200} \times 100 = 97.56\%$$

**Part (2): Half-load efficiency at unity power factor**

At $x = 0.5$ and $\cos\phi = 1.0$:
$$P_{out,0.5} = 0.5 \times 10000 \times 1.0 = 5000 \text{ W}$$

Copper loss at half load:
$$P_{cu,0.5} = (0.5)^2 \times 120 = 0.25 \times 120 = 30 \text{ W}$$

Total loss:
$$P_{losses} = 80 + 30 = 110 \text{ W}$$

Efficiency:
$$\eta_{0.5} = \frac{5000}{5000 + 110} \times 100 = \frac{5000}{5110} \times 100 = 97.85\%$$

**Part (3): Load at maximum efficiency**

$$x_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{P_i}{P_{cu,FL}}} = \sqrt{\frac{80}{120}} = \sqrt{0.6667} = 0.8165$$

So maximum efficiency occurs at **81.65% of full load**.

**Part (4): Maximum efficiency at $\cos\phi = 0.8$**

Output at $x_{max}$:
$$P_{out} = 0.8165 \times 10000 \times 0.8 = 6532 \text{ W}$$

At $\eta_{max}$, $P_{cu} = P_i = 80$ W. So total loss = $2 \times 80 = 160$ W.

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{6532}{6532 + 160} \times 100 = \frac{6532}{6692} \times 100 = 97.61\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Notice that in Part (2), even though the load is halved, the efficiency increased (97.85% > 97.56%). This is because the load is closer to the maximum efficiency point of 81.65%.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Performance Characteristic Curves

| **Machine Type** | **Key Performance Curves** | **What They Plot** |
|---|---|---|
| **DC Shunt Motor** | Speed vs. Armature Current, Torque vs. Armature Current, Efficiency vs. Output | Speed regulation, starting torque, peak efficiency point |
| **DC Series Motor** | Speed vs. Armature Current, Torque vs. Armature Current | High starting torque, no-load runaway risk |
| **Induction Motor** | Torque vs. Slip, Power Factor vs. Slip, Efficiency vs. Output | Starting torque, pull-out torque, full-load slip |
| **Synchronous Motor** | V-Curves, Power Factor vs. Field Current | Excitation effect on power factor |
| **Transformer** | Efficiency vs. Load, Regulation vs. Power Factor | Energy loss, voltage drop under load |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All performance curves are obtained by varying one quantity (load, field current, slip) and observing the effect on the dependent variable (speed, torque, efficiency).** The KTU examination frequently asks students to *sketch and label* these curves.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Power Flow Diagram in an Electrical Machine

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **flow of power** from input to output, identifying where each type of loss occurs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electrical Input Power Pin]:::input --> B[Stator Core]
    A --> C[Stator Copper Winding]
    B --> D[Iron Loss Pi]
    C --> E[Copper Loss Pcu]
    B --> F[Air Gap Field]
    F --> G[Rotor Core and Windings]
    G --> H[Mechanical Loss Pm]
    G --> I[Stray Loss Pstray]
    G --> J[Useful Mechanical Power Pout]:::output
    D --> K[Total Loss]:::loss
    E --> K
    H --> K
    I --> K
    K --> L[Waste Heat]
    classDef input fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef loss fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
```

### 4.2 Efficiency vs. Load Characteristic Curve (Schematic Representation)

The following block-level architecture shows the relationship between the different operating points on the efficiency curve.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Zero Load: Only Iron Loss]:::low --> B[Load Increases: Cu Loss Rises]
    B --> C[Point of Max Efficiency: Cu Loss = Iron Loss]:::peak
    C --> D[Beyond Max Point: Cu Loss Dominates]:::high
    D --> E[Full Load: Cu Loss Max]:::max
    classDef low fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef high fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef max fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,color:#FFF
    classDef peak fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Transformer Efficiency Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rated VA of Transformer] --> B[Output Power: S cos phi]
    C[OC Test Data] --> D[Iron Loss Pi]
    E[SC Test Data] --> F[Full Load Cu Loss Pcu FL]
    B --> G[Calculate Efficiency at Fractional Load x]
    D --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H{Find x for max eta}
    H --> I[Use formula: x = sqrt of Pi by Pcu FL]
    I --> J[Compute eta max at given power factor]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node identifiers used above (such as `A`, `B`, `C`) are purely alphanumeric to comply with Mermaid parser requirements. Node labels are written as plain uppercase text without bold, italics, or HTML tags to ensure clean rendering.

### 4.4 Efficiency Curve — Numerical Sketch Representation

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Transformer Efficiency vs. Load (PF = 0.8)"
    x-axis "Load (fraction of full)" [0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1, 1.25, 1.5]
    y-axis "Efficiency (%)" 90 --> 100
    line [90, 96.5, 97.8, 97.5, 96.5, 95.2, 93.8]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation of the Curve:**
> - Efficiency starts at 0% (no output at zero load).
> - Rises steeply as load increases.
> - Reaches **peak** near the load where copper loss = iron loss.
> - Decreases gradually beyond this point as copper loss dominates.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define efficiency of an electrical machine. What is the condition for maximum efficiency?

**Model Answer:**
The efficiency ($\eta$) of an electrical machine is defined as the ratio of useful output power to the total input power, expressed as a percentage.

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\% = \frac{P_{in} - P_{losses}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$$

**Condition for maximum efficiency:** The efficiency of an electrical machine is maximum when the **variable (copper) loss equals the constant (iron + mechanical) loss**. That is:

$$x^2 P_{cu,FL} = P_c$$

where $x$ is the fractional load and $P_{cu,FL}$ is the full-load copper loss.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Distinguish between conventional efficiency and all-day efficiency of a transformer.

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Conventional Efficiency** | **All-Day Efficiency** |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Ratio of output power to input power | Ratio of output energy (kWh) to input energy (kWh) over 24 hours |
| Used for | Power transformers (constant load) | Distribution transformers (variable load, 24h operation) |
| Formula | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}$ | $\eta_{ad} = \dfrac{\text{kWh output in 24h}}{\text{kWh input in 24h}}$ |
| Iron loss considered | At the operating load | Occurs for full 24 hours, regardless of load |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** All-day efficiency is lower than conventional efficiency for distribution transformers because the core (iron) loss continues 24/7, even when there is no load.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]**
A 50 kVA, 4400/220 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer has the following test results:
- **Open-Circuit Test** (LV side): 220 V, 9.5 A, 720 W
- **Short-Circuit Test** (HV side): 150 V, 11.4 A, 760 W

Calculate:
**(a)** [7 Marks] The efficiency of the transformer at:
- (i) Full load at 0.8 power factor lagging
- (ii) Half load at unity power factor

**(b)** [7 Marks] The load at which maximum efficiency occurs and the corresponding maximum efficiency at 0.8 power factor lagging.

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Rated apparent power: $S = 50$ kVA = 50000 VA
- From OC test: Iron loss $P_i = 720$ W
- From SC test: Full-load copper loss $P_{cu,FL} = 760$ W

**Part (a)(i): Full-load efficiency at $\cos\phi = 0.8$**

Output power at full load:
$$P_{out} = S \cdot \cos\phi = 50000 \times 0.8 = 40000 \text{ W}$$

Total loss at full load:
$$P_{loss} = P_i + P_{cu,FL} = 720 + 760 = 1480 \text{ W}$$

Efficiency:
$$\eta_{FL} = \frac{40000}{40000 + 1480} \times 100 = \frac{40000}{41480} \times 100 = 96.43\%$$

**Part (a)(ii): Half-load efficiency at $\cos\phi = 1.0$**

At $x = 0.5$, $\cos\phi = 1.0$:
$$P_{out} = 0.5 \times 50000 \times 1.0 = 25000 \text{ W}$$

Copper loss at half load:
$$P_{cu,0.5} = (0.5)^2 \times 760 = 190 \text{ W}$$

Total loss:
$$P_{loss} = 720 + 190 = 910 \text{ W}$$

Efficiency:
$$\eta_{0.5} = \frac{25000}{25000 + 910} \times 100 = \frac{25000}{25910} \times 100 = 96.49\%$$

**Part (b): Load at maximum efficiency and maximum efficiency value**

Load fraction at $\eta_{max}$:
$$x_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{P_i}{P_{cu,FL}}} = \sqrt{\frac{720}{760}} = \sqrt{0.9474} = 0.9734$$

So maximum efficiency occurs at **97.34% of full load** (i.e., 48.67 kVA).

At $\eta_{max}$, total loss = $2 P_i = 2 \times 720 = 1440$ W.

Output at $x_{max}$ and $\cos\phi = 0.8$:
$$P_{out,max} = 0.9734 \times 50000 \times 0.8 = 38936 \text{ W}$$

Maximum efficiency:
$$\eta_{max} = \frac{38936}{38936 + 1440} \times 100 = \frac{38936}{40376} \times 100 = 96.44\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistake #1:** Many students forget that at $\eta_{max}$, the total loss is **$2P_i$** (not just $P_i$). Both the iron loss and the copper loss equal $P_i$ at that point, summing to $2P_i$. This single error can cost **2 to 3 marks**.
>
> **Pitfall #2:** Students often confuse **fractional load** ($x$) with **percentage of full load** ($x \times 100$). For example, $x = 0.8165$ means **81.65% of full load**, not 0.8165 kW or 0.8165 A.
>
> **Pitfall #3:** In OC/SC test interpretation, $P_i$ comes from the **OC test wattmeter reading**, and $P_{cu,FL}$ comes from the **SC test wattmeter reading**. Mixing them up is a frequent error.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]**
**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the condition for maximum efficiency of an electrical machine. Explain the significance of the result.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A 200 V DC shunt motor takes a no-load current of 4 A and a full-load current of 40 A. The armature and shunt field resistances are 0.2 Ω and 200 Ω respectively. Calculate:
- (i) The efficiency of the motor at full load.
- (ii) The efficiency of the motor at half load.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a): Derivation**

The detailed derivation is as discussed in Section 3.1. Key steps:

1. Express efficiency at fractional load $x$:

$$\eta_x = \frac{x P_{out,FL}}{x P_{out,FL} + P_c + x^2 P_{cu,FL}}$$

2. Differentiate $\eta$ with respect to $x$ and set the result to zero.

3. After simplification (quotient rule and algebra), we obtain:

$$P_c = x^2 P_{cu,FL}$$

4. Therefore, the **load at maximum efficiency** is:

$$x_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{P_c}{P_{cu,FL}}}$$

**Significance:**
- The result shows that efficiency is maximum at a load where copper loss = iron loss.
- The result is independent of the power factor.
- Designers use this to optimize the operating point of a machine.
- In transformers, this principle is used to maximize efficiency at typical loading conditions (e.g., 75%–90% of full load).
- For lightly loaded machines, the efficiency is low because constant losses dominate.

**Part (b)(i): Full-load efficiency**

Given:
- Supply voltage: $V = 200$ V
- No-load current: $I_0 = 4$ A
- Full-load current: $I_{FL} = 40$ A
- Armature resistance: $R_a = 0.2$ Ω
- Shunt field resistance: $R_{sh} = 200$ Ω

Shunt field current (constant):
$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{200}{200} = 1 \text{ A}$$

Full-load armature current:
$$I_{a,FL} = I_{FL} - I_{sh} = 40 - 1 = 39 \text{ A}$$

Full-load armature copper loss:
$$P_{cu,FL} = I_{a,FL}^2 \cdot R_a = (39)^2 \times 0.2 = 1521 \times 0.2 = 304.2 \text{ W}$$

Constant loss (from no-load condition):
$$P_{c} = V I_0 - I_{sh}^2 R_{sh} = 200 \times 4 - (1)^2 \times 200 = 800 - 200 = 600 \text{ W}$$

Note: This 600 W is an approximation. More rigorously, we can treat the no-load input minus shunt copper loss as the constant loss.

Input power at full load:
$$P_{in,FL} = V \cdot I_{FL} = 200 \times 40 = 8000 \text{ W}$$

Output power at full load:
$$P_{out,FL} = P_{in,FL} - P_{losses} = 8000 - (600 + 304.2) = 7095.8 \text{ W}$$

Efficiency at full load:
$$\eta_{FL} = \frac{7095.8}{8000} \times 100 = 88.70\%$$

**Part (b)(ii): Half-load efficiency**

At $x = 0.5$, armature current is approximately half:
$$I_{a,0.5} = 0.5 \times I_{a,FL} = 0.5 \times 39 = 19.5 \text{ A}$$

Copper loss at half load:
$$P_{cu,0.5} = (19.5)^2 \times 0.2 = 380.25 \times 0.2 = 76.05 \text{ W}$$

Total loss at half load:
$$P_{loss} = 600 + 76.05 = 676.05 \text{ W}$$

Input at half load (approximate line current):
$$I_{0.5} = I_{a,0.5} + I_{sh} = 19.5 + 1 = 20.5 \text{ A}$$
$$P_{in,0.5} = 200 \times 20.5 = 4100 \text{ W}$$

Output at half load:
$$P_{out,0.5} = 4100 - 676.05 = 3423.95 \text{ W}$$

Efficiency at half load:
$$\eta_{0.5} = \frac{3423.95}{4100} \times 100 = 83.51\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistake #4:** In DC shunt motor problems, students often forget to **subtract the shunt field current** from the line current to get the armature current. This is a critical step. The armature current is $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$ for a motor.
>
> **Pitfall #5:** In the no-load condition, students sometimes treat the no-load current as the armature current only. The no-load line current includes the shunt field current, so the armature current at no-load is $I_{a,0} = I_0 - I_{sh}$.
>
> **Pitfall #6:** Always separate **constant losses** (iron + friction) from **variable losses** (armature copper). Mixing them up will give incorrect efficiency at different loads.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **Efficiency formula** — $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$. Memorize in three forms: with output/input, with losses, and with rated power × power factor.
- **Losses classification** — Constant losses ($P_c$): iron, mechanical. Variable losses: $I^2R$ copper, stray load.
- **Maximum efficiency condition** — Variable loss = Constant loss, i.e., $x^2 P_{cu,FL} = P_c$.
- **Load at $\eta_{max}$** — $x_{max} = \sqrt{P_c / P_{cu,FL}}$. Independent of power factor.
- **Maximum efficiency value** — Total loss at this point is $2P_i$ (iron loss counted twice).
- **OC test** gives iron loss ($P_i$); **SC test** gives full-load copper loss ($P_{cu,FL}$).
- **DC machine efficiency** — Always separate armature and shunt currents; the shunt copper loss is a constant loss.
- **Induction motor efficiency** — Computed from $P_{out} / P_{in} = \dfrac{\text{Shaft Power}}{\text{Stator Input}}$; rotor copper loss = slip × air-gap power.
- **All-day efficiency** — Used for distribution transformers; depends on load cycle over 24 hours.
- **Efficiency curve** — Rises to a peak at $x_{max}$, then falls. The peak is broader at higher power factors.
- **KTU answer-writing tip** — Always state the formula, substitute the values, and then state the result with units. For numerical answers, the **unit (%, W, kW)** is mandatory.
- **Common exam trick** — A question may give efficiency at one load and ask for full-load efficiency. Use the relation $\eta_x = \dfrac{x P}{xP + P_c + x^2 P_{cu,FL}}$ to back-calculate $P_{cu,FL}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Word:** Mastery of efficiency calculations requires careful identification of constant vs. variable losses, accurate use of test data, and discipline in algebraic manipulation. The "condition for maximum efficiency" is one of the **most repeated topics** in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations — both as a 3-mark direct question and as part of a 14-mark numerical.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Phase Induction Motor - Working
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST521 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Single Phase Induction Motor - Working

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔌 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Single Phase Induction Motor (SPIM)** is a self-starting... wait, technically **NOT self-starting** AC motor that operates on a single-phase AC supply. It consists of a **single-phase distributed stator winding** (producing a **pulsating**, not rotating, magnetic field) and a **squirrel-cage rotor** (identical to that of a 3-phase induction motor). Mechanical rotation is achieved only when the rotor is given an initial push or when an auxiliary starting mechanism creates a **phase-shifted secondary field** to produce a resultant **elliptical/rotating magnetic field** during start-up.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PENST521 – Module 3):**  
> The working of a SPIM is explained via the **Double Revolving Field Theory (DRFT)**. The two valid academic theories for its operation are:  
> 1. **Double Revolving Field Theory** (most common in KTU exam answers)  
> 2. **Cross-Field Theory** (also acceptable; uses stationary stator/rotor axes)  

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Spinning Coin Trick"

Imagine you tie a string to a coin and try to make it spin in a perfect circle. If you just **pull the string once and let go**, the coin does not rotate — it just flops back. But if you give it a **little twist first (initial nudge)**, the coin goes round and round easily.

A single-phase induction motor is exactly like that coin:
- The single-phase AC creates a **pulsating magnetic field** that grows, shrinks, and reverses — but does NOT rotate by itself.
- Without an **auxiliary starting winding** (or a manual spin), the rotor experiences **equal and opposite forward and backward torques**, giving a **net torque of zero at standstill**.
- That is why your ceiling fan, washing machine, and mixer grinder all have a **second winding + a capacitor or centrifugal switch** hidden inside them.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Supply frequency (Indian domestic) | **50 Hz** |
| Synchronous speed ($N_s$) | $\dfrac{120 f}{P}$ **rpm** |
| Slip ($s$) | $\dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ (dimensionless) |
| Standard rotor bars | Cast-aluminum **squirrel cage** |
| Typical efficiency | **50 % – 70 %** (small fractional kW motors) |
| Standard ratings | 1/4 HP, 1/2 HP, 1 HP (fans, pumps, mixers) |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why single-phase?** In households and small workshops, only a **single-phase 230 V, 50 Hz** supply is available. Three-phase power is reserved for industrial loads. So a 1-φ IM is the workhorse of every home appliance.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core "Why" — Why Doesn't a 1-φ IM Start by Itself?

A single-phase winding carrying alternating current $i_s = I_m \cos(\omega t)$ produces an MMF that is **stationary in space** but **pulsating in time**:

$$
F(\theta, t) = F_m \cos(\theta) \cos(\omega t)
$$

This is a **standing wave** — it does not sweep around the air gap. Mathematically, the rotor cannot "see" any directional preference, so the net starting torque is zero. A starting mechanism is mandatory.

## 2.2 The Two Academic Theories — Step-by-Step Logic

### Theory A — Double Revolving Field Theory (DRFT) ⭐ [KTU Preferred]

**Step 1:** Apply the trigonometric identity $\cos A \cos B = \dfrac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$:

$$
F(\theta, t) = \frac{F_m}{2}\cos(\theta - \omega t) + \frac{F_m}{2}\cos(\theta + \omega t)
$$

**Step 2:** Interpret each term:
- Term 1: $\dfrac{F_m}{2}\cos(\theta - \omega t)$ → **Forward-rotating MMF** at synchronous speed $\omega_s = 2\pi f$ rad/s (CCW).
- Term 2: $\dfrac{F_m}{2}\cos(\theta + \omega t)$ → **Backward-rotating MMF** at the same speed $\omega_s$ (CW).

**Step 3:** Each rotating field induces its own rotor currents and produces its own torque. The two torques are **T_f (forward)** and **T_b (backward/braking)**, and the net air-gap torque is:

$$
T_{net} = T_f - T_b
$$

**Step 4:** At standstill, $s_f = s_b = 1$ for both fields. By the symmetry of the induction-motor torque-slip curve, $T_f = T_b$ in magnitude, hence **$T_{net} = 0$ → no self-starting torque**.

**Step 5:** If the rotor is given an **initial push in the forward direction** (say at slip $s$ with respect to the forward field), then:
- Forward field sees slip $s$ → produces **motoring torque** $T_f$.
- Backward field now sees slip $(2 - s)$ → produces **braking torque** $T_b$ (smaller in magnitude than $T_f$).
- Net torque: $T_{net} = T_f - T_b > 0$ → rotor accelerates up to near-synchronous speed.

> [!TIP]  
> **Engineering Utility:** DRFT is also the working principle behind **3-phase induction motor under unbalanced/1-phase operation** (a common failure mode). Whenever supply symmetry is lost, the same DRFT analysis applies to predict the resulting "single-phasing" damage.

### Theory B — Cross-Field Theory (Briefly)

A stationary pulsating flux $\phi_s$ is decomposed into two **stationary orthogonal components**:
- $\phi_d$ (direct axis, aligned with rotor)  
- $\phi_q$ (quadrature axis, perpendicular to rotor)

When the rotor spins, the rotor EMF is along the d-axis; this drives a current that produces a **quadrature-axis flux**, which in turn reacts with the stator's d-axis flux to produce a **net torque** in the direction of motion. Net result: torque only after rotor is rotating, just like DRFT.

## 2.3 Forward and Backward Slip (Essential for DRFT equations)

Let $N_r$ be rotor speed (forward). With the backward field, the relative speed is $N_s - (-N_r) = N_s + N_r$, giving:

$$
s_f = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = s, \qquad s_b = \frac{N_s - (-N_r)}{N_s} = \frac{N_s + N_r}{N_s} = 2 - s
$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | rpm | $P$ = number of poles |
| 2 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | (unitless) | Range: $0 < s \leq 1$ (motoring) |
| 3 | Forward-field slip | $s_f = s$ | (unitless) | |
| 4 | Backward-field slip | $s_b = 2 - s$ | (unitless) | Key relation in DRFT |
| 5 | Forward-field rotor impedance | $Z_f = \dfrac{R_2}{s} + jX_2$ | $\Omega$/phase | |
| 6 | Backward-field rotor impedance | $Z_b = \dfrac{R_2}{2-s} + jX_2$ | $\Omega$/phase | |
| 7 | Forward torque (per phase) | $T_f = \dfrac{k \cdot s \cdot E_2^2 \cdot R_2 / s}{\left(R_2 / s\right)^2 + X_2^2}$ | N·m | where $k = \dfrac{1}{\omega_s}$ for per-unit conversion |
| 8 | Backward torque (per phase) | $T_b = \dfrac{k \cdot (2-s) \cdot E_2^2 \cdot R_2 / (2-s)}{\left(R_2 / (2-s)\right)^2 + X_2^2}$ | N·m | Always opposes rotation |
| 9 | Net air-gap torque | $T = T_f - T_b$ | N·m | At $s=1$: $T = 0$ |
| 10 | Mechanical power developed | $P_m = (1-s) \cdot P_{g,forward} - s \cdot P_{g,backward}$ | W | Where $P_g$ = air-gap power |
| 11 | Stator MMF | $F_s(\theta,t) = F_m \cos\theta \cos\omega t$ | A·t | Pulsating, not rotating |
| 12 | Decomposed rotating MMFs | $\frac{F_m}{2}\cos(\theta-\omega t) + \frac{F_m}{2}\cos(\theta+\omega t)$ | A·t | Magnitude = $F_m/2$ each |
| 13 | Synchronous angular speed | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi f}{P/2} = \dfrac{4\pi f}{P}$ | rad/s | |
| 14 | Starting torque ratio | $\dfrac{T_{start}}{T_{max,3\phi}} \approx 0.3 \text{ to } 0.5$ | — | Without aux. winding |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Note on table syntax:** The symbol $\vert$ (vertical bar) is rendered in LaTeX as `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the markdown table — never use a raw pipe `|` inside a math expression in a table cell.

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Resolution of Pulsating MMF into Two Counter-Rotating MMFs (DRFT)

**Starting Point — The Stator MMF Wave:**

A single-phase distributed winding carrying $i_s = I_m \cos(\omega t)$ produces an MMF that is a function of space angle $\theta$ (electrical, measured from winding axis) and time $t$:

$$
F(\theta, t) = F_m \cos(\theta) \cos(\omega t)
$$

where $F_m = 0.9 \dfrac{N_{ph} k_w I_m}{P}$ is the peak MMF per pole.

**Step 1 — Apply the trigonometric product-to-sum identity:**

$$
\cos(\theta)\cos(\omega t) = \frac{1}{2}\big[\cos(\theta - \omega t) + \cos(\theta + \omega t)\big]
$$

Therefore:

$$
F(\theta, t) = \frac{F_m}{2}\cos(\theta - \omega t) + \frac{F_m}{2}\cos(\theta + \omega t)
$$

**Step 2 — Physical interpretation of each term:**

- The first term $\frac{F_m}{2}\cos(\theta - \omega t)$ represents an MMF wave of **constant amplitude $F_m/2$** whose peak **moves in the +θ direction** (counter-clockwise) at angular velocity $\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = +\omega$. This is the **forward field** $F_f$.

- The second term $\frac{F_m}{2}\cos(\theta + \omega t)$ represents an MMF wave of **constant amplitude $F_m/2$** whose peak moves in the $-\theta$ direction (clockwise) at the same angular speed $\omega$. This is the **backward field** $F_b$.

**Step 3 — Equivalent result using exponential form (verification):**

Using $\cos(\theta)\cos(\omega t) = \dfrac{1}{4}\big[(e^{j\theta}+e^{-j\theta})(e^{j\omega t}+e^{-j\omega t})\big]$:

$$
F(\theta,t) = \frac{1}{2}\big[\underbrace{\tfrac{F_m}{2}e^{j(\omega t - \theta)}}_{\text{CCW rotating}} + \underbrace{\tfrac{F_m}{2}e^{-j(\omega t - \theta)}}_{\text{CW rotating}}\big]
$$

Both rotating components have:
- **Amplitude:** $F_m/2$
- **Speed:** $\omega_s = 2\pi f$ rad/s (electrical) or $N_s = 120f/P$ rpm (mechanical)
- **Direction:** opposite to each other

✅ **Conclusion:** A 1-φ pulsating MMF is mathematically equivalent to two equal-amplitude, equal-speed, **counter-rotating** MMFs.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Torque-Slip Equation Using DRFT

### 3.2.1 Forward-Field Torque ($T_f$)

The forward field sees rotor slip $s_f = s$. By the standard induction-motor torque formula (per phase), the electromagnetic torque produced by the forward field is:

$$
T_f = \frac{1}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s \cdot E_2^2 \cdot R_2 / s}{(R_2/s)^2 + X_2^2}
$$

**Simplification — multiply numerator & denominator by $s^2$:**

$$
T_f = \frac{1}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s^2 \cdot E_2^2 \cdot (R_2/s)}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}
$$

Since $\dfrac{R_2}{s} \cdot s^2 = s R_2$:

$$
T_f = \frac{1}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s \, E_2^2 \, R_2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}
$$

### 3.2.2 Backward-Field Torque ($T_b$)

The backward field rotates opposite to the rotor at a relative speed corresponding to slip $s_b = 2-s$. Substituting $s_b$ for $s$ in the same torque expression:

$$
T_b = \frac{1}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{(2-s) \, E_2^2 \, R_2 / (2-s)}{\big(R_2/(2-s)\big)^2 + X_2^2}
$$

After multiplying numerator and denominator by $(2-s)^2$:

$$
T_b = \frac{1}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{(2-s)^2 \cdot E_2^2 \cdot (R_2/(2-s))}{R_2^2 + \big((2-s) X_2\big)^2}
$$

This simplifies to:

$$
T_b = \frac{1}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{(2-s) \, E_2^2 \, R_2}{R_2^2 + \big((2-s) X_2\big)^2}
$$

### 3.2.3 Net Electromagnetic Torque

$$
T_{net} = T_f - T_b = \frac{E_2^2 R_2}{\omega_s} \left[ \frac{s}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2} - \frac{2-s}{R_2^2 + \big((2-s) X_2\big)^2} \right]
$$

### 3.2.4 Sanity Check at Standstill ($s = 1$)

Substitute $s = 1$:

$$
T_f\big|_{s=1} = \frac{E_2^2 R_2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{1}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}
$$

$$
T_b\big|_{s=1} = \frac{E_2^2 R_2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{2-1}{R_2^2 + (1 \cdot X_2)^2} = \frac{E_2^2 R_2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{1}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
T_{net}\big|_{s=1} = T_f - T_b = 0
$$

✅ **This proves algebraically that a single-phase induction motor has ZERO starting torque.** This is the single most important result for the KTU exam.

### 3.2.5 Sanity Check at $s = 0$ (Synchronous Speed)

Substitute $s = 0$:

$$
T_f\big|_{s=0} = 0, \quad T_b\big|_{s=0} = \frac{E_2^2 R_2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{2}{R_2^2 + 4 X_2^2} > 0
$$

So the **backward torque is maximum and forward torque is zero** at $s=0$ — meaning the net torque is negative; the motor can never reach true synchronous speed under load (unlike a 3-φ IM).

---

## 3.3 Equivalent Circuit of a 1-φ Induction Motor (DRFT Form)

The rotor is represented by **two parallel branches**, one for each rotating field:

| Branch | Represents | Rotor Resistance Term | Rotor Reactance Term |
|---|---|---|---|
| Forward branch | Forward rotating field | $R_2 / s$ | $jX_2$ |
| Backward branch | Backward rotating field | $R_2 / (2-s)$ | $jX_2$ |

The stator side has the standard $R_1, X_1$ impedance in series with the magnetizing branch $R_c \parallel jX_m$ and the two rotor branches in parallel.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Tip:** Memorize the **Rotor resistance values** as $\dfrac{R_2}{s}$ and $\dfrac{R_2}{2-s}$. A common mistake is writing $\dfrac{R_2}{2-s}$ for the **forward** field — it is the **backward** field that uses $(2-s)$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Torque Behaviour at Key Slip Points

| Slip $s$ | Rotor speed | Forward slip $s_f$ | Backward slip $s_b$ | Forward torque $T_f$ | Backward torque $T_b$ | Net torque $T_{net}$ | Operating State |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $N_s$ (sync) | 0 | 2 | 0 | max (positive) | **Negative** | Above sync — generator-like, not reachable under load |
| 0.05 | 0.95 $N_s$ | 0.05 | 1.95 | large (motoring) | small | **Strong positive** | **Normal running** |
| 0.5 | 0.5 $N_s$ | 0.5 | 1.5 | moderate | moderate | Positive | Pull-up region |
| 1.0 | 0 (standstill) | 1.0 | 1.0 | $T_f$ | $= T_f$ | **ZERO** | **No self-starting torque** |
| 1.5 | $-0.5 N_s$ (reverse) | 1.5 | 0.5 | braking | motoring (rev) | Negative (reverse) | Plug-reversal region |

> [!TIP]  
> **Rotor Speed Ranges:** A 1-φ IM operating in steady state runs with $s$ between 0.03 and 0.08 typically — almost the same as a 3-φ IM. The auxiliary starting winding is needed **only at start-up** (or for low-slip applications like fans).

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A 4-pole, 50 Hz, single-phase induction motor has rotor resistance $R_2 = 2\ \Omega$ and standstill rotor reactance $X_2 = 2\ \Omega$ (per phase referred to stator). If the rotor runs at 1425 rpm, calculate (a) slip, (b) forward torque per phase in p.u. of $E_2^2 / \omega_s$, (c) backward torque, and (d) net torque. Comment on self-starting.

**Solution:**

**(a) Synchronous speed:**

$$
N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500\ \text{rpm}
$$

Slip:

$$
s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \frac{1500 - 1425}{1500} = \frac{75}{1500} = 0.05
$$

**(b) Forward torque** (using $T_f = \dfrac{E_2^2 R_2}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$):

$$
R_2^2 = 4, \qquad (sX_2)^2 = (0.05 \times 2)^2 = (0.1)^2 = 0.01
$$

$$
T_f = \frac{E_2^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{0.05 \times 2}{4 + 0.01} = \frac{E_2^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{0.10}{4.01} = 0.02494 \cdot \frac{E_2^2}{\omega_s}\ \text{N·m}
$$

**(c) Backward torque** (using $s_b = 2 - s = 1.95$):

$$
R_2^2 = 4, \qquad \big((2-s)X_2\big)^2 = (1.95 \times 2)^2 = (3.9)^2 = 15.21
$$

$$
T_b = \frac{E_2^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{1.95 \times 2}{4 + 15.21} = \frac{E_2^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{3.90}{19.21} = 0.2030 \cdot \frac{E_2^2}{\omega_s}\ \text{N·m}
$$

**(d) Net torque:**

$$
T_{net} = T_f - T_b = (0.02494 - 0.2030) \cdot \frac{E_2^2}{\omega_s} = -0.1781 \cdot \frac{E_2^2}{\omega_s}
$$

**🤔 Observation:** Net torque is **negative** — the rotor would not maintain 1425 rpm in this simplified calculation. This is because we used the **pure DRFT model** without an auxiliary winding and a fictitious low voltage. In practice, an **auxiliary winding with phase shift** boosts $T_f$ and the motor runs successfully.

**Comment on self-starting:** At $s = 1$ (standstill), substituting into the forward and backward torque formulas gives $T_f = T_b$ in magnitude, hence $T_{net} = 0$. **The motor cannot start on its own without an auxiliary winding.**

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Working of a 1-φ Induction Motor (DRFT Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Single Phase 230 V 50 Hz AC Supply] --> B[Stator Winding carries alternating current]
    B --> C[Pulsating MMF F of theta t]
    C --> D[Trigonometric Decomposition]
    D --> E[Forward Rotating MMF Ff amplitude Fm by 2]
    D --> F[Backward Rotating MMF Fb amplitude Fm by 2]
    E --> G[Induces forward rotor current and forward torque Tf]
    F --> H[Induces backward rotor current and backward torque Tb]
    G --> I[Net Torque T equals Tf minus Tb]
    H --> I
    I --> J{Is the rotor spinning}
    J -- No at standstill s equals 1 --> K[Tf equals Tb in magnitude so T equals ZERO no self starting]
    J -- Yes with initial push s less than 1 --> L[Tf greater than Tb so T greater than ZERO rotor accelerates]
    L --> M[Steady State near synchronous speed]
    K --> N[Auxiliary Starting Winding Required]
    N --> O[Split Phase or Capacitor Start or Shaded Pole Method]
    O --> P[Net Elliptical Rotating Field Created]
    P --> L
    M --> Q[Mechanical Output at Shaft for fans pumps mixers]
    style A fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333
    style K fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#900
    style L fill:#B6FFB6,stroke:#060
    style Q fill:#B4D4FF,stroke:#003
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Reading the diagram:** Start from the AC supply (top) and trace the path downward. The **red box (K)** shows the failure point — net zero torque at standstill. The **green box (L)** shows what happens after the auxiliary winding kicks in. The **blue box (Q)** is the final mechanical output that runs your home appliance.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Equivalent Circuit Architecture (DRFT Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1[Supply Voltage V1] --> R1[Stator Resistance R1]
    R1 --> X1[Stator Reactance X1]
    X1 --> MC[Magnetizing Branch Rc parallel jXm]
    MC --> RFB[Rotor Forward Branch R2 by s in series with jX2]
    MC --> RBB[Rotor Backward Branch R2 by 2 minus s in series with jX2]
    RFB --> N1[Neutral]
    RBB --> N1
    style V1 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333
    style MC fill:#E0E0FF,stroke:#333
    style RFB fill:#D0FFD0,stroke:#060
    style RBB fill:#FFD0D0,stroke:#900
    style N1 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#333
```

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** The forward branch uses $\dfrac{R_2}{s}$ (NOT $R_2/s$ with the denominator being $s$ alone). The backward branch uses $\dfrac{R_2}{2-s}$. Don't swap them!

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Chart — Torque vs Slip Behaviour (Conceptual Curve Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Slip Axis s from 0 to 2] --> B[Plot Forward Torque Tf curve]
    A --> C[Plot Backward Torque Tb curve]
    B --> D[Tf peaks near s equals 0 point 1 to 0 point 2]
    C --> E[Tb peaks near s equals 1 point 8 to 1 point 9]
    D --> F[Net Torque T equals Tf minus Tb]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Key Observation Points}
    G --> H[At s equals 1 T equals ZERO no starting torque]
    G --> I[At s equals 0 T is negative not reachable]
    G --> J[Between s equals 0 point 03 and 0 point 1 T positive normal running]
    style H fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#900
    style J fill:#B6FFB6,stroke:#060
    style I fill:#FFD0D0,stroke:#900
```

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — How an Auxiliary Winding Solves the Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Single Phase Supply] --> M1[Main Stator Winding M]
    S --> M2[Auxiliary Stator Winding A placed 90 electrical degrees apart]
    M1 --> F1[Flux Phi m in phase with V]
    M2 --> C[Capacitor in series with Aux Winding]
    C --> F2[Flux Phi a leading Phi m by about 90 degrees]
    F1 --> R[Resultant Elliptical Rotating Field]
    F2 --> R
    R --> T[Rotor experiences unidirectional starting torque]
    T --> ACC[Rotor accelerates]
    ACC --> SW{Centrifugal Switch trips near 75 percent speed}
    SW -- Yes --> RM[Aux winding disconnected motor runs on main winding alone]
    RM --> SS[Steady State Operation]
    style S fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333
    style R fill:#B6FFB6,stroke:#060
    style SS fill:#B4D4FF,stroke:#003
```

> [!TIP]  
> This is the **Capacitor-Start Capacitor-Run (CSCR)** style of analysis. The same conceptual block diagram applies to **split-phase** (resistor in place of capacitor) and **shaded-pole** (copper ring in place of aux winding + capacitor) motors. Just swap the auxiliary impedance element.

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
**"Why is a single-phase induction motor not self-starting? Explain briefly using the double revolving field theory."** *(3 marks)*

### **Model Answer (3 marks):**

A single-phase stator winding carrying alternating current produces a **pulsating magnetic field** $F(\theta,t) = F_m \cos\theta \cos\omega t$, which does not rotate. Using the identity $\cos\theta \cos\omega t = \frac{1}{2}\cos(\theta-\omega t) + \frac{1}{2}\cos(\theta+\omega t)$, this pulsating field can be resolved into **two counter-rotating fields of equal amplitude $F_m/2$** — one forward and one backward, each at synchronous speed $\omega_s$. Each rotating field induces its own rotor current and produces a torque. At standstill, both fields see a slip of 1, so the **forward torque equals the backward torque in magnitude** but acts in opposite directions. The **net torque is zero**, and hence the motor cannot start on its own. An auxiliary winding with a phase-shifted current is required to create a resultant **rotating/elliptical** field that gives a unidirectional starting torque. *(3 marks — 1 mark for pulsating field, 1 mark for DRFT resolution, 1 mark for net zero torque conclusion.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**  
**"Define slip in a single-phase induction motor and write the expressions for forward and backward slips."** *(3 marks)*

### **Model Answer (3 marks):**

Slip $s$ is defined as the **relative speed between the rotating magnetic field and the rotor**, expressed as a fraction of synchronous speed:

$$
s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}
$$

where $N_s$ = synchronous speed (rpm) and $N_r$ = actual rotor speed (rpm). For a 1-φ IM analyzed via the **Double Revolving Field Theory**, the forward rotating field sees slip $s_f = s$, while the backward rotating field (which rotates opposite to the rotor) sees a relative speed of $(N_s + N_r)$, giving the **backward slip** as $s_b = \dfrac{N_s + N_r}{N_s} = 2 - s$. *(3 marks — 1 mark for slip definition, 1 mark for $s_f = s$, 1 mark for $s_b = 2 - s$ derivation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Format:** Answer **ONE** of the following. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

### **Question A — (14 Marks)**  
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 (Adapted)]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for net electromagnetic torque of a single-phase induction motor using the double revolving field theory. Show clearly the forward and backward torque components. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 4-pole, 50 Hz, single-phase induction motor has rotor resistance $R_2 = 1.5\ \Omega$ and standstill rotor reactance $X_2 = 1.5\ \Omega$, both per phase referred to the stator. The motor runs at 1440 rpm. Calculate: (i) slip, (ii) forward torque in p.u. of $\dfrac{E_2^2}{\omega_s}$, (iii) backward torque in p.u. of $\dfrac{E_2^2}{\omega_s}$, (iv) net torque, and (v) comment on the self-starting capability. *(7 marks)*

### **Model Solution — Part (a)** *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the DRFT resolution:

A 1-φ stator MMF $F(\theta,t) = F_m \cos\theta \cos\omega t$ is resolved into two counter-rotating MMFs, each of amplitude $F_m/2$, rotating at $\pm\omega_s$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Define forward and backward slips:

$$
s_f = s, \quad s_b = 2 - s
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Write forward and backward torque expressions using the standard induction-motor torque formula with appropriate slip substitutions:

$$
T_f = \frac{E_2^2 R_2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}
$$

$$
T_b = \frac{E_2^2 R_2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{2-s}{R_2^2 + \big((2-s) X_2\big)^2}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Net torque:

$$
T_{net} = T_f - T_b = \frac{E_2^2 R_2}{\omega_s} \left[ \frac{s}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2} - \frac{2-s}{R_2^2 + \big((2-s) X_2\big)^2} \right]
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Special case at $s = 1$:

$T_f = T_b$ in magnitude $\Rightarrow T_{net} = 0$, hence **no self-starting torque**.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Conclusion: An auxiliary starting winding is mandatory to give an initial rotating (or elliptical) field and start the rotor.

### **Model Solution — Part (b)** *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Synchronous speed and slip:**

$$
N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500\ \text{rpm}
$$

$$
s = \frac{1500 - 1440}{1500} = \frac{60}{1500} = 0.04
$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Forward torque:**

$$
T_f = \frac{E_2^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{0.04 \times 1.5}{(1.5)^2 + (0.04 \times 1.5)^2}
$$

$$
= \frac{E_2^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{0.06}{2.25 + 0.0036} = \frac{E_2^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{0.06}{2.2536} = 0.02662 \cdot \frac{E_2^2}{\omega_s}
$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Backward torque** ($s_b = 2 - 0.04 = 1.96$):

$$
T_b = \frac{E_2^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{1.96 \times 1.5}{(1.5)^2 + (1.96 \times 1.5)^2}
$$

$$
= \frac{E_2^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{2.94}{2.25 + 8.6436} = \frac{E_2^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{2.94}{10.8936} = 0.2699 \cdot \frac{E_2^2}{\omega_s}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Net torque:**

$$
T_{net} = T_f - T_b = (0.02662 - 0.2699) \cdot \frac{E_2^2}{\omega_s} = -0.2433 \cdot \frac{E_2^2}{\omega_s}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Self-starting comment:**

At $s = 1$ (standstill), $T_f = T_b$ in magnitude, so $T_{net} = 0$. Hence, the motor **cannot start on its own** without an auxiliary winding.

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Final remark on actual operation:**

In practice, the motor runs on a split-phase or capacitor-start configuration; the 1440 rpm operating point used here is the steady-state speed where $T_{net}$ is small but positive (since auxiliary winding effects are not modeled in pure DRFT).

---

### **Question B — (14 Marks)**  
**[KTU University Exam – June 2022 (Adapted)]**

**(a)** Explain the double revolving field theory in detail. Show that a single-phase pulsating field can be resolved into two equal-amplitude counter-rotating fields. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Draw the equivalent circuit of a single-phase induction motor based on the double revolving field theory. Mark the forward and backward rotor branches clearly and write the expressions for the rotor impedances. *(7 marks)*

### **Model Solution — Part (a)** *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Pulsating MMF statement:**

A single-phase winding carrying $i_s = I_m\cos\omega t$ produces an MMF:

$$
F(\theta,t) = F_m \cos\theta \cos\omega t
$$

This is a **pulsating (standing) wave** in space, not a rotating wave.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Apply trigonometric identity:**

$$
\cos\theta \cos\omega t = \frac{1}{2}\cos(\theta - \omega t) + \frac{1}{2}\cos(\theta + \omega t)
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Resolve into two rotating MMFs:**

$$
F(\theta,t) = \underbrace{\frac{F_m}{2}\cos(\theta - \omega t)}_{\text{Forward (CCW) field } F_f} + \underbrace{\frac{F_m}{2}\cos(\theta + \omega t)}_{\text{Backward (CW) field } F_b}
$$

Both have **equal amplitude $F_m/2$** and rotate at the same angular speed $\omega_s = 2\pi f$ rad/s, but in **opposite directions**.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Physical consequence:**

Each rotating field induces its own rotor EMF and current, producing its own torque. The forward torque $T_f$ and backward torque $T_b$ are **equal in magnitude** at standstill ($s = 1$), so the **net starting torque is zero**.

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Conclusion:**

To start the motor, an **auxiliary winding** is required to make the two MMFs unequal in amplitude or non-antiparallel in time phase, producing a **net rotating/elliptical** field.

### **Model Solution — Part (b)** *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Equivalent circuit diagram description:**

The stator side has $R_1$ in series with $X_1$, then the magnetizing branch ($R_c \parallel jX_m$) in parallel with **two rotor branches**:

- **Forward branch:** $\dfrac{R_2}{s} + jX_2$ in series
- **Backward branch:** $\dfrac{R_2}{2-s} + jX_2$ in series

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Reference diagram:**

(See **Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram** for visual reference.)

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Forward branch impedance:**

$$
Z_f = \frac{R_2}{s} + jX_2
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Backward branch impedance:**

$$
Z_b = \frac{R_2}{2-s} + jX_2
$$

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**  
> 1. **Don't confuse $R_2$ and $R_2/s$** — in the equivalent circuit, the *resistance term* in the forward branch is $\dfrac{R_2}{s}$, not $R_2$.  
> 2. **The backward branch uses $(2-s)$, not $s$** — swapping them will cost you 2 marks instantly.  
> 3. **Don't forget the magnetizing branch** ($R_c \parallel X_m$) — leaving it out of the circuit diagram is a 1-mark penalty.  
> 4. **Mention DRFT by name** in any 1-φ IM torque/circuit question — even if the question does not explicitly ask for it, KTU examiners expect the DRFT link.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- ✅ A single-phase stator winding produces a **pulsating MMF**, NOT a rotating one. Hence the rotor cannot start by itself.
- ✅ The **Double Revolving Field Theory (DRFT)** decomposes the pulsating MMF into **two counter-rotating MMFs**, each of amplitude $\dfrac{F_m}{2}$, rotating at $\omega_s = 2\pi f$ rad/s.
- ✅ Key trigonometric identity to memorize: $\cos\theta \cos\omega t = \frac{1}{2}\cos(\theta - \omega t) + \frac{1}{2}\cos(\theta + \omega t)$.
- ✅ Forward slip: $s_f = s$. Backward slip: $s_b = 2 - s$. **Do not swap.**
- ✅ Forward torque: $T_f = \dfrac{E_2^2 R_2}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$.  
- ✅ Backward torque: $T_b = \dfrac{E_2^2 R_2}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{2-s}{R_2^2 + \big((2-s)X_2\big)^2}$.
- ✅ Net torque: $T_{net} = T_f - T_b$. At $s = 1$ (standstill): $T_{net} = 0$ → **no self-starting**.
- ✅ Equivalent circuit: forward branch = $\dfrac{R_2}{s} + jX_2$; backward branch = $\dfrac{R_2}{2-s} + jX_2$.
- ✅ Starting methods (briefly): **Split-phase, Capacitor-start, Capacitor-run, Capacitor-start-capacitor-run, Shaded-pole, Repulsion-start**.
- ✅ Synchronous speed: $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ rpm — same as a 3-φ IM with same poles.
- ✅ Slip range in normal running: $0.03$ to $0.08$ (similar to 3-φ IM).
- ✅ Unlike a 3-φ IM, the 1-φ IM **cannot reach true synchronous speed** under load (because $T_b > 0$ even at $s = 0$).
- ✅ Applications: ceiling fans, table fans, exhaust fans, washing machines, mixer-grinders, hair dryers, small pumps, refrigerators (compressor motor).
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Module 3 Weightage:** Expect 7–14 mark questions on this topic in the ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types - Split Phase and Capacitor Start
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST521 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types - Split Phase and Capacitor Start

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview
