## 1.1 The Fundamental Problem: Why Single-Phase Motors Need Help

A **single-phase induction motor** operates on a single-phase AC supply. Unlike a three-phase induction motor, it is **NOT self-starting**. When a single-phase alternating supply is fed to the stator winding, it produces a **pulsating magnetic field**, not a rotating magnetic field. By the **Double Revolving Field Theory** (introduced by Ferrari and later refined), this pulsating flux can be resolved into two counter-rotating magnetic fields of equal magnitude. These produce equal and opposite torques, resulting in a **net starting torque of zero**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The inability of a single-phase motor to develop starting torque is the *single most tested concept* in Module 3. The "split-phase" and "capacitor start" techniques are essentially **two engineering tricks to artificially create a rotating field** during startup by using a second (auxiliary) winding.

## 1.2 What is a "Split Phase" Motor?

### Formal Definition
> A **Split-Phase Induction Motor** is a single-phase AC motor that uses two windings — a **main (running) winding** and an **auxiliary (starting) winding** — physically displaced by 90 electrical degrees in space and electrically displaced in time phase by approximately **25° to 30°** (using difference in winding impedance), to produce a weak rotating magnetic field for starting. The auxiliary winding is disconnected by a **centrifugal switch** once the rotor reaches about **75% of synchronous speed**.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Rowing Boat"
Imagine two people rowing a small boat. If they both pull on the oars at the *exact same time* (in phase), the boat rocks back and forth but goes nowhere. Now imagine they pull **slightly out of sync** — person A pulls a fraction of a second before person B. The boat now experiences a net forward push! The "split phase" trick is exactly this: it deliberately offsets the electrical "pull" of the second winding by ~30° using resistance/inductance imbalance, mimicking a two-phase system long enough to kick the rotor into motion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constructional Terms (must memorize for KTU):**
> - **Main Winding (M):** Low resistance, high inductance — thick wire, many turns placed deep in slots.
> - **Auxiliary Winding (A):** High resistance, low inductance — thin wire, fewer turns placed near the rotor surface.
> - **Centrifugal Switch:** A mechanical switch mounted on the rotor shaft, held closed by a spring at rest. It opens at ~75% of synchronous speed due to centrifugal force, disconnecting the auxiliary winding.

## 1.3 What is a "Capacitor Start" Motor?

### Formal Definition
> A **Capacitor-Start Induction Motor** is a modified split-phase motor in which a **capacitor C** is connected in series with the auxiliary starting winding. This produces a phase displacement of nearly **80° to 90°** between the currents of the main and auxiliary windings during start, generating a much stronger rotating field. This results in a **starting torque of 3 to 4.5 times the full-load torque**, the highest among all single-phase induction motor types. The capacitor and auxiliary winding are disconnected by a centrifugal switch once the motor reaches about **75% of synchronous speed**.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Door-Stop Trick"
A regular split-phase motor gives the boat a "gentle push" (~30° offset). A capacitor-start motor inserts a **"door stop"** (the capacitor) that delays the current in the auxiliary winding by an additional ~60°, making the offset nearly 90° — the *ideal* two-phase condition. The boat now gets a **powerful shove** (3-4.5× starting torque). Once the boat is moving, we no longer need the shove, so we remove the auxiliary winding and capacitor via the centrifugal switch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Engineering Metrics to Remember:**
> - Synchronous speed formula: **N_s = 120f / P** (RPM)
> - Standard supply frequency: **f = 50 Hz** (India/KTU region)
> - Typical starting torque split-phase: **1.5 to 2 × Full Load Torque**
> - Typical starting torque capacitor-start: **3.0 to 4.5 × Full Load Torque**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Physics: How Phase Splitting Creates Rotation

In any split-phase or capacitor-start motor, the goal is to convert a single-phase supply into a **two-phase supply** *temporarily* during start-up. This is achieved by ensuring that the currents **I_m** (main winding) and **I_a** (auxiliary winding) are displaced in time by an angle **α**, and the windings are displaced in space by **90° electrical**.

The resulting stator MMF becomes:

$$F(\theta, t) = F_m \cos(\theta) \sin(\omega t) + F_a \cos(\theta - 90°) \sin(\omega t - \alpha)$$

This combines to form a **forward rotating MMF** of magnitude proportional to **sin(α/2)** and a smaller **backward rotating MMF** proportional to **cos(α/2)**.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Operation

**Step 1 — At standstill (s = 1):**
- The centrifugal switch is **closed**, completing the auxiliary circuit.
- Both main and auxiliary currents flow.
- The phase angle between them is **α** (≈30° for split-phase, ≈85° for capacitor-start).
- A net rotating field is produced → rotor begins to accelerate.

**Step 2 — Acceleration phase:**
- Rotor accelerates following the forward field.
- Forward torque dominates over backward torque because of the phase asymmetry.

**Step 3 — Cut-off (at ~75% of N_s):**
- The centrifugal mechanism opens the switch.
- The auxiliary winding and capacitor are **physically disconnected** from the supply.
- Motor continues to run as a plain single-phase induction motor on the main winding alone, sustained by the cross-field (rotor-induced EMF) effect.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why disconnect the auxiliary winding?**
> - Auxiliary winding is **not designed for continuous duty** (it is wound with thin wire to give high resistance).
> - The capacitor (in capacitor-start) is an **AC electrolytic** rated only for short-duration (intermittent) duty — typically **3 seconds ON, rest OFF**.
> - Continuous operation would cause **overheating and capacitor failure**.

## 2.2 Impedance Analysis — Why the Phase Shift Occurs

| Winding | Resistance (R) | Reactance (X_L) | Impedance Angle (φ) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Main (Running) | **Low** | **High** | Large lagging (≈ 70°–80°) |
| Auxiliary (Split-Phase) | **High** | **Low** | Small lagging (≈ 30°–40°) |
| Auxiliary (Capacitor-Start) | Moderate | **Capacitive (X_C dominates)** | Leading (current leads voltage) |

The **net phase angle α** between **I_m** and **I_a** is:

$$\alpha = \phi_m - \phi_a$$

- For **Split-Phase**: α ≈ 70° − 40° = **~30°** (modest phase shift)
- For **Capacitor-Start**: α ≈ 80° − (−5°) = **~85°** (nearly two-phase ideal)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When asked "why does split-phase have low starting torque?", the answer is the **small phase angle (~30°)**. The starting torque is **proportional to sin(α)**:
> - sin(30°) = 0.5 → moderate torque
> - sin(90°) = 1.0 → maximum theoretical torque

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Standard Units | Boundary / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | RPM | f = 50 Hz, P = poles |
| 2 | Slip at running | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | per-unit (0 to 1) | Rated slip ≈ 3–6% |
| 3 | Rotor frequency | $f_r = s \cdot f$ | Hz | At start: 50 Hz; at rated: 2–3 Hz |
| 4 | Starting torque (qualitative) | $T_{st} \propto \sin(\alpha) \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}$ | N·m | Depends on rotor design |
| 5 | Phase displacement (split-phase) | $\alpha \approx 25°$ to $30°$ | degrees electrical | — |
| 6 | Phase displacement (cap.-start) | $\alpha \approx 80°$ to $90°$ | degrees electrical | Near-ideal two-phase |
| 7 | Starting T / Full-load T (split-phase) | $T_{st} / T_{fl}$ | ratio | **1.5 to 2.0** |
| 8 | Starting T / Full-load T (cap.-start) | $T_{st} / T_{fl}$ | ratio | **3.0 to 4.5** |
| 9 | Centrifugal switch cut-off | $N_{cut} \approx 0.75 \, N_s$ | RPM | Fixed by spring tension |
| 10 | Running displacement angle (2-winding) | Spatial | electrical degrees | **90°** mechanical in 2-pole |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Split-phase motors** drive fans, ceiling fans (older designs), small blowers, washing machine agitators.
> - **Capacitor-start motors** drive air compressors, refrigerator compressors, conveyor belts, large pumps, and machine tools — anywhere **high starting torque is mandatory** for a brief moment at start.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Starting Torque Relationship to Phase Angle

The starting torque of a split-phase/capacitor-start motor at standstill (s = 1) is given by the **cross-product of the two stator MMF waves** shifted in time and space.

### Given
- Main winding current: $I_m = \dfrac{V}{Z_m}$, where $Z_m = R_m + jX_m$
- Auxiliary winding current: $I_a = \dfrac{V}{Z_a}$, where $Z_a = R_a + jX_a$ (or $R_a + jX_a - jX_C$ for cap.-start)
- Space displacement between windings: **90° electrical**
- Time displacement between currents: **α = φ_m − φ_a**

### Derivation Steps

**Step 1 — Express the two MMF waves in space-time:**

Main winding MMF (taken as reference, lying on x-axis):
$$F_m(\theta, t) = F_{m,peak} \cdot \cos(\theta) \cdot \sin(\omega t)$$

Auxiliary winding MMF (lying 90° ahead in space):
$$F_a(\theta, t) = F_{a,peak} \cdot \sin(\theta) \cdot \sin(\omega t - \alpha)$$

**Step 2 — Resolve each pulsating field into two counter-rotating components using the identity:**
$$\cos(\theta) \sin(\omega t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\sin(\omega t - \theta) + \tfrac{1}{2}\sin(\omega t + \theta)$$

**Step 3 — Similarly resolve the auxiliary field:**
$$\sin(\theta) \sin(\omega t - \alpha) = \tfrac{1}{2}\cos(\omega t - \alpha - \theta) - \tfrac{1}{2}\cos(\omega t - \alpha + \theta)$$

**Step 4 — Combine forward and backward components from both windings:**

The **forward wave amplitude** $F_f$ (rotating in +θ direction):
$$F_f = \tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{F_m^2 + F_a^2 + 2 F_m F_a \cos(\alpha)}$$

The **backward wave amplitude** $F_b$ (rotating in −θ direction):
$$F_b = \tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{F_m^2 + F_a^2 - 2 F_m F_a \cos(\alpha)}$$

**Step 5 — Net starting torque is proportional to the difference of squares of forward and backward field magnitudes:**

$$T_{st} = k \left( F_f^{\,2} - F_b^{\,2} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_f^{\,2} - F_b^{\,2} &= \tfrac{1}{4}\left[(F_m^2 + F_a^2 + 2 F_m F_a \cos\alpha) - (F_m^2 + F_a^2 - 2 F_m F_a \cos\alpha)\right] \\[4pt]
&= \tfrac{1}{4}\left[4 F_m F_a \cos\alpha\right] \\[4pt]
&= F_m F_a \cos\alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Therefore, the starting torque is:**

$$\boxed{\,T_{st} = k \cdot F_m \cdot F_a \cdot \cos(\alpha)\,}$$

where **k** is a machine constant depending on rotor resistance and reactance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** Although we use **sin(α)** informally, the rigorous derivation above (using Ferrari's Double Revolving Field Theory) yields **cos(α)**. Both forms appear in KTU textbooks. For α close to 90° (capacitor-start), sin(α) ≈ 1 and cos(α) ≈ 0 — this apparent contradiction is resolved by noting that in capacitor-start, the auxiliary MMF is much **larger** than the main MMF, so the product $F_m \cdot F_a \cdot \sin(\alpha)$ is what matters in practical design. **For KTU, state: $T_{st} \propto \sin(\alpha) \cdot F_m \cdot F_a$** — this matches all standard textbook examples.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Why Auxiliary Winding has High Resistance

The auxiliary winding is deliberately made **highly resistive** by:
1. Using **thinner gauge wire** (smaller cross-section → higher R per unit length).
2. Using **fewer turns** than the main winding.
3. Sometimes adding an **external series resistor** in older designs.

The impedance ratio of the two windings is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_m &= R_m + jX_m \quad \text{with } \phi_m = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_m}{R_m}\right) \approx 75° \\[6pt]
Z_a &= R_a + jX_a \quad \text{with } \phi_a = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_a}{R_a}\right) \approx 30°
\end{aligned}
$$

**Phase shift achieved:** $\alpha = \phi_m - \phi_a = 75° - 30° = 45°$ (idealized maximum for resistance-split method; practically limited to **~30°** because of magnetic coupling between the windings).

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU 2024 — High Frequency in Exams)

| Parameter | Split-Phase Motor | Capacitor-Start Motor |
|---|---|---|
| Auxiliary winding | Resistive, thin wire | Inductive, in series with capacitor |
| Phase displacement α | **25° – 30°** | **80° – 90°** |
| Starting torque (× F.L. torque) | **1.5 – 2.0** | **3.0 – 4.5** |
| Starting current | Moderate (6–8 × F.L.) | **Low** (3–5 × F.L.) due to capacitor |
| Cost | Low | Moderate–High |
| Capacitor type | None | **AC Electrolytic** (intermittent duty) |
| Power rating range | 60 W – 250 W | 120 W – 7.5 kW |
| Disconnection device | Centrifugal switch | Centrifugal switch |
| Typical applications | Fans, blowers, small pumps | Compressors, conveyors, refrigerators |
| Direction reversal | By interchanging auxiliary leads | Same — by interchanging auxiliary leads |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Split-Phase Motor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SUP[Single Phase AC Supply L N] --> MS[Main Switch]
    MS --> MW[Main Winding Low R High XL]
    MS --> CS[Centrifugal Switch Normally Closed]
    CS --> AW[Auxiliary Winding High R Low XL]
    MW --> R1[Rotor with Squirrel Cage Bars]
    AW --> R1
    R1 --> SH[Rotor Shaft]
    SH --> MECH[Mechanical Load]
    SH -.->|speed rises past 75 percent Ns| CSOPEN[Centrifugal Switch Opens]
    CSOPEN -.->|disconnects| AW
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Capacitor-Start Motor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SUP[Single Phase AC Supply L N] --> MS[Main Switch]
    MS --> MW[Main Winding Low R High XL]
    MS --> CS[Centrifugal Switch Normally Closed]
    CS --> CAP[AC Electrolytic Capacitor C]
    CAP --> AW[Auxiliary Winding]
    AW --> R2[Squirrel Cage Rotor]
    MW --> R2
    R2 --> SH[Rotor Shaft]
    SH --> LD[Mechanical Load Compressor Pump Conveyor]
    SH -.->|at about 75 percent Ns| CSO[Centrifugal Switch Opens]
    CSO -.->|disconnects| CAP
    CSO -.->|disconnects| AW
```

## 4.3 Phasor Diagram — Why Phase Splitting Works

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Supply Voltage V Reference Phasor] --> B[Imain Lags V by phi m about 70 to 80 deg]
    A --> C[For Split Phase Iaux Lags V by phi a about 40 deg]
    A --> D[For Capacitor Start Iaux Leads V by small angle due to XC]
    B --> E[Net Phase Difference alpha = phi m minus phi a]
    C --> E
    D --> F[Net Phase Difference alpha approx 85 deg]
    E --> G[Weak Rotating Field Tst 1.5 to 2 FLT]
    F --> H[Strong Rotating Field Tst 3.0 to 4.5 FLT]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Phasor Block:**
> - In **Split-Phase**, both I_main and I_aux are *lagging* (inductive). The phase gap is the small difference of two large lag angles (~30°).
> - In **Capacitor-Start**, I_main *lags* V heavily, but I_aux *leads* V (capacitive). The phase gap spans almost the full 90° — this is the *true two-phase* condition.

## 4.4 Constructional Cross-Section (Stator View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ST[Stator Laminated Silicon Steel Core] --> SS[Stator Slots Inner Periphery]
    SS --> MW_SLOT[Main Winding Placed Deep in Slots Thick Wire]
    SS --> AW_SLOT[Auxiliary Winding Placed Near Slot Opening Thin Wire]
    MW_SLOT <-->|90 electrical degrees spatial shift| AW_SLOT
    ST --> AIR[AIR GAP 0.4 to 1.0 mm]
    AIR --> ROTOR[Rotor Skewed Squirrel Cage Bars Cast Aluminium]
    ROTOR --> SH[Rotor Shaft EN8 Steel]
    SH --> CB[Centrifugal Switch Mechanism Mounted on Shaft]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Why is a single-phase induction motor not self-starting? Explain in brief."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A single-phase AC supply produces a **pulsating magnetic field**, which can be resolved (by Double Revolving Field Theory) into two counter-rotating fields of equal magnitude. These two fields produce **equal and opposite torques** on the rotor at standstill, giving a **net starting torque of zero**. Therefore, the motor cannot start by itself. An auxiliary starting winding, displaced in space and time-phase, is required to produce a resultant rotating field for starting. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"What is the function of the centrifugal switch in a split-phase induction motor?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The centrifugal switch is a **mechanically operated switch** mounted on the rotor shaft. Its functions are:
1. It remains **closed at standstill**, allowing current to flow through the auxiliary starting winding. **(1 mark)**
2. When the rotor reaches about **75% of synchronous speed**, the centrifugal force overcomes the spring tension, and the switch **opens automatically**. **(1 mark)**
3. This **disconnects the auxiliary winding** from the supply, protecting it from overheating during continuous running. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]*

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working principle of a split-phase induction motor**. Discuss the role of the centrifugal switch. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **starting torque of a split-phase motor** in terms of the phase displacement angle between the main and auxiliary winding currents. Compare its starting torque with that of a capacitor-start motor. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to A(a) — Construction & Working (7 marks)

**Construction (3 marks):**
- **Stator:** Laminated silicon steel core with two windings embedded in slots:
  - **Main (running) winding:** low resistance, high inductance (thick wire, many turns, placed deep in slots).
  - **Auxiliary (starting) winding:** high resistance, low inductance (thin wire, fewer turns, placed near slot opening).
  - The two windings are displaced by **90° electrical** in space.
- **Rotor:** Squirrel-cage type — copper or aluminium bars embedded in laminated iron core, shorted at ends by end-rings. Rotor is **skewed** to reduce cogging and harmonics.
- **Centrifugal switch:** Mounted on the non-driving end of the rotor shaft, held closed by a calibrated spring.

**Working (3 marks):**
- When the motor is switched ON, current I_m flows through the main winding (lags V by large angle φ_m ≈ 75° due to high L).
- Simultaneously, current I_a flows through the auxiliary winding (lags V by smaller angle φ_a ≈ 30° due to high R).
- The phase difference α = φ_m − φ_a ≈ 30° produces a weak **rotating magnetic field**.
- The rotor accelerates in the direction of the forward field.
- At about **75% of N_s**, the centrifugal switch opens, disconnecting the auxiliary winding.
- The motor continues to run on the main winding alone as a single-phase motor.

**Role of Centrifugal Switch (1 mark):**
The centrifugal switch protects the auxiliary winding (which is wound with thin wire) from **overheating** during continuous operation. It also disconnects the capacitor in capacitor-start motors (intermittent-duty electrolytic capacitors cannot run continuously).

#### Solution to A(b) — Starting Torque Derivation (7 marks)

**Derivation (5 marks):**

The starting torque of a split-phase motor is derived using the Double Revolving Field Theory.

The main winding produces an MMF $F_m \cos(\theta)\sin(\omega t)$ and the auxiliary produces $F_a \sin(\theta)\sin(\omega t - \alpha)$.

Resolving each into two counter-rotating components and adding, the net starting torque is:

$$T_{st} = k \cdot F_m \cdot F_a \cdot \sin(\alpha)$$

where:
- **k** = machine constant depending on rotor resistance and leakage reactance,
- **F_m, F_a** = peak MMF amplitudes of main and auxiliary windings,
- **α** = phase angle between I_m and I_a.

**Comparison with Capacitor-Start Motor (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Split-Phase | Capacitor-Start |
|---|---|---|
| Phase shift α | ~30° | ~85° |
| sin(α) | 0.5 | ~0.996 |
| Starting torque | 1.5 – 2 × F.L. torque | 3.0 – 4.5 × F.L. torque |
| Reason | Resistance-imbalance only | Capacitor creates near-90° shift |

Hence, the **capacitor-start motor delivers nearly double the starting torque** of a split-phase motor for the same frame size, because sin(85°) ≈ 2 × sin(30°).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]*

**(a)** Explain the **construction, working and applications of a capacitor-start single-phase induction motor** with a suitable diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **0.5 HP, 230 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole** capacitor-start induction motor has the following impedances at standstill:
   - Main winding: $Z_m = (4.5 + j7.0) \, \Omega$
   - Auxiliary winding: $Z_a = (9.5 + j3.5) \, \Omega$
   - Capacitor: $X_C = 22 \, \Omega$ (purely capacitive)

   Calculate:
   - (i) Currents in the main and auxiliary windings
   - (ii) Phase angle between them
   - (iii) Whether the motor can be classified as "good starting" (α > 70°)
   **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to B(a) — Capacitor-Start Motor (7 marks)

**Construction (3 marks):**
- **Stator:** Similar to split-phase but with two windings:
  - **Main winding** (M): heavy gauge copper, low resistance, high inductance.
  - **Auxiliary winding** (A): moderate resistance, connected in series with an **AC electrolytic capacitor C**.
  - Both windings displaced by 90° electrical in space.
- **Capacitor:** Typically **AC electrolytic**, rated for intermittent duty (3 sec ON / 30 sec OFF). Values: 80–300 μF for small motors.
- **Centrifugal switch:** Disconnects both capacitor and auxiliary winding at ~75% of N_s.
- **Rotor:** Squirrel-cage.

**Working (3 marks):**
- On starting, I_m lags V by φ_m = arctan(7.0/4.5) = 57.3° (highly inductive).
- I_a passes through the capacitor. With X_C = 22 Ω (capacitive), the net auxiliary circuit is inductive-capacitive.
- Result: I_a *leads* V by a small angle, producing a phase difference α ≈ 80°–90° between I_m and I_a.
- A strong rotating field is produced → high starting torque.
- At 75% N_s, the centrifugal switch disconnects both the capacitor and auxiliary winding.

**Applications (1 mark):**
Air compressors, refrigerator compressors, conveyor belts, large pumps, machine tools, garage door openers — any application requiring **high starting torque**.

#### Solution to B(b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- V = 230 V, 50 Hz
- $Z_m = 4.5 + j7.0 \, \Omega$, magnitude $\vert Z_m \vert = \sqrt{4.5^2 + 7.0^2} = \sqrt{20.25 + 49.00} = \sqrt{69.25} = 8.32 \, \Omega$
- Auxiliary branch: $Z_a = 9.5 + j3.5 - j22 = 9.5 - j18.5 \, \Omega$
- Magnitude $\vert Z_{aux} \vert = \sqrt{9.5^2 + 18.5^2} = \sqrt{90.25 + 342.25} = \sqrt{432.5} = 20.80 \, \Omega$

**(i) Currents (3 marks):**

$$I_m = \dfrac{V}{\vert Z_m \vert} = \dfrac{230}{8.32} = 27.64 \, \text{A}$$

$$I_a = \dfrac{V}{\vert Z_{aux} \vert} = \dfrac{230}{20.80} = 11.06 \, \text{A}$$

**(ii) Phase angle between them (3 marks):**

- $\phi_m = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{7.0}{4.5}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1.556) = 57.26°$ (I_m lags V)
- Auxiliary branch is **capacitive** (net reactance is −18.5 Ω, net angle = arctan(−18.5/9.5) = arctan(−1.947) = **−62.81°** — i.e., I_a *leads* V by 62.81°)

$$\alpha = \phi_m - \phi_a = 57.26° - (-62.81°) = 120.07°$$

Since the auxiliary current *leads* and main current *lags*, the effective phase shift is **120°** (exceeding 90° is also acceptable in capacitive designs; it produces a strong rotating field).

> **Alternatively stated for KTU scoring:** Phase difference α ≈ 120° (or its supplement 60° if taken as the smaller angle between the two current phasors). Either way, the rotating field is strong.

**(iii) Classification (1 mark):**

Since **α > 70°** (in fact ~120°), the motor **qualifies as a high-starting-torque capacitor-start motor**, with $T_{st}/T_{fl} \approx 3.5$ to 4.5. ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 1–3 marks here):**
> 1. **Forgetting to take the magnitude** of complex impedance before computing current. Many students write $I = V/Z$ with $Z$ as a complex number — *always* compute $\vert Z \vert$ first.
> 2. **Sign error on capacitor reactance:** Use $X_C$ as a **negative imaginary** part ($+jX_L - jX_C$). The auxiliary branch in a capacitor-start motor is **net capacitive**, so I_a *leads* V.
> 3. **Mixing up the phase angle formula:** It is $\alpha = \phi_m - \phi_a$ where both are angles relative to V (with proper sign). Don't just subtract magnitudes of reactance ratios.
> 4. **Saying "split-phase has high starting torque":** Wrong — split-phase has only **1.5–2×** starting torque. The capacitor-start has **3.0–4.5×**.
> 5. **Forgetting the centrifugal switch:** Always mention it in construction/working answers. KTU examiners specifically allocate marks for it.
> 6. **Plotting the phasor with both currents lagging V:** This is the *split-phase* case. For *capacitor-start*, one current must *lead* V — this is the entire point of adding the capacitor.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print this before exam!)**

- [ ] A **single-phase induction motor is NOT self-starting** — net starting torque = 0 due to equal and opposite counter-rotating fields (Double Revolving Field).
- [ ] **Split-phase motor** uses a resistive auxiliary winding → phase shift α ≈ **25°–30°** → starting torque = **1.5–2 × full-load torque**.
- [ ] **Capacitor-start motor** uses a capacitive auxiliary branch → phase shift α ≈ **80°–90°** → starting torque = **3.0–4.5 × full-load torque**.
- [ ] **Centrifugal switch** disconnects the auxiliary winding (and capacitor) at **~75% of synchronous speed** to prevent overheating.
- [ ] Main winding: **low R, high L** (thick wire, deep in slots). Auxiliary winding: **high R, low L** (thin wire, near slot opening) — for split-phase. For capacitor-start, the *capacitor provides the phase lead*, so the auxiliary winding can be similar in design to the main winding.
- [ ] Direction of rotation can be reversed by **interchanging the auxiliary winding leads** (not the main winding).
- [ ] Synchronous speed: $N_s = 120f/P$. For **f = 50 Hz, 4-pole**: $N_s = 1500$ RPM.
- [ ] **Capacitor-start motors use AC electrolytic capacitors** — these are intermittent duty, not continuous. This is why the centrifugal switch is mandatory.
- [ ] Applications: Split-phase → fans, small blowers, washing machines. Capacitor-start → compressors, conveyors, pumps, refrigerators, machine tools.
- [ ] **KTU favorite question patterns:** (1) Why is single-phase motor not self-starting? (2) Compare split-phase vs capacitor-start. (3) Numerical on impedance and phase angle calculation. (4) Role of centrifugal switch.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Alternators - Working
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST521 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Alternators - Working

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ALTERNATOR (SYNCHRONOUS GENERATOR):** A rotating machine that converts **mechanical energy** into **electrical energy** in the form of an alternating EMF, operating at a **constant frequency** that is **synchronously locked** to the mechanical speed of rotation. The output frequency $f$ is governed by the strict relation $f = \frac{P \cdot N}{120}$, where $P$ is the number of poles and $N$ is the rotor speed in RPM.

In a three-phase alternator (the workhorse of modern power generation), three identical windings are physically displaced by **120 electrical degrees** in space on the stator. When the rotor (which carries the DC-excited field winding) is driven by a prime mover (steam turbine, hydro turbine, or diesel engine), a sinusoidal EMF is induced in each stator phase, yielding a balanced three-phase supply.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The term "Alternator" and "Synchronous Generator" are **synonymous** in KTU board examinations. The course code PENST521 (Electrical Technology) typically treats this as a **non-mathematical descriptive topic** in lower-semester modules, focusing on construction, working, EMF equation, and applications. Numerical problems are usually confined to EMF calculation, frequency calculation, and basic voltage regulation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bicycle Dynamo" Principle

Imagine a **bicycle dynamo** — that small cylindrical device that rubs against your bicycle wheel and powers the headlamp. As the wheel turns, a magnet inside the dynamo rotates past a fixed coil of wire. The changing magnetic flux through the coil pushes electrons in the wire, generating a small AC voltage that lights your lamp.

Now **scale this up a million times** for a power-station alternator:

| Component | Bicycle Dynamo | Power-Station Alternator |
|---|---|---|
| **Rotating Magnet** | Tiny permanent magnet | Large electromagnet (field winding) fed with DC |
| **Stationary Coil** | Few turns of thin wire | Heavy, multi-turn, insulated copper windings in slots |
| **Mechanical Input** | Your bicycle wheel | Steam / hydro / diesel turbine |
| **Output** | ~6 V AC for a lamp | **6.6 kV / 11 kV / 33 kV** for the grid |

The **fundamental physics is identical** — **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**. The only difference is the **scale, the use of electromagnets** (not permanent magnets, since we need a strong, controllable field), and the **water-cooling** or **hydrogen-cooling** systems needed to dissipate the massive I²R and windage losses.

## 1.3 Why "Synchronous"?

The alternator is called a **synchronous machine** because it operates at one **specific speed** (the synchronous speed) for a given grid frequency and pole count. Unlike an induction motor, there is **no relative slip** between the rotor magnetic field and the stator rotating field during steady-state operation — they rotate together, in perfect lockstep, as if locked by an invisible gear.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** In an alternator, the rotor field and the stator-generated field rotate at **identical angular velocities**. This synchronism is the defining feature of the machine and the reason it is used exclusively for **bulk power generation**, where grid stability demands exact frequency control.

## 1.4 The Two Main Constructional Families

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Favourite:** Examiners almost always ask a comparison question between **Salient Pole** and **Cylindrical (Non-Salient) Pole** rotor construction. Memorize this table thoroughly.

- **Salient Pole Rotor (Projecting Poles):** Poles physically project outward from the rotor surface. Used for **low-to-medium speed** machines (typically 100–1500 RPM), driven by **hydro turbines** or **diesel engines**. Mechanical construction is robust but windage losses are high.
- **Cylindrical Rotor (Smooth / Non-Salient Pole):** Rotor is a solid, smooth steel cylinder with slots milled into it for the field winding. Used for **high-speed** machines (1500 / 3000 RPM at 50 Hz), driven by **steam turbines**. Mechanically strong, low windage loss, and capable of withstanding high centrifugal stresses.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Step-by-Step "Why & How" of Alternator Working

The working of an alternator can be understood as a clean, four-stage logical chain:

### Stage 1: Excitation of the Rotor Field Winding
- A separate **DC source** (called the **exciter**) feeds the rotor field winding.
- This DC current produces a **stationary magnetic field** in the rotor body.
- The strength of this field is controlled by adjusting the **field current** — this is the primary means of controlling the **terminal voltage** of the alternator.

### Stage 2: Mechanical Rotation by the Prime Mover
- The **prime mover** (turbine or engine) rotates the rotor at a fixed mechanical speed $N$.
- The rotor's magnetic field now sweeps through space at angular velocity $\omega_m = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$ rad/s.

### Stage 3: Flux Linkage with the Stator Windings
- The stator houses a **three-phase winding** distributed in the slots of a laminated silicon-steel core.
- As the rotor field sweeps past each stator conductor, the **flux linkage $\lambda$** through that conductor varies sinusoidally with the mechanical angle $\theta$.

### Stage 4: EMF Induction by Faraday's Law
- By **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**:
$$e = -\frac{d\lambda}{dt} = -\frac{d\lambda}{d\theta} \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt}$$
- Since the flux distribution in the air gap is designed to be **sinusoidal** (using distributed and short-pitched windings), the induced EMF is also **sinusoidal**, with RMS value given by the classic formula derived in Section 3.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving any KTU alternator numerical. Reproduce this in your exam-hall mind-map.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Glossary | Boundary / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Synchronous Speed** | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ | $N_s$ in RPM, $f$ in Hz, $P$ = total poles | Always an integer multiple of 60/P |
| 2 | **Electrical Frequency** | $f = \dfrac{P \, N}{120}$ | $N$ in RPM | Hz |
| 3 | **Mechanical to Electrical Angle** | $\theta_e = \dfrac{P}{2} \, \theta_m$ | $\theta_e$ = electrical, $\theta_m$ = mechanical | radians |
| 4 | **EMF per Phase (RMS)** | $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi \, N_{ph} \, K_w$ | $\Phi$ = flux/pole in Wb, $N_{ph}$ = turns/phase | Volts |
| 5 | **Winding Factor** | $K_w = K_p \times K_d$ | $K_p$ = pitch, $K_d$ = distribution factor | Dimensionless, $< 1$ |
| 6 | **Pitch Factor** | $K_p = \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right)$ | $\alpha$ = chording (short-pitch) angle in electrical degrees | $0 \le \alpha \le 180°$ |
| 7 | **Distribution Factor** | $K_d = \dfrac{\sin(m\beta/2)}{m \sin(\beta/2)}$ | $m$ = slots/pole/phase, $\beta$ = slot angle | $m \le 1$ always |
| 8 | **Line EMF (Star)** | $E_L = \sqrt{3} \, E_{ph}$ | Star-connected stator | Volts |
| 9 | **Line EMF (Delta)** | $E_L = E_{ph}$ | Delta-connected stator | Volts |
| 10 | **Voltage Regulation** | $\text{VR} = \dfrac{E_0 - V}{V} \times 100\%$ | $E_0$ = no-load EMF, $V$ = full-load terminal V | % |
| 11 | **Synchronous Impedance** | $Z_s = \sqrt{R_a^2 + X_s^2}$ | $R_a$ = armature resistance, $X_s$ = synchronous reactance | Ohms / phase |
| 12 | **Power Developed (3-phase)** | $P = 3 \, V_{ph} \, I_{ph} \cos\phi$ | $\cos\phi$ = load power factor | Watts |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Alternators are the **backbone of every power grid on Earth**. Understanding their working is not just academic — it is essential for:

- **Power Station Design** — Choosing the right alternator type (salient vs. cylindrical) for the prime mover.
- **Grid Synchronization** — Before connecting an alternator to the live grid, three conditions must be satisfied: (i) same **voltage magnitude**, (ii) same **frequency**, (iii) same **phase sequence** and phase angle. This is the famous **"Synchroscope" or "Dark Lamp / Bright Lamp" synchronization procedure** tested in KTU labs.
- **Reactive Power Control** — Unlike induction generators, alternators can be **over-excited or under-excited** to absorb or supply reactive power (VAr), making them vital for grid voltage stability.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The EMF equation derivation is the **single most-asked 14-mark derivation** in PENST521 university examinations. You will be awarded full marks only if you show **every** algebraic step. The following derivation is exhaustive and matches the **board valuation key exactly**.

## 3.1 Derivation of the EMF Equation of an Alternator

### Given / Assumed:
- $P$ = total number of rotor poles
- $f$ = supply frequency (Hz)
- $\Phi$ = useful flux per pole (Wb)
- $N_{ph}$ = total number of **turns** per phase on the stator
- $K_w$ = winding factor (= $K_p \times K_d$), accounting for the non-ideal winding distribution

### Goal:
Derive the RMS value of the EMF induced per phase, $E_{ph}$.

---

### Step 1: Define Flux Linkage
Assume the flux $\Phi$ per pole links each stator phase **once per pole-pair traversal**. The total flux cut by a single conductor per complete revolution is:
$$\text{Flux cut per conductor per revolution} = P \cdot \Phi$$

---

### Step 2: Average EMF Induced
By **Faraday's Law**, the **average EMF** induced in a single conductor over one full cycle is given by:
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{avg,conductor}} &= \frac{\text{Flux cut per cycle}}{\text{Time for one cycle}} \\
&= \frac{P \cdot \Phi}{T} = P \cdot \Phi \cdot f
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Logic:** We used $1/T = f$ since frequency is the reciprocal of the time period. The average EMF accounts for the entire flux $\Phi$ being cut $P/2$ times (once per pole pair), but we keep it as $P \cdot \Phi$ per cycle for the full flux linkage.

---

### Step 3: From Average to RMS Using the Form Factor
For a **pure sinusoidal waveform**, the **Form Factor** is:
$$
k_f = \frac{E_{\text{rms}}}{E_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} \approx 1.11
$$

Therefore, the **RMS EMF per conductor** becomes:
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{rms,conductor}} &= k_f \cdot E_{\text{avg,conductor}} \\
&= 1.11 \cdot P \cdot \Phi \cdot f \\
&= \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} \cdot P \cdot \Phi \cdot f
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Logic:** Multiplying the average value by the sinusoidal form factor $\pi / (2\sqrt{2})$ converts the average to RMS, which is what voltmeters and grid regulations report.

---

### Step 4: Account for Series Turns
If there are $N_{ph}$ turns per phase connected in **series**, and assuming **full-pitched, concentrated windings** (ideal case), the total RMS EMF per phase is:
$$
E_{\text{ph,ideal}} = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} \cdot P \cdot \Phi \cdot f \cdot N_{ph}
$$

**Textual Logic:** Induced EMFs in series turns **add algebraically** only if every turn cuts the flux at the same instant. We relax this idealization in the next step.

---

### Step 5: Introduce the Winding Factor $K_w$
In **practical alternators**, the stator winding is:
- **Distributed** across multiple slots per pole per phase (not concentrated in one slot).
- **Short-pitched** (chorded) to suppress harmonics.

These two design choices cause the **phasor sum of individual coil EMFs to be less than their arithmetic sum**. The reduction is captured by the **winding factor** $K_w = K_p \cdot K_d$ (always $< 1$).

Multiplying through:
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{ph} &= \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} \cdot P \cdot \Phi \cdot f \cdot N_{ph} \cdot K_w
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 6: Final Simplification
Since $\frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} = 2.22 \approx 4.44 / 2$, we combine with the relation $P \cdot f = \frac{P \cdot N}{120} \cdot \frac{120}{1}$:

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Canonical Form (KTU Board Standard):**
> $$\boxed{\,E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi \, N_{ph} \, K_w\,}$$
> $$\boxed{\,E_{\text{line,star}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot E_{ph}\,}$$

**Textual Logic:** The constant **4.44** is the universal EMF constant in AC machine theory. It is nothing but $\frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} \cdot 2$ — a historical constant that appears in every transformer and alternator EMF equation.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical (KTU Style)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A 3-phase, 16-pole alternator has a star-connected stator with **180 turns per phase**, **flux per pole = 0.05 Wb**, **frequency = 50 Hz**, **pitch factor = 0.96**, and **distribution factor = 0.97**. Find the line EMF.

### Step-by-Step Model Solution

**Step 1 — Compute Winding Factor:**
$$
K_w = K_p \cdot K_d = 0.96 \times 0.97 = 0.9312
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Phase EMF:**
$$
E_{ph} = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot \Phi \cdot N_{ph} \cdot K_w = 4.44 \times 50 \times 0.05 \times 180 \times 0.9312
$$
$$
E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 50 \times 0.05 \times 180 \times 0.9312 = 1861.2 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Line EMF (Star):**
$$
E_L = \sqrt{3} \times E_{ph} = 1.732 \times 1861.2 = 3223.6 \text{ V} \approx 3.22 \text{ kV}
$$

**Step 4 — Cross-Verification of Synchronous Speed:**
$$
N_s = \frac{120 \cdot f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{16} = 375 \text{ RPM}
$$
(Used for hydro-alternator context, not required for EMF computation, but **always include** in KTU board answers to demonstrate conceptual understanding.)

---

## 3.3 The Phasor Diagram of a Cylindrical-Rotor Alternator

For a loaded alternator, the relationship between **induced EMF $E_0$**, **terminal voltage $V$**, **armature current $I_a$**, and **synchronous impedance $Z_s = R_a + jX_s$** is:
$$
\boxed{\,E_0 = V + I_a (R_a + jX_s)\,}$$

The phasor diagram (drawn with $V$ as reference) is:
- $V$ is the reference phasor along the horizontal axis.
- $I_a$ is drawn at an angle $\phi$ below $V$ (lagging load).
- $I_a R_a$ is drawn parallel to $I_a$ (in phase with it).
- $I_a X_s$ is drawn perpendicular to $I_a$ (leading by 90°).
- $E_0$ is the phasor sum, leading $V$ by an angle $\delta$ called the **load angle** or **power angle**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **power angle $\delta$** determines how much **real power** the alternator delivers to the grid: $P = \dfrac{3 \, V \, E_0}{X_s} \sin\delta$. Maximum power transfer occurs at $\delta = 90°$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Alternator Working — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PM[PRIME MOVER\nSteam Hydro or Diesel Turbine] -->|Mechanical Torque| ROT[ROTOR ASSEMBLY\nField Winding on Shaft\nCarries DC Excited Field]
    EXC[EXCITER\nDC Source or AC Exciter\nwith Rectifier] -->|DC Field Current| ROT
    ROT -->|Rotating Magnetic Flux\nCuts Stator Conductors| STAT[STATOR ASSEMBLY\n3 Phase Distributed\nWinding in Laminated Core]
    STAT -->|Induced Sinusoidal EMF| OUT[OUTPUT TERMINALS\n3 Phase AC at\nFrequency f = PN by 120]
    OUT -->|Supplies| LOAD[LOAD or GRID\nIndustrial Motor\nDomestic Consumers]
    LOAD -->|Feedback Voltage and Current| AVR[AVR Automatic Voltage Regulator]
    AVR -->|Adjusts Field Current| EXC
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**
- The **Prime Mover** supplies mechanical power.
- The **Exciter** supplies DC to the rotor field winding (the "magnet" of the machine).
- The **Rotor** rotates this magnetic field past the stator.
- The **Stator** converts the changing flux into three-phase AC EMF (Faraday's Law).
- The **AVR (Automatic Voltage Regulator)** closes the feedback loop — if load draws more current, terminal voltage drops, AVR senses this and increases field current to restore the voltage.

## 4.2 Stator Construction Cross-Section View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STATOR_CORE[STATOR CORE LAMINATED SILICON STEEL]
        S1[Slot 1 Phase A]
        S2[Slot 2 Phase B]
        S3[Slot 3 Phase C]
        S4[Slot 4 Phase A Return]
        S5[Slot 5 Phase B Return]
        S6[Slot 6 Phase C Return]
    end
    STATOR_CORE --> AIRGAP[Cylindrical Air Gap]
    AIRGAP --> ROTOR_BODY[ROTOREither Salient Pole Projecting or Smooth Cylindrical]
    ROTOR_BODY --> FIELD_COIL[DC Field Winding on Pole Shoes]
```

**Interpretation:** The stator has a **distributed, double-layer winding** — each phase has conductors in slots separated by exactly 60° electrical in a 3-phase system (since 180°/3 = 60° between phases). The rotor is the rotating part with a uniform air gap (cylindrical) or a non-uniform air gap (salient pole).

## 4.3 Excitation System Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SHAFT[Alternator Shaft Coupled to Prime Mover] --> PMG[Permanent Magnet Generator PMG]
    PMG -->|3 Phase AC| REC1[Rotating Rectifier Bridge]
    REC1 -->|DC| MAIN[Main Alternator Field Winding on Rotor]
    SHAFT -->|Also drives| EXC2[AC Exciter Stator Winding]
    EXC2 -->|Induced AC in Exciter Rotor| REC2[Stationary Rectifier]
    REC2 -->|DC to Slip Rings| MAIN
```

**Engineering Insight:** Modern large alternators (e.g., 500 MW turbine generators) use **brushless excitation** — the AC exciter is on the same shaft, and rotating diodes rectify its output, eliminating slip rings and brushes. This increases reliability and reduces maintenance.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following question bank is calibrated to the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Evaluation)** pattern: **Part A (3 marks × 2 = 6 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks × 1 with internal choice)**. Mark allocations, language style, and cognitive levels (Bloom's L1–L4) match the actual KTU university question papers.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Explain why the rotor of an alternator is excited by DC and not by AC.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. The rotor must produce a **stationary (in space, rotating with the rotor) magnetic field** of **constant magnitude** so that the flux cut by the stator conductors is a clean sinusoid. (1 mark)
2. If the rotor were excited by AC, the rotor field itself would alternate in magnitude, causing **double-frequency pulsations** in the induced stator EMF and severe **torque ripples**. (1 mark)
3. DC excitation provides a **controllable, steady magnetic field** whose strength can be varied via the field current to control the terminal voltage — a fundamental requirement of all power-grid alternators. (1 mark)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Define the term "winding factor" of an alternator. Why is it always less than unity?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. The **winding factor $K_w$** is defined as the ratio of the **phasor sum** of EMFs induced in the actual distributed winding to the **arithmetic sum** of EMFs that would be induced if all turns of the winding were concentrated in a single slot. (1.5 marks)
2. $K_w = K_p \times K_d$, where $K_p$ is the pitch factor and $K_d$ is the distribution factor. (0.5 mark)
3. In any real winding, the EMFs in adjacent slots are **slightly phase-shifted** from each other (distribution effect) and **chorded** coils do not embrace the full pitch (pitch effect). This **phasor addition always yields a sum less than the arithmetic sum**, hence $K_w \le 1$. (1 mark)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme provides an **internal choice** within each module. You may attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B below. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **working principle of a three-phase synchronous generator (alternator)**. Clearly state **Faraday's Law** and identify the role of the **prime mover**, **exciter**, **field winding**, and **armature winding**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **EMF equation of an alternator**, defining every symbol used. A 3-phase, **Y-connected**, **1500 RPM**, **50 Hz**, **4-pole** alternator has **240 turns per phase**, a flux per pole of **0.08 Wb**, and a winding factor of **0.96**. Calculate the **induced EMF per phase** and the **line voltage**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Working Principle (7 Marks):**
- **Faraday's Law Statement:** "The EMF induced in a coil is equal to the negative rate of change of magnetic flux linkage with respect to time," expressed as $e = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$. (1 mark)
- **Prime Mover Role:** Supplies **mechanical input power** to rotate the rotor at synchronous speed $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$. (1 mark)
- **Exciter Role:** Provides **DC current** to the rotor field winding to set up the **working magnetic flux**. (1 mark)
- **Field Winding (Rotor):** Produces a **rotating magnetic field** of strength proportional to the field current. (1 mark)
- **Armature Winding (Stator):** Three-phase windings cut by the rotating flux; **sinusoidal EMFs** are induced, displaced by 120° in time. (1.5 marks)
- **Block Diagram:** (Mandatory — see Section 4.1 of these notes) Draw a clean labelled diagram showing Prime Mover → Rotor → Stator → Load, with the Exciter feeding the rotor. (1.5 marks)

**(b) EMF Equation Derivation & Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Derivation (4 Marks):** Follow the six-step derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes. Conclude with the final formula:
$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi \, N_{ph} \, K_w$$

**Numerical (3 Marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{ph} &= 4.44 \times 50 \times 0.08 \times 240 \times 0.96 \\
&= 4.44 \times 50 \times 0.08 \times 240 \times 0.96 \\
&= 4.44 \times 50 = 222 \\
&\quad 222 \times 0.08 = 17.76 \\
&\quad 17.76 \times 240 = 4262.4 \\
&\quad 4262.4 \times 0.96 = 4091.9 \text{ V} \approx \mathbf{4092 \text{ V}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Line Voltage:**
$$
E_L = \sqrt{3} \times E_{ph} = 1.732 \times 4092 = \mathbf{7087.3 \text{ V} \approx 7.09 \text{ kV}}
$$

**Verification of Synchronous Speed:**
$$
N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ RPM} \quad \checkmark
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Compare the **salient pole** and **cylindrical rotor** constructions of an alternator. State **two examples of prime movers** for each type and justify your choice. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **concept of voltage regulation** of an alternator. A 3-phase, star-connected alternator delivers **1500 A** at **6.6 kV (line)** at a **0.8 lagging power factor**. The armature resistance per phase is **0.05 Ω** and the synchronous reactance per phase is **0.6 Ω**. Calculate the **no-load EMF per phase** and the **percentage voltage regulation**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison (7 Marks):**

| Feature | Salient Pole | Cylindrical Rotor |
|---|---|---|
| Construction | Projecting poles, laminated pole shoes | Smooth cylindrical steel rotor with milled slots |
| Speed | Low (100–500 RPM) | High (1500, 3000 RPM) |
| Prime Mover | Hydro turbine, diesel engine | Steam turbine, gas turbine |
| Mechanical Strength | Lower (projecting poles limit speed) | Very high (withstands centrifugal stress) |
| Diameter vs Length | Large diameter, short axial length | Small diameter, long axial length |
| Field Winding | Concentrated around pole bodies | Embedded in slots (distributed) |
| Cooling | Air-cooled (open structure) | Hydrogen / water-cooled (sealed) |
| Typical Use | Hydro-electric power stations | Thermal & nuclear power stations |

- Salient pole **prime movers** (1 mark each): Pelton / Francis / Kaplan hydro turbines, large diesel engines.
- Cylindrical rotor **prime movers** (1 mark each): Steam turbines, gas turbines — they require high RPM for thermodynamic efficiency.
- **Justification:** (2 marks) Salient pole construction cannot withstand the high centrifugal forces at >1500 RPM; cylindrical rotors are mechanically balanced for high-speed operation.

**(b) Voltage Regulation Concept & Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Concept (2 Marks):** Voltage regulation is the **change in terminal voltage** when the full load is removed (at constant speed and field current), expressed as a **percentage of the full-load terminal voltage**:
$$\text{VR\%} = \frac{E_0 - V}{V} \times 100$$
where $E_0$ is the no-load EMF (internal induced EMF at no load) and $V$ is the full-load terminal voltage.

**Numerical (5 Marks):**

Given:
- Line voltage $V_L = 6600$ V → $V_{ph} = \dfrac{6600}{\sqrt{3}} = 3810.5$ V
- Line current $I_L = 1500$ A → $I_{ph} = 1500$ A (star)
- $\cos\phi = 0.8$ lagging → $\sin\phi = 0.6$
- $R_a = 0.05 \, \Omega/\text{phase}$
- $X_s = 0.6 \, \Omega/\text{phase}$

**Phasor calculation (taking $V_{ph}$ as reference):**
$$
E_0 = \sqrt{(V_{ph}\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V_{ph}\sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2}
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Real component: } & V_{ph}\cos\phi + I_a R_a = 3810.5 \times 0.8 + 1500 \times 0.05 \\
&= 3048.4 + 75 = 3123.4 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Imaginary component: } & V_{ph}\sin\phi + I_a X_s = 3810.5 \times 0.6 + 1500 \times 0.6 \\
&= 2286.3 + 900 = 3186.3 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_0 &= \sqrt{(3123.4)^2 + (3186.3)^2} = \sqrt{9755627 + 10152488} \\
&= \sqrt{19908115} \approx \mathbf{4461.8 \text{ V per phase}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Percentage Voltage Regulation:**
$$
\text{VR\%} = \frac{4461.8 - 3810.5}{3810.5} \times 100\% = \frac{651.3}{3810.5} \times 100\% \approx \mathbf{17.09\%}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert line values to phase values** before applying $E_0 = V + I_a Z_s$. The phasor equation is **always per phase**. Converting 6600 V line to 3810 V phase is a mandatory first step. (-2 marks if missed.)
> 2. **Sign error on $I_a X_s$:** For **lagging** loads, $I_a X_s$ adds to the reactive drop. For **leading** loads, it subtracts. Always draw the phasor diagram and check the direction.
> 3. **Mixing up $E_0$ and $V$:** $E_0$ is the **internal generated EMF** (what would be measured at the terminals if the load were disconnected), and $V$ is the **actual terminal voltage under load**. The regulation formula subtracts the loaded from the no-load.
> 4. **Ignoring $R_a$:** Some students drop $R_a$ because $X_s \gg R_a$ in large machines. In **KTU 14-mark numericals**, $R_a$ is given intentionally — **never neglect it**.
> 5. **Missing the unit of $E_0$:** Always state "$E_0 = 4462$ V **per phase**" and then compute line value separately if asked.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **The 12 Golden Points to Remember for Alternator — Working (KTU PENST521):**

1. An **alternator** is a **synchronous AC generator** that produces electrical energy from mechanical input. It is the workhorse of **all** commercial power generation.

2. The **frequency** of the induced EMF is **strictly determined** by $f = \dfrac{P \cdot N}{120}$. You cannot change $f$ without changing either the speed or the poles.

3. **Stator** = the stationary part carrying the **armature (output) winding** — typically a **3-phase, distributed, double-layer winding** in laminated silicon-steel slots.

4. **Rotor** = the rotating part carrying the **DC-excited field winding**. Two types: **salient pole** (low speed) and **cylindrical** (high speed).

5. The **working principle** is **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**: $e = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$. A rotating magnetic field cuts stationary conductors, inducing sinusoidal EMF.

6. The **prime mover** (turbine/engine) provides mechanical torque; the **exciter** (DC source or brushless AC exciter) provides field current; the **AVR** maintains constant terminal voltage.

7. The **EMF equation** is the most-asked derivation: $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi \, N_{ph} \, K_w$, where $K_w = K_p \times K_d$ is the winding factor.

8. **Pitch factor** $K_p = \cos(\alpha/2)$ accounts for short-pitched (chorded) coils — used to suppress harmonics. **Distribution factor** $K_d < 1$ accounts for the fact that winding is spread across multiple slots per pole per phase.

9. **Star-connected line EMF** $E_L = \sqrt{3} \, E_{ph}$; **delta-connected** $E_L = E_{ph}$. Star is the standard for high-voltage alternators.

10. **Synchronous impedance** $Z_s = R_a + jX_s$ causes a **voltage drop** under load. The phasor equation is $E_0 = V + I_a Z_s$, with $E_0$ leading $V$ by the **load angle $\delta$**.

11. **Voltage regulation** = $(E_0 - V)/V \times 100\%$. It is typically **positive** (voltage rises when load is removed) and increases for lower power factors.

12. **Real power** delivered: $P = \dfrac{3 \, V \, E_0}{X_s} \sin\delta$. The alternator can also supply/absorb **reactive power** based on field excitation (over-excited = supplies VAr, under-excited = absorbs VAr).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Construction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST521 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Construction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme** for **PENST521 - Electrical Technology and Instrumentation**, the term **"Construction"** in **Module 3** refers to the **physical and electromagnetic architecture of static and rotating electrical machines** — primarily the **DC Machine**, the **Single-Phase & Three-Phase Transformer**, and (at a foundational level) **AC Rotating Machines**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit):**
> *"Construction refers to the detailed physical arrangement of the principal parts of an electrical machine — the magnetic core (yoke, pole core, pole shoe), the armature/secondary winding, the commutator/brush assembly (for DC), the high-voltage (HV) and low-voltage (LV) windings (for transformers), the tank, conservator, bushings, and cooling arrangements — together with the choice of materials that ensure efficient electromechanical energy conversion within safe thermal and mechanical limits."*

### 1.2 The Two Broad Families Covered

| Family | Type | Construction Focus |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Static Machines** | Transformer | Core, windings, tank, bushings, cooling |
| **Rotating Machines** | DC Machine | Yoke, poles, armature, commutator, brushes |
| **Rotating Machines** | AC Machine (Intro) | Stator, rotor (squirrel-cage / slip-ring) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Water-Pump" Analogy for DC Machine Construction:**
> Imagine a **DC Machine** as a sophisticated **mechanical water-pump system**:
> - The **Yoke** is the **outer iron casing** of the pump — gives mechanical strength and completes the magnetic flux path (like a return pipe).
> - The **Pole Core & Pole Shoe** are the **valve seats** that shape and direct the magnetic field (water flow).
> - The **Field Winding** is the **solenoid coil** that magnetizes the poles — without it, there is no flux, just as without energizing a solenoid, there is no magnetic "push."
> - The **Armature Core** is the **rotor chamber** where energy conversion happens.
> - The **Armature Winding** is the set of **paddle-wheels** placed in the rotor chamber.
> - The **Commutator** acts as a **mechanical rectifier / rotating switch** — converting the AC generated inside the coils to DC at the terminals.
> - The **Brushes** are the **stationary electrical contacts** that ride on the commutator, just like **sliding contact shoes**.

For a **Transformer**, think of a **two-room house sharing a common wall**:
- The **Common Wall** is the **magnetic core** (shared magnetic path).
- **Room A** is the **Primary Winding** (input side).
- **Room B** is the **Secondary Winding** (output side).
- The **tank, oil, and radiator** are the **HVAC and insulation systems** of the house.

### 1.4 Core Materials Used (Highlighted)

- **Yoke / Core (Magnetic Path):** **Cast iron, Cast steel, or CRGO (Cold Rolled Grain Oriented) silicon steel laminations.** CRGO reduces **hysteresis loss** and **eddy current loss**.
- **Pole Core & Pole Shoe:** **Annealed steel / soft iron laminations** (Laminated to reduce eddy current losses).
- **Armature Core:** **Silicon steel laminations (0.35 mm to 0.5 mm thick)**, insulated from one another by a thin coating of **varnish or oxide layer**.
- **Conductors (Windings):** **Copper** (preferred, higher conductivity) or **Aluminum** (cheaper, lighter).
- **Insulation:** **Class A, B, F, or H** insulation (cotton, mica, glass fiber, epoxy resin) — see **[Table 1.1]**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Insulating Material Classes (IEC 60085):**
> - **Class A:** 105 °C (cotton, silk, paper in oil)
> - **Class B:** 130 °C (mica, fiberglass with binder)
> - **Class F:** 155 °C (mica, glass fiber, silicone resin)
> - **Class H:** 180 °C (silicone elastomer, mica)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Construction of a DC Machine — Component-by-Component

A **DC Machine** is built with two principal mechanical assemblies:
1. **The Stator (Stationary Part)**
2. **The Rotor / Armature (Rotating Part)**

#### 2.1.1 The Stator (Stationary Part)

The **stator** of a DC machine consists of the following components:

**(a) Yoke (Frame)**
- **Function:** Serves as the **outer mechanical frame** and provides a **return path for the magnetic flux**.
- **Material:**
  - *Smaller machines:* **Cast iron** (cheap, good castability, high permeability).
  - *Larger machines (>10 kW):* **Cast steel or fabricated rolled steel** (better mechanical strength, lower magnetic saturation).
  - *Modern machines:* **CRGO laminations** to reduce iron losses.
- **Shape:** **Cylindrical** (modern) or **octagonal** (older designs).
- **Key Feature:** Houses the **pole cores**, **pole shoes**, and provides mounting for **end shields / bearing housings**.

**(b) Pole Core and Pole Shoe**
- **Pole Core:** The **straight cylindrical portion** attached to the yoke. It carries the **field winding**.
- **Pole Shoe:** The **wide, flared end** of the pole (also called the *pole face*).
  - **Function:**
    1. **Spreads the magnetic flux uniformly** over the air-gap → ensures smooth flux distribution.
    2. **Supports the field winding** mechanically.
    3. **Reduces the reluctance** of the air-gap by increasing the cross-section at the air-gap.
- **Material:** **Laminated annealed steel** (thickness 0.5–1 mm) to **reduce eddy current losses** caused by the alternating flux (armature reaction pulsations).
- **Lamination:** Each lamination is **insulated by varnish or paper**.

**(c) Field Winding (Field Coils)**
- **Function:** When DC current flows through the field winding, it **produces the working magnetic flux** (Φ) in the machine.
- **Types of Field Winding (based on how it is connected):**
  1. **Separately Excited** — Field winding supplied from an independent DC source.
  2. **Self-Excited:**
     - **Shunt Winding** — Many turns of thin wire, connected in *parallel* with the armature. High resistance (200–400 Ω). Carries a small current (~1–5 % of rated armature current).
     - **Series Winding** — Few turns of thick wire, connected in *series* with the armature. Low resistance (~0.1–1 Ω). Carries full armature current.
     - **Compound Winding** — Combination of shunt + series on each pole (cumulative or differential).

**(d) Interpoles (Commutating Poles)**
- **Location:** Mounted on the yoke **between the main poles**, in the **inter-polar region**.
- **Function:** To **neutralize the reactance voltage** and **armature reaction flux** in the **commutating zone** (the region where a coil is undergoing commutation), thereby **preventing sparking at the brushes**.
- **Winding:** Carries the **armature current** (connected in series with the armature) and is wound such that its polarity is the **same as the next main pole** ahead (in the direction of rotation).

**(e) Compensating Winding**
- **Location:** Embedded in **slots cut into the pole shoes** of the main poles.
- **Function:** **Neutralizes the cross-magnetizing effect of armature reaction** under heavy overloads, especially in machines subjected to sudden load changes.
- **Application:** Used in **large DC motors** (rolling mills, traction, printing presses) and in **DC generators** feeding highly variable loads.

#### 2.1.2 The Rotor (Armature)

The **armature** is the rotating part of a DC machine.

**(a) Armature Core**
- **Function:** Houses the **armature conductors** in its slots and provides a **low-reluctance magnetic path** between the poles.
- **Material:** **Laminated silicon steel (0.35 mm thickness)**, each lamination coated with **varnish** to reduce **eddy current losses**.
- **Key Design Features:**
  - **Slots:** Either **open slots, semi-closed slots, or closed slots** — most common is **open slots with wedges** to retain the windings.
  - **Ventilation Ducts:** Radial ducts are provided for **air circulation** to remove heat.
  - **Spider / Armature Hub:** A spoked structure that supports the laminated core on the shaft.

**(b) Armature Winding**
- **Function:** The **working winding** where EMF is induced (generator) or where current interacts with flux to produce torque (motor).
- **Two Fundamental Types:**
  1. **Lap Winding** — Used in **high-current, low-voltage** machines. Number of parallel paths $A = P$ (poles).
  2. **Wave Winding** — Used in **low-current, high-voltage** machines. Number of parallel paths $A = 2$ (independent of pole number).
- **Conductor Material:** **Copper** (most common), insulated with **enamel, cotton, mica, or glass fiber** depending on insulation class.
- **Retention:** Coils are held in slots by **fibre wedges**.

**(c) Commutator**
- **The Heart of a DC Machine.** Without the commutator, a DC machine would be an AC machine.
- **Construction:** A **cylindrical assembly of hard-drawn copper segments** (each segment insulated from the next by **mica strips** of thickness 0.5–1 mm).
- **Each commutator segment** is connected to one or more **armature coil ends** via **risers**.
- **Mechanical Features:** Held together by a **V-shaped clamping ring (V-ring)**, insulated from the segments by **mica V-rings**, and tightened by a **lock nut**.

**(d) Brushes and Brush Gear**
- **Function:** Provide a **sliding electrical contact** between the stationary external circuit and the **rotating commutator**.
- **Material:** **Carbon, Graphite, Electrographite, or Copper-Graphite** (composition chosen based on current density and voltage).
- **Brush Holder:** Maintains the brush at a **constant pressure** (typically 1.5–2.5 N/cm²) on the commutator surface.
- **Brush Position:** Placed in the **magnetic neutral axis (MNA)** — the point on the commutator where the **commutating poles (interpoles)** are located.

**(e) Shaft, Bearings, and End Shields**
- **Shaft:** Made of **high-grade steel** (e.g., EN8, EN9) to transmit mechanical torque and support the rotor.
- **Bearings:** Either **ball bearings** (small/medium machines) or **roller bearings** (heavy machines).
- **End Shields:** Cast-iron or cast-steel covers that house the bearings and protect the rotating parts.

---

### 2.2 Construction of a Single-Phase / Three-Phase Transformer

A **Transformer** is a **static electromagnetic device** that transfers electrical energy between two or more circuits through **mutual electromagnetic induction** without changing the frequency.

#### 2.2.1 Major Constructional Parts

**(a) Magnetic Core**
- **Function:** Provides a **low-reluctance path** for the magnetic flux linking the primary and secondary windings.
- **Material:** **CRGO (Cold Rolled Grain-Oriented) silicon steel laminations**, thickness **0.27 mm, 0.30 mm, or 0.35 mm**, coated with a thin layer of **insulating varnish (Carlite coating)**.
- **Two Standard Constructions:**

| Construction | Description | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Core Type** | Windings surround a major part of the core. Single magnetic circuit. | Most power transformers |
| **Shell Type** | Core surrounds a major part of the windings. Double magnetic circuit. | Small distribution transformers, special applications |

**(b) Windings (HV and LV)**
- **LV (Low-Voltage) Winding:** Placed **closer to the core** (because it is easier to insulate the LV winding from the grounded core).
- **HV (High-Voltage) Winding:** Placed **outside the LV winding** (on top of it).
- **Two Winding Geometries:**

| Type | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Concentric (Cylindrical)** | HV and LV coils share the same axis, one inside the other. Most common. |
| **Sandwich (Pancake)** | HV and LV coils are interleaved as alternating discs. Used for high-current low-voltage applications. |

- **Conductor Material:** **Copper (preferred)** or **Aluminum (cheaper, lighter)**.
- **Insulation:** **Enamel**, **paper (kraft paper)**, **cotton tape**, **epoxy resin**, depending on voltage class and temperature rating.

**(c) Insulating Oil (Mineral Oil)**
- **Functions:**
  1. **Provides electrical insulation** between live parts and between windings and tank.
  2. **Cools the transformer** by circulating and transferring heat to the tank wall and radiators.
- **Properties Required:**
  - High **dielectric strength** (≥ 30 kV / 25 mm gap).
  - Low **viscosity** for good circulation.
  - High **flash point** (≥ 140 °C) for fire safety.
  - Chemically stable, non-corrosive to copper and steel.

**(d) Transformer Tank**
- **Function:** Encloses the **core-and-coil assembly** and holds the insulating oil.
- **Material:** **Mild steel plates** (welded construction), with **corrugated walls** (for small distribution transformers) or **radiators** (for larger power transformers) to enhance heat dissipation.
- **Accessories mounted on the tank:**
  - **Conservator** — An expansion tank that allows oil to expand/contract with temperature.
  - **Breather (Silica Gel Breather)** — Prevents moisture from entering the oil.
  - **Oil Level Gauge (Magnetic Type)**
  - **Buchholz Relay** — A gas-actuated protection relay mounted on the pipe between the main tank and the conservator.
  - **Thermometer / Temperature Indicator**
  - **Tap Changer (Off-Load / On-Load)**

**(e) Bushings**
- **Function:** Bring out the **HV and LV terminals** from the oil-filled tank to the external circuit, providing insulation against the high voltage.
- **Types:**
  - **Porcelain Bushings** (for outdoor use, oil-impregnated paper inside).
  - **Epoxy-Resin Bushings** (for indoor use, dry-type transformers).
  - **Oil-Filled Capacitive Bushings** (for very high voltages > 66 kV).

**(f) Cooling Arrangements (Standard Cooling Designations)**
| Designation | Cooling Type | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **ONAN** | Oil Natural Air Natural | Small/medium power transformers |
| **ONAF** | Oil Natural Air Forced | Large power transformers |
| **OFAF** | Oil Forced Air Forced | Very large power transformers |
| **OFWF** | Oil Forced Water Forced | Generator transformers in power plants |

---

### 2.3 Constructional Features of AC Machines (Brief)

> [!NOTE]
> Module 3 of PENST521 typically introduces the **construction of a three-phase induction motor** and a **synchronous machine** at a foundational level.

#### 2.3.1 Three-Phase Induction Motor
- **Stator:** Stator core built from **CRGO laminations**, slots housing a **three-phase distributed winding** (similar to a transformer LV winding) — produces a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)**.
- **Rotor:**
  - **Squirrel-Cage Rotor:** Solid laminated rotor with **copper or aluminum bars** short-circuited at both ends by **end rings**. No external electrical connection — extremely robust.
  - **Slip-Ring (Wound) Rotor:** Three-phase winding on the rotor, brought out through **slip rings and brushes** to external **rheostats** for speed/torque control.

#### 2.3.2 Synchronous Machine
- **Stator (Armature):** Similar construction to induction motor stator — three-phase winding embedded in slots of laminated core.
- **Rotor:** **Salient-pole rotor** (for low-speed machines, e.g., hydro generators) or **cylindrical / non-salient rotor** (for high-speed machines, e.g., turbo-generators). Rotor carries the **DC field winding**, excited via **slip rings**.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **directly relevant** to construction-based design and analysis questions in the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE. **No machine is "built" without these equations.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | EMF Equation (DC Generator) | $E_g \;=\; \dfrac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A}$ | $\Phi$ in Wb, $Z$ = total conductors, $N$ in rps, $P$ = poles, $A$ = parallel paths |
| 2 | EMF Equation (Transformer) | $E \;=\; 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N$ | $f$ in Hz, $\Phi_m$ = peak flux in Wb, $N$ = turns |
| 3 | Voltage Ratio (Transformer) | $\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} \;=\; \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} \;=\; \dfrac{I_2}{I_1}$ | Ideal transformer relation |
| 4 | Frequency of Induced EMF | $f \;=\; \dfrac{P \, N}{120}$ | $N$ in rpm (US notation) |
| 5 | Flux per Pole | $\Phi \;=\; B_{av} \cdot A_i \;=\; B_{av} \cdot \dfrac{\pi \, D \, L}{P}$ | $A_i$ = iron area at air gap |
| 6 | Synchronous Speed | $N_s \;=\; \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ | rpm |
| 7 | Slip of Induction Motor | $s \;=\; \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Per-unit (pu), dimensionless |
| 8 | Rotor Frequency | $f_r \;=\; s \, f$ | Hz |
| 9 | EMF in a Rotating Machine (General) | $E_{ph} \;=\; 4.44 \, k_w \, f \, \Phi_m \, T_{ph}$ | $k_w$ = winding factor, $T_{ph}$ = turns per phase |
| 10 | Power Developed (Mechanical) | $P_{dev} \;=\; E_b \cdot I_a$ | W (for DC motor) |
| 11 | Shaft Torque | $T_{sh} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{out} \cdot 60}{2\pi \, N}$ | N·m |
| 12 | Gross Torque | $T_g \;=\; \dfrac{E_b \, I_a}{2 \pi \, N \, / \, 60}$ | N·m |

> [!NOTE]
> The **conductor symbol $Z$**, **parallel path symbol $A$**, and **flux $\Phi$** are the **three most heavily marked variables** in any DC-machine construction problem on a KTU exam paper.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of Construction Knowledge

- **Transformer Design Engineers** use the EMF equation ($E = 4.44 f \Phi_m N$) to select core cross-section, number of turns, and conductor size.
- **DC Machine Manufacturers** use the EMF equation ($E_g = \Phi Z N P / 60A$) to size the armature, choose lap vs. wave winding, and select lamination thickness.
- **Maintenance Engineers** identify **commutator wear, brush grade degradation, and insulation class failures** using construction knowledge.
- **Power Utilities** rely on understanding **cooling designations (ONAN, ONAF, OFAF)** to operate transformers within safe thermal limits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: EMF Equation of a DC Generator

The EMF equation is the **single most important equation** in DC machine construction. It connects the **constructional parameters** ($\Phi, Z, P, A$) to the **electrical output** ($E_g$).

#### 3.1.1 Given Data (Standard Problem Statement)

> **Problem Setup:** A 4-pole DC generator with **simplex lap winding** has **300 armature conductors**. The flux per pole is **0.05 Wb**, and the armature is rotated at **1500 rpm**. Find the EMF generated.

#### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the constructional parameters:**

$$A = P \quad \text{(for simplex lap winding)}$$

$$A = 4 \quad \text{(parallel paths)}$$

**Step 2 — Convert rotational speed to revolutions per second (rps):**

$$N = \dfrac{1500}{60} \;=\; 25 \text{ rps}$$

**Step 3 — Time for one complete revolution:**

$$t = \dfrac{1}{N} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{25} \;=\; 0.04 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Flux cut by one conductor in one revolution:**

Each conductor, in one revolution, cuts a flux equal to:

$$d\Phi_{\text{per conductor}} = \Phi \cdot P$$

> *Reasoning:* A conductor in a 4-pole machine passes under **4 poles** in one full revolution, so it cuts the flux of all 4 poles in sequence.

$$d\Phi = 0.05 \times 4 = 0.20 \text{ Wb}$$

**Step 5 — Average EMF induced in one conductor (by Faraday's Law):**

$$e_{\text{avg per conductor}} = \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = \dfrac{0.20}{0.04} = 5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 6 — Total EMF per parallel path (A conductors in series per path):**

Number of conductors in series per parallel path:

$$Z_{\text{series}} = \dfrac{Z}{A} = \dfrac{300}{4} = 75$$

Therefore, EMF per parallel path:

$$E_g = e_{\text{avg per conductor}} \times Z_{\text{series}}$$

$$E_g = 5 \times 75 = 375 \text{ V}$$

**Step 7 — Verify using the consolidated EMF equation:**

$$E_g = \dfrac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A}$$

$$E_g = \dfrac{0.05 \times 300 \times 1500 \times 4}{60 \times 4}$$

$$E_g = \dfrac{90{,}000}{240} = 375 \text{ V}$$

> **The two methods agree → 375 V generated EMF.** ✅

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: EMF Equation of a Transformer

This equation is **the foundation of transformer design** — it determines the **number of turns** for a given core, or vice versa.

#### 3.2.1 Starting Point — Faraday's Law

$$e = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

#### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Express the time-varying flux:**

The flux in the core varies sinusoidally:

$$\Phi(t) = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate flux with respect to time:**

$$\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = \Phi_m \, \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Faraday's Law:**

$$e = -N \Phi_m \, \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

$$e = N \omega \Phi_m \sin\!\left(\omega t - \dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Determine the peak (maximum) EMF:**

$$\hat{E} = N \omega \Phi_m = N \cdot 2 \pi f \cdot \Phi_m$$

**Step 5 — Convert peak to RMS (divide by $\sqrt{2}$):**

$$E_{\text{rms}} = \dfrac{\hat{E}}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{2 \pi N f \Phi_m}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical simplification:**

$$\dfrac{2 \pi}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{2 \pi \cdot \sqrt{2}}{2} = \pi \sqrt{2} \approx 4.44$$

$$E = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N$$

$$\boxed{E = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N}$$

**Step 7 — Worked Numerical Example:**

> A single-phase transformer has **500 turns on the primary**, connected to a **50 Hz, 230 V** supply. Find the **peak flux $\Phi_m$** in the core.

$$E_1 = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N_1$$

$$230 = 4.44 \times 50 \times \Phi_m \times 500$$

$$230 = 111{,}000 \cdot \Phi_m$$

$$\Phi_m = \dfrac{230}{111{,}000} = 2.072 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Wb}$$

$$\boxed{\Phi_m \approx 2.07 \text{ mWb}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Synchronous Speed and Slip Relationship

**Step 1 — Synchronous speed (rotating magnetic field speed):**

$$N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$$

**Step 2 — Slip (per-unit relative speed):**

$$s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for rotor speed $N_r$:**

$$N_r = N_s (1 - s)$$

**Step 4 — Rotor EMF frequency (key for slip-ring rotor design):**

$$f_r = s \cdot f$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At **standstill** ($N_r = 0$), $s = 1$, so $f_r = f$ (transformer-like action). At **synchronous speed** ($N_r = N_s$), $s = 0$, so $f_r = 0$ (no induced EMF in the rotor). This explains why a **slip-ring induction motor cannot run at synchronous speed**.

**Step 5 — Numerical Example:**

> A 4-pole, 50 Hz, three-phase induction motor runs at **1440 rpm**. Calculate the slip.

$$N_s = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$

$$s = \dfrac{1500 - 1440}{1500} = \dfrac{60}{1500} = 0.04 \text{ pu (or 4\%)}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Material Selection Guide (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Component | Small Machine | Large Machine | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Yoke / Frame** | Cast iron | Cast steel or fabricated rolled steel | Larger machines need higher mechanical strength; lower saturation in cast steel |
| **Pole Core** | Solid annealed steel | Laminated silicon steel | Large machines: pulsating armature reaction flux → eddy currents → must laminate |
| **Armature Core** | Laminated Si-steel (0.5 mm) | Laminated Si-steel (0.35 mm) + radial vents | More armature reaction → thinner laminations + cooling |
