A typical ocean-going vessel may carry **3 to 4 identical diesel-generator (DG) sets**, each rated to meet the **full operational load** — that is, **(n−1) × P_rated ≥ P_peak**.

When the ship is under way at constant cruising speed, the **main propulsion shaft** is mechanically coupled (via a Power Take-Off / PTO) to a **shaft generator (SG)**. This unit recovers otherwise-wasted rotational energy, providing significant fuel savings of **3 % to 8 %** on transoceanic voyages.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why synchronous alternators and not induction generators on ships?** Because a ship's "grid" is a **standalone microgrid** (no infinite bus). Induction generators *demand* reactive power from the bus; when the grid is isolated, the bus has no other source of vars. Synchronous alternators, with their **DC field excitation**, can independently supply reactive power via **Automatic Voltage Regulators (AVR)**, maintaining voltage within **±2.5 %** of nominal.

### 2.1.2 Main Switchboard (MSB) Architecture

The **Main Switchboard** is the heart of the vessel's electrical nervous system. It is partitioned into **vertical panels**:

- **Generator panels** — incoming circuit breakers (ACB), reverse-power relays, synchronizing panel.
- **Bus-tie panel** — interconnects two halves of the split bus (e.g., MSB-1 ↔ MSB-2), allowing load sharing and transfer.
- **Feeder panels** — outgoing feeders to propulsion, bow thruster, auxiliaries.
- **Synchronizing panel** — synchroscope, lamps, and governor controls for paralleling alternators.

Two operational configurations are possible:

- **Split bus** (default in port / harbor): generators isolated, fault on one half cannot propagate to the other.
- **Closed bus** (underway / full operation): bus-tie closed, all generators paralleled, load shared.

## 2.2 Electric Propulsion Systems (Diesel-Electric / Gas Turbine-Electric)

In modern vessels (cruise ships, icebreakers, naval combatants, offshore supply vessels, drilling ships, submarines), mechanical drive via long propeller shaft is replaced by **electric propulsion**:

$$ \text{Prime Mover} \longrightarrow \text{Generator} \longrightarrow \text{Converter (VFD)} \longrightarrow \text{Propulsion Motor} \longrightarrow \text{Propeller} $$

This **diesel-electric (CODLAG / CODLAD)** or **gas-turbine-electric (COGAG)** arrangement offers:

- **Flexible engine layout** — prime movers can be placed anywhere in the hull, freeing the shaft-line.
- **Fuel efficiency** at part-load via running only the required number of generators.
- **Excellent low-speed manoeuvring** (azimuth thrusters, podded propulsion such as ABB's *Azipod*).
- **Reduced acoustic signature** — vital for naval vessels and research ships.

## 2.3 Shipyard Electrical Systems

The shipyard is fundamentally an **intermittent, high-impact, megawatt-scale industrial consumer**. Distinct subsystems include:

1. **Goliath / Slewing / Gantry Cranes** — powered by wound-rotor induction motors or VFD-fed squirrel-cage motors. Modern cranes use **SCR/IGBT VFDs** for precision load handling. A single goliath crane may have an **installed capacity of 1 MW to 5 MW** with peak demand much higher during acceleration.
2. **Welding Power Sources** — conventional transformer-rectifier (silicon-controlled-rectifier) or inverter-based (IGBT, 20 kHz switching) units. A modern shipyard may have **200+ welding stations** running simultaneously, demanding centralized compensation and harmonic filtering.
3. **Shore Supply (Cold-Ironing / AMP)** — shore-to-ship high-voltage (6.6 kV / 11 kV) supply so that diesel generators can be shut down in port, reducing emissions at berth.
4. **Dry-Dock and Berth Lighting** — high-IP (IP65+) floodlights on gantries.

## 2.4 Instrumentation Specific to Ships and Shipyards

The marine environment imposes unique instrumentation requirements. Critical sensor systems include:

- **Gyrocompass** — provides true-north reference; feeds autopilot, ARPA radar, ECDIS.
- **Echo Sounder** — measures depth below keel using 50 kHz / 200 kHz transducers.
- **Speed Log** — measures vessel speed through water (Pitot/static) or over ground (Doppler/EM).
- **RADAR / ARPA** — X-band (9.4 GHz) for high resolution, S-band (3 GHz) for long range & weather penetration.
- **AIS (Automatic Identification System)** — VHF-based transponder exchange of position/course with nearby vessels.
- **GPS / DGPS** — primary position-fixing.
- **ECDIS** — Electronic Chart Display & Information System, mandatory under SOLAS for new builds.
- **Tank Sounding Systems** — capacitive, ultrasonic, or radar-type level measurement for bunker, ballast, cargo tanks.
- **Engine Telegraph / Bridge Control** — order telegraph transferring RPM commands from bridge to ECR.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every equation you must commit to memory for the KTU 2024 ESE on this topic. **Vertical bars are written as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.**

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Three-phase real power | $P = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \cos\phi$ | W (or kW, MW) | $V_L$ = line voltage, $I_L$ = line current |
| 2 | Three-phase apparent power | $S = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L$ | VA | |
| 3 | Three-phase reactive power | $Q = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \sin\phi$ | VAR | |
| 4 | Power factor | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{P}{S}$ | dimensionless | Class societies typically mandate $\cos\phi \geq 0.8$ lagging |
| 5 | Generator redundancy (n−1) rule | $(n-1)\, P_{\text{rated}} \geq P_{\text{peak}}$ | W | Ensures one unit can fail without black-out |
| 6 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120\, f}{p}$ | rpm | $f$ = Hz, $p$ = number of poles |
| 7 | Shaft generator fuel saving | $\eta_{\text{sg}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{sg}}}{P_{\text{shaft,loss}}}$ | % | Typical 3 – 8 % |
| 8 | Voltage drop in feeder | $\Delta V = \sqrt{3}\, I\, L\, (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$ | V | L = length in km, R, X in $\Omega$/km |
| 9 | Battery capacity (Peukert-corrected) | $C_{\text{act}} = \dfrac{I^n\, t}{C_n^{\,n-1}}$ | Ah | $n \approx 1.1$ to $1.3$ for lead-acid |
| 10 | Emergency power duration (SOLAS) | $t_{\text{em}} \geq 18\text{ h}$ for cargo; 36 h for passenger | h | SOLAS Ch. II-1 Reg 43 |
| 11 | Crane acceleration power | $P_{\text{acc}} = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2 / t + m g v \sin\theta / \eta$ | W | Lifts heavy hull-blocks |
| 12 | Harmonic distortion limit (THD) | $THD_v \leq 5\,\%$, $THD_i \leq 8\,\%$ | % | IEEE 519 / IEC 60092-101 |
| 13 | Synchronizing condition | $V_{\text{gen}} = V_{\text{bus}},\ f_{\text{gen}} = f_{\text{bus}},\ \phi = 0$ | — | All three must be satisfied before closing ACB |
| 14 | Ship's load balance | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_{G_i} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} P_{L_j} + P_{\text{losses}}$ | W | Active power equilibrium |
| 15 | Reactive power equilibrium | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_{G_i} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} Q_{L_j}$ | VAR | Maintained by AVR |
| 16 | Synchronizing lamp (dark) condition | $f_{\text{gen}} = f_{\text{bus}}$ | Hz | Synchroscope needle at 12 o'clock |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding marine electrical systems is foundational for careers in:

- **Shipbuilding & Ship-Repair Yards** (Cochin Shipyard, Mazagon Dock, GRSE).
- **Classification Societies** (ABS, DNV, LR — surveyors and plan approval engineers).
- **Naval Architecture & Marine Design Consultancies**.
- **Port & Harbour Electrical Infrastructure** (shore-power projects).
- **Offshore Oil & Gas** (FPSO, drillship power management).
- **Maritime Regulatory Bodies** (DG Shipping, IMO).
- **Marine Equipment OEMs** (ABB Marine, Wärtsilä, Siemens Marine, GE Marine).

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Insight:** The "all-electric ship" concept (full electric propulsion + DC zonal distribution at ±10 kV MVDC) is the active R&D frontier. The US Navy's * Zumwalt*-class destroyer and the Royal Navy's *Queen Elizabeth*-class carriers are textbook case studies of full electric propulsion.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section contains the **worked numerical problems** that the KTU 2024 valuation key expects you to demonstrate under the **"Show full working"** clause. Skipping any step can attract a **−2 mark penalty** per intermediate logic gap.

---

## 3.1 Worked Problem 1 — Ship's Load Estimation & Generator Sizing (14-Mark Pattern)

> **Problem Statement:** A bulk carrier is to be designed with the following on-board loads. Estimate **(a)** the peak sustained load, **(b)** the required number and rating of diesel generator sets, and **(c)** the load on each generator during harbour (cold-ironing) mode, assuming an operating diversity factor of **0.7** and generator redundancy **(n−1)**.
>
> | Load | Installed (kW) | Number in use (sea) | Number in use (harbour) |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | Bow thruster | 750 | 1 | 0 |
> | Main cooling seawater pump | 90 | 2 | 1 |
> | Fuel-oil purifier | 55 | 2 | 0 |
> | HVAC (accommodation) | 180 | 1 | 1 |
> | Galley & laundry | 120 | 1 | 1 |
> | Steering gear | 60 | 2 | 0 |
> | Navigation & communication | 25 | 1 | 1 |
> | Deck machinery (cargo winches) | 440 | 4 | 0 |
> | Lighting | 80 | 1 | 1 |
> | Battery chargers | 30 | 1 | 1 |

### 3.1.1 Step A — Sea-going Peak Sustained Load

We sum, row by row, the installed power × number of units in use at sea:

$$ P_{\text{sea,installed}} = (750 \times 1) + (90 \times 2) + (55 \times 2) + (180 \times 1) + (120 \times 1) + (60 \times 2) + (25 \times 1) + (440 \times 4) + (80 \times 1) + (30 \times 1) $$

$$ P_{\text{sea,installed}} = 750 + 180 + 110 + 180 + 120 + 120 + 25 + 1760 + 80 + 30 $$

$$ P_{\text{sea,installed}} = 3355 \text{ kW} $$

Apply the **diversity factor (0.7)** to obtain the peak sustained demand:

$$ P_{\text{sea,peak}} = 0.7 \times 3355 = 2348.5 \text{ kW} $$

### 3.1.2 Step B — Number and Rating of Generator Sets

We need to satisfy the **(n − 1) redundancy rule** with **3 identical DG sets** (standard bulk-carrier practice). Each generator must be capable of carrying the entire peak load alone:

$$ (n-1) \times P_{\text{rated}} \geq P_{\text{sea,peak}} $$

$$ 2 \times P_{\text{rated}} \geq 2348.5 \text{ kW} $$

$$ P_{\text{rated}} \geq \dfrac{2348.5}{2} = 1174.25 \text{ kW} $$

A **standard size of 1250 kW** is selected (next-up rating). Thus:

$$ \boxed{3 \times 1250\text{ kW DG sets, each running at }\dfrac{2348.5}{3} = 782.83\text{ kW (62.6\% loading)}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 60–80 % loading?** Diesel engines are most efficient at **75 % to 85 % of MCR** (Maximum Continuous Rating). Sizing for 50–60 % average load ensures good specific fuel-oil consumption (SFOC ≈ 195 g/kWh) and reserves margin for transient loads like bow-thruster engagement.

### 3.1.3 Step C — Harbour (Cold-Ironing) Load

$$ P_{\text{harbour,installed}} = (90 \times 1) + (180 \times 1) + (120 \times 1) + (25 \times 1) + (80 \times 1) + (30 \times 1) = 525 \text{ kW} $$

$$ P_{\text{harbour,peak}} = 0.7 \times 525 = 367.5 \text{ kW} $$

In harbour, classification rules permit running **one DG set** if it is rated to cover all emergency + essential services:

$$ P_{\text{harbour,one DG}} = 367.5 \text{ kW } \ll 1250 \text{ kW rating (29.4\% loading)} $$

This is **permissible but inefficient** — modern vessels therefore use the **shore supply (cold-ironing)** to plug in, completely shutting down DGs and avoiding **unburnt fuel, wet-stacking, and harbour emissions**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Problem 2 — Voltage Drop in a Shipboard Feeder

> **Problem Statement:** A 440 V, 3-phase, 50 Hz feeder of length **80 m** supplies a 3-phase induction motor drawing **150 A** at **0.85 power factor lagging**. Conductor impedance is **R = 0.12 $\Omega$/km, X = 0.08 $\Omega$/km**. Calculate the percentage voltage drop. Is it within the **±2.5 %** class-society limit?

### 3.2.1 Step A — Compute R and X for the 80 m Run

$$ R_{\text{line}} = 0.12 \times \dfrac{80}{1000} = 0.0096 \,\Omega $$

$$ X_{\text{line}} = 0.08 \times \dfrac{80}{1000} = 0.0064 \,\Omega $$

### 3.2.2 Step B — Apply the Voltage-Drop Formula

$$ \Delta V = \sqrt{3}\, I\, (R_{\text{line}}\cos\phi + X_{\text{line}}\sin\phi) $$

We need $\sin\phi$ given $\cos\phi = 0.85$:

$$ \sin\phi = \sqrt{1 - 0.85^2} = \sqrt{1 - 0.7225} = \sqrt{0.2775} \approx 0.5268 $$

Substitute:

$$ \Delta V = \sqrt{3} \times 150 \times (0.0096 \times 0.85 + 0.0064 \times 0.5268) $$

$$ \Delta V = 1.732 \times 150 \times (0.00816 + 0.00337) $$

$$ \Delta V = 1.732 \times 150 \times 0.01153 $$

$$ \Delta V = 259.8 \times 0.01153 = 2.996 \text{ V} $$

### 3.2.3 Step C — Percentage Voltage Drop

$$ \%\Delta V = \dfrac{\Delta V}{V_L} \times 100 = \dfrac{2.996}{440} \times 100 = 0.681 \,\% $$

### 3.2.4 Step D — Compliance Check

$$ 0.681\,\% \;\ll\; 2.5\,\% \;\text{(class-society limit)} \;\;\therefore\;\; \text{Design is COMPLIANT} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always quote the **% drop in the final line** and explicitly state whether it satisfies the regulatory limit. Skipping this conclusion is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction in the KTU key.

---

## 3.3 Worked Problem 3 — Shaft-Generator Fuel Saving Calculation

> **Problem Statement:** A cargo ship's main engine delivers **9000 kW** at the shaft at a cruising speed of 18 knots. The shaft generator is rated at **600 kW** with an efficiency of **94 %**. Calculate the **net fuel saving in kg/h** if the SFOC of the auxiliary DG (replaced by shaft generator) is **210 g/kWh** and the additional specific fuel consumed by the main engine to drive the SG is **8 g/kWh**.

### 3.3.1 Step A — Fuel Saved by Stopping the Auxiliary DG

$$ m_{\text{fuel,aux,off}} = P_{\text{SG,out}} \times \text{SFOC}_{\text{aux}} = 600 \times 210 = 126{,}000 \text{ g/h} = 126 \text{ kg/h} $$

### 3.3.2 Step B — Additional Fuel Burned by the Main Engine

The mechanical power absorbed by the shaft generator (at the input shaft) is:

$$ P_{\text{SG,in}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{SG,out}}}{\eta_{\text{SG}}} = \dfrac{600}{0.94} = 638.30 \text{ kW} $$

$$ m_{\text{fuel,ME,extra}} = P_{\text{SG,in}} \times \Delta\text{SFOC} = 638.30 \times 8 = 5106.4 \text{ g/h} \approx 5.11 \text{ kg/h} $$

### 3.3.3 Step C — Net Fuel Saving

$$ \Delta m_{\text{fuel}} = 126 - 5.11 = 120.89 \text{ kg/h} $$

$$ \text{Percentage saving} = \dfrac{120.89}{126} \times 100 \approx 95.95\,\% \text{ of the auxiliary fuel that would have been burned} $$

This demonstrates the **overwhelming net benefit** of the shaft generator at cruise, where the main engine is anyway producing its rated power.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Marine Faults & Instrument Response

| # | Fault / Abnormal Condition | Detected By | Auto-Action | Manual Reset? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Reverse power** (moto-running) | Reverse-power relay (ANSI 32) | Trip generator ACB, trip shore-supply | Yes |
| 2 | **Loss of excitation (LOE)** | Under-excitation limiter (ANSI 40) | Reduce reactive load, alarm | No (auto restore) |
| 3 | **Over-current** | IDMTL relay (ANSI 51) | Trip after time delay | Yes |
| 4 | **Earth fault** (insulation drop) | Insulation monitoring relay | Audible alarm, non-tripping | No |
| 5 | **Under-voltage / loss of bus** | UV relay (ANSI 27) | Start emergency DG, shed non-essential loads | Yes |
| 6 | **Over-speed of prime mover** | Mechanical governor + electric overspeed trip | Fuel rack shut, ACB trip | Yes |
| 7 | **Fire in engine room** | Flame/heat/smoke detector | CO₂ / FM-200 flood, vent closure, fuel shut-off | Manual (after investigation) |
| 8 | **Bilge high level** | Float / capacitance sensor | Auto-start bilge pump, alarm | No |
| 9 | **Shaft bearing temperature** | RTD (Pt-100) | Slow-down, alarm at 75 °C, trip at 90 °C | Yes |
| 10 | **Black-out** | Master trip relay (ANSI 86) | Auto-start standby DG, restore via preferential-trip | Yes |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often write *"preferential trip"* as *"preference trip"*. The correct term is **preferential tripping** (also called **load shedding**), where the least-essential consumers (cabin fans, domestic equipment) are automatically disconnected to keep the bus alive.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All Mermaid diagrams below follow the **alphanumeric node-ID rule** (e.g., `gen1`, `bus2`, `propA`) and use **clean uppercase labels** without embedded markdown formatting.

## 4.1 Block Architecture of a Modern Diesel-Electric Vessel

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ME1[MAIN ENGINE 1] -->|PTO| SG1[SHAFT GEN 1]
    ME2[MAIN ENGINE 2] -->|PTO| SG2[SHAFT GEN 2]
    DG1[DIESEL GEN 1 1250 kW] --> MSB1[MAIN SWITCHBOARD BUS 1]
    DG2[DIESEL GEN 2 1250 kW] --> MSB1
    DG3[DIESEL GEN 3 1250 kW] --> MSB2[MAIN SWITCHBOARD BUS 2]
    SG1 --> MSB1
    SG2 --> MSB2
    MSB1 <-->|BUSTIE| MSB2
    SHORE[SHORE SUPPLY 440 V] -.->|COLD IRONING| MSB1
    MSB1 --> CONV1[PROPULSION VFD 1]
    MSB2 --> CONV2[PROPULSION VFD 2]
    CONV1 --> PM1[PROPULSION MOTOR 1 POD]
    CONV2 --> PM2[PROPULSION MOTOR 2 POD]
    MSB1 --> AUX1[BOW THRUSTER]
    MSB1 --> AUX2[STEERING GEAR]
    MSB1 --> AUX3[HVAC AND PUMPS]
    MSB1 -->|TRUNK| ESB1[EMERGENCY SWITCHBOARD]
    ESB1 --> EDG[EMERGENCY DG 250 kW]
    ESB1 --> BATT[BATTERY BANK 220 V DC 18 HRS]
    MSB2 --> NAV[NAVIGATION ECDIS RADAR AIS GPS]
    MSB2 --> DECK[DECK MACHINERY CRANES]
```

## 4.2 Synchronizing Logic for Paralleling Alternators

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[G1 ALTERNATOR] --> V1[V CHECK EQUAL]
    A2[G2 INCOMING] --> V1
    V1 --> F1[F CHECK EQUAL]
    F1 --> P1[PHASE SEQ MATCH]
    P1 --> S1[SYNCHROSCOPE 12 OCLOCK]
    S1 --> Q1{BREAKER CLOSE}
    Q1 -->|YES| R1[PARALLEL BUS ACTIVE]
    Q1 -->|NO| R2[WAIT AND ADJUST GOVERNOR AND AVR]
    R2 --> S1
```

## 4.3 Shipyard Electrical Distribution (Goliath Crane Feed)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    UTIL[UTILITY GRID 11 kV] --> TR1[YARD STEP DOWN 11 kV TO 440 V]
    TR1 --> YMSB[YARD MAIN SWITCHBOARD]
    YMSB --> C1[CRANE 1 VFD 500 kW]
    YMSB --> C2[CRANE 2 VFD 500 kW]
    YMSB --> C3[CRANE 3 VFD 500 kW]
    YMSB --> WS1[WELDING STATION 1 SCR RECTIFIER]
    YMSB --> WS2[WELDING STATION 2 INVERTER IGBT]
    YMSB --> WS3[WELDING STATION 3]
    YMSB --> PFC[POWER FACTOR CORRECTION CAPACITOR BANK 600 kVAR]
    YMSB --> DOCK[DRY DOCK LIGHTING 440 BY 220 V]
    YMSB --> COMP[COMPRESSED AIR PLANT]
```

## 4.4 Emergency Power Take-Over Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MAIN[MAIN BUS NORMAL] -->|BLACKOUT| EDG1[EMERGENCY DG AUTO START WITHIN 45 SEC]
    EDG1 --> ESB[EMERGENCY SWITCHBOARD]
    ESB --> CRIT[CRITICAL LOADS: STEERING NAV FIRE PUMP]
    MAIN -->|FAIL| BATT[BATTERIES SUPPLY FOR 30 MIN]
    BATT --> ESB
    BATT --> TRANS[UPS FOR COMPUTERS ECDIS RADAR]
    MAIN -->|RECOVERED| RESYNC[RE SYNC TO MAIN BUS]
    RESYNC --> ESB
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (2024 Scheme):** Two recurring mistakes lead to **2-mark deductions** in every Module-3 question on this topic:
> 1. **Confusing *emergency DG* with *standby DG*.** The emergency DG is **mandated by SOLAS**, starts **automatically within 45 s**, and must carry the **SOLAS-defined emergency loads for 18 h**. A "standby" DG is just a redundant operational unit and is not regulated.
> 2. **Forgetting the reverse-power relay.** Any alternator that motorises (i.e., absorbs active power due to a prime-mover failure) **must be tripped within 10 s** to prevent the healthy alternators from driving the failed one. This is a **favourite 3-mark question.**

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024, May]**
*Define "Cold Ironing" in the context of marine electrical systems. List **two** advantages and **one** regulatory driver for the same.*

**Model Answer:**

Cold ironing (also called *Alternative Maritime Power*, AMP, or *shore-supply*) is the process of supplying electrical power to a ship at berth from the **shore-side grid** (typically at 6.6 kV or 11 kV, 50/60 Hz) so that the ship's **auxiliary diesel generators can be completely shut down** in port.

**Advantages (any two):**
1. **Drastic reduction in port-side air pollution** — eliminates emission of NO_x, SO_x, PM and CO_2 from auxiliary engines while berthed.
2. **Lower noise levels** at the quay, improving conditions for port workers and shore communities.
3. **Reduced wear-and-tear** on auxiliary DG sets (less running hours, longer TBO).
4. **Compliance with green-port / ECA regulations** where berthing ships must cut emissions.

**Regulatory Driver:** **MARPOL Annex VI** (under IMO) sets progressively stringent sulphur and NO_x emission limits in **Emission Control Areas (ECAs)**, mandating shore-power or equivalent clean-energy alternatives.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024, Dec]**
*Why are **synchronous alternators** universally used for marine power generation, instead of induction generators?*

**Model Answer:**

A ship at sea operates its electrical system as a **standalone isolated microgrid** (no utility infinite bus to draw vars from). For a generator to **sustain terminal voltage** in such an isolated grid, it must be capable of **independently supplying reactive power (Q)** in addition to active power (P).

- An **induction generator** requires the grid to *supply* its magnetising current (i.e., it absorbs Q). On a dead/isolated bus, an induction machine **cannot self-excite** unless external capacitor banks are present — and even then, voltage regulation is poor.
- A **synchronous alternator** with **DC-excited rotor** can independently deliver any required Q via its **Automatic Voltage Regulator (AVR)**, holding the terminal voltage within **±2.5 %** of rated.

Additional advantages include **ease of paralleling** (via synchroscope), **short-circuit contribution** for selective relay coordination, and **independent control of P (via governor) and Q (via excitation)** — making the synchronous machine the natural and only practical choice for shipboard generation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme, ESE Pattern, Module 3]**

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled single-line diagram, describe the **architecture of a ship's main electrical power plant** for a modern cargo vessel carrying 4 diesel-generator sets. Explain the function of the **Main Switchboard (MSB)**, **bus-tie breaker**, and **Emergency Switchboard (ESB)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bulk carrier has the following load schedule. Determine the **number and rating of diesel generators** required to comply with the (n − 1) redundancy rule of classification societies. Assume diversity factor 0.65. **(7 marks)**

| Load | kW | Qty in use (sea) | Qty in use (harbour) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Main engine aux pumps | 110 | 3 | 1 |
| Cargo cranes | 360 | 3 | 0 |
| Ballast pumps | 200 | 2 | 1 |
| Accommodation HVAC | 150 | 1 | 1 |
| Galley / laundry | 90 | 1 | 1 |
| Navigation / bridge | 35 | 1 | 1 |
| Steering gear | 75 | 2 | 0 |
| Battery charger | 20 | 1 | 1 |

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Architecture (7 marks)**

**Main Generation** (4 × 1250 kW DG sets, each 440 V, 3-phase, 60 Hz, 900 rpm) feeding the **Main Switchboard (MSB)**. The MSB is **split-bus** (MSB-1 and MSB-2) with a **normally-open bus-tie breaker** between them.

- **In port / harbour:** Bus-tie open → MSB-1 carries essential services, MSB-2 carries cargo gear. A single fault on either side does not propagate.
- **Underway / at sea:** Bus-tie closed → all four DGs parallel, load shared equally via the **AVR/governor load-sharing module**. Loss of one DG is covered by the other three (n−1 = 3 ≥ 2.6 MW peak demand).

**Synchronising Panel:** contains synchroscope, dark-lamp pair, governor trim, AVR adjust — required to **parallel alternators** satisfying $V_{\text{gen}} = V_{\text{bus}}, f_{\text{gen}} = f_{\text{bus}}, \phi = 0$.

**Emergency Switchboard (ESB):** fed from MSB under normal conditions via an **interconnecting feeder**. On black-out, the **Emergency DG** (typically 250 kW, automatic start within 45 s) takes over the ESB. SOLAS-mandated emergency consumers — **steering gear, fire pump, navigation lights, communication, fire detection** — are supplied by ESB. The **transitional source** is the **battery bank (220 V DC, 18 h capacity)** which supplies critical loads (bridge, computer, radio) for the first 30 minutes before the EDG reaches rated speed/voltage.

**Preferential Trip (Load Shedding):** on overload, non-essential loads are shed in a pre-programmed priority order to keep the ESB alive.

**(b) — Generator Sizing (7 marks)**

Installed loads at sea:

$$ P_{\text{sea,inst}} = (110 \times 3) + (360 \times 3) + (200 \times 2) + (150 \times 1) + (90 \times 1) + (35 \times 1) + (75 \times 2) + (20 \times 1) $$

$$ P_{\text{sea,inst}} = 330 + 1080 + 400 + 150 + 90 + 35 + 150 + 20 = 2255 \text{ kW} $$

Apply diversity factor:

$$ P_{\text{sea,peak}} = 0.65 \times 2255 = 1465.75 \text{ kW} $$

For **4 identical DGs** (n − 1 = 3):

$$ P_{\text{rated}} \geq \dfrac{1465.75}{3} = 488.58 \text{ kW} $$

Select **standard 500 kW rating** → 4 × 500 kW.

**Check loading:**

$$ \text{Loading per DG} = \dfrac{1465.75}{4} = 366.44 \text{ kW } (\text{i.e.}, 73.3\,\% \text{ of MCR — efficient and within SFOC optimum}) $$

$$ \boxed{4 \times 500 \text{ kW DG sets, operating at } 73.3\,\% \text{ loading at sea, with full redundancy}} $$

**Harbour (cold-ironing preferred):** For interest, $P_{\text{harbour,inst}} = 110 + 150 + 90 + 35 + 200 + 20 = 605$ kW, $P_{\text{harbour,peak}} = 0.65 \times 605 = 393.25$ kW. One DG (500 kW) suffices at 78.6 % loading, but **shore power is the preferred** environmentally-friendly alternative.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme, ESE Pattern, Module 3 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **principle of electric propulsion** in modern vessels. Compare **diesel-mechanical**, **diesel-electric (CODLAG)**, and **full-electric (COGLAG)** propulsion architectures. List **three advantages** and **two challenges** of full-electric propulsion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 440 V, 3-phase, 50 Hz, shipboard feeder of length **120 m** supplies a synchronous motor (used as a generator in harbour-mode) drawing **85 A** at **0.92 power factor leading**. The conductor impedance is **R = 0.15 $\Omega$/km** and **X = 0.09 $\Omega$/km**. Compute the **percentage voltage drop** in the feeder and comment on whether it meets the **±2.5 %** class-society limit. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Electric Propulsion Principles (7 marks)**

**Definition:** Electric propulsion denotes the use of **electric motors (typically induction or synchronous)** to drive the ship's propeller(s), with the prime movers (diesel engines or gas turbines) coupled to **electrical generators** rather than directly to the propeller shaft.

**Architectural Comparison:**

| Architecture | Layout | Typical Vessel |
|---|---|---|
| **Diesel-Mechanical** | Engine → reduction gear → shaft → propeller | Older cargo ships, small tugs |
| **CODLAG (Combined Diesel-eLectric And Gas-turbine)** | Diesel or gas turbine drives generator; in electric mode, motor drives propeller; in mechanical mode, clutch engages engine to shaft | Frigates, corvettes, some cruise ships |
| **COGLAG / Full-Electric** | All prime movers drive only generators; propeller driven exclusively by electric motor (via VFD) | *Queen Elizabeth*-class carrier, *Zumwalt*-class destroyer, modern cruise liners |

**Three Advantages of Full-Electric Propulsion:**
1. **Acoustic Stealth** — decouples prime-mover noise from the hull, dramatically reducing radiated noise — vital for naval vessels.
2. **Prime Mover Placement Flexibility** — engines can be placed low in the hull for stability and freed shaft-lines permit larger payload / hangar / weapon spaces.
3. **Optimised Fuel Efficiency** — only the required generators run; load follows a peak-shaving strategy.

**Two Challenges:**
1. **High Capital Cost** — VFDs and large propulsion motors are expensive.
2. **Complex Power Management** — requires sophisticated PMS (Power Management System) to prevent blackout; large fault-current contribution requires robust protection co-ordination.

**(b) — Voltage Drop Calculation (7 marks)**

Given: $V_L = 440$ V, $I_L = 85$ A, $\cos\phi = 0.92$ (leading), $L = 120$ m = 0.12 km, $R = 0.15$ $\Omega$/km, $X = 0.09$ $\Omega$/km.

**Impedances for the run:**

$$ R_{\text{line}} = 0.15 \times 0.12 = 0.018 \,\Omega $$

$$ X_{\text{line}} = 0.09 \times 0.12 = 0.0108 \,\Omega $$

**Compute $\sin\phi$:**

$$ \sin\phi = \sqrt{1 - 0.92^2} = \sqrt{1 - 0.8464} = \sqrt{0.1536} = 0.3920 $$

**Substitute into voltage drop formula:**

$$ \Delta V = \sqrt{3}\, I\, (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi) $$

$$ \Delta V = 1.732 \times 85 \times (0.018 \times 0.92 + 0.0108 \times 0.3920) $$

$$ \Delta V = 147.22 \times (0.01656 + 0.004234) $$

$$ \Delta V = 147.22 \times 0.020794 = 3.061 \text{ V} $$

**Percentage voltage drop:**

$$ \%\Delta V = \dfrac{3.061}{440} \times 100 = 0.696\,\% $$

**Compliance Check:**

$$ 0.696\,\% \;\; < \;\; 2.5\,\% \;\;(class\text{-}society\ limit) \;\;\therefore\;\; \textbf{Design is COMPLIANT} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Leading PF Note:** In this problem, the synchronous machine operates at 0.92 leading (over-excited), **supplying reactive power to the bus**. Although the magnitude of the voltage drop is similar to a lagging case, leading current can cause a **Ferranti-like voltage rise** at lightly-loaded ends — particularly relevant for long shipboard feeders in harbour mode. The formula used above (R cos ϕ + X sin ϕ) automatically accounts for this by retaining the algebraic sign of $\sin\phi$ in the X-component.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Mile Revision Checklist — Keep this list on your desk for the night before the exam.**

- [ ] **Three-phase power formulas**: $P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$, $S = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L$, $Q = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \sin\phi$.
- [ ] **Synchronous speed**: $N_s = 120f/p$ — be able to calculate for 50/60 Hz and 2/4/6-pole alternators (3000/1500/1000 rpm at 50 Hz).
- [ ] **Generator redundancy**: **n − 1 rule**; minimum 2 × 100 % DGs (sometimes 3 × 50 %).
- [ ] **Synchronizing conditions**: voltage match, frequency match, phase sequence match, in-phase (synchroscope at 12 o'clock).
- [ ] **Reverse-power trip** in 10 s to prevent motorised alternator.
- [ ] **Emergency DG starts within 45 s** and must supply emergency loads for **18 h** (cargo) or **36 h** (passenger).
- [ ] **Voltage drop limit**: **±2.5 %** under any running condition.
- [ ] **THD limits**: $THD_v \leq 5\,\%$, $THD_i \leq 8\,\%$ (IEEE 519).
- [ ] **Cold ironing / AMP** = shore supply at berth, eliminating auxiliary emissions.
- [ ] **Shaft generator** delivers 3–8 % fuel saving at cruise.
- [ ] **Diesel-electric advantages**: flexibility, stealth, efficiency; **challenges**: cost, complexity.
- [ ] **Shipyard heavy loads**: gantry cranes (VFD), welding (SCR/inverter), shore supply (6.6/11 kV).
- [ ] **Marine navigation suite**: Gyrocompass + ARPA Radar + ECDIS + AIS + GPS + Echo Sounder + Speed Log.
- [ ] **24 V DC / 220 V DC** standard for control / battery; **440 V 3-phase** for power; **220 V 1-phase** for lighting.
- [ ] **Classification societies**: ABS, DNV, LR, BV, IRS — *never* omit this list in a 14-mark answer.
- [ ] **SOLAS** + **MARPOL** are international, *not* classification rules — distinguish carefully.
- [ ] **Preferential tripping (load shedding)** is an automatic function — not manual.
- [ ] **Battery / UPS** serves as the **transitional source** between black-out and EDG full loading (≈ 30 s).
- [ ] **Top of the most-frequently-asked 3-mark items**: "Why synchronous alternators?", "What is cold ironing?", "What is a bus-tie breaker?", "State SOLAS emergency duration."

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner's Caution:** When you draw the single-line diagram in Question A(a), **always show the Emergency Switchboard with a feeder from the MSB and a separate feed from the Emergency DG** — a *one-line diagram without the emergency feed* will attract a **−2 mark** deduction in the 2024 valuation scheme.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Instrumentation - Classification of Instruments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST521 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Instrumentation - Classification of Instruments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Instrumentation?

**Instrumentation** is the science and technology of developing, designing, and applying instruments for the purpose of measurement, observation, control, and recording of physical quantities within a process or system. The term originates from the Latin word *instrumentum*, meaning "a tool or device."

A measuring instrument, on the other hand, is a device used to determine the value of a physical quantity, also known as the **measurand**. The measurand is the quantity that we seek to measure, such as temperature, pressure, voltage, current, force, or flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** An *instrument* is a device or mechanism used to determine the present value of a quantity under observation. *Instrumentation* is the collective term for the group of instruments and their application to a particular field of study or work.

## 1.2 Functional Elements of a Measurement System

Every measurement system, regardless of the measurand, can be broken down into three primary functional elements. These elements act as a chain in which the physical signal passes from one to the next in a specific order.

1. **Primary Sensing Element (Sensor):** The first element that comes in direct contact with the measurand and performs the initial conversion. It senses the physical quantity and converts it into a more usable form, typically a mechanical or electrical signal. Example: A thermocouple junction sensing temperature.
2. **Variable Conversion Element (Transducer):** The signal from the primary sensor is conditioned or converted into a form suitable for further processing. Example: A Wheatstone bridge converting resistance variation into a voltage.
3. **Data Presentation Element (Display/Output):** The final stage where the measured value is presented to the observer or used for control. It may be an indicating dial, a recorder, or a digital display. Example: A moving-coil voltmeter showing voltage on a calibrated scale.

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember:** Sensor ≠ Transducer in the strictest KTU sense. A *transducer* is a general device that converts one form of energy into another, whereas a *sensor* is a specialized type of transducer that converts a physical measurand into an electrical output.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: Instrumentation as a Doctor's Diagnosis

Think of the human body as a process plant and instrumentation as a doctor’s diagnostic tools. A doctor cannot directly see a patient’s blood pressure, glucose level, or heart rate. Instead, the doctor uses:
- A **stethoscope** (Primary Sensor) to hear heartbeats → converts acoustic waves into audible sound.
- A **sphygmomanometer** (Variable Conversion Element) that converts cuff pressure into a numeric reading via a calibrated scale.
- A **display** (Data Presentation Element) showing the final reading in mm of Hg.

Just like a doctor needs precise tools to diagnose illness, an engineer needs accurate instruments to monitor and control industrial processes. Without instrumentation, a power plant operator would be "blind" to the temperature inside a boiler, leading to catastrophic failure. Instrumentation gives engineers the *senses* of the process plant.

## 1.4 Applications of Instrumentation

Instrumentation forms the backbone of modern engineering. Its primary applications include:

- **Industrial Process Control:** Maintaining temperature, pressure, and flow in chemical plants.
- **Power Systems:** Monitoring voltage, current, frequency, and power in generating stations and substations.
- **Medical Field:** ECG, MRI, blood pressure monitors, pulse oximeters.
- **Automotive:** Speedometers, fuel gauges, engine control units.
- **Aerospace:** Altimeters, flight control systems, navigation instruments.
- **Research and Development:** Data acquisition and scientific experimentation.
- **Manufacturing:** Quality control and assurance.

## 1.5 Need and Scope of Instrumentation

The need for instrumentation arises because:
- Human senses are **limited in accuracy and range**; a person cannot feel a 0.1 °C change in temperature.
- Some **hazardous environments** (high voltage, high temperature, radiation) prevent direct human observation.
- **Continuous and remote monitoring** is impossible without instruments.
- **Fast transient phenomena** (like lightning strokes or switchgear operations) occur in microseconds.
- **Data storage and analysis** requires conversion of physical signals into recordable forms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The quality of any industrial product is directly proportional to the quality of the instruments used in its manufacturing process. Hence, instrumentation is the silent pillar of industrial automation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Measuring Instruments

Measuring instruments can be classified based on several criteria. Understanding the classification is a high-weightage topic in KTU examinations and forms the basis for choosing the right instrument for a given application.

### 2.1.1 Based on the Nature of Output

| S. No. | Type | Description | Example |
|:------:|:-----|:------------|:--------|
| 1 | **Indicating Instruments** | Show the instantaneous value of the measurand on a dial or digital display. | Voltmeter, Ammeter, Pressure gauge |
| 2 | **Recording Instruments** | Continuously record the value of the measurand over time on a paper chart or electronic memory. | ECG machine, Strip-chart recorder |
| 3 | **Integrating Instruments** | Totalize or integrate the measurand over a period of time. | Energy meter (kWh meter), Water meter |
| 4 | **Controlling Instruments** | Compare measured value with a reference and take corrective action automatically. | Thermostat, PID controller, Governor |

### 2.1.2 Based on the Method of Measurement

| S. No. | Type | Description | Example |
|:------:|:-----|:------------|:--------|
| 1 | **Direct Measurement** | The value is obtained directly without comparison or calculation. | Vernier caliper measuring length |
| 2 | **Indirect Measurement** | The value is computed from direct measurements of related quantities. | Measuring resistance using V/I |
| 3 | **Absolute Measurement** | Based on fundamental physical constants; no calibration required. | Tangent galvanometer, Michelson interferometer |
| 4 | **Secondary Measurement** | Requires prior calibration against a reference standard. | Most commercial instruments like voltmeters |

### 2.1.3 Based on the Mode of Operation

| S. No. | Type | Description | Example |
|:------:|:-----|:------------|:--------|
| 1 | **Deflection-Type** | The output deflects proportionally to the measurand. | Moving-coil ammeter, Bourdon gauge |
| 2 | **Null-Type** | The output is brought to zero (null) by balancing against a known reference. | Wheatstone bridge, Potentiometer |
| 3 | **Comparison-Type** | Compares the unknown with a known standard. | Balance scale |

### 2.1.4 Based on the Physical Principle of Operation

| S. No. | Type | Description | Example |
|:------:|:-----|:------------|:--------|
| 1 | **Mechanical** | Uses mechanical principles like springs, levers, gears. | Bourdon pressure gauge |
| 2 | **Electrical/Electronic** | Uses electrical properties. | Voltmeter, Oscilloscope, LVDT |
| 3 | **Hydraulic** | Uses fluid pressure for operation. | Hydraulic press, Manometer |
| 4 | **Optical** | Uses light phenomena. | Spectrometer, Interferometer |
| 5 | **Thermal** | Uses heat transfer properties. | Thermocouple, RTD |
| 6 | **Chemical** | Uses chemical reactions. | pH meter, Gas analyzer |

### 2.1.5 Based on the Form of Output

| S. No. | Type | Description | Example |
|:------:|:-----|:------------|:--------|
| 1 | **Analog** | Output varies continuously with the measurand. | Thermometer, Analog multimeter |
| 2 | **Digital** | Output is in discrete numerical form. | Digital multimeter, Digital stopwatch |
| 3 | **Smart / Intelligent** | Has built-in microprocessors, self-calibration, and communication. | Smart transmitter, Smart sensor |

### 2.1.6 Based on the Application Domain

- **Industrial Instruments:** Used in process industries.
- **Laboratory Instruments:** Used in research and testing labs.
- **Medical Instruments:** Used in healthcare.
- **Scientific Instruments:** Used in fundamental research.

## 2.2 Static and Dynamic Characteristics

The performance of an instrument is judged by two broad categories of characteristics: **static** (when the measurand is constant or varies slowly) and **dynamic** (when the measurand varies rapidly with time).

### 2.2.1 Static Characteristics

| Characteristic | Definition |
|:---------------|:-----------|
| **Accuracy** | The degree of closeness of an indicated value to the true value. |
| **Precision** | The degree of repeatability of measurements under unchanged conditions. |
| **Sensitivity** | The ratio of change in output to the corresponding change in input. |
| **Resolution** | The smallest change in the measurand that the instrument can detect. |
| **Range** | The minimum to maximum value of measurand the instrument can measure. |
| **Span** | The difference between maximum and minimum range values. |
| **Linearity** | The degree to which the output-input relationship follows a straight line. |
| **Drift** | The gradual change of output with time when the input is constant. |
| **Hysteresis** | The maximum difference in output for a given input when approached from opposite directions. |
| **Dead Zone** | The range of input values for which the output remains unchanged. |
| **Threshold** | The minimum input value needed to produce a detectable output. |
| **Static Error** | The difference between the true value and the measured value. |
| **Reproducibility** | The ability of the instrument to give the same output for the same input over time. |

### 2.2.2 Dynamic Characteristics

| Characteristic | Definition |
|:---------------|:-----------|
| **Speed of Response** | How quickly the instrument responds to a change in input. |
| **Fidelity** | The degree to which the instrument reproduces the input faithfully. |
| **Lag** | The delay between the input change and the corresponding output change. |
| **Dynamic Error** | The difference between the true value (varying with time) and the indicated value. |
| **Time Constant** | The time required for the output to reach 63.2% of its final value in a first-order system. |
| **Settling Time** | Time required for the output to stabilize within a specified tolerance band. |
| **Overshoot** | The maximum amount by which the output exceeds the final value before settling. |
| **Damping** | The suppression of oscillations in the output response. |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following formulas are the most frequently asked mathematical relations in KTU exams for this module. Memorize them with units.

| Quantity | Formula | Description |
|:---------|:--------|:------------|
| Absolute Error | $e = A_m - A_t$ | $A_m$ = measured value, $A_t$ = true value |
| Relative Error | $e_r = \frac{e}{A_t} = \frac{A_m - A_t}{A_t}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| Percentage Error | $e_{\%} = e_r \times 100$ | Expressed as a percentage |
| Accuracy Ratio | $A = 1 - \vert e_r \vert$ | Always between 0 and 1 |
| Percentage Accuracy | $A_{\%} = (1 - \vert e_r \vert) \times 100$ | Used in calibration reports |
| Sensitivity | $S = \frac{\Delta q_o}{\Delta q_i}$ | $\Delta q_o$ = change in output, $\Delta q_i$ = change in input |
| Linearity (Non-linearity) | $N = \frac{\text{Max. deviation from straight line}}{\text{Full scale output}}$ | Expressed as % of full scale |
| Span | $\text{Span} = A_{\max} - A_{\min}$ | $A_{\max}$ = max range, $A_{\min}$ = min range |
| First-order Time Response | $q_o(t) = q_{o,ss}(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | $\tau$ = time constant |
| Steady-state Error (Step Input) | $e_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} [q_i(t) - q_o(t)]$ | For unit step input, $e_{ss} = 1 - K_p$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick:** When the question asks for "combined sensitivity" of two cascaded instruments, multiply the individual sensitivities: $S_{\text{total}} = S_1 \times S_2 \times S_3 \times \cdots$ This is a guaranteed 2-3 mark question in ESE papers.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

The classification system is not just academic — it has profound real-world implications. For instance:
- In a **nuclear reactor control room**, only **null-type** instruments are used because they offer higher accuracy (no scale reading error).
- In a **high-speed aircraft altimeter**, **dynamic characteristics** dominate because altitude changes rapidly.
- For **billing purposes** (electricity meter), **integrating** type is mandatory.
- For **safety alarms**, **indicating** type with audible/visual output is essential.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides rigorous, step-by-step mathematical derivations of the core formulas that govern instrumentation errors and system responses. Each transition is explicitly justified.

## 3.1 Derivation of Static Error and Percentage Accuracy

### Statement
An ammeter reads **5.25 A** when the true current is **5.00 A**. Determine the (i) absolute error, (ii) relative error, (iii) percentage error, and (iv) percentage accuracy.

### Step 1: Identify the Given Data
From the problem:
- Measured value (indicated value): $A_m = 5.25 \text{ A}$
- True value (actual value): $A_t = 5.00 \text{ A}$

### Step 2: Compute the Absolute Error
The absolute error is defined as the arithmetic difference between the indicated value and the true value.

$$
e = A_m - A_t
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
e = 5.25 - 5.00 = 0.25 \text{ A}
$$

The instrument reads **0.25 A higher** than the actual value.

### Step 3: Compute the Relative Error
Relative error is the ratio of absolute error to the true value, giving a dimensionless number.

$$
e_r = \frac{e}{A_t}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
e_r = \frac{0.25}{5.00} = 0.05
$$

### Step 4: Compute the Percentage Error
Percentage error is simply the relative error expressed as a percentage by multiplying by 100.

$$
e_{\%} = e_r \times 100
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
e_{\%} = 0.05 \times 100 = 5 \%
$$

### Step 5: Compute the Percentage Accuracy
Percentage accuracy is the complement of the percentage error, indicating how close the reading is to the true value.

$$
A_{\%} = (1 - \vert e_r \vert) \times 100
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
A_{\%} = (1 - 0.05) \times 100 = 0.95 \times 100 = 95 \%
$$

### Final Analytical Result
- Absolute Error: **+0.25 A**
- Relative Error: **0.05**
- Percentage Error: **5%**
- Percentage Accuracy: **95%**

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention:** A positive error means the instrument over-reads (reads high). A negative error means it under-reads (reads low). The sign is critical in calibration certificates.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Combined Sensitivity in a Cascaded Measurement System

### Statement
A measurement system has three cascaded elements with individual sensitivities $S_1$, $S_2$, and $S_3$. Prove that the overall sensitivity is the product of the individual sensitivities.

### Step 1: Define Sensitivity for Each Stage
For the $i$-th stage, sensitivity is defined as:

$$
S_i = \frac{\Delta q_{o,i}}{\Delta q_{i,i}}
$$

where $\Delta q_{i,i}$ is the input to stage $i$, and $\Delta q_{o,i}$ is the output of stage $i$.

### Step 2: Chain the Stages
By physical connection, the output of stage 1 is the input to stage 2, and so on:

$$
\Delta q_{o,1} = \Delta q_{i,2} \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta q_{o,2} = \Delta q_{i,3}
$$

The final output is $\Delta q_{o,3}$ and the initial input is $\Delta q_{i,1}$.

### Step 3: Multiply the Sensitivities
Multiplying the sensitivities of all three stages:

$$
S_1 \times S_2 \times S_3 = \frac{\Delta q_{o,1}}{\Delta q_{i,1}} \times \frac{\Delta q_{o,2}}{\Delta q_{i,2}} \times \frac{\Delta q_{o,3}}{\Delta q_{i,3}}
$$

### Step 4: Apply the Chaining Condition
Using the relationship $\Delta q_{o,1} = \Delta q_{i,2}$ and $\Delta q_{o,2} = \Delta q_{i,3}$, intermediate terms cancel:

$$
S_1 \times S_2 \times S_3 = \frac{\Delta q_{o,1}}{\Delta q_{i,1}} \times \frac{\Delta q_{o,2}}{\Delta q_{o,1}} \times \frac{\Delta q_{o,3}}{\Delta q_{o,2}}
$$

### Step 5: Simplify the Telescoping Expression
The middle terms cancel out (telescoping product):

$$
S_1 \times S_2 \times S_3 = \frac{\Delta q_{o,3}}{\Delta q_{i,1}} = S_{\text{total}}
$$

### Conclusion
For $n$ cascaded elements, the overall sensitivity is:

$$
S_{\text{total}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} S_i
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Time Constant for a First-Order Measurement System

### Statement
Show that for a first-order instrument, the time required to reach 63.2% of the steady-state output is the time constant $\tau$.

### Step 1: Write the Standard First-Order Equation
A first-order measurement system is modeled by the differential equation:

$$
\tau \frac{dq_o}{dt} + q_o(t) = K_p \cdot q_i(t)
$$

where $K_p$ is the static sensitivity and $q_i$ is the input.

### Step 2: Apply Unit Step Input
For a unit step input:

$$
q_i(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & t < 0 \\ 1, & t \geq 0 \end{cases}
$$

The steady-state solution is $q_{o,ss} = K_p$.

### Step 3: Solve the Differential Equation
The general solution to the differential equation is the sum of steady-state and transient parts:

$$
q_o(t) = q_{o,ss} + (q_o(0) - q_{o,ss}) e^{-t/\tau}
$$

Assuming the initial output is zero, $q_o(0) = 0$:

$$
q_o(t) = K_p (1 - e^{-t/\tau})
$$

### Step 4: Substitute $t = \tau$
To find the percentage of steady state at $t = \tau$:

$$
q_o(\tau) = K_p (1 - e^{-1})
$$

Using the numerical value $e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$:

$$
q_o(\tau) = K_p (1 - 0.3679) = K_p \times 0.6321
$$

### Step 5: Interpret the Result
At $t = \tau$, the output reaches approximately **63.21%** of its final steady-state value. This is the formal definition of the time constant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** "63.2 in $\tau$" — 63.2% in one time-constant. For a 95% response, $t = 3\tau$. For 99.3% response, $t = 5\tau$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Instrument for the Job

The following decision matrix helps in selecting the appropriate instrument class for a given application, which is a common KTU application-level question.

| Application | Recommended Class | Reason |
|:------------|:------------------|:-------|
| Domestic electricity billing | Integrating (kWh meter) | Need to totalize energy consumption over time |
| Aircraft altitude display | Indicating, fast dynamic response | Real-time display of rapidly changing quantity |
| Industrial boiler temperature alarm | Indicating with trip contact | Immediate threshold alert required |
| Laboratory potentiometer | Null-type | Highest accuracy; human error in scale reading eliminated |
| ECG machine in a hospital | Recording | Need permanent record over time for diagnosis |
| Engine speed control (governor) | Controlling | Continuous feedback for regulation |
| Laboratory weighing balance | Comparison type | Direct comparison with standard masses |
| Smart home temperature sensor | Smart / Digital | Microprocessor-based, communicates with phone |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

This section presents the architecture of a generalized measurement system and the classification tree of instruments using Mermaid diagrams.

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Generalized Measurement System

    D[Data Transmission Element CABLES TELEMETRY] --> E
    E[Data Presentation Element DISPLAY RECORDER] --> F[Observer or Controller]

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

This section is modeled strictly on the KTU 2024 End-Semester Examination (ESE) pattern. Mark distribution and choice pattern follow the official KTU directives: **Part A = 3 marks** (short answer), **Part B = 14 marks** (full descriptive, with internal choice).

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the following terms with one example each:**
(i) Measurand
(ii) Sensor
(iii) Transducer

### Model Answer:
**(i) Measurand:** A *measurand* is the physical quantity whose value is sought to be determined by measurement, e.g., temperature, pressure, voltage.  
**(ii) Sensor:** A *sensor* is a primary sensing element that detects a physical quantity and produces a corresponding output signal, usually electrical, e.g., a thermistor that changes resistance with temperature.  
**(iii) Transducer:** A *transducer* is a device that converts one form of energy into another, e.g., a piezoelectric crystal converting mechanical stress into an electrical voltage.

> [!NOTE]
> All sensors are transducers, but all transducers are not sensors. This is a common KTU 2-mark/3-mark conceptual distinction question.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Accuracy and Precision in measurement with suitable examples.**

### Model Answer:
| Aspect | Accuracy | Precision |
|:-------|:---------|:----------|
| Definition | Closeness of a measurement to the true value | Closeness of two or more measurements to each other |
| Affected by | Calibration error, systematic error | Repeatability, random errors |
| Example | A thermometer reading 100.0 °C when true is 100.0 °C | Three readings 99.8, 99.9, 99.8 °C of the same water bath (precise, but not accurate) |
| Improvement | Calibration, zero-set adjustment | Better sensor design, averaging techniques |

> [!NOTE]
> An instrument can be *precise without being accurate*. Think of a clock that loses 5 minutes consistently — every reading is consistent (precise) but off by 5 minutes (inaccurate).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` (Module 4, Full Marks)
**Explain in detail the functional elements of a measurement system with the help of a neat block diagram. Discuss any four static characteristics of measuring instruments.**

#### (a) Functional Elements of a Measurement System (7 Marks)

**Introduction:** Any measurement system, irrespective of its complexity, contains three mandatory functional elements. These are connected in series such that the output of one element becomes the input to the next.

**The three mandatory elements are:**

1. **Primary Sensing Element (Sensor):**
   The first element that receives energy from the measurand and produces a usable output.  
   *Example:* A strain gauge bonded to a cantilever beam converts mechanical strain into a change in electrical resistance.

2. **Variable Conversion Element (Transducer):**
   Converts the output of the sensor into a more suitable form, often an electrical signal. It may also amplify or filter the signal.  
   *Example:* A Wheatstone bridge circuit converts resistance change into a proportional voltage.

3. **Data Presentation Element (Display):**
   Presents the measured information to the observer through a visual display, audio alarm, or recording medium.  
   *Example:* A digital panel meter showing the temperature in °C.

**Two Optional Elements (for completeness):**
- **Variable Manipulation Element:** Performs amplification, filtering, integration, or differentiation of the signal.
- **Data Transmission Element:** Transfers data from the measurement site to a remote display/control room using cables, telemetry, or wireless.

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Measurand] → [Sensor] → [Transducer] → [Amplifier/Filter] → [Transmission] → [Display/Recorder] → [Observer]
```

**Conclusion:** The functional elements transform a physical quantity into observable, recordable information. The quality of each element determines the overall quality of the measurement.

#### (b) Four Static Characteristics of Measuring Instruments (7 Marks)

**1. Accuracy:**
The accuracy of an instrument is defined as the degree of closeness of an indicated value to the true value of the measurand. An instrument with 0.1% accuracy means the error is within 0.1% of the true value at all points in its range. Accuracy is improved through careful calibration against primary standards.

**2. Precision:**
Precision refers to the degree of repeatability or reproducibility of a measurement. An instrument is said to be highly precise if it gives nearly the same reading each time the same quantity is measured under identical conditions. Precision depends on the design quality and is affected by random errors, not systematic ones.

**3. Sensitivity:**
Sensitivity is the ratio of the change in output signal to the corresponding change in the input (measurand). Mathematically:

$$
S = \frac{\Delta q_o}{\Delta q_i}
$$

A highly sensitive instrument will produce a large output change for a small input change. Sensitivity must be uniform across the entire range to ensure linear operation.

**4. Linearity:**
Linearity is the degree to which the output of an instrument follows a straight-line relationship with the input. An instrument is said to be perfectly linear if the calibration curve is a straight line. Non-linearity is quantified as:

$$
N = \frac{\text{Max. deviation of calibration curve from straight line}}{\text{Full scale output}} \times 100\%
$$

A linear instrument is easier to calibrate and read because equal increments of input produce equal increments of output.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part B Question A:**
> - Many students forget to draw the **block diagram** and lose 2-3 marks. Always include a labeled block diagram.
> - In static characteristics, do not list all 12+ characteristics. Stick to the 4 asked and **define each with a formula and an example**.
> - Never confuse **range** with **span**; range is a band, span is the difference between the two extremes.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` (Module 4, Full Marks)
**Classify the measuring instruments based on (i) the nature of output, (ii) the method of measurement, and (iii) the mode of operation. Explain with suitable examples and discuss the merits of null-type instruments over deflection-type instruments.**

#### (a) Classification Based on Three Criteria (10 Marks)

**(i) Classification Based on Nature of Output (4 Marks):**

| Type | Description | Example |
|:-----|:------------|:--------|
| **Indicating** | Gives instantaneous value on a calibrated scale | Voltmeter, Pressure gauge |
| **Recording** | Records value continuously over time | Strip-chart recorder, ECG |
| **Integrating** | Totalizes the measurand over a period | Energy meter, Gas meter |
| **Controlling** | Compares with reference and corrects | Thermostat, PID controller |

**(ii) Classification Based on Method of Measurement (3 Marks):**

- **Direct Measurement:** Value is obtained by direct comparison without any computation. *Example:* Measuring length with a ruler.
- **Indirect Measurement:** The value is calculated from measurements of other related quantities. *Example:* Electrical resistance measured as $R = V/I$ using voltmeter and ammeter.
- **Absolute Measurement:** Depends on physical constants; no prior calibration needed. *Example:* Michelson interferometer for length, tangent galvanometer for current.
- **Secondary Measurement:** Requires calibration against a known standard. *Example:* Commercial voltmeters and ammeters.

**(iii) Classification Based on Mode of Operation (3 Marks):**

- **Deflection-Type:** Output deflects in proportion to the measurand. Easy to use, fast response, but the operator must read a scale, introducing parallax error. *Example:* Moving-coil voltmeter.
- **Null-Type:** The output is brought to zero by balancing against a known reference. The unknown value is determined from the reference, not from a scale reading. *Example:* Wheatstone bridge, DC potentiometer.
- **Comparison-Type:** The unknown is directly compared with a known standard. *Example:* Beam balance.

#### (b) Merits of Null-Type over Deflection-Type Instruments (4 Marks)

1. **Higher Accuracy:** Null-type instruments rely on reference standards, so they are inherently more accurate. The accuracy depends on the reference, not on scale reading.
2. **No Calibration Drift:** Since the output is always nulled to zero, there are no errors due to scale non-linearity or pointer misalignment.
3. **High Sensitivity:** The balance detection is usually very sensitive, allowing detection of very small differences.
4. **Freedom from Scale Reading Errors:** The operator does not need to interpolate a scale; the measurement is reduced to setting a null and reading a calibrated reference.
5. **Insensitive to Power Supply Variations:** In DC potentiometers, the galvanometer only needs to detect zero, not measure a value.

**Demerit of Null-Type:** They are slower, require manual balancing, and are not suitable for rapid transient measurement.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part B Question B:**
> - When asked to classify based on a criterion, students often **mix classifications from different criteria** and lose marks. Stick strictly to the asked criterion.
> - Merits must be **listed as bullet points, not paragraphs**, for clarity and easy valuation.
> - Always provide **at least one numerical example or industrial application** to fetch full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4, Topic: Introduction to Instrumentation**

### Key Definitions
- **Instrument:** A device to determine the present value of a quantity under observation.
- **Instrumentation:** The art and science of using instruments for measurement and control.
- **Measurand:** The physical quantity being measured.
- **Transducer:** Converts one form of energy into another.
- **Sensor:** A transducer that converts a physical quantity into an electrical signal.

### Three Functional Elements
1. **Primary Sensing Element (Sensor)**
2. **Variable Conversion Element (Transducer/Manipulator)**
3. **Data Presentation Element (Display/Recorder)**

### Classifications to Remember
- **Nature of Output:** Indicating, Recording, Integrating, Controlling.
- **Method of Measurement:** Direct, Indirect, Absolute, Secondary.
- **Mode of Operation:** Deflection-Type, Null-Type, Comparison-Type.
- **Form of Output:** Analog, Digital, Smart/Intelligent.
- **Principle:** Mechanical, Electrical, Electronic, Optical, Thermal, Chemical.

### Key Formulas to Memorize
- Absolute error: $e = A_m - A_t$
- Relative error: $e_r = \frac{e}{A_t}$
- Percentage error: $e_{\%} = e_r \times 100$
- Percentage accuracy: $A_{\%} = (1 - \vert e_r \vert) \times 100$
- Sensitivity: $S = \frac{\Delta q_o}{\Delta q_i}$
- Combined sensitivity (cascaded): $S_{\text{total}} = S_1 \times S_2 \times S_3$
- First-order response: $q_o(t) = K_p (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$
- **Time constant rule:** At $t = \tau$, output reaches **63.2%** of final value.
- **4.6 τ rule:** At $t = 4.6\tau$, output reaches **99%** of final value.

### High-Yield Distinctions
- **Sensor vs Transducer:** Sensor is a subset; all sensors are transducers, not vice versa.
- **Accuracy vs Precision:** Accuracy is closeness to *true* value; precision is closeness to *each other* among repeated readings.
- **Null vs Deflection:** Null is *more accurate but slower*; Deflection is *faster but less accurate*.
- **Range vs Span:** Range is the band; span is the *width* of that band.
- **Analog vs Digital:** Analog is *continuous*; Digital is *discrete* and quantised.

### Common Examiner Traps
- Confusing static error with dynamic error.
- Writing the formula for percentage error as a positive number (always show magnitude).
- Forgetting to include a labeled block diagram in 14-mark answers.
- Not providing examples alongside definitions (2-mark deduction per question).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standards and Calibration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST521 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Standards and Calibration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Standards in Measurement

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
A **Measurement Standard** is a physical device, artifact, or system that defines, represents, or reproduces a unit of measurement (or a multiple or fraction of it) with a known and documented accuracy, serving as the reference against which measuring instruments are calibrated and compared.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Standard**
> A standard is the embodiment of a physical quantity with a defined value, used as the basis of comparison for other measurements. It is the "reference truth" against which unknown quantities are validated.

#### Classification of Standards (Hierarchy)

The international measurement system is built on a strict hierarchy to ensure global uniformity:

1. **International Standards (Primary Standards)**:
   Maintained and disseminated by international bodies such as the **BIPM (Bureau International des Poids et Mesures)** in Sèvres, France.
   These are the highest-accuracy standards, defined by physical constants (e.g., the cesium-133 atom defines the second).

2. **Primary Standards (National Standards)**:
   Maintained by national metrology institutes such as **NIST (USA)**, **NPL (India - National Physical Laboratory, New Delhi)**, **NPL (UK)**, and **PTB (Germany)**.
   Calibrated against international standards; used to calibrate secondary standards.

3. **Secondary Standards (Reference Standards)**:
   Used in industrial calibration laboratories. Calibrated periodically against primary standards. Serve as working references for industrial instruments.

4. **Working Standards (Laboratory Standards)**:
   Used daily in workshops and laboratories to calibrate general-purpose measuring instruments. Calibrated regularly against secondary standards.

5. **Transfer Standards**:
   Portable standards used to transfer a known value from one location to another (e.g., standard cells, standard resistors transported between labs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point**
> The chain of traceability is: *Working Instrument ← Working Standard ← Secondary Standard ← Primary Standard ← International Standard*. Any break in this chain invalidates the measurement's traceability.

#### Common Electrical Standards Examples

- **Standard Resistor**: A precision resistor (e.g., 1 Ω, 10 kΩ) with very low temperature coefficient and long-term stability.
- **Standard Cell (Weston Cell)**: A saturated cadmium cell with an EMF of **1.018636 V at 20 °C** (used historically as a voltage reference).
- **Standard Capacitor / Inductor**: Used for AC bridge calibration.
- **Atomic Frequency Standard (Cesium Clock)**: Defines time/frequency with accuracy better than 1 part in 10¹⁵.
- **Zener Diode-Based Voltage Reference**: Modern solid-state voltage standard (e.g., 10.000 V with ±0.001% stability).

### 1.2 Calibration

**Definition:**
**Calibration** is the process of comparing the output of an instrument or measuring system against a standard of known accuracy, and adjusting (or recording) the deviations so that the instrument's readings are compatible with the standard.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Calibration**
> Calibration = *Comparison* + *Documentation* + (Optionally) *Adjustment*.
> It answers the question: *"How close is the instrument's reading to the true value?"*

#### Conceptual Analogy — The Weighing Scale at a Grocery Shop

Imagine you go to a vegetable shop and place a **1 kg standard iron weight** on the vendor's digital scale. The scale reads **0.98 kg**. The vendor has two options:

- **Option A (No Adjustment, Just Document)**: He notes that the scale reads 0.98 kg for a true 1 kg. He can mentally add 0.02 kg correction for every reading. This is *calibration without adjustment* — a **calibration certificate** is issued with the correction values.
- **Option B (Adjust)**: He presses the "calibrate" button on the scale, places the 1 kg weight, and the scale auto-corrects itself to read exactly 1.000 kg. This is *calibration with adjustment*.

**Key Insight from the Analogy**:
- A standard (the 1 kg iron weight, traceable to the International Prototype of the Kilogram) is essential.
- The instrument's error is quantified and documented.
- **Calibration does NOT mean the instrument is "fixed" forever** — drift, aging, and environmental effects mean calibration must be repeated periodically.

### 1.3 Why Standards and Calibration Matter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance**
> - **Safety**: A miscalibrated medical infusion pump could deliver 2× the prescribed drug dose.
> - **Quality (ISO 9001 Compliance)**: Manufacturing processes require traceable measurements.
> - **Inter-laboratory Comparison**: Two labs measuring the same quantity must agree — only possible via a common standard.
> - **Legal Metrology**: Electricity meters in households are calibrated against legal standards; errors here mean revenue loss or overcharging.

### 1.4 Physical Constants Used in Modern Standards

- Speed of light in vacuum: **c = 299,792,458 m/s** (defines the metre since 1983).
- Cesium-133 hyperfine transition frequency: **ΔνCs = 9,192,631,770 Hz** (defines the second).
- Planck constant: **h = 6.62607015 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** (defines the kilogram since 2019).
- Elementary charge: **e = 1.602176634 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** (defines the ampere since 2019).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Classification of Calibration

Calibration can be classified along multiple axes:

#### A. By Method of Comparison

**1. Primary Calibration (Direct Comparison)**
- The instrument is compared directly with a primary standard of the same quantity.
- Highest accuracy, but expensive and rarely done in industry.
- *Example*: Calibrating a top-grade voltmeter using a Josephson Voltage Standard.

**2. Secondary Calibration (Indirect Comparison)**
- The instrument is compared with a secondary or working standard that has itself been calibrated against a primary standard.
- This is the **most common industrial practice**.
- *Example*: Calibrating a workshop ammeter against a lab-grade digital multimeter (DMM).

#### B. By Use of Standards

**1. Direct Calibration**
- The unknown instrument reads the same quantity produced by the standard simultaneously.
- *Example*: Connecting a standard voltmeter in parallel with the instrument under test, both measuring the same voltage source.

**2. Indirect Calibration (Comparison via Bridge or Comparator)**
- A null-deflection bridge or comparator is used; the instrument is balanced against the standard.
- *Example*: Wheatstone bridge calibration of an unknown resistance using standard resistors.

#### C. By Static vs Dynamic Behaviour

**1. Static Calibration**
- Performed under steady-state (DC) conditions.
- Inputs are held constant; output is measured after settling.
- Used to determine: **accuracy, linearity, hysteresis, dead zone, repeatability, drift**.

**2. Dynamic Calibration**
- Performed with time-varying inputs (step, ramp, sinusoidal).
- Used to determine: **time constant, rise time, damping ratio, frequency response, bandwidth**.

### 2.2 Calibration Procedure (Standard Industrial Workflow)

1. **Selection of Standard**: Choose a standard with accuracy at least 4× better than the instrument under test (the **4:1 Test Accuracy Ratio, TAR**).
2. **Environmental Conditioning**: Stabilize temperature (typically **23 °C ± 1 °C**), humidity, and pressure.
3. **Pre-conditioning**: Allow the instrument to warm up and stabilize.
4. **As-Found Reading**: Record the instrument's reading before any adjustment.
5. **Apply Known Input**: Apply the standard input in increasing steps (0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100% of range).
6. **Record Readings**: Note instrument output at each step (ascending and descending — to detect hysteresis).
7. **Reverse Sweep**: Decrease input in steps to check for hysteresis and dead band.
8. **Compute Errors**: Calculate error, linearity, repeatability, hysteresis.
9. **Adjustment (if authorized)**: Adjust the instrument to bring it within tolerance.
10. **Post-Adjustment Verification**: Re-run the test to confirm.
11. **Calibration Certificate**: Document standard used, environmental conditions, results, uncertainty, traceability chain, and validity date.

### 2.3 Key Calibration Performance Metrics

Let the true value be $T$ and the instrument reading be $R$.

- **Absolute Error**: 
$$E_{abs} = R - T$$

- **Relative Error** (fractional): 
$$E_{rel} = \frac{R - T}{T}$$

- **Percentage Error** (% of Full Scale, FS): 
$$E_{\%FS} = \frac{R - T}{T_{FS}} \times 100\%$$

- **Percentage Error** (% of True Value, Reading): 
$$E_{\%R} = \frac{R - T}{T} \times 100\%$$

- **Accuracy** (as a percentage): 
$$A = 100\% - \vert E_{\%FS} \vert$$

- **Linearity (Non-linearity)**: Maximum deviation of the calibration curve from the best-fit straight line, expressed as % of FS.
$$N_L = \frac{(R_{actual} - R_{ideal})_{max}}{T_{FS}} \times 100\%$$

- **Hysteresis Error**: Difference between upscale and downscale readings at the same input.
$$E_H = R_{up} - R_{down}$$

- **Repeatability (Reproducibility)**: Maximum variation in readings when the same input is applied repeatedly under identical conditions.
$$E_R = R_{max} - R_{min}$$

- **Drift**: Change in output with time at a constant input, often expressed as % per year or ppm/year.

- **Sensitivity** (static): 
$$K = \frac{\Delta \text{Output}}{\Delta \text{Input}}$$

- **Resolution**: Smallest change in input that produces a detectable change in output.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Typical Use |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Absolute Error | $E_{abs} = R - T$ | Same as measured quantity | Direct error magnitude |
| 2 | Relative Error | $E_{rel} = (R-T)/T$ | Dimensionless | Fractional deviation |
| 3 | % Error (of FS) | $E_{\%FS} = (R-T)/T_{FS} \times 100$ | % | Instrument class rating |
| 4 | % Error (of Reading) | $E_{\%R} = (R-T)/T \times 100$ | % | Process variable error |
| 5 | Accuracy | $A = 100 - \vert E_{\%FS} \vert$ | % | Quality metric |
| 6 | Non-linearity | $N_L = (R_{act} - R_{ideal})_{max}/T_{FS} \times 100$ | % | Linearity check |
| 7 | Hysteresis | $E_H = R_{up} - R_{down}$ | Same as measured | Backlash detection |
| 8 | Repeatability | $E_R = R_{max} - R_{min}$ | Same as measured | Precision metric |
| 9 | Sensitivity (static) | $K = \Delta Y / \Delta X$ | Output/Input units | Calibration slope |
| 10 | Test Accuracy Ratio | $TAR = \text{Accuracy}_{\text{UUT}} / \text{Accuracy}_{\text{Std}}$ | Dimensionless | Should be $\geq 4:1$ |
| 11 | Combined Uncertainty (RSS) | $u_c = \sqrt{u_1^2 + u_2^2 + \dots}$ | Same as measured | Uncertainty propagation |
| 12 | Drift Rate | $D = (\Delta R / \Delta t) / T_{FS} \times 100\%$ | % per unit time | Long-term stability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Reminder**: Many students confuse % of Full Scale with % of Reading. A 0–100 V meter with ±1% FS accuracy has a constant error band of ±1 V, while a ±1% RDG meter has an error that scales with the reading. Always read the instrument specification sheet carefully.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Power Meters**: Calibrated using precision wattmeters and reference shunts traceable to NIST/NPL.
- **Temperature Sensors (RTD, Thermocouple)**: Calibrated using fixed-point cells (ice point 0 °C, triple point of water 0.01 °C, gallium melt 29.7646 °C).
- **Pressure Gauges**: Dead-weight testers serve as primary standards.
- **Frequency Counters**: Calibrated against GPS-disciplined rubidium or cesium oscillators.
- **Oscilloscopes**: Calibrated using a calibrator that supplies precision amplitude, rise time, and time markers.
