# 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the term "Gauge Factor" of a strain gauge. What is its typical value for a metallic strain gauge?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Gauge Factor (GF)** of a strain gauge is defined as the ratio of the fractional change in electrical resistance ($\Delta R / R$) to the mechanical strain ($\varepsilon$) applied to the gauge.

$$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$

For a metallic strain gauge (e.g., Constantan), the gauge factor has a typical value of **2.0 to 2.2**. This relatively low value is because the piezoresistive term is negligible in metals, leaving only the geometric contribution:

$$GF_{\text{metal}} \approx 1 + 2\nu$$

where $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio (approximately 0.30 for Constantan).

*(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for the typical value with physical reasoning)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State the working principle of a strain gauge. Why is a Wheatstone bridge used with it?**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle:** A strain gauge works on the principle of **piezoresistivity** — the property by which the electrical resistance of a conductor changes when it is subjected to mechanical strain. The resistance change occurs due to two effects:
1. **Geometric change** — The conductor becomes longer and thinner.
2. **Piezoresistive change** — The intrinsic resistivity of the material changes.

**Why Wheatstone Bridge?** A strain gauge produces an extremely small change in resistance (typically less than 0.1 $\Omega$) when bonded to a specimen. Such a small change is difficult to measure directly with a multimeter. A **Wheatstone bridge** converts this small resistance change into a measurable voltage signal. When the bridge is balanced, the output is zero; any strain unbalances the bridge and produces a voltage proportional to the strain. Additionally, the bridge provides excellent **temperature compensation** when a dummy gauge is used.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for principle, 1 mark for both effects, 1 mark for bridge justification)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, With Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the gauge factor of a strain gauge, clearly explaining the physical significance of each term in the final equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A strain gauge has a nominal resistance of $350 \, \Omega$ and a gauge factor of $2.05$. It is connected in a Wheatstone bridge with the other three arms being fixed $350 \, \Omega$ resistors. The bridge excitation voltage is $6 \, V$. If the gauge experiences a strain of $500 \, \mu\varepsilon$, calculate:
- (i) The change in resistance $\Delta R$
- (ii) The output voltage $V_o$
- (iii) The corresponding stress, given $E = 210 \, GPa$ **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Derivation of Gauge Factor** (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Start with the fundamental resistance formula for a uniform conductor:

$$R = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{A}$$

where $\rho$ is resistivity, $L$ is length, $A$ is cross-sectional area.

**Step 2:** Take natural logarithms:

$$\ln R = \ln \rho + \ln L - \ln A$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate both sides:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $\dfrac{dL}{L} = \varepsilon$ (definition of axial strain):

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon - \frac{dA}{A}$$

**Step 5:** For a circular conductor of radius $r$, $A = \pi r^2$, so $\dfrac{dA}{A} = 2\dfrac{dr}{r}$. By Poisson's ratio, $\dfrac{dr}{r} = -\nu \varepsilon$. Thus:

$$\frac{dA}{A} = -2\nu \varepsilon$$

**Step 6:** Substituting back:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon + 2\nu \varepsilon = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + (1 + 2\nu) \varepsilon$$

**Step 7:** Dividing by $\varepsilon$:

$$\boxed{GF = \frac{dR/R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{d\rho/\rho}{\varepsilon}}$$

**Physical Significance of Each Term:**

| Term | Physical Meaning |
|------|------------------|
| $1$ | **Geometric length effect** — Resistance increases as conductor stretches. |
| $2\nu$ | **Poisson area effect** — Cross-section shrinks, increasing resistance. |
| $\dfrac{\Delta\rho/\rho}{\varepsilon}$ | **Piezoresistive effect** — Intrinsic resistivity changes under stress. |

For **metallic gauges**, the piezoresistive term is negligible ($\approx 0.002$), so $GF \approx 1 + 2\nu \approx 2.0$ to $2.2$.

For **semiconductor gauges**, the piezoresistive term dominates ($\approx 100$ to $200$), giving $GF \approx 100$ to $200$.

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Problem** (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $R = 350 \, \Omega$
- $GF = 2.05$
- $V_{ex} = 6 \, V$
- $\varepsilon = 500 \, \mu\varepsilon = 500 \times 10^{-6} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$
- $E = 210 \, GPa = 210 \times 10^9 \, Pa$

**(i) Change in resistance $\Delta R$:**

Using the relation $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon$:

$$\Delta R = R \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon = 350 \times 2.05 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\Delta R = 350 \times 1.025 \times 10^{-3} = 0.35875 \, \Omega$$

$$\boxed{\Delta R \approx 0.359 \, \Omega}$$

**(ii) Output voltage $V_o$:**

For a quarter-bridge configuration with one active gauge:

$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R}{4R} = \frac{6 \times 0.35875}{4 \times 350}$$

$$V_o = \frac{2.1525}{1400} = 1.5375 \times 10^{-3} \, V$$

$$\boxed{V_o \approx 1.54 \, mV}$$

**Alternative check using direct formula:**

$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}{4} = \frac{6 \times 2.05 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}}{4}$$

$$V_o = \frac{6.15 \times 10^{-3}}{4} = 1.5375 \times 10^{-3} \, V \approx 1.54 \, mV \checkmark$$

**(iii) Corresponding stress:**

Using Hooke's Law:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = (210 \times 10^9) \times (5 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$\sigma = 105 \times 10^6 \, Pa = 105 \, MPa$$

$$\boxed{\sigma = 105 \, MPa}$$

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 - Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a **Wheatstone bridge** used for strain measurement. Derive the expression for the output voltage when one arm contains a single active strain gauge. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **quarter-bridge**, **half-bridge**, and **full-bridge** configurations in terms of sensitivity, temperature compensation, and number of active gauges. A steel cantilever beam is instrumented with a full-bridge circuit using four strain gauges (two on the top surface in tension, two on the bottom surface in compression). The gauges have $GF = 2.0$ and $R = 120 \, \Omega$. The excitation voltage is $5 \, V$. If the output voltage is $2.5 \, mV$, find the strain experienced by the beam. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Wheatstone Bridge Operation and Derivation** (7 marks)

**Circuit Description:** The Wheatstone bridge consists of four resistive arms $R_1$, $R_2$, $R_3$, $R_4$ arranged in a diamond (or rhombus) shape. An excitation voltage $V_{ex}$ is applied between the top node (A) and the bottom node (C). The output voltage $V_o$ is measured between the left node (B) and the right node (D). One of the arms (say $R_1$) is the active strain gauge; the other three are precision fixed resistors.

**Working Principle:**

- In the **unstrained** condition, the bridge is balanced: $R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$. The output voltage $V_o = 0$.
- When **strain** is applied, $R_1$ changes by $\Delta R$, unbalancing the bridge. The output voltage becomes non-zero and proportional to $\Delta R$.

**Derivation:**

The potential at node B (voltage divider across $R_1$ and $R_2$):

$$V_B = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

The potential at node D (voltage divider across $R_3$ and $R_4$):

$$V_D = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}$$

The output voltage is:

$$V_o = V_B - V_D = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right)$$

For an initially balanced bridge with $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$, and applying strain only to $R_1$ (so $R_1 = R + \Delta R$):

$$V_o = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R}{2R + \Delta R} - \frac{1}{2} \right)$$

For small $\Delta R$ (using the approximation $\dfrac{1}{2+x} \approx \dfrac{1}{2} - \dfrac{x}{4}$ for small $x$):

$$V_o \approx \frac{V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R}{4R}$$

Substituting $\Delta R = R \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$:

$$\boxed{V_o = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}{4}}$$

---

#### **Part (b) — Bridge Comparison and Full-Bridge Calculation** (7 marks)

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Quarter-Bridge | Half-Bridge | Full-Bridge |
|-----------|----------------|-------------|-------------|
| Active Gauges | 1 | 2 | 4 |
| Sensitivity Factor | $1/4$ | $1/2$ | $1$ |
| Temperature Compensation | External dummy gauge needed | Built-in | Built-in |
| Non-linearity Error | Highest | Moderate | Lowest |
| Cost | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |

**Numerical Calculation for Full-Bridge:**

**Given:**
- Full-bridge configuration: 4 active gauges
- $GF = 2.0$
- $R = 120 \, \Omega$
- $V_{ex} = 5 \, V$
- $V_o = 2.5 \, mV = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \, V$

**Formula for full-bridge output:**

$$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

Solving for $\varepsilon$:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{V_o}{V_{ex} \cdot GF} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 2.0}$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{10} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = 250 \, \mu\varepsilon}$$

**Verification using the quarter-bridge equivalent:**

For a full-bridge, the sensitivity is $4\times$ that of a quarter-bridge. So the equivalent single-gauge output for the same strain would be:

$$V_o^{\text{quarter}} = \frac{2.5 \, mV}{4} = 0.625 \, mV$$

Using the quarter-bridge formula:

$$0.625 \times 10^{-3} = \frac{5 \times 2.0 \times \varepsilon}{4} = 2.5 \cdot \varepsilon$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{0.625 \times 10^{-3}}{2.5} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} = 250 \, \mu\varepsilon \checkmark$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Students often forget to convert $\mu\varepsilon$ to dimensionless strain. Always write $\varepsilon = 500 \times 10^{-6}$ before substituting. Failure to do this results in off-by-a-factor-of-$10^6$ errors.
>
> 2. **Wrong Bridge Formula:** Using $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$ when the problem states a **quarter-bridge** (single active gauge) costs 2 marks. Always check how many gauges are active.
>
> 3. **Sign of Output:** The output voltage $V_o$ can be positive or negative depending on the direction of strain. Examiners look for $|V_o|$ unless polarity is specified.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Factor of 4:** In a quarter-bridge, students often write $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$ instead of $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}{4}$. This is the most common error — memorize the factor of 4!
>
> 5. **Poisson's Ratio Direction Error:** The relation $\dfrac{dA}{A} = -2\nu \cdot \dfrac{dL}{L}$ has a **negative sign**. Dropping this sign gives a sign error in the gauge factor derivation, costing 2 marks.
>
> 6. **Confusing $V_{ex}$ with $V_o$:** In numerical problems, $V_{ex}$ (excitation) is always much larger than $V_o$ (output). The ratio $V_o / V_{ex}$ is on the order of $10^{-4}$ for typical strains. Sanity-check your answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 Rapid Revision Checklist — Strain Gauge**

- ✅ A **strain gauge** is a passive resistive transducer that converts mechanical strain into a change in electrical resistance.
- ✅ Strain $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ (dimensionless, often in $\mu\varepsilon$).
- ✅ The **Gauge Factor** $GF = (\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon$ is the key sensitivity parameter.
- ✅ Master equation: $GF = 1 + 2\nu + (\Delta\rho/\rho)/\varepsilon$ — memorize the three terms.
- ✅ Metallic gauges: $GF \approx 2.0$ to $2.2$ (piezoresistive term negligible).
- ✅ Semiconductor gauges: $GF \approx 100$ to $200$ (piezoresistive term dominant).
- ✅ Constantan (Cu-Ni alloy) is the most common gauge material due to low temperature coefficient of resistance.
- ✅ A **Wheatstone bridge** converts the tiny $\Delta R$ into a measurable voltage $V_o$.
- ✅ **Quarter-bridge:** $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon / 4$ (1 active gauge).
- ✅ **Half-bridge:** $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon / 2$ (2 active gauges).
- ✅ **Full-bridge:** $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$ (4 active gauges, 4× sensitivity).
- ✅ A **dummy gauge** on an unstrained specimen provides temperature compensation.
- ✅ **Hooke's Law:** $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ links measured strain to engineering stress.
- ✅ Typical gauge resistance values: $120 \, \Omega$, $350 \, \Omega$, $1000 \, \Omega$.
- ✅ Strain gauge applications: load cells, pressure sensors, torque sensors, structural health monitoring, fatigue analysis.
- ✅ **Major pitfall in exams:** Forgetting the divisor of 4 in the quarter-bridge output equation.
- ✅ **Poisson's ratio sign convention:** $\dfrac{dA}{A} = -2\nu \cdot \varepsilon$ — the negative sign is essential.
- ✅ For a metallic gauge with $\nu = 0.33$, the gauge factor $GF = 1 + 2(0.33) = 1.66$, which is close to the standard 2.0 (slight piezoresistive contribution included).
- ✅ Bonded gauges use epoxy/cyanoacrylate adhesive; unbonded gauges use mechanical clamping.
- ✅ In the **transverse direction**, a strain gauge should ideally have zero sensitivity (practical gauges have $\approx 1\%$ transverse sensitivity).
- ✅ The **gauge length** affects spatial resolution — shorter gauges measure more localized strain.
- ✅ KTU 2024 weightage: Strain gauge is a **high-yield topic** appearing in 2-mark definitions, 7-mark derivations, and 14-mark numerical problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: L.V.D.T. (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST521 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** L.V.D.T. (Linear Variable Differential Transformer) # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)** is a **passive, electromechanical, inductive transducer** that converts **linear displacement** (a mechanical input) into a proportional **differential AC voltage output** (electrical signal). It operates on the principle of mutual induction between a primary winding and two secondary windings, the coupling of which is varied by the axial movement of a ferromagnetic core (also called an armature). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** LVDT is classified as a **transducer** (not a sensor) because it requires an external AC excitation (typically **5 V RMS at 50 Hz to 20 kHz**) to operate, and is widely listed under *passive inductive transducers* in the instrumentation module. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **screwdriver-and-nut** mechanism: - A standard transformer has a **fixed** iron core — power flows from primary to secondary, and the output is constant. - Now imagine the same transformer, but the **iron core is mounted on a spring-loaded rod** (like a screwdriver sliding in and out of a nut). - When the rod moves **right**, the iron core enters **Coil A** more, increasing magnetic coupling with A. - When the rod moves **left**, it enters **Coil B** more, increasing magnetic coupling with B. - The **difference** between the two outputs tells you *how far* and *in which direction* the rod moved. This is exactly how an LVDT works — a **"smart" transformer whose core slides linearly**, and whose output is the **arithmetic difference** between two identical secondary windings. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Metric | Standard Value | |---|---| | Excitation frequency | **400 Hz to 20 kHz** (commonly **1 kHz to 5 kHz**) | | Excitation voltage | **1 V to 24 V RMS** | | Operating temperature | **−55 °C to +150 °C** (military-grade up to **+600 °C**) | | Linearity | **±0.25 %** of full scale (high-grade units) | | Resolution | **Infinite** (limited by readout electronics) | | Core material | **Soft magnetic material** (Nickel–Iron alloy, e.g., Mu-metal or 50-50 NiFe) | > [!NOTE] > The **LVDT is a frictionless device** — the core never touches the windings, eliminating mechanical wear and giving it theoretically infinite life. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Constructional Anatomy (Step-by-Step Logic) The LVDT consists of the following physical components: 1. **Primary Winding (P):** Wound centrally on a hollow cylindrical former; energized by an external AC excitation source. 2. **Two Identical Secondary Windings (S₁ and S₂):** Wound symmetrically on either side of the primary, with **equal number of turns (N_s)** and wound in **series opposition** (i.e., their EMFs subtract). 3. **Movable Ferromagnetic Core:** A high-permeability cylindrical slug (Ni–Fe alloy) that slides coaxially inside the former, mechanically coupled to the displacement being measured. 4. **Coil Former / Bobbin:** A non-magnetic support that holds the windings in precise spatial alignment. > [!IMPORTANT] > The two secondaries are connected in **series opposition** (anti-phasor connection). This is the core reason the LVDT produces a *differential* output. ## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic **Step 1 — Null Position (Core Centered):** - The core sits symmetrically between S₁ and S₂. - Mutual inductance between P → S₁ equals mutual inductance between P → S₂. - Induced EMFs: **E₁ = E₂**. - Output: **V_out = E₁ − E₂ = 0** (theoretically). **Step 2 — Core Displaced Toward S₁ (Forward Displacement):** - Flux linkage with S₁ **increases** → **E₁ increases**. - Flux linkage with S₂ **decreases** → **E₂ decreases**. - Output: **V_out = E₁ − E₂ > 0** (in-phase with primary voltage). **Step 3 — Core Displaced Toward S₂ (Reverse Displacement):** - Flux linkage with S₂ **increases** → **E₂ increases**. - Flux linkage with S₁ **decreases** → **E₁ decreases**. - Output: **V_out = E₁ − E₂ < 0** (180° phase shift with respect to primary). **Step 4 — Phase-Sensitive Demodulation:** - The magnitude of V_out is proportional to displacement magnitude. - The **phase** of V_out (with respect to the primary excitation) indicates the **direction** of displacement. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Quantity | Equation | Units | Description | |---|---|---|---| | Primary voltage | $V_p = V_{excitation} \angle 0^{\circ}$ | V (RMS) | Excitation input | | Secondary induced EMF (S₁) | $E_1 = j \omega N_s \, \dfrac{d\phi_1}{dt}$ | V | Proportional to flux $\phi_1$ | | Secondary induced EMF (S₂) | $E_2 = j \omega N_s \, \dfrac{d\phi_2}{dt}$ | V | Proportional to flux $\phi_2$ | | Differential output voltage | $V_{out} = E_1 - E_2$ | V (RMS) | Net signal at output terminals | | Output at null (center) | $V_{out} = 0$ | V | When core is geometrically centered | | Output (displaced) | $V_{out} = K \cdot x$ | V | Linear relation; $K$ = sensitivity (V/unit) | | Sensitivity | $K = \dfrac{V_{out,full}}{X_{full}}$ | V/mm | Output per unit displacement | | Mutual inductance relation | $M_1 = \dfrac{N_1 \phi_1}{I_p}$ | H | Fundamental linkage | | General transformer EMF | $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \phi_{max}$ | V | Standard transformer equation | > [!NOTE] > The **linearity region** of an LVDT spans approximately **±5 mm** (miniature) to **±500 mm** (industrial) from the null position. Beyond this, the output saturates and becomes non-linear. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility LVDTs are deployed in: - **Aircraft flight-control surface position feedback** (Boeing, Airbus). - **Hydraulic cylinder piston position** in earth-moving equipment. - **Nuclear reactor control rod position** monitoring. - **Civil engineering** — structural displacement of bridges and dams. - **Manufacturing** — automated tool-setting on CNC machines. - **Scientific** — dilatometry and creep testing in materials science. The **frictionless, contactless** nature makes it ideal for **high-reliability, long-life, high-vibration, and hazardous-environment** measurements. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of LVDT Output Voltage Equation ### Setup and Assumptions Let: - $V_p$ = RMS primary excitation voltage. - $N_p$ = number of primary turns. - $N_s$ = number of turns on each secondary winding. - $f$ = frequency of AC excitation. - $\phi$ = mutual flux linking primary and secondary. By the standard transformer EMF equation: $$ E_{sec} = 4.44 \, f \, N_s \, \phi_{max} $$ ### Step 1 — Mutual Inductance at Null Position When the core is at the **null (central) position**, the core is equidistant from both secondaries. Hence the mutual inductance is symmetric: $$ M_1 = M_2 = M $$ Therefore, the EMFs induced in both secondaries are equal: $$ E_1 = j \omega M \, I_p = E_2 $$ ### Step 2 — Output at Null Since the two secondaries are connected in **series opposition**: $$ V_{out} = E_1 - E_2 = 0 $$ The output is theoretically zero. (In practice, a small **residual voltage (V_res)** exists due to imperfect symmetry, called the **null voltage** or **quadrature voltage**.) ### Step 3 — Core Displaced by Distance $x$ Toward S₁ Let us parameterize the flux linkage linearly with displacement. The change in flux linkage in each secondary is proportional to the displacement of the core from the center: $$ \phi_1 = \phi_0 \left( 1 + \dfrac{x}{X} \right) $$ $$ \phi_2 = \phi_0 \left( 1 - \dfrac{x}{X} \right) $$ where $X$ is the **half-range** of the LVDT, and $\phi_0$ is the flux at null. ### Step 4 — Induced EMFs in the Displaced State Substituting into the transformer EMF equation: $$ E_1 = 4.44 \, f \, N_s \, \phi_0 \left( 1 + \dfrac{x}{X} \right) $$ $$ E_2 = 4.44 \, f \, N_s \, \phi_0 \left( 1 - \dfrac{x}{X} \right) $$ ### Step 5 — Differential Output Subtracting (series-opposition connection): $$ V_{out} = E_1 - E_2 $$ $$ V_{out} = 4.44 \, f \, N_s \, \phi_0 \left[ \left( 1 + \dfrac{x}{X} \right) - \left( 1 - \dfrac{x}{X} \right) \right] $$ The "1" terms cancel: $$ V_{out} = 4.44 \, f \, N_s \, \phi_0 \cdot \dfrac{2x}{X} $$ ### Step 6 — Final Compact Form Define the **sensitivity constant** $K$: $$ K = \dfrac{8.88 \, f \, N_s \, \phi_0}{X} $$ Therefore, the final linear equation is: $$ \boxed{\,V_{out} = K \cdot x\,} $$ This is the **golden equation** for the LVDT — output is **linearly proportional** to displacement. The proportionality constant $K$ depends on design parameters (turns, frequency, core flux, geometry). ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard) > **Problem:** An LVDT has a primary winding of 2000 turns and two secondaries of 1000 turns each. The primary is excited by 5 V RMS at 1 kHz. If the core moves 2 mm from the null and the full-scale range is 10 mm, calculate the differential output voltage. Assume ideal coupling. **Solution:** Given: - $N_p = 2000$, $N_s = 1000$, $V_p = 5$ V, $f = 1000$ Hz, $x = 2$ mm, $X = 5$ mm (half of 10 mm full range). Step 1 — Compute turns ratio: $$ a = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} = \dfrac{1000}{2000} = 0.5 $$ Step 2 — Compute ideal secondary EMF at null (for reference): $$ E_0 = a \cdot V_p = 0.5 \times 5 = 2.5 \text{ V (RMS)} $$ Step 3 — Apply the displacement equation: $$ V_{out} = E_0 \cdot \dfrac{2x}{X} = 2.5 \times \dfrac{2 \times 2}{5} $$ $$ V_{out} = 2.5 \times \dfrac{4}{5} = 2.5 \times 0.8 = 2.0 \text{ V (RMS)} $$ > **Result:** $V_{out} = 2.0$ V RMS, **in-phase** with the primary excitation (assuming forward displacement). ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Operating States of the LVDT | Core Position | $\phi_1$ vs. $\phi_2$ | $E_1$ vs. $E_2$ | $V_{out} = E_1 - E_2$ | Phase of $V_{out}$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Far left (out of range S₁)** | $\phi_1 \approx 0$ | $E_1 \approx 0$ | $V_{out} \approx -E_2$ (large negative) | 180° (out of phase) | | **Midway S₂** | $\phi_1 < \phi_2$ | $E_1 < E_2$ | $V_{out} < 0$ | 180° | | **Null (center)** | $\phi_1 = \phi_2$ | $E_1 = E_2$ | $V_{out} = 0$ | — | | **Midway S₁** | $\phi_1 > \phi_2$ | $E_1 > E_2$ | $V_{out} > 0$ | 0° (in phase) | | **Far right (out of range S₂)** | $\phi_2 \approx 0$ | $E_2 \approx 0$ | $V_{out} \approx +E_1$ (large positive) | 0° (in phase) | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Physical Construction Schematic (Mermaid Block Architecture) The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of an LVDT, its excitation chain, and signal-conditioning interface — the standard KTU expectation for instrumentation topics. ```mermaid flowchart TB OSC["AC OSCILLATOR
5 V RMS 1 kHz to 5 kHz"] PRI["PRIMARY WINDING
N_p turns centered on former"] SEC1["SECONDARY S1
N_s turns LEFT
wound in series opposition"] SEC2["SECONDARY S2
N_s turns RIGHT
wound in series opposition"] CORE["FERROMAGNETIC CORE
Ni-Fe cylindrical slug
mechanically coupled to target"] SIG["DIFFERENTIAL OUTPUT
V_out = E1 - E2"] PSD["PHASE SENSITIVE
DEMODULATOR"] DC["DC OUTPUT SIGNAL
V_dc proportional to x"] OSC --> PRI PRI --> SEC1 PRI --> SEC2 CORE -.-> SEC1 CORE -.-> SEC2 SEC1 --> SIG SEC2 --> SIG SIG --> PSD PSD --> DC ``` ## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Constructional View (Mermaid Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["FORMER
(non-magnetic
cylindrical bobbin)"] B["SECONDARY S1
(N_s turns, wound on former)"] C["PRIMARY P
(N_p turns, center)"] D["SECONDARY S2
(N_s turns, wound on former)"] E["MOVABLE CORE
(soft magnetic slug)"] F["TARGET /
MECHANICAL
DISPLACEMENT INPUT"] G["SCREENING /
SHIELD CASING"] F -->|displaces| E E -.->|slides inside| A B -->|adjacent to| A C -->|adjacent to| A D -->|adjacent to| A G ---|encloses| A ``` ## 4.3 Signal-Conditioning Chain (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB LVDT["LVDT SENSOR
produces V_out proportional to x"] AMP["INSTRUMENTATION
AMPLIFIER
(high CMRR)"] DEMOD["PHASE-SENSITIVE
DETECTOR (PSD)"] LPF["LOW-PASS FILTER
(smoothing)"] ADC["ADC / DISPLAY
(digital readout)"] LVDT --> AMP --> DEMOD --> LPF --> ADC ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Tip:** A common exam ask is to draw the **LVDT with a phase-sensitive demodulator** (also called a *synchronous detector*). It is **mandatory** to label the **reference signal** from the oscillator feeding into the PSD, because this reference is what determines the **sign** (direction) of the displacement. ## 4.4 Characteristics Curve (Output vs. Displacement) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph LINEAR["LINEAR OPERATING REGION"] direction LR X1["x = -X"] --> Y1["V_out = -K·X (180 deg)"] X2["x = 0"] --> Y2["V_out = 0 V (null)"] X3["x = +X"] --> Y3["V_out = +K·X (0 deg)"] end ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define LVDT. List any two of its advantages.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Definition (2 Marks):** > A Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT) is a passive inductive transducer used to convert linear displacement into a proportional electrical (AC voltage) signal. It consists of one primary winding and two secondary windings wound on a cylindrical former, with a movable ferromagnetic core. The output is the **differential** voltage between the two secondaries, connected in series opposition. > **Any two advantages (1 Mark):** > 1. **Infinite resolution** — being contactless, it has no friction, hence no mechanical wear; the only resolution limit is the external signal conditioner. > 2. **High sensitivity** — capable of resolving displacements as small as **0.1 micrometres** with proper excitation. > 3. **Robust and reliable** — operates over wide temperature ranges, immune to harsh environments, and has a long operational life. > 4. **Bidirectional measurement** — the phase of the output indicates the direction of displacement. ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **What is the function of the ferromagnetic core in an LVDT?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The ferromagnetic core (typically a nickel–iron alloy such as **Mu-metal**) serves as a **flux path controller**. As the core moves linearly inside the former: - It **channels the magnetic flux** from the primary winding into either S₁ or S₂, depending on its axial position. - At the **null position**, the flux is divided equally between the two secondaries, making the induced EMFs equal. - When displaced, the core **asymmetrically couples** the primary flux — increasing coupling with one secondary and decreasing with the other — thereby producing a **differential output voltage** proportional to the displacement. In essence, the core acts as a **"magnetic tap"** that samples the flux distribution between the two halves of the magnetic circuit. --- ## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 — Model Question Paper Module 4]** **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working principle of an LVDT**. Derive the expression for its output voltage. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the **role of a phase-sensitive demodulator** in an LVDT measurement system. How does it determine the **direction of displacement**? **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks **Construction (3 Marks):** - A hollow cylindrical **former** of non-magnetic material holds three windings: **one primary (P)** at the centre and **two secondaries (S₁ and S₂)** symmetrically placed on either side. - S₁ and S₂ have an **equal number of turns (N_s)** and are connected in **series opposition**. - A high-permeability **ferromagnetic core** (Ni–Fe alloy) slides coaxially inside the former and is mechanically coupled to the displacement being measured. - The whole assembly is enclosed in a **magnetic shield** to prevent external interference. **Working Principle (2 Marks):** - The primary is energized by an **AC excitation** ($V_p$, typically 1–5 kHz). - This produces an alternating flux in the core. - At the **null (centre) position**, the flux coupling to S₁ and S₂ is equal, so the induced EMFs are equal, and the **differential output is zero**. - When the core displaces by $+x$ towards S₁: $E_1$ increases, $E_2$ decreases → $V_{out} = E_1 - E_2 > 0$, **in phase** with the primary. - When the core displaces by $-x$ towards S₂: $E_1$ decreases, $E_2$ increases → $V_{out} = E_1 - E_2 < 0$, **180° out of phase** with the primary. **Derivation (2 Marks):** Using the transformer EMF equation and the linear approximation of flux vs. displacement, the output voltage is: $$ V_{out} = 4.44 \, f \, N_s \, \phi_0 \cdot \dfrac{2x}{X} = K \cdot x $$ where $K$ is the sensitivity constant. The output is **linearly proportional** to displacement $x$. #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Role of Phase-Sensitive Demodulator (PSD) (4 Marks):** - The LVDT output is a **modulated AC signal** of the same frequency as the excitation. - A **phase-sensitive demodulator (PSD)** — also called a *synchronous detector* — multiplies the LVDT output by a **reference signal** derived from the same oscillator driving the primary. - The product is then passed through a **low-pass filter** to obtain a **DC output voltage** proportional to displacement: - $V_{dc} = K' \cdot x$ - The **magnitude** of the DC output indicates the **magnitude of displacement**. - The **polarity (sign)** of the DC output indicates the **direction** of displacement. **Direction Detection (3 Marks):** - If the LVDT output is **in phase** with the reference, the multiplier yields a **positive DC** value → **forward displacement**. - If the LVDT output is **180° out of phase** with the reference, the multiplier yields a **negative DC** value → **reverse displacement**. - A **null position** gives a DC output of **zero volts**. - The PSD thus converts an AC amplitude-and-phase signal into a **signed DC voltage** suitable for display, recording, or digital conversion. --- ### QUESTION B — (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative* **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 — Alternative Pattern]** **(a)** Draw the **block diagram of an LVDT measurement system** and explain each block in detail. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **advantages, disadvantages, and applications** of the LVDT. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks **Block Diagram (3 Marks):** ``` [LVDT Sensor] → [Instrumentation Amplifier] → [Phase-Sensitive Demodulator] → [Low-Pass Filter] → [Display / Recorder] ↑ ↑ ↑ [Mechanical [Reference signal from same oscillator] Displacement x] ``` **Explanation of Each Block (4 Marks):** 1. **LVDT Sensor:** Produces a differential AC voltage $V_{out} = E_1 - E_2$ proportional to the displacement $x$ of the core. The signal is small (millivolts to a few volts) and is the raw measurement. 2. **Oscillator (Excitation Source):** Provides a stable, low-distortion AC sine wave (typically 1–5 kHz, 5 V RMS) to energize the primary winding. Frequency stability directly affects measurement accuracy. 3. **Instrumentation Amplifier:** Amplifies the weak differential LVDT output to a usable level. Has high **CMRR** (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio) to reject common-mode noise picked up in the cable run from the sensor. 4. **Phase-Sensitive Demodulator:** As explained in Q.A(b), it recovers a signed DC voltage whose magnitude and sign correspond to displacement magnitude and direction. 5. **Low-Pass Filter:** Removes residual AC ripple and noise from the demodulated DC signal, providing a clean output. 6. **Display / Recorder / ADC:** The final conditioned signal is shown on a meter, recorded on a chart, or digitized for further processing. #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Advantages (3 Marks):** 1. **Frictionless operation** — no mechanical contact, hence **infinite resolution** and long life. 2. **High sensitivity** — can detect displacements of the order of **micrometres**. 3. **Linearity** — output is linear over a wide range of core displacement. 4. **Robust** — operates in extreme environments (high temperature, pressure, radiation). 5. **Bidirectional** — the output phase indicates displacement direction natively. 6. **No hysteresis** — the magnetic core does not exhibit remanent magnetization-induced hysteresis in the operating range. **Disadvantages (2 Marks):** 1. **Requires AC excitation** — cannot operate on DC; needs an external oscillator. 2. **Susceptible to external magnetic fields** — must be shielded. 3. **Limited range** — linearity holds only over a finite core travel (typical ±5 mm to ±100 mm). 4. **Dynamic measurements are tricky** — at high displacement speeds, the LVDT behaves as a velocity transducer (induced voltage is proportional to $d\phi/dt$, hence to $dx/dt$), not a position transducer. 5. **Null voltage / quadrature voltage** — small residual output exists at null due to imperfect symmetry, which must be compensated. **Applications (2 Marks):** - Aircraft and missile control surface position feedback. - Hydraulic and pneumatic cylinder piston position sensing. - Machine tool positioning on CNC equipment. - Civil engineering — structural health monitoring (bridge pylon displacement). - Nuclear reactor control-rod position. - Scientific instruments — dilatometers, creep-test rigs. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the "series-opposition" connection** of the two secondaries — this is the *defining* feature of an LVDT and is worth 1–2 marks. > 2. **Confusing "sensitivity" and "resolution"** — sensitivity is V per mm; resolution is the smallest detectable increment. > 3. **Skipping the derivation of $V_{out} = K \cdot x$** — KTU expects the algebraic steps (mutual inductance → flux terms → series-opposition subtraction). > 4. **Not labelling the phase relationship** (in-phase vs. 180° out-of-phase) when the core moves in opposite directions. > 5. **Drawing the LVDT as a generic transformer** without showing the movable core — the core *must* be drawn and labelled. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **LVDT** = **L**inear **V**ariable **D**ifferential **T**ransformer — converts **linear displacement** to a **differential AC voltage**. - It is a **passive inductive transducer** requiring an **external AC excitation** (typically 1–5 kHz, 5 V RMS). - **Construction:** one **primary** at centre, two **identically wound secondaries** (S₁ and S₂) on either side, connected in **series opposition**, with a **movable ferromagnetic core** (Ni–Fe alloy). - **Working:** at **null**, $E_1 = E_2$ → $V_{out} = 0$. At **displacement $x$**, flux becomes asymmetric → $V_{out} \neq 0$. - **Phase of output** indicates **direction**; **magnitude of output** indicates **amount** of displacement. - **Golden equation:** $V_{out} = K \cdot x$ (linear in the design range). - **Sensitivity** $K$ depends on frequency $f$, secondary turns $N_s$, core flux $\phi_0$, and geometry. - **Null voltage** is the small non-zero residual output at null — must be **nulled** via a phase-sensitive demodulator. - **Phase-Sensitive Demodulator (PSD)** is mandatory for **direction sensing** and DC output. - **Advantages:** frictionless, infinite resolution, high sensitivity, robust, bidirectional, no hysteresis. - **Disadvantages:** requires AC excitation, limited linear range, susceptible to external fields, complex signal conditioning. - **Applications:** aerospace, hydraulics, CNC, civil structural monitoring, nuclear reactors, scientific instruments. - **Frequency range:** use **higher excitation frequency** for **dynamic (vibratory) measurements** to avoid 50 Hz mains interference. - **Material of core:** soft magnetic Ni-Fe alloy (high permeability, low coercivity) — **never** use hard magnetic materials. - **Difference between LVDT and RVDT:** RVDT measures **rotational (angular)** displacement; LVDT measures **linear** displacement — both work on the same principle. - **Static and dynamic use:** LVDT works for both, but at high frequencies the output is proportional to **velocity**, not position — demodulation techniques must be applied.

topics.title: Application of Various Instruments in Shipbuilding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST521 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Application of Various Instruments in Shipbuilding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Marine Instrumentation** in shipbuilding refers to the integrated suite of electrical, electronic, and electromechanical devices installed onboard a vessel to monitor, control, automate, and protect all critical shipboard systems — ranging from bridge navigation and engine room machinery to cargo handling, hull-stress monitoring, and onboard safety networks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "The aggregate of measuring instruments, sensors, transducers, signal conditioners, recorders, displays, and automatic control devices used in the design, construction, testing, and operational lifetime of a ship is collectively termed **Ship Instrumentation**. It spans the three phases of ship life: **Construction Phase (Yard)**, **Pre-Commissioning Phase (Sea Trials)**, and **Operational Phase (Sailing)**."

In the **2024 NEP-aligned scheme**, the course outcome mapping for this topic typically falls under:

| CO Code | Course Outcome | Cognitive Level (Bloom's) |
|---|---|---|
| **CO4** | Apply electrical and electronic instrumentation principles to industrial/strategic domains such as shipbuilding, power plants, and process industries. | Apply / Analyze |
| **CO5** | Evaluate the suitability of specific instruments for hostile environments (marine, thermal, corrosive). | Evaluate |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Ship as a Floating Industrial City"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a large ocean-going vessel as a *self-contained, mobile industrial township*. Just as a modern city needs electricity, water, traffic signals, fire alarms, sewage monitoring, and communication towers, a ship requires:
>
> - **Traffic signals** → Radar, GPS, ECDIS (Electronic Chart Display)
> - **Electricity grid** → Marine switchboards, generators, UPS systems
> - **Fire alarms** → Smoke/heat/flame detectors, fire-fighting pump controls
> - **Water supply monitor** → Level sensors, flow meters in tanks
> - **Sewage system** → Bilge level alarms, oily-water separator monitors
> - **Communication tower** → GMDSS, VHF radio, AIS
> - **Building inspection** → Strain gauges, vibration monitors on the hull
>
> The *instrumentation engineer* is the **"city planner of the ship"** — every signal, every alarm, every control loop must work in a **corrosive, vibrating, salt-spray, and rolling environment** that no land-based industry faces simultaneously.

## 1.3 Why Instrumentation is Critical in Shipbuilding

A modern vessel (e.g., a Very Large Crude Carrier, VLCC, or a Naval Frigate) is one of the most instrument-dense moving structures in existence. Justification for extensive instrumentation:

1. **SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea) Compliance** — IMO-mandated instruments.
2. **Class Society Rules** — Det Norske Veritas (DNV), Lloyd's Register, Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS).
3. **Unmanned Machinery Space (UMS) operation** — Engine rooms run 24×7 with no human presence.
4. **Hull monitoring under cyclic wave loading** — millions of stress cycles per voyage.
5. **Cargo safety** — especially for oil, LNG, and chemicals (inert gas, gas detection, tank level).
6. **Weapons and combat readiness** — for naval ships (fire control radar, sonar, CMS).

## 1.4 Classification of Shipboard Instruments

| Category | Examples | Phase of Ship Life |
|---|---|---|
| **Navigation & Bridge** | Gyrocompass, Radar, GPS, ECDIS, AIS, Echo Sounder, Autopilot | Operational |
| **Engine Room** | Pressure, Temperature, RPM, Flow, Level, Viscosity, SO₂ sensors | Operational + Trials |
| **Electrical Power** | Generator panels, AVR, Power Management System (PMS), Insulation monitors | All phases |
| **Steering Gear** | Rudder angle indicator, feedback tiller, hydraulic pressure sensor | Operational |
| **Cargo & Ballast** | Tank level (radar/pressure), valve position, draft gauges | Operational |
| **Hull Integrity** | Strain gauges, accelerometers, fatigue monitoring | Sea Trials + Operational |
| **Safety & Fire** | Smoke/heat detectors, gas detectors (CH₄, H₂S), sprinkler flow switches | Operational |
| **Communication** | GMDSS, VHF, INMARSAT, sound-powered telephones | Operational |
| **Yard/Construction** | Welding current monitors, plate alignment lasers, paint thickness gauges | Construction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marine environmental standard:** All shipboard instruments must comply with **IEC 60945** (Maritime navigation and radiocommunication equipment – General requirements) and the relevant sections of **IP rating IPX6 / IPX7** for splash and immersion resistance.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Navigation & Bridge Instruments

### 2.1.1 Gyrocompass
- **Principle:** A fast-spinning rotor (≈ 24,000 rpm) experiences a precessional torque due to Earth's rotation, aligning its spin axis with the **true meridian (True North)**.
- **Why it is needed at sea:** Magnetic compasses are unreliable due to ship's steel hull, cargo, and electrical interference.
- **Master–Slave arrangement:** The master gyrocompass on the bridge drives 5–10 repeater compasses throughout the ship (wings, engine room, chart room).

### 2.1.2 Marine Radar
- **Principle:** Pulse-echo ranging. A magnetron transmits a microwave pulse (typically **X-band: 9.4 GHz, wavelength 3 cm** or **S-band: 3 GHz, wavelength 10 cm**); the time taken for the echo to return from a target gives its **range**.
- **Two-band strategy:** X-band gives high resolution but is attenuated by rain; S-band penetrates rain and is used for long-range detection in heavy weather.

### 2.1.3 GPS (Global Positioning System)
- The ship's GPS receiver triangulates position from ≥ 4 satellites using **trilateration** based on time-of-flight of ranging signals.

### 2.1.4 Echo Sounder
- A transducer on the hull bottom transmits an ultrasonic pulse (typically **50 kHz or 200 kHz**) downward. The time of return gives **depth of water** below the keel.
- **Critical during berthing** in shallow harbours and during river navigation.

### 2.1.5 ECDIS (Electronic Chart Display & Information System)
- Integrates GPS + gyrocompass + radar + echo sounder into a digital chart, satisfying IMO carriage requirements since 2018.

### 2.1.6 AIS (Automatic Identification System)
- VHF-based transponder that automatically broadcasts the ship's identity, position, course, and speed to nearby vessels and shore stations every **2–10 seconds**.

---

## 2.2 Engine Room Instruments

### 2.2.1 Cylinder Pressure Indicator
- Measures peak pressure inside a diesel engine cylinder; used during **sea trials** to confirm rated power.

### 2.2.2 Exhaust Gas Temperature (EGT) Pyrometer
- Thermocouple (Type K, Chromel–Alumel) at each cylinder exhaust. Unequal EGTs indicate faulty fuel injectors or valve issues.

### 2.2.3 Lubricating Oil Pressure & Temperature
- Bourdon-tube pressure gauge + RTD (Pt-100) for temperature.

### 2.2.4 Jacket Cooling Water & Sea Water Flow
- Orifice plate + differential pressure transmitter; or magnetic flow meter for sea-water lines (electrode-based, no moving parts — ideal for corrosive brine).

### 2.2.5 Fuel Viscosity Controller
- A viscometer continuously measures HFO (Heavy Fuel Oil) viscosity; a controller regulates the steam-heated coil to keep viscosity at the injector-friendly value (typically **15–25 cSt at 100 °C**).

### 2.2.6 SO₂ / NOₓ Analyser
- For vessels operating inside **ECA (Emission Control Areas)**, exhaust gas analysers monitor SO₂ content to ensure compliance with **MARPOL Annex VI** (0.1% S limit).

---

## 2.3 Hull & Structural Monitoring (Construction & Trials Phase)

### 2.3.1 Strain Gauges
- Foil-type bonded resistance strain gauges (e.g., HBM, Vishay) welded/glued to the hull at high-stress points (deck-to-side knuckles, bilge struts).
- **Quarter-bridge or full-bridge configurations** with temperature compensation.
- Used during the **inclining experiment** and **fatigue trials**.

### 2.3.2 Inclining Experiment
- A series of known weights (≈ 10–50 tonnes) are shifted across the deck; the resulting heeling angle (measured by a U-tube or inclinometer) is used to compute **GM (metacentric height)**:

$$GM = \frac{W \cdot d \cdot \cos\theta}{W \cdot \tan\theta} \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta}$$

where:
- $W$ = shifting weight
- $d$ = transverse distance of shift
- $\theta$ = heeling angle
- $\Delta$ = displacement (tonnes)

### 2.3.3 Shaft RPM & Torsionmeter
- Strain gauges on intermediate shaft measure torsional stress; a magnetic pickup on a toothed wheel measures RPM.

---

## 2.4 Electrical Power & Control Instruments

### 2.4.1 Power Management System (PMS)
- A PLC/DCS-based system that automatically starts/stops diesel generators based on **load-dependent** and **preferential-trip** logic.
- **Preferential trips:** non-essential loads (e.g., galley, air-conditioning) are shed first to protect the main bus.

### 2.4.2 Automatic Voltage Regulator (AVR)
- Senses generator terminal voltage and adjusts the **DC field current** of the synchronous generator to maintain voltage within ± 2.5% of nominal (typically **440 V, 60 Hz** or **380 V, 50 Hz**).

### 2.4.3 Insulation Resistance Monitor (IR Monitor)
- A continuous line-to-ground insulation monitor (e.g., Bender IRDH series) detects gradual insulation deterioration before it becomes a short circuit — critical on ship because high humidity + salt + vibration degrade insulation.

### 2.4.4 Steering Gear Feedback
- A rotary potentiometer or absolute encoder on the rudder stock provides **rudder angle feedback** to the autopilot and the rudder-angle indicator on the bridge.

---

## 2.5 Safety, Fire & Bilge Instruments

| Sensor | Physical Principle | Location |
|---|---|---|
| **Smoke detector (optical)** | Light scattering by smoke particles | Accommodation, corridors |
| **Heat detector (rate-of-rise)** | Air expansion in diaphragm | Galley, engine room |
| **Flame detector (IR/UV)** | Spectral emission of flame | Engine room, paint store |
| **Hydrocarbon gas detector** | Catalytic bead / IR absorption | Pump room, double bottom |
| **Bilge level float switch** | Magnetic float reed switch | Engine room bilge well |
| **Oily-water separator monitor** | Capacitance probe on effluent | 15 ppm bilge separator outlet |

---

## 2.6 Cargo & Ballast Tank Instruments

- **Radar-type level gauge** (non-contact, FMCW at 10 GHz) — used in petrochemical tankers; safe for flammable cargo.
- **Servo level gauge** — a servo-follower touches the liquid surface; the displacement of the float is encoded. Accuracy ≈ **± 1 mm** — used for **ullage and trim** calculation on crude carriers.
- **Ballast tank pressure transducer** — ensures positive pressure on empty ballast tanks when entering hot ports to prevent hull collapse.

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula sheet** for this topic. Every quantity has its SI unit, and the typical magnitude is given so you can sanity-check answers.

| # | Quantity / Law | Equation | Typical Value / Unit | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Radar range** | $R = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s | Radar, echo sounder |
| 2 | **Maximum unambiguous radar range** | $R_{max} = \dfrac{c \cdot PRI}{2} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot PRF}$ | PRI = pulse repetition interval | Radar design |
| 3 | **Two-way transmission loss (radar)** | $L = 32.5 + 20\log_{10}(R) + 20\log_{10}(f) + \Gamma$ | $R$ in km, $f$ in MHz | Echo strength |
| 4 | **GPS pseudo-range** | $\rho = c \cdot (t_{rx} - t_{tx}) + c \cdot \Delta t_{clock}$ | km | Satellite ranging |
| 5 | **Gyrocompass apparent drift** | $\omega_{app} = \Omega_E \cdot \cos\phi$ | rad/s, $\phi$ = latitude | Latitude compensation |
| 6 | **Gyroscope precession rate** | $\omega_P = \dfrac{\tau}{H}$ | rad/s | Gyro theory |
| 7 | **Strain gauge factor** | $G_F = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$ | 2.0 – 2.1 (constantan) | Hull stress |
| 8 | **Strain to voltage (quarter bridge)** | $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon$ | V | Bridge output |
| 9 | **Wheatstone bridge output** | $V_o \approx \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot (\delta_1 - \delta_2 + \delta_3 - \delta_4)$ | V | Transducer output |
| 10 | **Inclining experiment GM** | $GM = \dfrac{W \cdot d \cdot \cos\theta}{\Delta \cdot \tan\theta}$ | metres | Stability test |
| 11 | **Magnetic flux in generator** | $E = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot N \cdot \phi \cdot k_w$ | V per phase | AVR design |
| 12 | **Bourdon tube pressure** | $P = \dfrac{E \cdot t^2 \cdot \delta}{1.5 \cdot D^2}$ | Pa | Pressure gauge |
| 13 | **RTD resistance (Pt-100)** | $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$ | $\alpha = 3.85 \times 10^{-3}$ /°C | Temperature |
| 14 | **Thermocouple Seebeck EMF** | $V = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} S(T) \, dT$ | mV | EGT |
| 15 | **Hydrostatic level** | $P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | Pa | Tank level |
| 16 | **Magnetic flow meter EMF** | $E = B \cdot D \cdot v$ | V (B=T, D=m, v=m/s) | Sea water flow |
| 17 | **Shaft torsional stress** | $\tau = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi D^3}$ | Pa | Torsionmeter |
| 18 | **Shaft torsional twist** | $\theta = \dfrac{32 T L}{\pi G D^4}$ | rad | Torsionmeter |
| 19 | **Two-way insertion loss (echo sounder)** | $TL = 20 \log_{10} R + 2 \alpha R$ | dB | Depth sounding |
| 20 | **Battery (lead-acid) EMF** | $E = 2.04 + \text{Nernst corr.}$ | V/cell | Emergency power |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Shipyard (Construction Phase):** welding monitors, plate-thickness ultrasonic gauges, alignment lasers, paint-coating thickness gauges, GPS receivers for block erection.
- **Dock Trials (in port):** All engine room alarms, switchboard protection, steering gear feedback, generator paralleling, harbour generator test.
- **Sea Trials (open sea):** Inclining experiment, speed–power trials, fuel-consumption trials, crash-stop, turning circle, steering gear trial under SOLAS.
- **Operational life:** All navigation, safety, and monitoring instruments work 24×7; preventive maintenance based on **Condition-Based Monitoring (CBM)** is now standard.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Class-society standard reference:** Most exam problems assume the vessel follows **Lloyd's Register (LR) Rules for Ships and Naval Submarines** or **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) Part 16 Series** for instrumentation placement.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivations are the **most likely 14-mark question** in a KTU University Exam. Each step is shown explicitly so you can write it on the answer sheet exactly as a Board examiner expects.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Range of a Marine Radar from Pulse-Echo Ranging

A marine radar transmits a high-power microwave pulse of duration $\tau$ at the **Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF)**. The pulse travels to a target at range $R$ and is reflected back. The receiver measures the round-trip time $\Delta t$.

**Given:** A vessel's X-band radar transmits a pulse. The PRF is **1200 Hz**. The display shows a target echo at a round-trip delay of **66.7 μs**. Find the target range and the maximum unambiguous range.

**Step 1 — Fundamental pulse-echo equation:**

The pulse travels outward and back, covering a total distance of $2R$. The time-of-flight is $\Delta t$.

$$2R = c \cdot \Delta t$$

where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s (speed of electromagnetic wave in air, very close to vacuum for ship distances).

**Step 2 — Solve for the range $R$:**

$$R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$R = \frac{(3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}) \cdot (66.7 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s})}{2}$$

$$R = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \cdot 66.7 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$

$$R = \frac{20010}{2} = 10005 \text{ m}$$

$$R \approx 10.0 \text{ km}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum unambiguous range:**

The radar cannot resolve a target whose echo arrives after the next pulse has been transmitted. The Pulse Repetition Interval (PRI) is:

$$PRI = \frac{1}{PRF} = \frac{1}{1200} \text{ s} = 833.33 \, \mu s$$

**Step 5 — Maximum unambiguous range equation:**

$$R_{max} = \frac{c \cdot PRI}{2} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot PRF}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute:**

$$R_{max} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 1200} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2400}$$

$$R_{max} = 1.25 \times 10^5 \text{ m} = 125 \text{ km}$$

**Step 7 — Engineering interpretation:** Any target beyond 125 km will be displayed as a "second-trace echo" at a wrong range, hence marine radars switch PRF to maintain unambiguous display.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Hull Stress using a Quarter-Bridge Strain Gauge

A strain gauge with **gauge factor $G_F = 2.05$** and **resistance $R = 350 \, \Omega$** is bonded to a ship's hull plate. The plate experiences an axial strain $\varepsilon$. The gauge is connected in a **quarter Wheatstone bridge** with excitation $V_{ex} = 5$ V. Find the bridge output voltage for $\varepsilon = 1000 \, \mu\varepsilon$.

**Step 1 — Change in resistance of the active gauge:**

By definition of the gauge factor:

$$G_F = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} \;\Longrightarrow\; \Delta R = R \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$\varepsilon = 1000 \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-3}$$

$$\Delta R = 350 \cdot 2.05 \cdot 10^{-3} = 0.7175 \, \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Quarter-bridge output voltage equation:**

For a single active gauge in a quarter bridge:

$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$V_o = \frac{5}{4} \cdot 2.05 \cdot 10^{-3}$$

$$V_o = 1.25 \cdot 2.05 \times 10^{-3} = 2.5625 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}$$

$$V_o \approx 2.56 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 5 — Comment:** A typical signal conditioner (e.g., HBM QuantumX) amplifies this 2.56 mV to a 0–10 V or 4–20 mA signal for the bridge console.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Inclining Experiment (Metacentric Height)

A 12,000-tonne naval frigate undergoes an inclining experiment. A **W = 30 tonne** weight is shifted by **d = 8 m** across the deck. The pendulum inclinometer reads a heeling angle of $\theta = 2.5°$. Find the metacentric height $GM$.

**Step 1 — Heeling moment from shifting weight:**

The moment causing list is:

$$M_{heel} = W \cdot d \cdot \cos\theta$$

**Step 2 — Righting moment equals heeling moment at equilibrium:**

For a small angle (in radians), the righting moment is $M_R = \Delta \cdot GM \cdot \theta$ (in radians). Equating:

$$W \cdot d \cdot \cos\theta = \Delta \cdot GM \cdot \tan\theta$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $GM$:**

$$GM = \frac{W \cdot d \cdot \cos\theta}{\Delta \cdot \tan\theta}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$W = 30 \times 10^3 \text{ kg} \quad \text{(or, use tonnes consistently)}$$

$$\Delta = 12{,}000 \text{ tonnes} \quad ; \quad d = 8 \text{ m} \quad ; \quad \theta = 2.5°$$

$$GM = \frac{30 \cdot 8 \cdot \cos(2.5°)}{12{,}000 \cdot \tan(2.5°)}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate trigonometric values:**

$$\cos(2.5°) = 0.99905$$

$$\tan(2.5°) = 0.04366$$

**Step 6 — Compute numerator and denominator:**

$$\text{Numerator} = 30 \cdot 8 \cdot 0.99905 = 239.77 \text{ tonne·m}$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 12{,}000 \cdot 0.04366 = 523.92 \text{ tonne·rad (equivalently tonnes)}$$

**Step 7 — Final value of $GM$:**

$$GM = \frac{239.77}{523.92} = 0.4576 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{GM \approx 0.46 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 8 — Engineering interpretation:** For a frigate, GM in the range 0.4 – 0.6 m is normal — providing a **stiff, seakindly** ship. A value below 0.15 m would make the ship dangerously tender (slow roll period, large heel angles).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selecting the Right Instrument for Each Shipboard Application

| Shipboard Application | Suitable Instrument | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Measuring depth below keel | Echo sounder (200 kHz) | Direct, reliable, no human error |
| True bearing in mid-ocean | Gyrocompass | Earth-rotation based, no magnetic deviation |
| Hull-deck stress | Foil strain gauge (full-bridge) | High accuracy, temperature compensated |
| Lubricating oil pressure | Bourdon-tube gauge | Direct reading, no power needed |
| Jacket cooling water temperature | Pt-100 RTD | Linear, stable, ranges to 600 °C |
| Exhaust gas temperature | K-type thermocouple | High range (0–1100 °C), fast response |
| Sea-water flow | Magnetic flow meter | Non-obstructive, immune to salt conductivity |
| Cargo tank level (flammable) | Radar (FMCW) level gauge | Non-contact, intrinsically safe |
| Bilge level (alarm) | Float switch + capacitance probe | Fail-safe dual |
| Generator voltage | Potential transformer + AVR | Closed-loop, 440 V class |
| Insulation health | IR monitor (Bender) | Continuous online |
| Shaft RPM | Magnetic pickup + toothed wheel | No contact, no wear |
| Rudder angle | Absolute encoder (SSI) | 12-bit, ± 0.1° |
| Bridge–engine communication | Shaft-line telegraph / voice pipe | Mechanical backup, no cyber risk |
| LNG tank temperature | PT-100 Class A + cryogenic accuracy | –163 °C application |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In the KTU answer sheet:** Always state the **physical principle** of the chosen instrument *first*, *then* the **engineering reason** for the choice. This is the Board-valuation key pattern for "selection" type 7-mark sub-questions.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical: Echo Sounder for Port Entry

A supertanker enters a port with a maximum permissible **Under-Keel Clearance (UKC) of 1.5 m**. The hull draft is **22.0 m**. The echo sounder displays a depth of **23.2 m** below the transducer. The transducer is fitted **1.0 m below the waterline** on the hull. Find the safe UKC and check if port entry is permitted.

**Step 1 — Depth of water from seabed to transducer face = 23.2 m (echo sounder reading).**

**Step 2 — Depth from waterline to seabed:**

$$D_{wl} = D_{trans} + \text{transducer depth below WL}$$

$$D_{wl} = 23.2 + 1.0 = 24.2 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Static draft of vessel = 22.0 m.**

**Step 4 — Static UKC:**

$$UKC_{static} = 24.2 - 22.0 = 2.2 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Account for squat (dynamic sinkage) and tide correction:**
Assume squat = 0.4 m, tide correction = +0.3 m (favourable tide).

$$UKC_{dynamic} = UKC_{static} - \text{squat} + \text{tide correction}$$

$$UKC_{dynamic} = 2.2 - 0.4 + 0.3 = 2.1 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Compare to permissible UKC of 1.5 m:**

Since $2.1 \, \text{m} > 1.5 \, \text{m}$, port entry is **permitted with adequate safety margin**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam tip:** If the question gives you a 7-mark sub-part to "select the most appropriate instrument for underwater depth measurement and explain with calculation", combine a working formula + numerical result + one-sentence justification. The examiner awards the 7th mark for the *engineering conclusion*.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The questions below follow the **2024 KTU ESE (End-Semester Examination)** pattern. Part A is 3 marks each. Part B is 14 marks with internal choice (Part A 7 marks + Part B 7 marks). **Bloom's level tags** are included in each question's metadata.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: List any three bridge navigational instruments used on a modern ship. State the physical principle of operation of the gyrocompass.** **[3 marks] [CO4, Bloom: Remember/Understand]**

**Model Answer:**

1. Gyrocompass, GPS receiver, Marine Radar (X-band).
2. The gyrocompass uses a **fast-spinning rotor whose spin axis is forced to align with Earth's meridian** through the **precessional effect** caused by Earth's rotation ($\omega_E = 7.27 \times 10^{-5}$ rad/s). Apparent drift is $\omega_{app} = \omega_E \cdot \cos\phi$, where $\phi$ is latitude — the instrument compensates for this drift using a weight-pendulum at the bottom of the rotor case.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Distinguish between a strain gauge and a load cell. Why are strain gauges preferred for ship hull-stress monitoring during sea trials? Give one reason.** **[3 marks] [CO4, Bloom: Understand]**

**Model Answer:**

- A strain gauge is a single resistive element; a **load cell** is a complete transducer that contains one or more strain gauges in a Wheatstone bridge configuration with temperature compensation and overload protection.
- Strain gauges are preferred for **hull stress monitoring** because they can be **directly bonded to curved, large surfaces** of the hull at multiple points without altering the structural behaviour, whereas a load cell would only sense the force at one discrete location.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 marks, with internal choice)

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Option A)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Question, Module 4, CO4]**

**Q: (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a marine radar. Derive the equation for the maximum unambiguous range. A vessel's radar has a PRF of 800 Hz. Calculate the maximum unambiguous range.** **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Working of marine radar (5 marks):**

The marine radar consists of:
1. **Transmitter:** A magnetron generates a high-power microwave pulse (typically 25 kW peak, 0.1–1 μs pulse width) at X-band (9.4 GHz).
2. **Duplexer / TR Cell:** Routes the transmitted pulse to the antenna and protects the receiver during transmission.
3. **Antenna:** A slotted-waveguide array rotates at 24–42 rpm, producing a fan beam ~1° wide horizontally.
4. **Receiver:** A superheterodyne receiver amplifies the weak echo and down-converts to IF.
5. **Display:** Plan Position Indicator (PPI) — a circular CRT/LCD with the ship at the centre, echo brightness indicating target strength, radial distance indicating range, and bearing indicated by the angular position.

**Part (a) — Maximum unambiguous range derivation (2 marks):**

The pulse must return to the receiver **before the next pulse is transmitted** to avoid range ambiguity. Let PRI be the pulse repetition interval, and PRF its reciprocal.

$$R_{max} = \frac{c \cdot PRI}{2} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot PRF}$$

**Part (a) — Numerical substitution:**

Given $PRF = 800$ Hz:

$$R_{max} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 800} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1600}$$

$$R_{max} = 1.875 \times 10^5 \text{ m} = 187.5 \text{ km}$$

**Q: (b) Explain the function of the following instruments in shipbuilding: (i) Inclinometer in the inclining experiment, (ii) Servo level gauge in a tanker, (iii) Insulation resistance monitor.** **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Inclinometer in the inclining experiment (2.5 marks):**
- A long pendulum (or U-tube manometer) is suspended inside a quiet compartment near the ship's centre.
- Known weights (10–50 t) are shifted across the deck by a measured distance.
- The heeling angle (typically 1° to 4°) is read from the inclinometer.
- The result is used to compute **GM (metacentric height) = (W·d·cosθ) / (Δ·tanθ)**, which determines the ship's initial stability.
- A second-pass check is done by moving the weight in the opposite direction and re-reading.

**(ii) Servo level gauge in a tanker (2.5 marks):**
- Used for **ullage and trim** in crude-oil and product tankers.
- A small **servo-driven float** is lowered through a stilling well until it contacts the liquid surface.
- The vertical displacement of the float is **encoded by a precision potentiometer or LVDT**.
- Accuracy ≈ **± 1 mm**; suitable for cargo-accountability and customs declarations.

**(iii) Insulation resistance monitor (2 marks):**
- Continuously measures the **line-to-ground insulation resistance** of the ungrounded (IT) shipboard electrical distribution network.
- Detects gradual insulation deterioration due to humidity, salt, and vibration **before it becomes a phase-to-ground short circuit**.
- Class-society rule: **minimum 100 kΩ per phase for ≤ 1 kV systems** is the trip threshold; some monitors (Bender ISOMETER) sound pre-alarm at 300 kΩ.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Option B)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Question, Module 4, CO4]**

**Q: (a) Describe the working principle of a magnetic flow meter. Why is it preferred for sea-water flow measurement on ships? A magnetic flow meter has a pipe diameter of 200 mm, magnetic flux density of 0.4 T, and the fluid velocity is 1.5 m/s. Calculate the induced EMF.** **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Working principle (3 marks):**
A magnetic flow meter is based on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**. When a conductive fluid flows through a magnetic field perpendicular to the direction of flow, an EMF is induced across two electrodes placed perpendicular to both the flow and the field.

$$E = B \cdot D \cdot v$$

**Part (a) — Why preferred for sea water (2 marks):**
- The sea water is highly conductive (≈ 4 S/m), making the induced EMF large and the measurement accurate.
- It has **no moving parts, no pressure drop, and no obstruction**, so it does not trap marine growth or debris.
- It is unaffected by changes in fluid density, viscosity, temperature, or suspended solids.

**Part (a) — Numerical substitution (2 marks):**

Given $D = 200$ mm $= 0.2$ m, $B = 0.4$ T, $v = 1.5$ m/s:

$$E = 0.4 \cdot 0.2 \cdot 1.5 = 0.12 \text{ V} = 120 \text{ mV}$$

**Q: (b) With a block diagram, explain the working of a Power Management System (PMS) on a multi-generator ship. What is preferential tripping?** **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (b) — PMS working (5 marks):**
- A **PLC-based PMS** continuously monitors the **bus voltage, bus frequency, and total load current** through potential transformers (PT) and current transformers (CT).
- It executes a **load-dependent start-stop logic**: e.g., start DG-2 when load exceeds 70% of running capacity, start DG-3 when load exceeds 90%.
- It manages **automatic generator paralleling** — synchronising frequency, phase angle, and voltage before closing the breaker.
- It controls **preferential trip logic** during overload.
- Provides HMI on the ECR and remote annunciation on the bridge.
- Records all events for class-society audit.

**Part (b) — Preferential tripping (2 marks):**
- When the running generators are unable to meet the demand (e.g., a generator trips), the PMS sheds non-essential loads in a **pre-programmed priority order** to protect the bus.
- **Priority 1 (shed first):** Galley, air-conditioning, laundry, water-maker.
- **Priority 2 (shed later):** Cargo hold fans, bow thruster.
- **Never shed:** Steering gear, navigation lights, fire-fighting pumps, radio.
- The total sheddable load is typically 20–30% of the running generator capacity.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit errors:** Forgetting to convert mm → m, °C → K, or microstrain → strain. Marks deducted in numerical sub-parts.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in radar range:** Many students write $R = c \cdot \Delta t$ instead of $R = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$. This is the **#1 mark-losing error** for this topic.
> 3. **Confusing $G_F$ (gauge factor, dimensionless) with $\Delta R$ (resistance change, in ohms):** $G_F$ has no unit. Mention this in the answer.
> 4. **Omitting the engineering conclusion** in selection-type questions. After writing a formula and a number, always end with **"∴ the system meets / does not meet the requirement"** to earn the application mark.
> 5. **In inclining experiment derivation:** $\theta$ is in **radians** inside the $\tan$ and $\theta$-multiplication terms. Converting $2.5°$ to $0.04366$ rad is essential.
> 6. **Magnetic flow meter:** Many students wrongly say "it works for oil and air". It works **only for electrically conductive liquids** (water, slurry, sea water). It will **not** work for hydrocarbons or gases.
> 7. **Gyrocompass vs magnetic compass:** A common mistake is to say "gyrocompass is more accurate because it uses magnets". The gyrocompass uses **no magnets** — it uses the **spinning-rotor precession effect** under Earth's rotation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this list on the night before the exam. Each bullet is a 1-mark recall point.**

### A. Navigation & Bridge
- [ ] Gyrocompass uses **Earth's rotation + precession**; **not** magnetism.
- [ ] X-band = **9.4 GHz** (3 cm λ); S-band = **3 GHz** (10 cm λ).
- [ ] Radar range: $R = c \Delta t / 2$ (the factor of **2** is non-negotiable).
- [ ] Max unambiguous range: $R_{max} = c / (2 \cdot PRF)$.
- [ ] Echo sounder transducer at **200 kHz or 50 kHz**, mounted flush with the hull.
- [ ] AIS broadcasts every **2–10 s** on VHF.
- [ ] SOLAS requires **1 radar X-band and 1 S-band** on vessels > 3000 GT.

### B. Engine Room
- [ ] EGT measured with **Type K thermocouple** (Chromel–Alumel); 0–1100 °C.
- [ ] Lube oil pressure with **Bourdon tube** (C-bourdon for high pressure, helical for higher).
- [ ] Sea water flow with **magnetic flow meter**: $E = B D v$.
- [ ] Jacket cooling temperature with **Pt-100 RTD** ($\alpha = 3.85 \times 10^{-3}$ /°C).
- [ ] UMS = Unmanned Machinery Space; requires redundant instrumentation.

### C. Hull & Sea Trials
- [ ] Strain gauge: $G_F = (\Delta R/R)/\varepsilon$, typical $G_F = 2.0$–2.1.
- [ ] Quarter-bridge output: $V_o = (V_{ex}/4) \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon$.
- [ ] Inclining experiment: $GM = (W d \cos\theta) / (\Delta \tan\theta)$.
- [ ] Inclining weight is **10–50 t**; heeling angle is **1–4°**.

### D. Electrical
- [ ] Marine standard: **440 V, 60 Hz** (some ships 380 V, 50 Hz).
- [ ] Generator EMF: $E = 4.44 f N \phi k_w$.
- [ ] AVR holds voltage to **± 2.5%** of nominal.
- [ ] Insulation monitor threshold: **100 kΩ/phase** for ≤ 1 kV.

### E. Safety
- [ ] Smoke detector: optical, light-scattering.
- [ ] Heat detector: rate-of-rise, dual-element.
- [ ] Bilge alarm: float switch + capacitance probe (fail-safe dual).
- [ ] Oily-water monitor must trip at **15 ppm** (MARPOL).

### F. Cargo & Tankers
- [ ] Radar FMCW level gauge: **non-contact**, intrinsically safe.
- [ ] Servo level gauge: **± 1 mm** accuracy, contact-type.
- [ ] Inert gas O₂ analyser on tankers: trip at **8% O₂** (safety).
- [ ] LNG cargo: Pt-100 Class A at **–163 °C**.

### G. Communication
- [ ] GMDSS = Global Maritime Distress and Safety System.
- [ ] VHF DSC = Digital Selective Calling on **Channel 70**.
- [ ] INMARSAT: geostationary satellites for ship-shore comm.
- [ ] EPIRB: 406 MHz satellite beacon.
- [ ] SART: 9 GHz radar transponder, used in lifeboats.

### H. Standards
- [ ] **IEC 60945** for marine electronics environmental testing.
- [ ] **IP X6** = water jets; **IP X7** = immersion to 1 m.
- [ ] **NMEA 0183** = serial protocol; **NMEA 2000** = CAN-based.
- [ ] **Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, IRS, BV, RINA** = major class societies.

### I. Top Exam Traps
- [ ] Always state the **physical principle** first; **engineering justification** second; **number** third; **interpretation** fourth.
- [ ] A 7-mark sub-question = 1 principle (2 marks) + 1 working (2 marks) + 1 diagram (2 marks) + 1 conclusion (1 mark).
- [ ] A 14-mark question = 2 sub-parts of 7 marks each. Plan **8 minutes per sub-part**.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 — Application of Various Instruments in Shipbuilding. Best of luck for the KTU 2024 University Examination!**
